questions.question: The complex numbers z = x + iy which satisfy the equation $\left|\frac{z-3 i}{z+3 i}\right|=1$ lie on
questions.question_raw: The complex numbers z = x + iy which satisfy the equation {tex}\left|\frac{z-3 i}{z+3 i}\right|=1{/tex} lie on

options.text: a circle passing through the origin

questions.explanation:
Given{tex}\left|\frac{z-3 i}{z+3 i}\right|=1{/tex}
Putting z = x + iy , we get
{tex}\left|\frac{x+i y-3 i}{x+i y+3 i}\right|=1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow\left| {{{x+iy - 3i} }} \right| =\left| {{{x+iy + 3i} }} \right|{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow\left| {{{x+i(y - 3)} }} \right| =\left| {{{x+i(y + 3)} }} \right|{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt { { x }^{ 2 }+{ \left( { y-3 } \right) }^{ 2 } } =\sqrt { { x }^{ 2 }+{ \left( { y+3 } \right) }^{ 2 } } {/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \left| x+iy \right| =\sqrt { { x }^{ 2 }+{ y }^{ 2 } } \right]{/tex}
Squaring on both sides, we get
{tex}{ x }^{ 2 }+{ \left( { y-3 } \right) }^{ 2 }={ x }^{ 2 }+{ \left( { y+3 } \right) }^{ 2 }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow { x }^{ 2 }+{ y }^{ 2 }-6y+9={ x }^{ 2 }+{ y }^{ 2 }+6y+9{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow 12y=0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow y=0{/tex}
Which means the X-axis.

questions.question: The inequality |z - 4| < |z - 2| represents the region given by
questions.question_raw: The inequality |z - 4| < |z - 2| represents the region given by
questions.explanation:
Given |z - 4| < |z - 2|
Putting z = x + iy, we get
|x + iy - 4| < |x + iy - 2|
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |(x - 4) + iy| < |(x - 2) + iy| 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{(x-4)^{2}+y^{2}}{/tex} < {tex}\sqrt{(x-2)^{2}+y^{2}}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} |x + iy| = {tex}\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}}{/tex}]
Squaring on both sides we get
(x - 4)2 + y2 < (x - 2)2 + y2 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 + 16 - 8x + y2 < x2 + 4 - 4x + y2 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 16 - 4 < 8x - 4x
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 12 < 4x
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x > 3

questions.question: If Z = $\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}+\frac{i}{2}$, Z^3 then equals
questions.question_raw: If Z = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}+\frac{i}{2}{/tex}, Z3 then equals
questions.explanation:
Given Z = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}+\frac{i}{2}{/tex} 
Z3 = {tex}\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}+\frac{i}{2}\right)^{3}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} (a + b)3 = a3 + b3 + 3ab(a + b)]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Z3 = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}^{3}+\frac{i^{3}}{2}^{3}+3 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \cdot \frac{i}{2}\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}+\frac{i}{2}\right){/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Z3 = {tex}\frac{3 \sqrt{3}}{8}+\frac{i^{3}}{8}+\frac{3 \sqrt{3} i}{8}(\sqrt{3}+i){/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Z3 = {tex}\frac{3 \sqrt{3}-i+9 i-3 \sqrt{3}}{8}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} i2 = -1]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Z3 = {tex}\frac{8 i}{8}{/tex} = i

questions.question: If ${\left( {\sqrt 3 + i} \right)^{10}} = a + ib;a,b \in R,$ then a and b are respectively :
questions.question_raw: If {tex}{\left( {\sqrt 3 + i} \right)^{10}} = a + ib;a,b \in R,{/tex} then a and b are respectively :
questions.explanation:
First we will find the polar representation of the complex number {tex}\sqrt { 3 } +i{/tex}
Let {tex}\sqrt{3}+i=r(\cos \theta+i \sin \theta) \Rightarrow r \cos \theta=\sqrt{3} a n d \quad r \sin \theta=1{/tex}
{tex}\therefore r^{2}\left(\cos ^{2} \theta+\sin ^{2} \theta\right)=3+1=4 \Rightarrow r^{2}=4 \Rightarrow r=2{/tex}

Now {tex}cos\theta =\frac { \sqrt { 3 } }{ 2 } ,\quad sin\theta =\frac { 1 }{ 2 } {/tex} , both are positive .

So Amplitude {tex}=\theta =\frac { \Pi }{ 6 } {/tex}

Hence {tex}\sqrt { 3 } +i=2\left( cos\frac { \Pi }{ 6 } +isin\frac { \Pi }{ 6 } \right) =2{ e }^{ \frac { i\Pi }{ 6 } }{/tex}

Now,
{tex}(\sqrt{3}+i)^{10}=\left(2 e^{\frac{i \pi}{6}}\right)^{10}=2^{10} e^{\frac{i 5 \pi}{3}}=2^{10}\left(\cos \left(\frac{5 \Pi}{3}\right)+i \sin \left(\frac{5 \Pi}{3}\right)\right){/tex}
{tex}=2^{10}\left(\cos \left(2 \Pi-\frac{\Pi}{3}\right)+i \sin \left(2 \Pi-\frac{\Pi}{3}\right)\right)=2^{10}\left(\cos \left(\frac{-\pi}{3}\right)+i \sin \left(\frac{-\pi}{3}\right)\right){/tex}
{tex}=2^{10}\left(\cos \left(\frac{\Pi}{3}\right)-i \sin \left(\frac{\Pi}{3}\right)\right)=2^{10}\left(\frac{1}{2}-i \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)=2^{10}\left(\frac{1-\sqrt{3} i}{2}\right)=2^{9}(1-\sqrt{3} i){/tex}

Hence  {tex}{\left( {\sqrt 3 + i} \right)^{10}} = a + ib{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow { 2 }^{ 9 }\left( 1-\sqrt { 3 } i \right) =a+ib{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow a={ 2 }^{ 9 }=512\quad and\quad b={ -2 }^{ 9 }\sqrt { 3 } =-512\sqrt { 3 } \\ {/tex}

questions.question: Let $x,y \in R,$then x + iy is a non real complex number if
questions.question_raw: Let {tex}x,y \in R,{/tex}then x + iy is a non real complex number if
questions.explanation: If a complex number has to be a  non real complex number  then its imaginary part should not be zero {tex}\Rightarrow iy\neq 0\Rightarrow y\neq 0{/tex}

questions.question: Let x, y $ \in$ R, then x + iy is a purely imaginary number if
questions.question_raw: Let x, y {tex} \in{/tex} R, then x + iy is a purely imaginary number if
questions.explanation:
A purely imaginary number is a complex number which has only an imaginary part (iy) 
But if y = 0 the complex number iy will become 0 which is real.
Hence the condition for a number to be purely imaginary is x = 0 and {tex} y\neq 0{/tex}

questions.question: Multiplicative inverse of the non zero complex number x + iy (x, y $\in$ R)
questions.question_raw: Multiplicative inverse of the non zero complex number x + iy (x, y {tex}\in{/tex} R)
questions.explanation: Multiplicative inverse of the complex number x + iy = {tex}\frac{1}{x+i y}=\frac{1}{x+i y} \cdot \frac{x-i y}{x-i y}=\frac{x-i y}{x^2+y^2}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} i2 = -1]

questions.question: If z_1 and z_2 are non-real complex numbers such that z_1 and z_2 and z_1z_2 are real numbers, then
questions.question_raw: If z1 and z2 are non-real complex numbers such that z1 and z2 and z1z2 are real numbers, then
questions.explanation:
Let z1 = x1 + iy1 and z2 = x2 + iy2 
Given z1 and z2 are non-real complex numbers, so we have {tex}{ y }_{ 1 }\neq 0,{ y }_{ 2 }\neq 0{/tex} 
z1 + z2 = (x + iy1) + (x2 + iy2) = (x1 + x2) + i(y1 + y2) 
z1 + z2 is a real number 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y1 + y2 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y1 = -y2 .....(i)
Now z1z2 = (x1 + iy1) {tex}\cdot{/tex} (x2 + iy2) = (x1x2 - y1y2) + i(x1y1 + x1y2)
z1z2 is a real number {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2y1 + x1y2 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2y1 = -x1y2 ....(ii)
Now from (i) and (ii) we get {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2y1 = x1y1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 = x1  
Hence we get z1 = x2 - iy2 and z2 = x2 + iy2 
So {tex}{z_1} = \overline {{z_2}} {/tex}

questions.question: The inequality |z - 6| < |z - 2| represents the region given by
questions.question_raw: The inequality |z - 6| < |z - 2| represents the region given by
questions.explanation:
Given |z - 6| < |z - 2|
Putting z = x + iy, we get
|x + iy - 6| <|x + iy - 2|
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |(x - 6) + iy| < |(x - 2) + iy|
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{(x-6)^{2}+y^{2}}{/tex} < {tex}\sqrt{(x-2)^{2}+y^{2}}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} |x + iy| = {tex}\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}}{/tex}]
Squaring on both sides we get
(x - 6)2 + y2 < (x - 2)2 + y2 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 + 36 - 12x + y2 < x2 + 4 - 4x + y2 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 36 - 4 < 12x - 4x
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 32 < 8x
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x > 4

questions.question: z + $\overline {z} \ne 0$ if and only if
questions.question_raw: z + {tex}\overline {z} \ne 0{/tex} if and only if
questions.explanation:
Let Z = x + iy then we have {tex}\bar { Z } {/tex} = x - iy
So Z + {tex}\bar { Z }{/tex} = 2x
Now z + {tex}\overline {z} \ne 0{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x {tex}\ne{/tex} 0 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x {tex}\ne{/tex} 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Re(z) {tex}\ne{/tex} 0

questions.question: Distance of the representative of the number 1 + i from the origin (in Argand’s diagram) is
questions.question_raw: Distance of the representative of the number 1 + i from the origin (in Argand’s diagram) is
questions.explanation:
Let P(x, y) represent the complex number Z = x + iy in the complex plane(Argand plane) and let O(0, 0) be the origin
Then we have OP ={tex}\sqrt { { x }^{ 2 }+{ y }^{ 2 } } {/tex} 
Here Z = 1 + i so that P(x, y) = P(1, 1)
Hence OP ={tex}\sqrt { 1+1 } =\sqrt { 2}{/tex}

questions.question: If $\omega $ is a cube root of unity, then $\left( {1 + \omega } \right)\left( {1 + {\omega ^2}} \right)\left( {1 + {\omega ^4}} \right)\left( {1 + {\omega ^8}} \right)...$ upto 2n factors is
questions.question_raw: If {tex}\omega {/tex} is a cube root of unity, then {tex}\left( {1 + \omega } \right)\left( {1 + {\omega ^2}} \right)\left( {1 + {\omega ^4}} \right)\left( {1 + {\omega ^8}} \right)...{/tex} upto 2n factors is
questions.explanation:
{tex}\left( {1 + \omega } \right)\left( {1 + {\omega ^2}} \right)\left( {1 + {\omega ^4}} \right)\left( {1 + {\omega ^8}} \right){/tex} .... upto 2n factors
= {tex}\left( {1 + \omega } \right)\left( {1 + {\omega ^2}} \right)\left( {1 + {\omega ^3.\omega}} \right)\left( {1 + {\omega ^3.\omega ^3.\omega^2}} \right)...{/tex} upto 2n factors
= {tex}\left( {1 + \omega } \right)\left( {1 + {\omega ^2}} \right)\left( {1 +{\omega}} \right)\left( {1 + {\omega^2}} \right)....{/tex} upto 2n factors [ since {tex}{ \omega }^{ 3 }=1{/tex} ]
={tex}\left(- {\omega ^2 } \right)\left( {- {\omega }} \right)\left(- {\omega ^2 } \right)\left( {- {\omega }} \right)...{/tex} upto 2n factors  {tex}\left[ \because 1+\omega +{ \omega }^{ 2 }=0 \right] {/tex} 
= {tex}\left(- {\omega ^2 } \right)\left( {- {\omega }} \right)\left(- {\omega ^2 } \right)\left( {- {\omega }} \right)...{/tex}upto 2n factors  
= {tex}\left[\left(-\omega^{2}\right)(-\omega)\right]\left[\left(-\omega^{2}\right)(-\omega)\right]{/tex} ... n factors
=  {tex}\omega^{3} \cdot \omega^{3} \cdot \omega^{3} \dots{/tex} n factors [Since {tex}{ \omega }^{ 3 }{/tex} = 1]
= 1.1.1 .... n factos
= 1n = 1

questions.question: If |z - 2| = |z - 6| then locus of z is given by :
questions.question_raw: If |z - 2| = |z - 6| then locus of z is given by :

options.text: a straight line parallel to x axis
a straight line parallel to y axis

questions.explanation:
|z - 2| = |z - 6|
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |x - 2 + iy| = |x - 6 + iy|
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{(x-2)^{2}+y^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{(x-6)^{2}+y^{2}}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 + 4 - 4x + y2 = x2 + 36 - 12x + y2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 8x = 32
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 4
We have any line of the form x = a constant is a line parallel to Y-axis.

questions.question: If k, l, m, n are four consecutive integers, then i^k + i^l + i^m + i^n is equal to :
questions.question_raw: If k, l, m, n are four consecutive integers, then ik + il + im + in is equal to :
questions.explanation:
Given k, l, m, n are four consecutive integers,
So we have l = k + 1, m = k + 2, n = k + 3
ik + il + im + in
= ik + ik+1 + ik+2 + ik+3
= ik (1 + i + i2 + i3) 
= ik(1 + i - 1 - i)
= ik.0 =  0  [{tex}\because{/tex} i2 = -1 and i3 = -i]

questions.question: i^2 + i^4 + i^6 + ..... up to 2k + 1 terms, for all k belongs to natural numbers N.
questions.question_raw: i2 + i4 + i6 + ..... up to 2k + 1 terms, for all k belongs to natural numbers N.
questions.explanation:
i2 + i4 + i6 + ..... upto 2k + 1 terms
i2 + (i2)2 + (i2)4 ..... upto 2k + 1 terms
= (i2)1 + (i2)2 + (i2)3 ..... + (i2)2k + (i2)2k + 1  
= (i2)1 + [(i2)2 + (i2)3] + [((i2)4 + (i2)5] + ..... + [(i2)2k + (i2)2k + 1]  [{tex}\because{/tex} i2 = -1]
= (-1) + [1 + (-1)] + [1 + (-1)] + .....+ [1 + (-1)]
= -1

questions.question: 1 + i + i^2 + i^3 + .... up to 4n terms is equal to
questions.question_raw: 1 + i + i2 + i3 + .... up to 4n terms is equal to
questions.explanation:
1 + i + i2 + i3 + i4 + i5 + ..... up to 4n terms
= {tex}1 + i + {i^2} + {i^2} .i+ {i^2} .{i^2}+ {i^2} .{i^2}.i+...{/tex} up to 4n terms
= {tex}1 + i + {(-1)} + {(-1.i)} + {(-1).(-1)} + {(-1).(-1).i} +...{/tex} up to 4n terms
= {tex}1 + i + {(-1)} + {(-i)} + {(1)} + {(i)} +.......{/tex} up to 4n terms
= {tex}[1 + i + {(-1)} + {(-i)} ]+ [ {(1)} + {(i)} + {(-1)} + {(-i)}]...{/tex} up to 4n terms  [combining four terms to get a single term]
= 0 + 0 + 0 ... up to n terms
= 0.n = 0

questions.question: $1 + {i^2} + {i^4} + {i^6} + {i^8} + .......$ up to 1001 terms is equal to
questions.question_raw: {tex}1 + {i^2} + {i^4} + {i^6} + {i^8} + .......{/tex} up to 1001 terms is equal to
questions.explanation:
{tex}1+i^{2}+i^{4}+i^{6}+i^{8}+\ldots \ldots \ldots . \text { upto } 1001 \text { terms }{/tex}
{tex}=\left(i^{2}\right)^{0}+\left(i^{2}\right)^{1}+\left(i^{2}\right)^{2}+\left(i^{2}\right)^{3}+\ldots \ldots . \text { upto } 1001 \quad \text { terms }{/tex}
{tex}=\left(i^{2}\right)^{0}+\left(i^{2}\right)^{1}+\left(i^{2}\right)^{2}+\left(i^{2}\right)^{3}+\ldots \ldots \ldots+\left(i^{2}\right)^{1000}{/tex}
{tex}=\left[\left(i^{2}\right)^{0}+\left(i^{2}\right)^{1}\right]+\left[\left(i^{2}\right)^{2}+\left(i^{2}\right)^{3}\right]+\ldots \ldots \ldots \ldots+\left[\left(i^{2}\right)^{998}+\left(i^{2}\right)^{999}\right]+\left[\left(i^{2}\right)^{1000}\right]{/tex}
{tex}=[1-1]+[1-1]+\ldots \ldots \ldots+[1-1]+1{/tex}
=1

questions.question: If $\omega $ is a non-real cube root of unity and ${\left( {1 + \omega } \right)^9} = a + b\omega ;a,b \in R,$ then a and b are respectively the numbers :
questions.question_raw: If {tex}\omega {/tex} is a non-real cube root of unity and {tex}{\left( {1 + \omega } \right)^9} = a + b\omega ;a,b \in R,{/tex} then a and b are respectively the numbers :
questions.explanation:
We have {tex}(1+\omega)^{9}{/tex} = {tex}\left(-\omega^{2}\right)^{9}{/tex} = {tex}(-1)^{9}\left(\omega^{2}\right)^{9}{/tex} = {tex}-\omega^{18}{/tex} = {tex}-\left(\omega^{3}\right)^{6}{/tex} = (-1)6 = -1 [{tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\omega^{3}=1,1+\omega+\omega^{2}{/tex} = 0]
Given {tex}{\left( {1 + \omega } \right)^9} = a + b\omega {/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -1 = a + b{tex}\omega{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -1 + 0.{tex}\omega{/tex} = a + b{tex}\omega{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = -1, b = 0

questions.question: If |z| = 1, then $\frac{z-1}{z+1}, z \neq-1$ is
questions.question_raw: If |z| = 1, then {tex}\frac{z-1}{z+1}, z \neq-1{/tex} is
questions.explanation:
Let Z = x + iy
Given |z| = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt { { x }^{ 2 }+{ y }^{ 2 } } =1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 + y2 = 1
Now, {tex}\frac{Z-1}{Z+1}=\frac{x+i y-1}{x+i y+1}=\frac{x-1+i y}{x+1+i y}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{x-1+i y}{x+1+i y} \cdot \frac{x+1-i y}{x+1-i y}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\left(x^{2}-1\right)+y^{2}+i[y(x+1)-y(x-1)]}{(x+1)^{2}+(y)^{2}}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{x^{2}+y^{2}-1+2 i y}{(x+1)^{2}+(y)^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1-1+2 i y}{(x+1)^{2}+(y)^{2}}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} x2 + y2 = 1] 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{Z-1}{Z+1}{/tex} = 0 + {tex}\frac{2 y}{(x+1)^{2}+(y)^{2}} i{/tex}
Which is purely imaginary number.
Also when y = 0, the number will become zero

questions.question: The equation |4z - 1| = 4|z - 5| represents :
questions.question_raw: The equation |4z - 1| = 4|z - 5| represents :
questions.explanation:
Given |4z - 1| = 4|z - 5|
Let Z = x + iy 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |4(x + iy) - 1| = 4|x + iy - 5|
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |(4x - 1) + 4iy| = 4(x - 5) + iy|
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{(4 x-1)^{2}+(4 y)^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}4 \sqrt{(x-5)^{2}+(y)^{2}}{/tex} 
Squaring on both sides we get
(4x - 1)2 + (4y)2 = 16[(x - 5)2 + (y)2]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 16x2 + 1 - 8x + 16y2 = 16[x2 + 25 - 10x + y2]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 16x2 + 1 - 8x + 16y2 - 16x2 - 400 + 160x - 16y2 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 152x - 399 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\frac{399}{152}{/tex}
We have x = a constant is a straight line parallel to Y-axis.

questions.question: If ${\left( { - 1 + \sqrt 3 i} \right)^n} + {\left( { - 1 - \sqrt 3 i} \right)^n} = {2^n}\alpha $ then $\alpha $ is equal to :
questions.question_raw: If {tex}{\left( { - 1 + \sqrt 3 i} \right)^n} + {\left( { - 1 - \sqrt 3 i} \right)^n} = {2^n}\alpha {/tex} then {tex}\alpha {/tex} is equal to :
questions.explanation:
We have {tex}-1+i \sqrt{3}=2 \omega{/tex} and {tex}-1-i \sqrt{3}=2 \omega^{2}{/tex} 
Now  
{tex}(-1+\sqrt{3} i)^{n}+(-1-\sqrt{3} i)^{n}{/tex} = {tex}(2 \omega)^{n}+\left(2 \omega^{2}\right)^{n}{/tex} = {tex}2^{n}\left[(\omega)^{n}+\left(\omega^{2}\right)^{n}\right]{/tex}

questions.question: If ( x + iy ) ( 3 – 4i ) = (5 + 12i ) , then $\sqrt {{x^2} + {y^2}} = $
questions.question_raw: If ( x + iy ) ( 3 – 4i ) = (5 + 12i ) , then {tex}\sqrt {{x^2} + {y^2}} = {/tex}
questions.explanation:
Given   ( x + iy ) ( 3 – 4i ) = (5 + 12i )
{tex}\Rightarrow x+i y=\frac{5+12 i}{3-4 i}=\frac{(5+12 i)(3+4 i)}{(3-4 i)(3+4 i)}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{(15-48)+i(20+36)}{9+16}=\frac{-33+56 i}{25}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x=\frac{-33}{25}, y=\frac{56}{25}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}}=\sqrt{\left(\frac{-33}{25}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{56}{25}\right)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{\frac{1089+3136}{625}}=\sqrt{\frac{4225}{625}}=\frac{65}{25}=\frac{13}{5}{/tex}

questions.question: The locus of the equation $\left|\frac{3 z-1}{z-1}\right|$ = 1 is
questions.question_raw: The locus of the equation {tex}\left|\frac{3 z-1}{z-1}\right|{/tex} = 1 is
questions.explanation:
Let Z = x + iy
Now {tex}\left|\frac{3 z-1}{z-1}\right|{/tex} = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |3z - 1| = |z - 1|
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |(3 (x + iy) - 1| = |(x + iy) - 1|
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |(3x - 1) + 3iy| = |(x - y) + iy| 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{(3 x-1)^{2}+(3 y)^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{(x-1)^{2}+(y)^{2}}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (3x - 1)2 + (3y)2 = (x - 1)2 + (y)2 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 9x2 + 1 - 6x + 9y2 = x2 + 1 - 2x + y2 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 8x2 + 8y2 - 4x = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 + y2 - {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}x = 0
We have this is a circle with centre ({tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex}, 0) and radius = {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex}.

questions.question: If $\alpha = \frac z {\overline z}$, then ${\text{|}}\alpha {\text{|}}$ is equal to:
questions.question_raw: If {tex}\alpha = \frac z {\overline z}{/tex}, then {tex}{\text{|}}\alpha {\text{|}}{/tex} is equal to:
questions.explanation:
Given {tex}\alpha = {\frac z {z }}{/tex} 
Then {tex}|\alpha|{/tex} = {tex}\left|\frac{z}{\bar{z}}\right|=\frac{|z|}{|\bar{z}|}{/tex} = 1 [{tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\left|\frac{z_{1}}{z_{2}}\right|=\frac{\left|z_{1}\right|}{\left|z_{2}\right|}{/tex}, {tex}|z|=|\bar{z}|{/tex}]

questions.question: If z = x + iy ; x, y $\in $ R then :

questions.question: The smallest positive integer n for which $\left(\frac{1+i}{1-i}\right)^n=$ 1 is :
questions.question_raw: The smallest positive integer n for which {tex}\left(\frac{1+i}{1-i}\right)^n={/tex} 1 is :
questions.explanation:
We have {tex}\frac { 1+i }{ 1-i } =\frac { 1+i }{ 1-i } \times \frac { 1+i }{ 1+i } =\frac { { \left( 1+i \right) }^{ 2 } }{ 1+1 } {/tex}{tex}=\frac { 1+2i-1 }{ 2 } =\frac { 2i }{ 2 } =i{/tex}
Therefore {tex}\left(\frac{1+i}{1-i}\right)^n=i^n{/tex}
By inspection we have the smallest positive integer such that {tex}{{i} ^n}=1{/tex} is n=4.

questions.question: If $\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}+\frac{i}{2}\right)^5+\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}-\frac{i}{2}\right)^5$, then
questions.question_raw: If {tex}\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}+\frac{i}{2}\right)^5+\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}-\frac{i}{2}\right)^5{/tex}, then
questions.explanation:
{tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}+i}{2}=r(\cos \theta+i \sin \theta) \Rightarrow r \cos \theta=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}{/tex}, {tex} r \sin \theta=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore r^2\left(\cos ^2 \theta+\sin ^2 \theta\right)=\frac{3+1}{4}=1 {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow r^2=1 \Rightarrow r=1{/tex}
{tex}\cos \theta=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}, \quad \sin \theta=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Amplitude = {tex}\theta=\frac{\pi}{6}{/tex}
{tex}\frac { \sqrt { 3 } +i }{ 2 } =1\left( cos\frac { \pi }{ 6 } +isin\frac { \pi }{ 6 } \right) ={ e }^{ \frac { i\pi }{ 6 } }{/tex} ...(i)
Since {tex}\frac { \sqrt { 3 } -i }{ 2 } {/tex} lies in the fourth quadrant, the amplitude {tex}=-\theta =-\frac { \pi }{ 6 } {/tex}
{tex}\frac { \sqrt { 3 } -i }{ 2 } =1\left( cos\frac { -\pi }{ 6 } +isin\frac { -\pi }{ 6 } \right) ={ e }^{ \frac { -i\pi }{ 6 } }{/tex} ...(i)
{tex}z=\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}+i}{2}\right)^5+\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}-i}{2}\right)^5{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow z=\left(e^{\frac{ { i\pi }}{6}}\right)^5+\left(e^{\frac{-i \mathrm{\pi}}{6}}\right)^5=e^{\frac{i 5 \mathrm{\pi}}{6}}+e^{-\frac{i 5 \mathrm{\pi}}{6}}{/tex} = {tex}=\cos \left(\frac{5 \pi}{6}\right)+i \sin \left(\frac{5 \pi}{6}\right)+\cos \left(\frac{-5 \pi}{6}\right){/tex}{tex}+i \sin \left(\frac{-5 \pi}{6}\right)=2 \cos \left(\frac{5 \pi}{6}\right){/tex}
Which is purely real [imaginary part of z = 0]

questions.question: If the cube roots of unity are 1, $\omega ,{\omega ^2},$ then roots of the equation (x - 1)^3 + 8 = 0 are :
questions.question_raw: If the cube roots of unity are 1, {tex}\omega ,{\omega ^2},{/tex} then roots of the equation (x - 1)3 + 8 = 0 are :
questions.explanation:
(x - 1)3 + 8 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x - 1)3 = -8
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x - 1)3 = (-2)3
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left(\frac{x-1}{-2}\right)^{3}{/tex} = 1
Let {tex}\frac { x-1 }{ -2 } {/tex} = m (say)
So we get m3  = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow m=1, \omega ,{\omega ^2}{/tex}  [cube roots of unity]
Now m = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{x-1}{-2}{/tex} = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x - 1 = -2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = -1
m = {tex}\omega{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{x-1}{-2}=\omega{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x - 1 = -2{tex}\omega{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 1 - 2{tex}\omega{/tex} 
Also m = {tex}\omega^2{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{x-1}{-2}=\omega^{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x -1 = {tex}-2 \omega^{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 1 - {tex}2 \omega^{2}{/tex} 
Hence the roots of the given equation are {tex} - 1,1 - 2\omega ,1 - 2{\omega ^2}{/tex}

questions.question: The value of arg (x) when x < 0 is:
questions.question_raw: The value of arg (x) when x < 0 is:
questions.explanation:
Given x {tex}\in {/tex} R and x {tex}<{/tex} 0, therefore  the number is {tex}-x+0i{/tex} and this will be a point inthe second quadrant as x is negative 
We have arg({tex}-x+0i{/tex})={tex}\theta ={/tex}  {tex}\tan ^{ -1 }{ \left| \frac { y }{ x } \right| } =\tan ^{ -1 }{ \left| \frac { 0 }{ -1 } \right| } =\tan ^{ -1 }{ 0 } =0{/tex}
We have in the second quadrant the principal value of argument=  {tex}\Pi -\theta =\Pi -0 =\Pi{/tex}
Hence Arg. ( x ) , x {tex}\in {/tex} R and x {tex}<{/tex} 0 is {tex}\pi {/tex}

questions.question: The number of solutions of the equation Im (z^2) = 0, |z| = 2 is
questions.question_raw: The number of solutions of the equation Im (z2) = 0, |z| = 2 is
questions.explanation:
Let z = x + iy, then we have {tex}\bar { z }{/tex} = x - iy 
x2 = (x + iy)2 = x2 - y2 + 2xyi 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} im(z2) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2xy = 0 .....(i)
|z| = 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}}{/tex} = 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 + y2 = 4 .....(ii)
(x2 - y2)2 = (x2 + y2)2 - 4x2y2 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x2 - y2)2 = 16
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 - y2 = {tex}\pm{/tex} 4 .....(iii)
When x2 - y2 = 4 from (ii) and (iii) we get 2x2 = 8 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 = 4 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\pm{/tex} 2
Now from (i) we get y = 0
Hence z = {tex}\pm 2{/tex} 
Now when x2 - y2 = -4 from (ii) and (iii) we get 2y2 = 8 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y2 = 4 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = {tex}\pm{/tex} 2
Now from (i) we get x = 0
Hence z = {tex}\pm 2i{/tex} 
Therefore the solutions of the equation Im (z2) = 0, |z| = 2 are z = {tex}\pm 2i{/tex} and z = {tex}\pm 2{/tex} and so number of solutions = 4.

Let z = x + iy
Then z - 2 - 3i = (x + iy) - 2 - 3i = (x - 2) + i(y - 3)
Let {tex}\theta {/tex} be the amplitude of z.
Then Amp (z - 2 - 3i) = {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} tan-1{tex}\left(\frac{y-3}{x-2}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{\Pi}{4}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{y-3}{x-2}=\tan \frac{\Pi}{4}{/tex} = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y - 3 = x - 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x - y + 1 = 0

questions.question: If z_1 and z_2 are non-real complex numbers such that |z_1| = |z_2| and Amp. z_1 + Amp. z_2 = $\pi$, then z_1 =
questions.question_raw: If z1 and z2 are non-real complex numbers such that |z1| = |z2| and  Amp. z1 + Amp. z2 = {tex}\pi{/tex}, then z1 =
questions.explanation:
Let z1 = {tex}r_{1}\left(\cos \theta_{1}+i \sin \theta_{1}\right){/tex} and z2 = {tex}r_{2}\left(\cos \theta_{2}+i \sin \theta_{2}\right){/tex}, where r1 = |z1|, r2 = |z2| and {tex}\theta_{1}, \theta_{2}{/tex} are the amplitudef of the complex numbers.
Given |z1| = |z2| and{tex}Amp.{z_{1}} + Amp.{z_2} = \pi {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r1 = r2 and {tex} { \theta }_{ 1 }+{ \theta }_{ 2 }=\pi {/tex} 
Now z1 = {tex}{ r }_{ 1 }\left( cos{ \theta }_{ 1 }+isin{ \theta }_{ 1 } \right){/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} z1 = {tex}r_{2}\left[\cos \left(\pi-\theta_{2}\right)+i \sin \left(\pi-\theta_{2}\right)\right]{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} z1 = {tex}r_{2}\left(-\cos \theta_{2}+i \sin \theta_{2}\right){/tex} = {tex}-\left[r_{2}\left(\cos \theta_{2}-i \sin \theta_{2}\right)\right]{/tex} = {tex}-\overline{z_{2}}{/tex}

questions.question: The complex numbers sinx + i cos2x and cosx – i sin2x are conjugate to each other, for
questions.question_raw: The complex numbers sinx + i cos2x and cosx – i sin2x are conjugate to each other, for
questions.explanation:
Let
z1 = sinx + icos2x and z2 = cosx–isin2x
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}{tex}\overline z{/tex}1 = sinx − icos2x, {tex}\overline z{/tex}2 = cosx + isin2x

Now cos2x = sin2x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2cos2x − 1 = 2sinx cosx

Hence we can say there is no solution for the system of equations

questions.question: The value of ${\left( { - 1 + \sqrt { - 3} } \right)^2} + {\left( { - 1 - \sqrt { - 3} } \right)^2}$is

We have {tex}\sqrt { -3 } =\sqrt { -1.3 } =\sqrt { -1 } \sqrt { 3 } = \sqrt { 3 }i{/tex}
{tex}\therefore-1+\sqrt{-3}=-1+i \sqrt{3}=2 \omega \quad \text { and } \quad-1-\sqrt{-3}=-1-i \sqrt{3}=2 \omega^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\left[\because \omega=\frac{-1+i \sqrt{3}}{2} \quad \text { and } \quad \omega^{2}=\frac{-1-i \sqrt{3}}{2}\right]{/tex}
{tex}(-1+\sqrt{-3})^{2}+(-1-\sqrt{-3})^{2}{/tex} {tex}=(2 \omega)^{2}+\left(2 \omega^{2}\right)^{2}=4 \omega^{2}+4 \omega^{4}=4\left(\omega^{2}+\omega^{3} \cdot \omega\right){/tex}
={tex}4\left(\omega^{2}+\omega\right)=4 \times-1=-4{/tex} {tex}\left[\because \omega^{3}=1,1+\omega+\omega^{2}=0\right]{/tex}

questions.question: If $\alpha $ is a complex number such that ${\alpha ^2} + \alpha + 1 = 0$ then ${\alpha ^{31}}$ is

When {tex}\alpha=\omega \Rightarrow \alpha^{31}=\omega^{31}{/tex} = {tex}\left(\omega^{3}\right)^{10} \cdot \omega=1 \cdot \omega=\omega=\alpha{/tex} 
Also when {tex}\alpha=\omega^{2} \Rightarrow \alpha^{31}=\left(\omega^{2}\right)^{31}{/tex} = {tex}\omega^{62}=\left(\omega^{3}\right)^{20} \cdot \omega^{2}=1 . \omega^{2}=\omega^{2}=\alpha{/tex} 
Hence {tex}{\alpha ^{31}}{/tex} = {tex}\alpha {/tex}

questions.question: The equation z^2 = $ \bar z$ has

Let z = x + iy

questions.question: The points of the complex plane given by the condition arg. (z) = (2n + 1) $\pi $, n $\in $ I lie on
questions.question_raw: The points of the complex plane given by the condition arg. (z) = (2n + 1) {tex}\pi {/tex}, n {tex}\in {/tex} I lie on

options.text: the imaginary semi axis z = iy, y < 0
the imaginary semi axis z = iy, y > 0
the positive real semi axis z = x, x > 0
the negative real semi axis z = x, x < 0

questions.explanation:
Let z = x + iy then value of {tex}\theta{/tex} which satisfies the equation {tex}tan\theta =\frac { y }{ x } {/tex} is called an argument or amplitude.
Then Arg (z)= {tex}\tan ^{ -1 }{ \left( \frac { y }{ x } \right) } {/tex} 
Now arg. (z) = (2n + 1) {tex}\pi {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\tan ^{ -1 }{ \left( \frac { y }{ x } \right) } = ( 2n + 1 ) \pi{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} tan (2n + 1){tex}\pi {/tex} = {tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{\sin (2 n+1) \pi}{\cos (2 n+1) \pi}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{0}{(-1)^{2 n+1}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{0}{-1}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -y = 0
Hence the points lie on the negative real semi-axis z = x, x < 0

Now {tex}\bar{b} z+b \bar{z}{/tex} = c {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}z_{1}+\overline{z_{1}}{/tex} = c

Which is a straight line.

questions.question: The complex number z which satisfies the condition $\left|\frac{i+z}{i-z}\right|=1$, lies on

{tex}\left|\frac{i+z}{i-z}\right|=1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow|i+z|=|i-z|{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow|i+x+i y|=|i-(x+i y)|{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt{(1+y)^2+x^2}=\sqrt{-(x)^2+(1-y)^2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow(1+y)^2+x^2=-(x)^2+(1-y)^2{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y^2+2 y+1+x^2=x^2+y^2-2 y+1{/tex}

questions.question: (1 + i)^2n + (1 - i)^2n, n $ \in$ N, is
questions.question_raw: (1 + i)2n + (1 - i)2n, n {tex} \in{/tex} N, is
questions.explanation:
(1 + i)2n + (1 - i)2n = [(1 + i)2]n + [(1 - i)2]n = [1 + 2i - 1]n + [1 - 2i - 1]n = (2i)n + (-2i)n = 2n[(i)n + (-i)n]
When n is an odd natural number say n = 2k + 1, (-i)n = -(i)2k+1 so that (i)n + (-i)n = (i)2k+1 - (i)2k+1 = 0
When n is an even natural number say n = 2k, (-i)n = (-i)2k = (i)2k so that, (i)n + (-i)n = (i)2k + (i)2k = 2(i2)k = 2(-1)k, k {tex}\in{/tex} N
Hence (1 + i)2n + (1 - i)2n is always a purely real number.

questions.question: $A m p \cdot\left(\sin \frac{6 \pi}{5}+i\left(1+\cos \frac{6 \pi}{5}\right)\right)$ is equal to

z = x + it = {tex}\sin \frac{6 \pi}{5}+i\left(1+\cos \frac{6 \pi}{5}\right){/tex}
{tex}\therefore \tan \theta=\frac{y}{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \tan \theta=\frac{1+\cos \frac{6 \mathrm{\pi}}{5}}{\sin \frac{6 \mathrm{\pi}}{5}}=\frac{2 \cos ^2 \frac{6 \mathrm{\pi}}{10}}{2 \sin \frac{6 \mathrm{\pi}}{10} \cos \frac{6 \mathrm{\pi}}{10}}=\tan \frac{6 \mathrm{\pi}}{10}{/tex} {tex}\left[\because 1+\cos \theta=2 \cos ^2 \frac{\theta}{2}, \sin \theta=2 \sin \frac{\theta}{2} \cos \frac{\theta}{2}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \theta=\frac{6 \pi}{10}{/tex}

questions.question: If $\omega $ is a cube root of unity, then the linear factors of x^3 + y^3 in complex numbers are

questions.question: If $\alpha {\rm{ }},{\rm{ }}\beta $ are non-real cube roots of unity then $\alpha \beta $ + ${\alpha ^5} + {\beta ^5}$ equals
questions.question_raw: If {tex}\alpha {\rm{ }},{\rm{ }}\beta {/tex} are non-real cube roots of unity then {tex}\alpha \beta {/tex} + {tex}{\alpha ^5} + {\beta ^5}{/tex} equals
questions.explanation:
If {tex}\alpha {\rm{ }},{\rm{ }}\beta {/tex} are non-real cube roots of unity then we have either {tex}\alpha=\omega, \beta=\omega^{2}{/tex} or {tex}\alpha=\omega^{2}, \beta=\omega{/tex} 
{tex}\alpha=\omega, \beta=\omega^{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\alpha^{5}+\beta^{5}+\alpha \cdot \beta{/tex} = {tex}\omega^{5}+\left(\omega^{2}\right)^{5}+\omega \cdot \omega^{2}{/tex} = {tex}\omega^{3} \cdot \omega^{2}+\left(\omega^{3}\right)^{3} \cdot \omega^{1}+\omega^{3}{/tex} = {tex}1 . \omega^{2}+1 . \omega+1{/tex} = 0
{tex}\alpha=\omega^{2}, \beta=\omega{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\alpha^{5}+\beta^{5}+\alpha . \beta{/tex} = {tex}\left(\omega^{2}\right)^{5}+\omega^{5}+\omega . \omega^{2}{/tex} = {tex}\left(\omega^{3}\right)^{3} \cdot \omega^{1}+\omega^{3} \cdot \omega^{2}+\omega^{3}{/tex} = {tex}1 . \omega+1 . \omega^{2}+1{/tex} = 0  [{tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\omega^{3}=1,1+\omega+\omega^{2}=0{/tex} ]

questions.question: The complex numbers z = x + iy; x , y $\in $ R which satisfy the equation $\left|\frac{z-3 i}{z+3}\right|=1$ lies on
questions.question_raw: The complex numbers z = x + iy; x , y {tex}\in {/tex} R which satisfy the equation {tex}\left|\frac{z-3 i}{z+3}\right|=1{/tex} lies on

options.text: the line parallel to y axis

questions.explanation:
Let z=x+iy

Now {tex}\left|\frac{z-3 i}{z+3}\right|=1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow|z-3 i|=|z+3|{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow|(x+i y)-3 i|=|x+i y+3|{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow|x+i(y-3)|=|(x+3)+i y|{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt{(y-3)^{2}+x^{2}}=\sqrt{(x+3)^{2}+(y)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow(y-3)^{2}+x^{2}=(x+3)^{2}+(y)^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y^{2}-6 y+9+x^{2}=x^{2}+6 x+9+y^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 6 x+6 y=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x+y=0{/tex}

questions.question: If z_1 = 2+3i and z_2 = 5+2i, then find z_1-z_2.
questions.question_raw: If z1 = 2+3i and z2 = 5+2i, then find z1-z2.
questions.explanation:
In subtracting one complex number from other, difference of corresponding parts of two complex numbers is calculated. So,
z1-z2 will be:
= (2-5) + (3-2) i
= -3+1i.

questions.question: If z = $\bar z$, then
questions.question_raw: If z = {tex}\bar z{/tex}, then
questions.explanation:
Let z = x + iy
Now z = {tex}\bar{z} \Rightarrow{/tex} x + iy = x - iy {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2iy = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 0
Which means z is purely real.

questions.question: The complex number $\frac{(1+i)^n}{(1-i)^{n-2}}$ is equal to
questions.question_raw: The complex number {tex}\frac{(1+i)^n}{(1-i)^{n-2}}{/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation:
{tex}\frac { { \left( { 1+i } \right) }^{ n } }{ { \left( { 1-i } \right) }^{ n-2 } } =\frac { { \left( { 1+i } \right) }^{ n } }{ { \left( { 1-i } \right) }^{ n }.{ \left( { 1-i } \right) }^{ -2 } } =\frac { { \left( { 1+i } \right) }^{ n }.{ \left( { 1-i } \right) }^{ 2 } }{ { \left( { 1-i } \right) }^{ n } } {/tex} {tex}={ \left( \frac { 1+i }{ 1-i } \right) }^{ n }.{ \left( { 1-i } \right) }^{ 2 }{/tex}
Now {tex}\frac { 1+i }{ 1-i } =\frac { 1+i }{ 1-i } .\frac { 1+i }{ 1+i } =\frac { { \left( { 1+i } \right) }^{ 2 } }{ 1+1 } =\frac { 1-1+2i }{ 1+1 } =\frac { 2i }{ 2 } =i{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac { { \left( { 1+i } \right) }^{ n } }{ { \left( { 1-i } \right) }^{ n-2 } } {/tex} {tex}={ i }^{ n }.{ \left( { 1-i } \right) }^{ 2 }={ i }^{ n }.\left( 1-1-2i \right) =-2i.{ i }^{ n }=-2{ i }^{ n+1 }{/tex} {tex}=-2.{ i }^{ 2 }.{ { i }^{ n-1 } }=-2.-1.{ { i }^{ n-1 } }=2.{ { i }^{ n-1 } }{/tex}

questions.question: The least value of n for which ${ \left( \frac { 1+i }{ 1-i } \right) }^{ n }$ is a positive integer is
questions.question_raw: The least value of n for which {tex}{ \left( \frac { 1+i }{ 1-i } \right) }^{ n }{/tex} is a positive integer is
questions.explanation:
{tex}\frac{1+i}{1-i}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1+i}{1-i} \cdot \frac{1+i}{1+i}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{(1+i)^{2}}{1+1}=\frac{1-1+2 i}{1+1}=\frac{2 i}{2}{/tex} = i
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\left(\frac{1+i}{1-i}\right)^{n}{/tex} = in 
We have i4 = 1 which is positive.
Hence the least value of n is 4.

questions.question: If (x + iy)(p + iq) = (x^2 + y^2)i, then
questions.question_raw: If (x + iy)(p + iq) = (x2 + y2)i, then
questions.explanation:
(x + iy)(p + iq) = (x2 + y2)i 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (xp - yq) + i(xq + yp) = (x2 + y2)i 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} xp - yp = 0 and xq = + yp = x2 + y2 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} p = {tex}\frac{y q}{x}{/tex} ....(i) and xq + yp = x2 + y2 ...(ii)
Substituting (i) in (ii), we get 
xq + {tex}y\left(\frac{y q}{x}\right){/tex} = x2 + y2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2q + y2q = x(x2 + y2)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} q(x2 + y2) = x(x2 + y2) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} q = x
Now from (i), we get p = y

questions.question: If z = x + yi and w = $\frac{1-i z}{z-i}$, then |w| = 1 implies that, in the complex plane
questions.question_raw: If z = x + yi and w = {tex}\frac{1-i z}{z-i}{/tex}, then |w| = 1 implies that, in the complex plane

options.text: z lies on the unit square
z lies on the imaginary axis
z lies on the real axis
z lies on the unit circle

questions.explanation:
{tex}\left| { { w } } \right| =1\Rightarrow \left| \frac { 1-iz }{ z-i } \right| =1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \left| { { 1-iz } } \right| =\left| { { z-i } } \right|{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \left| { { 1-i\left( x+iy \right) } } \right| =\left| { \left( x+iy \right) -i } \right|{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \left| { { \left( 1+y \right) -ix } } \right| =\left| { { x+i\left( y-1 \right) } } \right|{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt { { \left( 1+y \right) }^{ 2 }+{ \left( x \right) }^{ 2 } } =\sqrt { { \left( y-1 \right) }^{ 2 }+{ \left( x \right) }^{ 2 } }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow { \left( 1+y \right) }^{ 2 }+{ \left( x \right) }^{ 2 }={ \left( y-1 \right) }^{ 2 }+{ \left( x \right) }^{ 2 }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow 1+{ y }^{ 2 }+2y+{ x }^{ 2 }=1+{ y }^{ 2 }-2y+{ x }^{ 2 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y=0{/tex}
Which is the real axis.

questions.question: If p^2 + p + 1 = 0, then, the value of p^3n is equal to
questions.question_raw: If p2 + p + 1 = 0, then, the value of p3n is equal to
questions.explanation:
p2 + p + 1 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} p = {tex}\frac{-(1) \pm \sqrt{1^{2}-4 \cdot 1 \cdot 1}}{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-1 \pm \sqrt{-3}}{2}=\frac{-1 \pm \sqrt{3} i}{2}=\omega, \omega^{2}{/tex} 
Now p = {tex}\omega{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} p3n = {tex}(\omega^3)^n{/tex} = 1n = 1 or -1, depending n is even or odd
Also p = {tex}\omega^2{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} p3n = {tex}(\omega^2)^{3n}{/tex} = {tex}(\omega^3)^{2n}{/tex} = 12n = 1
Hence p = 1, -1

questions.question: The solution of the equation |z| = z + 1 + 2i is
questions.question_raw: The solution of the equation |z| = z + 1 + 2i is
questions.explanation:
Let z = x + iy
Now 
|z| = z + 1 + 2i
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x + 1 + i(y + 2)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}}{/tex} = x + 1 and y + 2 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = -2 and x2 + 4 = (x + 1)2 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = -2 and x = {tex}\frac{3}{2}{/tex} 
Hence z = {tex}\frac{3}{2}{/tex} - 2i

questions.question: a + ib $ \leq $ c + id is meaningful only when
questions.question_raw: a + ib {tex} \leq {/tex} c + id is meaningful only when
questions.explanation:
We have in the complex numbers system it is meaningless to say one is smaller or bigger than the other since the order relation does not exist in complex numbers.
So a + ib {tex} \leq{/tex} c + id will be meaningful only when these numbers are real which means their imaginary parts are zero.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b = 0 and d = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b2 + d2 = 0

questions.question: The value of $\frac{1}{1+\omega}+\frac{1}{1+\omega^2}$ =
questions.question_raw: The value of {tex}\frac{1}{1+\omega}+\frac{1}{1+\omega^2}{/tex} =
questions.explanation:
{tex}1,\omega ,{ \omega }^{ 2 }{/tex} are the three cube roots of unity and we have {tex}1+\omega +{ \omega }^{ 2 }=0 , { \omega }^{ 3 }=1{/tex}
{tex}{1 \over {1 + \omega }} + {1 \over {1 + {\omega ^2}}} = {/tex}{tex} {1 \over { - {\omega ^2}}}+{1 \over {-\omega }} = {/tex} {tex}-1\left(\frac{1}{\omega^{2}}+\frac{1}{\omega}\right)=-1\left(\frac{\omega+\omega^{2}}{\omega^{2} \cdot \omega}\right){/tex} = {tex}-1\left(\frac{-1}{\omega^{3}}\right)=\frac{1}{\omega^{3}}=1{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\omega+\omega^{2}=-1, \omega^{3}=1{/tex}]

questions.question: If $x = {\omega ^2} - \omega - 3,\omega $ being a non real cube root of unity, then the value of x^4 + 6x^3 + 10x^2 - 12x - 19 is
questions.question_raw: If {tex}x = {\omega ^2} - \omega - 3,\omega {/tex} being a non real cube root of unity, then the value of x4 + 6x3 + 10x2 - 12x - 19 is
questions.explanation:
x = {tex}\omega^{2}-\omega-3{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x + 3 {tex}=\omega^{2}-\omega{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 + 6x + 9 = {tex}\omega^{4}-2 \omega^{3}+\omega^{2}{/tex} = {tex}\omega-2+\omega^{2}{/tex} = -3
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 + 6x + 12 = 0
Now x4 + 6x3 + 10x2 - 12x - 19 = (x2 - 2)(x2 + 6x + 12) + 5 = (x2 - 2) {tex}\times{/tex} 0 + 5 = 5

questions.question: The region of Argand’s plane represented by the inequality |1 - z| $\leq $ |1 + z| is
questions.question_raw: The region of Argand’s plane represented by the inequality |1 - z| {tex}\leq {/tex} |1 + z| is
questions.explanation:
Let Z = x + iy
Given |1 - z| {tex} \leq{/tex} |1 + z| 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |1 - (x + iy)| {tex} \leq{/tex} |1 + x + iy|
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |(1 - x) - iy|  {tex} \leq{/tex} |(1 + x) + iy|
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{(1-x)^{2}+y^{2}}{/tex} {tex} \leq{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{(1+x)^{2}+(y)^{2}}{/tex} 
Squaring on both sides we get 
(1 - x)2 + y2 {tex} \leq{/tex} (1 + x)2 + (y)2 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1 + x2 - 2x + y2 {tex} \leq{/tex} 1 + x2 + 2x + y2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -2x {tex} \leq{/tex} 2x 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4x {tex} \geq{/tex} 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x {tex} \geq{/tex} 0
Hence we get x = Re (z) {tex}≥{/tex} 0

questions.question: If z is purely real and Re ( z) > 0, then Amp. (z) is
questions.question_raw: If z is purely real and Re ( z) > 0, then Amp. (z) is
questions.explanation:
To find the amplitude of a complex number Z = x + iy first find a value of {tex}\theta {/tex} which satisfy the equation {tex}\theta =\tan ^{ -1 }{ \left| \frac { y }{ x } \right| } ,0\le \theta \le \frac { \Pi }{ 2 } {/tex} 
Now depending on the complex number lies in the first, second, third or fourth quadrants the amplitudes will be {tex}\theta ,\left( \Pi -\theta \right) ,-\left( \Pi -\theta \right) ,-\left( \theta \right) {/tex} respectively.
Given z is purely real and Re (z) > 0
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Z = x + 0i, x > 0
Now {tex}\tan ^{ -1 }{ \left| \frac { y }{ x } \right| } =\tan ^{ -1 }{ \left| \frac { 0 }{ 1 } \right| } {/tex} = tan-1 0 = 0
Also Z = x + 0i, x > 0 lies in the first quadrant 
Hence Amp(Z) = 0

questions.question: If z is purely real and Re(z) < 0, then Arg.(z) is
questions.question_raw: If z is purely real and Re(z) < 0, then Arg.(z) is
questions.explanation:
To find the amplitude of a complex number Z = x + iy first find a value of {tex}\theta {/tex} which satisfy the equation {tex}\theta =\tan ^{ -1 }{ \left| \frac { y }{ x } \right| } ,0\le \theta \le \frac { \Pi }{ 2 } {/tex} 
Now depending on the complex number lies in the first, second, third or fourth quadrants the amplitudes will be {tex}\theta ,\left( \Pi -\theta \right) ,-\left( \Pi -\theta \right) ,-\left( \theta \right) {/tex} respectively.
Given z is purely real and Re (z) {tex}<{/tex} 0
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Z = -x + 0i, x > 0
Now {tex}\tan ^{ -1 }{ \left| \frac { y }{ x } \right| } =\tan ^{ -1 }{ \left| \frac { 0 }{ -1 } \right| }{/tex} = tan-1 0 = 0
Also Z = -x + 0i, x > 0 lies in the second quadrant.
Hence Amp(Z) = {tex}\Pi -\theta =\Pi -0=\Pi {/tex}

questions.question: Find argument of the complex number (0 + 0i)
questions.question_raw: Find argument of the complex number  (0 + 0i)
questions.explanation:
Let Z = 0 + 0i = {tex}r\left( cos\theta +isin\theta \right) {/tex} 
Then comparing the real and imaginary parts, we get 
r cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = 0 and r sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = 0
{tex}\therefore{/tex} r2 (cos2 {tex}\theta{/tex} + sin2 {tex}\theta{/tex}) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r2 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = 0 and sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = 0                                          
Which is not possible for any value of {tex}\theta{/tex}.

questions.question: Find the Amplitude of i
questions.question_raw: Find the Amplitude of i
questions.explanation:
Let Z = i = {tex}r\left( cos\theta +isin\theta \right) {/tex} 
Then comparing the real and imaginary parts, we get
r cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = 0 and r sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = 1
{tex}\therefore{/tex} r2(cos2 {tex}\theta{/tex} + sin2 {tex}\theta{/tex}) = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r2 = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r = 1
{tex}\therefore{/tex} cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = 0 and sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = 1 {tex}\left[ cos\left( \frac { \Pi }{ 2 } \right) = 0, sin\left( \frac { \Pi }{ 2 } \right) =1 \right] {/tex} [Format of amplitude is {tex}\left( \Pi -\theta \right) {/tex}in the second quadrant]
Since {tex}\theta{/tex} lies in the second quadrant, we have the principal value of the argument (Amplitude) = {tex}\left( \Pi -\theta \right) =\left( \Pi -\frac { \Pi }{ 2 } \right) =\frac { \Pi }{ 2 }{/tex}

questions.question: 1/z is _________________ for complex number z.
questions.question_raw: 1/z is _________________ for complex number z.
questions.explanation:
multiplicative inverse
On multiplying reciprocal of complex number (1/z) to complex number z, we get multiplying inverse one i.e. z*1=z.

questions.question: Find the Amplitude of -1 - i
questions.question_raw: Find the Amplitude of -1 - i
questions.explanation:
Let Z = -1 - i = {tex}r\left( cos\theta +isin\theta \right) {/tex} 
Then comparing the real and imaginary parts, we get
r cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = -1 and r sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = -1
{tex}\therefore{/tex} r2(cos2 {tex}\theta{/tex} + sin2 {tex}\theta{/tex}) = 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r2 = 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r = {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{2} \cos \theta{/tex} = -1 and {tex}\sqrt{2} \sin \theta{/tex} = -1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\cos \theta=\frac{-1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} and {tex}\sin \theta=\frac{-1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} [{tex}\operatorname{cos}\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)=\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}]
Since both  sin {tex}\theta{/tex} and cos {tex}\theta{/tex} are negative and we have sin {tex}\theta{/tex} and cos {tex}\theta{/tex} are negative in the third quardrant, the principal value of the argument (Amplitude) = {tex}-\left( \pi -\frac { \pi }{ 4 } \right) =-\frac { 3\pi }{ 4 } {/tex}

questions.question: If z is any complex number, then $\frac{z-\bar{z}}{2 i}$ is
questions.question_raw: If z is any complex number, then {tex}\frac{z-\bar{z}}{2 i}{/tex} is
questions.explanation:
Let z = x + iy
Then {tex}\bar {z }{/tex} = x - iy
{tex}\therefore{/tex} z - {tex}\bar { z}{/tex} = (x + iy) - (x - iy) = 2iy
Now {tex}\frac{z-\bar{z}}{2 i}{/tex} = y
Hence {tex}\frac{z-\bar{z}}{2 i}{/tex} is purely real.
