questions.question: Let z $\in$ C with Im (z) = 10 and it satisfies $\frac{2 z-n}{2 z+n}=2 i-1$ for some natural number n, then
questions.question_raw: Let z {tex}\in{/tex} C with Im (z) = 10 and it satisfies {tex}\frac{2 z-n}{2 z+n}=2 i-1{/tex} for some natural number n, then
questions.explanation:
Let z = x + 10i, as Im (z) = 10 (given).
Since z satisfies,
{tex}\frac{2 z-n}{2 z+n}=2 i-1{/tex}, n {tex}\in{/tex} N,
{tex}\therefore{/tex} (2x + 20i - n) = (2i - 1) (2x + 20i + n)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (2x - n) + 20i = (- 2x - n - 40) + (4x + 2n - 20)i
On comparing real and imaginary parts, we get
2x - n = - 2x - n - 40 and 20 = 4x + 2n - 20
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4x = -40 and 4x + 2n =40
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = - 10 and - 40 + 2n = 40 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} n = 40
So, n = 40 and x = Re (z) = - 10

questions.question: If $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are the roots of the quadratic equation, x^2 + x sin $\theta$ - 2 sin $\theta$ = 0, $\theta$ $\in\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right)$, then $\frac{\alpha^{12}+\beta^{12}}{\left(\alpha^{-12}+\beta^{-12}\right)(\alpha-\beta)^{24}}$ is equal to
questions.question_raw: If {tex}\alpha{/tex} and {tex}\beta{/tex} are the roots of the quadratic equation, x2 + x sin {tex}\theta{/tex} - 2 sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = 0, {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\in\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex}, then {tex}\frac{\alpha^{12}+\beta^{12}}{\left(\alpha^{-12}+\beta^{-12}\right)(\alpha-\beta)^{24}}{/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation:
Given quadratic equation is
{tex}x^{2}+x \sin \theta-2 \sin \theta=0, \theta \in\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex}
and its roots are {tex}\alpha{/tex} and {tex}\beta{/tex}
So, sum of roots {tex}=\alpha+\beta=-\sin \theta{/tex}
and product of roots {tex}=\alpha \beta=-2 \sin \theta{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \quad \alpha \beta=2(\alpha+\beta){/tex} ... (i)
Now, the given expression is {tex}\frac{\alpha^{12}+\beta^{12}}{\left(\alpha^{-12}+\beta^{-12}\right)(\alpha-\beta)^{24}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\alpha^{12}+\beta^{12}}{\left(\frac{1}{\alpha^{12}}+\frac{1}{\beta^{12}}\right)(\alpha-\beta)^{24}}=\frac{\alpha^{12}+\beta^{12}}{\left(\frac{\beta^{12}+\alpha^{12}}{\alpha^{12} \beta^{12}}\right)(\alpha-\beta)^{24}}{/tex}
{tex}=\left[\frac{\alpha \beta}{(\alpha-\beta)^{2}}\right]^{12}=\left(\frac{\alpha \beta}{(\alpha+\beta)^{2}-4 \alpha \beta}\right)^{12}{/tex} 
From Eq. (i)]
{tex}=\left(\frac{2}{(\alpha+\beta)-8}\right)^{12}=\left(\frac{2}{-\sin \theta-8}\right)^{12}[\because \alpha+\beta=-\sin \theta]{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{2^{12}}{(\sin \theta+8)^{12}}{/tex}

questions.question: All the points in the set $S=\left\{\frac{\alpha+i}{\alpha-i}: \alpha \in \mathbf{R}\right\}(i=\sqrt{-1})$ lie on a
questions.question_raw: All the points in the set {tex}S=\left\{\frac{\alpha+i}{\alpha-i}: \alpha \in \mathbf{R}\right\}(i=\sqrt{-1}){/tex} lie on a
questions.explanation:
Let x + iy = {tex}\frac{\alpha+i}{\alpha-i}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x+i y=\frac{(\alpha+i)^{2}}{\alpha^{2}+1}=\frac{\left(\alpha^{2}-1\right)+(2 \alpha) i}{\alpha^{2}+1}=\frac{\alpha^{2}-1}{\alpha^{2}+1}+\left(\frac{2 \alpha}{\alpha^{2}+1}\right) i{/tex}
On comparing real and imaginary parts, we get
{tex}x=\frac{\alpha^{2}-1}{\alpha^{2}+1} \text { and } y=\frac{2 \alpha}{\alpha^{2}+1}{/tex}
Now, {tex}x^{2}+y^{2}=\left(\frac{\alpha^{2}-1}{\alpha^{2}+1}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{2 \alpha}{\alpha^{2}+1}\right)^{2}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\alpha^{4}+1-2 \alpha^{2}+4 \alpha^{2}}{\left(\alpha^{2}+1\right)^{2}}=\frac{\left(\alpha^{2}+1\right)^{2}}{\left(\alpha^{2}+1\right)^{2}}=1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 + y2 = 1
Which is an equation of circle with centre (0, 0) and radius 1 unit.
So, {tex}S=\left\{\frac{\alpha+i}{\alpha-i} ; \alpha \in \mathbf{R}\right\}{/tex} lies on a circle with radius 1.

questions.question: Let p, q $\in$ R. If 2 - $\sqrt3$ is a root of the quadratic equation, x^2 + px + q = 0, then
questions.question_raw: Let p, q {tex}\in{/tex} R. If 2 - {tex}\sqrt3{/tex} is a root of the quadratic equation, x2 + px + q = 0, then
questions.explanation:
Given quadratic equation is x2 + px + q = 0, where {tex}p, q \in \mathbf{R}{/tex} having one root {tex}2-\sqrt{3}{/tex}, then other root is {tex}2+\sqrt{3}{/tex} (conjugate of {tex}2-\sqrt{3}{/tex}) [{tex}\because{/tex} irrational roots of a quadratic equation always occurs in pairs]
So, sum of roots = -p = 4 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} p = -4
and product of roots = q = 4 - 3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} q = 1
Now, from options p2 - 4q - 12 = 16 - 4 - 12 = 0

questions.question: Let z $\in$ C be such that |z| < 1. If $\omega=\frac{5+3 z}{5(1-z)}$, then
questions.question_raw: Let z {tex}\in{/tex} C be such that |z| < 1. If {tex}\omega=\frac{5+3 z}{5(1-z)}{/tex}, then
questions.explanation:
Given complex number {tex}\omega=\frac{5+3 z}{5(1-z)}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 5{tex}\omega{/tex} - 5{tex}\omega{/tex} z = 5 + 3z
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (3 + 5{tex}\omega{/tex})z = 5{tex}\omega{/tex} - 5
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |3 + 5{tex}\omega{/tex}| |z| = |5 {tex}\omega{/tex} - 5| ...(i)
[applying modulus both sides and |z1z2| = |z1||z2|]
{tex}\because{/tex} |z| < 1
{tex}\therefore{/tex} |3 + 5{tex}\omega{/tex}|>|5 {tex}\omega{/tex} - 5| [from Eq. (i)]
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad\left|\omega+\frac{3}{5}\right|>|\omega-1|{/tex}
Let {tex}\omega{/tex} = x + iy, then {tex}\left(x+\frac{3}{5}\right)^{2}+y^{2}>(x-1)^{2}+y^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad x^{2}+\frac{9}{25}+\frac{6}{5} x>x^{2}+1-2 x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{16 x}{5}>\frac{16}{25} \Rightarrow x>\frac{1}{5} \Rightarrow 5 x>1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 5 \operatorname{Re}(\omega)>1{/tex}

questions.question: Let $\left(-2-\frac{1}{3} i\right)^{3}=\frac{x+i y}{27}(i=\sqrt{-1})$ where x and y are real numbers, then y - x equals
questions.question_raw: Let {tex}\left(-2-\frac{1}{3} i\right)^{3}=\frac{x+i y}{27}(i=\sqrt{-1}){/tex} where x and y are real numbers, then y - x equals
questions.explanation:
We have, {tex}\frac{x+i y}{27}=\left(-2-\frac{1}{3} i\right)^{3}=\left[\frac{-1}{3}(6+i)\right]^{3}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{x+i y}{27}=-\frac{1}{27}{/tex} (216 + 108i + 18i2 + i3)
{tex}=-\frac{1}{27}{/tex} (198 + 107i)
[{tex}\because{/tex} (a + b)3 = a3 + b3 + 3a2b + 3ab2, i2 = - 1, i3 = -i]
On equating real and imaginary part, we get
x = - 198 and y = -107
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y - x = - 107 + 198 = 91

questions.question: If m is chosen in the quadratic equation (m^2 + 1)x^2 - 3x+(m^2 + 1)^2 = 0 such that the sum of its roots is greatest, then the absolute difference of the cubes of its roots is
questions.question_raw: If m is chosen in the quadratic equation (m2 + 1)x2 - 3x+(m2 + 1)2 = 0 such that the sum of its roots is greatest, then the absolute difference of the cubes of its roots is
questions.explanation:
Given quadratic equation is
(m2 + 1)x2 - 3x + (m2 + 1)2 = 0 ...... (i)
Let the roots of quadratic Eq. (i) are {tex}\alpha{/tex} and {tex}\beta{/tex}, so
{tex}\alpha + \beta = \frac{3}{{{m^2} + 1}}{/tex} and {tex}\alpha \beta = {m^2} + 1{/tex}
According to the question, the sum of roots is greatest and it is possible only when "(m2 + 1) is minimum" and "minimum value of of m2 + 1 = , when m = 0."
{tex}\therefore \alpha + \beta = 3{/tex} and {tex}\alpha \beta = 1,{/tex} as m = 0
Now, the absolute difference of the cubes of roots    
 =  {tex}\left| {{\alpha ^3} - {\beta ^3}} \right|{/tex}
{tex}=\left| {\alpha - \beta } \right|\left| {{\alpha ^2} + {\beta ^2} + \alpha \beta } \right|{/tex}
{tex} = \sqrt {{{(\alpha + \beta )}^2} - 4\alpha \beta } \left| {{{(\alpha + \beta )}^2} - \alpha \beta } \right|{/tex}
{tex} = \sqrt {9 - 4} \left| {9 - 1} \right| = 8\sqrt 5 {/tex}

questions.question: Let $A=\left\{\theta \in\left(-\frac{\pi}{2}, \pi\right): \frac{3+2 i \sin \theta}{1-2 i \sin \theta} \text { is purely imaginary }\right\}$ Then, the sum of the elements in A is
questions.question_raw: Let {tex}A=\left\{\theta \in\left(-\frac{\pi}{2}, \pi\right): \frac{3+2 i \sin \theta}{1-2 i \sin \theta} \text { is purely imaginary }\right\}{/tex} Then, the sum of the elements in A is
questions.explanation:
Let z = {tex}\left(\frac{3+2 i \sin \theta}{1-2 i \sin \theta}\right) \times\left(\frac{1+2 i \sin \theta}{1+2 i \sin \theta}\right){/tex} (rationalising the denominator)
{tex}=\frac{3-4 \sin ^{2} \theta+8 i \sin \theta}{1+4 \sin ^{2} \theta}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} a2 - b2 = (a + b)(a - b) and i2 = -1]
{tex}=\left(\frac{3-4 \sin ^{2} \theta}{1+4 \sin ^{2} \theta}\right)+\left(\frac{8 \sin \theta}{1+4 \sin ^{2} \theta}\right) i{/tex}
As z is purely imaginary, so real part of z = 0
{tex}\therefore \frac{3-4 \sin ^{2} \theta}{1+4 \sin ^{2} \theta}=0 \Rightarrow 3-4 \sin ^{2} \theta=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \sin ^{2} \theta=\frac{3}{4} \Rightarrow \sin \theta=\pm \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \theta \in\left\{-\frac{\pi}{3}, \frac{\pi}{3}, \frac{2 \pi}{3}\right\}{/tex}
Sum of values of {tex}\theta=\frac{2 \pi}{3}{/tex}

questions.question: A value of $\theta$ for which $\frac{2+3 i \sin \theta}{1-2 i \sin \theta} $ is purely imaginary, is
questions.question_raw: A value of {tex}\theta{/tex} for which {tex}\frac{2+3 i \sin \theta}{1-2 i \sin \theta} {/tex} is purely imaginary, is
questions.explanation:
Let z = {tex}\frac{2+3 i \sin \theta}{1-2 i \sin \theta} {/tex} is purely imaginary. Then, we have Re(z) = 0
Now, consider z = {tex}\frac{2+3 i \sin \theta}{1-2 i \sin \theta} {/tex}
{tex}=\frac{(2+3 i \sin \theta)(1+2 i \sin \theta)}{(1-2 i \sin \theta)(1+2 i \sin \theta)}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{2+4 i \sin \theta+3 i \sin \theta+6 i^{2} \sin ^{2} \theta}{1^{2}-(2 i \sin \theta)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{2-6 \sin ^{2} \theta}{1+4 \sin ^{2} \theta}+i \frac{7 \sin \theta}{1+4 \sin ^{2} \theta}{/tex}
{tex}\because{/tex} Re(z) = 0
{tex}\therefore \quad \frac{2-6 \sin ^{2} \theta}{1+4 \sin ^{2} \theta}=0 \Rightarrow 2=6 \sin ^{2} \theta{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \sin ^{2} \theta=\frac{1}{3}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \sin \theta=\pm \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \theta=\sin ^{-1}\left(\pm \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right)=\pm \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right){/tex}

questions.question: If $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are the roots of the equation x^2 -2x + 2 = 0, then the least value of n for which $\left(\frac{\alpha}{\beta}\right)^{n}$ = 1 is
questions.question_raw: If {tex}\alpha{/tex} and {tex}\beta{/tex} are the roots of the equation x2 -2x + 2 = 0, then the least value of n for which {tex}\left(\frac{\alpha}{\beta}\right)^{n}{/tex} = 1 is
questions.explanation:
Given, {tex}\alpha {/tex} and {tex}\beta {/tex} are the roots of the quadratic equation,
{tex}{x^2} - 2x + 2 = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x - 1)2 + 1 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x - 1)2 = -1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x-1=\pm i{/tex} [where {tex}i=\sqrt{-1}{/tex}]
 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x=(1+i) \;or \;(1-i){/tex}
Clearly, if {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 1 + i, then {tex}\beta{/tex} = 1 - i
According to question  {tex}\left(\frac{\alpha}{\beta}\right)^{n}{/tex} = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad\left(\frac{1+i}{1-i}\right)^{n}=1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad\left(\frac{(1+i)(1+i)}{(1-i)(1+i)}\right)^{n}=1{/tex} [by rationalization]
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad\left(\frac{1+i^{2}+2 i}{1-i^{2}}\right)^{n}=1 \Rightarrow\left(\frac{2 i}{2}\right)^{n}=1 \Rightarrow i^{n}=1{/tex}
So, minimum value of n is 4. {tex}\left[\because i^{4}=1\right]{/tex}

questions.question: The number of integral values of m for which the equation (1 + m^2)x^2 - 2(1 + 3m)x + (1 + 8m) = 0, has no real root is
questions.question_raw: The number of integral values of m for which the equation (1 + m2)x2 - 2(1 + 3m)x + (1 + 8m) = 0, has no real root is
questions.explanation:
Given quadratic equation is
(1 + m2)x2 - 2(1 + 3m)x + (1 + 8m) = 0 ..... (i)
Now, discriminant
D = [-2(1 + 3m)]2 - 4(1 + m2)(1 + 8m)
= 4[(1 + 3m)2 - (1 + m2)(1 + 8m)]
= 4[1 + 9m2 + 6m - (1 + 8m + m2 + 8m3)]
= 4[-8m3 + 8m2 - 2m]
= -8m(4m2 - 4m + 1) = -8m(2m - 1)2
According to the question there is no solution of the quadratic Eq. (i), then
D < 0
{tex}\therefore-8 m(2 m-1)^{2}<0 \Rightarrow m>0{/tex}
So, there are infinitely many values of 'm' for which, there is no solution of the given quadratic equation.

questions.question: If $\left|\begin{array}{rrr}{6 i} & {-3 i} & {1} \\ {4} & {3 i} & {-1} \\ {20} & {3} & {i}\end{array}\right|=x+i y$, then
questions.question_raw: If {tex}\left|\begin{array}{rrr}{6 i} & {-3 i} & {1} \\ {4} & {3 i} & {-1} \\ {20} & {3} & {i}\end{array}\right|=x+i y{/tex}, then
questions.explanation:
Given {tex}\left|\begin{array}{rrr}{6 i} & {-3 i} & {1} \\ {4} & {3 i} & {-1} \\ {20} & {3} & {i}\end{array}\right|=x+i y{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad-3 i\left|\begin{array}{rrr}{6 i} & {1} & {1} \\ {4} & {-1} & {-1} \\ {20} & {i} & {i}\end{array}\right|{/tex} = x + i y
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x + iy = 0 [{tex}\because{/tex} C2 and C3 are identical]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 0, y = 0

questions.question: The number of integral values of m for which the quadratic expression, (1 + 2m) x^2 - 2(1 + 3m) x + 4(1 + m), x $\in$ R, is always positive, is
questions.question_raw: The number of integral values of m for which the quadratic expression, (1 + 2m) x2 - 2(1 + 3m) x + 4(1 + m), x {tex}\in{/tex} R, is always positive, is
questions.explanation:
The quadratic expression
ax2 + bx + c, {tex}x \in R{/tex} is always positive,
If a > 0 and D < 0.
So, the quadratic expression
(1 + 2m)x2 - 2(1 + 3m)x + 4(1 + m), {tex}x \in R{/tex} will be
always positive, if 1 + 2m > 0 .... (i)
and D = 4(1 + 3m)2 - 4(2m + 1) 4(1 + m) < 0 ...... (ii)
From inequality Eq. (i), we get
{tex}m>-\frac{1}{2}{/tex} ...... (iii)
From inequality Eq. (ii), we get
1 + 9m2 + 6m - 4(2m2 + 3m + 1) < 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} m2 - 6m - 3 < 0
{tex}\Rightarrow[m-(3+\sqrt{12})][m-(3-\sqrt{12})]<0{/tex}
{tex}\left[\because m^{2}-6 m-3=0\right.{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow m=\frac{6 \pm \sqrt{36+12}}{2}=3 \pm \sqrt{12}{/tex}]
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 3-\sqrt{12}<m<3+\sqrt{12}{/tex} ...... (iv)
From inequalities Eqs. (iii) and (iv), the integral values of m are 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6
Hence, the number of integral values of m is 7.

questions.question: The value of sum $\sum_\limits{n=1}^{13}\left(i^{n}+i^{n+1}\right)$, where $i=\sqrt{-1}$, equals
questions.question_raw: The value of sum {tex}\sum_\limits{n=1}^{13}\left(i^{n}+i^{n+1}\right){/tex}, where {tex}i=\sqrt{-1}{/tex}, equals
questions.explanation:
{tex}\sum_\limits{n=1}^{13}\left(i^{n}+i^{n+1}\right)=\sum_\limits{n=1}^{13} i^{n}(1+i)=(1+i) \sum_\limits{n=1}^{13} i^{n}{/tex}
= (1 + i)(i + i2 + i3 + ... + i13) = (1 + i) {tex}\left[\frac{i-\left(1-i^{13}\right)}{1-i}\right]{/tex}
= (1 + i) {tex}\left[\frac{i(1-i)}{1-i}\right]{/tex} = (1 + i)i = i - 1
Alternate Solution:
Since, sum of any four consecutive powers of iota is zero.
{tex}\therefore \sum_\limits{n=1}^{13}\left(i^{n}+i^{n+1}\right){/tex}= (i + i2 + ... + i13) + (i2 + i3 + ... + i14) = i + i2 = i - 1

questions.question: The smallest positive integer n for which $\left(\frac{1+i}{1-i}\right)^{n}=1$, is
questions.question_raw: The smallest positive integer n for which {tex}\left(\frac{1+i}{1-i}\right)^{n}=1{/tex}, is
questions.explanation:
Since, {tex}\left(\frac{1+i}{1-i}\right)^{n}=1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\left(\frac{1+i}{1-i} \times \frac{1+i}{1+i}\right)^{n}=1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad\left(\frac{2 i}{2}\right)^{n}=1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad i^{n}=1{/tex}
The smallest positive integer n for which in = 1 is 4.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} n = 4

questions.question: If $\lambda$ be the ratio of the roots of the quadratic equation in x, 3m^2x^2 + m(m - 4)x +2=0, then the least value of m for which $\lambda+\frac{1}{\lambda}=1$, is
questions.question_raw: If {tex}\lambda{/tex} be the ratio of the roots of the quadratic equation in x, 3m2x2 + m(m - 4)x +2=0, then the least value of m for which {tex}\lambda+\frac{1}{\lambda}=1{/tex}, is
questions.explanation:
Let the given quadratic equation in x,
3m2x2 + m(m - 4)x + 2 = 0, {tex}m \neq 0{/tex} have roots {tex}\alpha{/tex} and {tex}\beta{/tex}, then
{tex}\alpha+\beta=-\frac{m(m-4)}{3 m^{2}}{/tex} and {tex}\alpha \beta=\frac{2}{3 m^{2}}{/tex}
Also, let {tex}\frac{\alpha}{\beta}=\lambda{/tex}
Then, {tex}\lambda+\frac{1}{\lambda}=1 \Rightarrow \frac{\alpha}{\beta}+\frac{\beta}{\alpha}=1{/tex} (given)
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \alpha^{2}+\beta^{2}=\alpha \beta{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad(\alpha+\beta)^{2}=3 \alpha \beta{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{m^{2}(m-4)^{2}}{9 m^{4}}=3 \frac{2}{3 m^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad(m-4)^{2}=18{/tex} {tex}[\because m \neq 0]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad m-4=\pm 3 \sqrt{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad m=4\pm 3 \sqrt{2}{/tex}
The least value of m {tex}=4-3 \sqrt{2}{/tex}

questions.question: The equation |z - i| = |z - 1|, $i=\sqrt{-1}$, represents
questions.question_raw: The equation |z - i| = |z - 1|, {tex}i=\sqrt{-1}{/tex}, represents

options.text: the line passing through the origin with slope 1
the line passing through the origin with slope -1

questions.explanation:
Let the complex number z = x + iy
Also given, | z - i| = | z - 1 |
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} | x + iy - i | = | x + iy - 1 |
{tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt{x^{2}+(y-1)^{2}}=\sqrt{(x-1)^{2}+y^{2}}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} | z | = {tex}\sqrt{(\operatorname{Re}(z))^{2}+(\operatorname{Im}(z))^{2}}{/tex}]
On squaring both sides, we get
x2 + y2 - 2y + 1 = x2 + y2 - 2x + 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = x, which represents a line through the origin with slope 1.

questions.question: If a > 0 and $z=\frac{(1+i)^{2}}{a-i}$, has magnitude $\sqrt{\frac{2}{5}}$, then $\bar{z}$ is equal to
questions.question_raw: If a > 0 and {tex}z=\frac{(1+i)^{2}}{a-i}{/tex}, has magnitude {tex}\sqrt{\frac{2}{5}}{/tex}, then {tex}\bar{z}{/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation:
The given complex number{tex}z=\frac{(1+i)^{2}}{a-i}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{(1-1+2 i)(a+i)}{a^{2}+1}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} i2 = -1]
{tex}=\frac{2 i(a+i)}{a^{2}+1}=\frac{-2+2 a i}{a^{2}+1}{/tex} ...(i)
{tex}\because \quad|z|=\sqrt{2 / 5}{/tex} [given]
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \sqrt{\frac{4+4 a^{2}}{\left(a^{2}+1\right)^{2}}}=\sqrt{\frac{2}{5}} \Rightarrow \frac{2}{\sqrt{1+a^{2}}}=\sqrt{\frac{2}{5}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{4}{1+a^{2}}=\frac{2}{5} \Rightarrow a^{2}+1=10{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad a^{2}=9 \Rightarrow a=3 \quad[\because a>0]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad z=\frac{-2+6 i}{10}{/tex} [From Eq. (i)]
So, {tex}\bar{z}=\left(\frac{-2+6 i}{10}\right)=\left(-\frac{1}{5}+\frac{3}{5} i\right) \Rightarrow \bar{z}=-\frac{1}{5}-\frac{3}{5} i{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} if z = x + iy, then {tex}\over Z{/tex} = x - iy]

questions.question: If one real root of the quadratic equation 81x^2+ kx+ 256 = 0 is cube of the other root, then a value of k is
questions.question_raw: If one real root of the quadratic equation 81x2+ kx+ 256 = 0 is cube of the other root, then a value of k is
questions.explanation:
Given quadratic equation is
81x2 + kx + 256 = 0
Let one root be {tex}\alpha{/tex}, then other is {tex}{\alpha ^3}{/tex}
Now, {tex}\alpha+\alpha^{3}=-\frac{k}{81}{/tex} and {tex}\alpha \cdot \alpha^{3}=\frac{256}{81}{/tex}
[{tex}\because{/tex} for ax2 + bx + c = 0, sum of roots {tex}=-\frac{b}{a}{/tex} and product of roots {tex}=\frac{c}{a}{/tex}]
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \alpha^{4}=\left(\frac{4}{3}\right)^{4} \Rightarrow \alpha=\pm \frac{4}{3}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad k=-81\left(\alpha+\alpha^{3}\right){/tex}
{tex}=-81 \alpha\left(1+\alpha^{2}\right){/tex}
{tex}=-81\left(\pm \frac{4}{3}\right)\left(1+\frac{16}{9}\right)=\pm 300{/tex}

questions.question: Let z_1 and z_2 be two complex numbers satisfying |z_1| = 9 and |z_2 - 3 - 4i| = 4. Then, the minimum value of |z_1 - z_2| is
questions.question_raw: Let z1 and z2 be two complex numbers satisfying |z1| = 9 and |z2 - 3 - 4i| = 4. Then, the minimum value of |z1 - z2| is
questions.explanation:
Clearly |z1| = 9, represents a circle having centre C1(0, 0) and radius r1= 9. and |z2 - S - 4 i| = 4 represents a circle having centre C2(3, 4) and radius r2 = 4.
The minimum value of |z1- z2| is equals to minimum distance between circles |z1| = 9 and |z2- 3 - 4i| = 4.
{tex}\because C_{1} C_{2}=\sqrt{(3-0)^{2}+(4-0)^{2}}=\sqrt{9+16}=\sqrt{25}=5{/tex} and |r1 - r2| = |9 - 4| = 5 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C1C2 =|r1 - r2|
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Circles touches each other internally.
Hence, |z1 - z2|min = 0

questions.question: If 5, 5r, 5r^2 are the lengths of the sides of a triangle, then r cannot be equal to
questions.question_raw: If 5, 5r, 5r2 are the lengths of the sides of a triangle, then r cannot be equal to
questions.explanation:
Let a = 5, b = 5r and c = 5r2
We know that, in a triangle sum of 2 sides is always greater than the third side.
{tex}\therefore a+b>c ; b+c>a{/tex} and c + a > b
Now, a + b > c
{tex}\Rightarrow 5+5 r>5 r^{2}{/tex}{tex}\Rightarrow 5 r^{2}-5 r-5<0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow r^{2}-r-1<0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow\left[r-\left(\frac{1-\sqrt{5}}{2}\right)\right]\left[r-\left(\frac{1+\sqrt{5}}{2}\right)\right]<0{/tex}
[{tex}\because{/tex} roots of ax2 + bx + c = 0 are given by
{tex}x=\frac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^{2}-4 a c}}{2 a}{/tex} and r2 - r - 1 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow r=\frac{1 \pm \sqrt{1+4}}{2}=\frac{1 \pm \sqrt{5}}{2}{/tex}]

Similarly, b + c > a
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 5r + 5r2 > 5
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r2 + r - 1 > 0
{tex}\left[r-\left(\frac{-1-\sqrt{5}}{2}\right)\right]\left[r-\left(\frac{-1+\sqrt{5}}{2}\right)\right]>0{/tex}
{tex}\left[\because r^{2}+r-1=0 \Rightarrow r=\frac{-1 \pm \sqrt{1+4}}{2}=\frac{-1 \pm \sqrt{5}}{2}\right]{/tex}

and c + a > b
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 5r2 + 5 > 5r
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r2 - r + 1 > 0
{tex}\Rightarrow r^{2}-2 \cdot \frac{1}{2} r+\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}+1-\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}>0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad\left(r-\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}+\frac{3}{4}>0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad r \in R{/tex} ..... (iii)
From Eqs. (i), (ii) and (iii), we get
{tex}r \in\left(\frac{-1+\sqrt{5}}{2}, \frac{1+\sqrt{5}}{2}\right){/tex}

and {tex}\frac{7}{4}{/tex} is the only value that does not satisfy.

questions.question: If $\frac{z-\alpha}{z+\alpha}(\alpha \in R)$ is a purely imaginary number and |z| = 2, then a value of $\alpha$ is
questions.question_raw: If {tex}\frac{z-\alpha}{z+\alpha}(\alpha \in R){/tex} is a purely imaginary number and |z| = 2, then a value of {tex}\alpha{/tex} is
questions.explanation:
Since, the complex number {tex}\frac{z-\alpha}{z+\alpha}(\alpha \in R){/tex} is purely imaginary number, therefore
{tex}\frac{z-\alpha}{z+\alpha}+\frac{\bar{z}-\alpha}{\bar{z}+\alpha}=0 \quad[\because \alpha \in R]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad z \bar{z}-\alpha \bar{z}+\alpha z-\alpha^{2}+z \bar{z}-\alpha z+\alpha \bar{z}-\alpha^{2}=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2|z|^{2}-2 \alpha^{2}=0 \quad\left[\because z \bar{z}=|z|^{2}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a2 = | z |2 = 4            [ |z| = 2 (given)]
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \alpha=\pm 2{/tex}

questions.question:
Let z be a complex number such that |z| + z = 3 + i (where $i=\sqrt{-1}$).
Then, |z| is equal to
questions.question_raw:
Let z be a complex number such that |z| + z = 3 + i (where {tex}i=\sqrt{-1}{/tex}).
Then, |z| is equal to
questions.explanation:
We have, |z| + z = 3 + i
Let z = x + iy
{tex}\therefore \quad \sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}}{/tex} + x + iy = 3 + i
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad(x+\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}}){/tex} + iy = 3 + i
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad x+\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}}{/tex} = 3 and y = 1
Now, {tex}\sqrt{x^{2}+1}=3-x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 + 1 = 9 - 6x + x2
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 6 x=8 \Rightarrow x=\frac{4}{3}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad z=\frac{4}{3}+i{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad|z|=\sqrt{\frac{16}{9}+1}=\sqrt{\frac{25}{9}} \Rightarrow|z|=\frac{5}{3}{/tex}

questions.question: The value of $\lambda$ such that sum of the squares of the roots of the quadratic equation, x^2+ (3 - $\lambda$)x + 2 = $\lambda$ has the least value is
questions.question_raw: The value of {tex}\lambda{/tex} such that sum of the squares of the roots of the quadratic equation, x2+ (3 - {tex}\lambda{/tex})x + 2 = {tex}\lambda{/tex} has the least value is
questions.explanation:
Given quadratic equation is
x2 + (3 - {tex}\lambda {/tex})x + 2 = {tex}\lambda {/tex}
x2 + (3 - {tex}\lambda {/tex})x + (2 - {tex}\lambda {/tex}) = 0 .... (i)
Let Eq. (i) has roots {tex}\alpha{/tex} and {tex}\beta{/tex}, then {tex}\alpha + \beta = \lambda - 3{/tex} and {tex}\alpha \beta = 2 - \lambda{/tex}
[{tex}\because{/tex} For ax2 + bx + c = 0, sum of roots = {tex} - \frac{b}{a}{/tex} and product of roots = {tex}\frac{c}{a}{/tex}]
Now, {tex}{\alpha ^2} + {\beta ^2} ={(\alpha + \beta )^2} - 2\alpha \beta {/tex}
{tex} = {(\lambda - 3)^2} - 2(2 - \lambda ){/tex}
{tex} = {\lambda ^2} - 6\lambda + 9 - 4 + 2\lambda {/tex}
{tex} = {\lambda ^2} - 4\lambda + 5 = ({\lambda ^2} - 4\lambda + 4) + 1{/tex}
{tex} = {(\lambda - 2)^2} + 1{/tex}
Clearly, {tex}{\alpha ^2} + {\beta ^2}{/tex} will be least when {tex}\lambda = 2{/tex}

questions.question: The number of all possible positive integral values of $\alpha$ for which the roots of the quadratic equation, 6x^2 -11x + $\alpha$ = 0 are rational numbers is
questions.question_raw: The number of all possible positive integral values of {tex}\alpha{/tex} for which the roots of the quadratic equation, 6x2 -11x + {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 0 are rational numbers is
questions.explanation:
For the roots of quadratic equation ax2 + bx + c = 0 to be
rational D = (b2 - 4ac) should be perfect square.
In the equation 6x2 - 11x + {tex}\alpha {/tex} = 0
a = 6, b = -11 and c = {tex}\alpha {/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} For roots to be rational
D = (-11)2 - 4(6) ({tex}\alpha {/tex}) should be a perfect square.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} D({tex}\alpha {/tex}) = 121 - 24{tex}\alpha {/tex} should be a perfect square
Now,
D(1) = 121 - 24 = 97 is not a perfect square.
D(2) = 121 - 24 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 73 is not a perfect square.
D(3) = 121 - 24 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 = 49 is a perfect square.
D(4) = 121 - 24 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 = 25 is a perfect square.
D(5) = 121 - 24 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 = 1 is a perfect square.
and for {tex}\alpha \geq 6, D(\alpha)<0{/tex}, hence imaginary roots.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} For 3 values of {tex}\alpha(\alpha=3,4,5){/tex}, the roots are rational.

questions.question: Let $\alpha$ and $\beta$ be two roots of the equation x^2 + 2x + 2 = 0, then $\alpha$^15 + $\beta$^15 is equal to
questions.question_raw: Let {tex}\alpha{/tex} and {tex}\beta{/tex} be two roots of the equation x2 + 2x + 2 = 0, then {tex}\alpha{/tex}15 + {tex}\beta{/tex}15 is equal to
questions.explanation:
We have, x2 + 2x + 2 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow x=\frac{-2 \pm \sqrt{4-8}}{2}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} roots of ax2 + bx + c = 0 are given by {tex}x=\frac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^{2}-4 a c}}{2 a}{/tex}]
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad x=-1 \pm i{/tex}
Let {tex}\alpha{/tex} = -1 + i and {tex}\beta{/tex} = -1 - i
Then, {tex}\alpha^{15}+\beta^{16}=(-1+i)^{15}+(-1-i)^{15}{/tex}
{tex}=-\left[(1-i)^{15}+(1+i)^{15}\right]{/tex}
{tex}=-\left[\left\{\sqrt{2}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}-\frac{i}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right\}^{15}+\left\{\sqrt{2}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}+\frac{i}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right\}^{15}\right]{/tex}
{tex}=-\left[\begin{array}{l}{\left\{\sqrt{2}\left(\cos \frac{\pi}{4}-i \sin \frac{\pi}{4}\right)\right\}^{15}} \\ {+\left\{\sqrt{2}\left(\cos \frac{\pi}{4}+i \sin \frac{\pi}{4}\right)\right\}^{15}}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}=-(\sqrt{2})^{15}\left[\left(\cos \frac{15 \pi}{4}-i \sin \frac{15 \pi}{4}\right)+\left(\cos \frac{15 \pi}{4}+i \sin \frac{15 \pi}{4}\right)\right]{/tex}
[using De' Moivre's theorem {tex}(\cos \theta \pm i \sin \theta)^{n}=\cos n \theta \pm i \sin n \theta, n \in Z{/tex}]
{tex}=-(\sqrt{2})^{15}\left[2 \cos \frac{15 \pi}{4}\right]=-(\sqrt{2})^{15}\left[2 \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\left[\because \cos \frac{15 \pi}{4}=\cos \left(4 \pi-\frac{\pi}{4}\right)=\cos \frac{\pi}{4}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right]{/tex}
{tex}=-(\sqrt{2})^{16}=-2^{8}=-256{/tex}

questions.question:
A complex number z is said to be unimodular, if $|z| \neq 1$. If z_1 and z_2 are complex numbers such that $\frac{z_{1}-2 z_{2}}{2-z \bar{z}_{2}}$ is unimodular and z_2 is not unimodular.
Then, the point z_2 lies on a
questions.question_raw:
A complex number z is said to be unimodular, if {tex}|z| \neq 1{/tex}. If z1 and z2 are complex numbers such that {tex}\frac{z_{1}-2 z_{2}}{2-z \bar{z}_{2}}{/tex} is unimodular and z2 is not unimodular.
Then, the point z2 lies on a
questions.explanation:
If z is unimodular, then | z| = 1. Also, use property of modulus i.e. {tex}\bar{z}z{/tex}=| z|2
Given, z2 is not unim odular i.e. |z2| {tex}\ne{/tex} 1 and {tex}\frac{z_{1}-2 z_{2}}{2-z_{1} \bar{z}_{2}}{/tex} is unimodular.
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad\left|\frac{z_{1}-2 z_{2}}{2-z_{1} \bar{z}_{2}}\right|=1 \Rightarrow\left|z_{1}-2 z_{2}\right|^{2}=\left|2-z_{1} \bar{z}_{2}\right|^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow\left(z_{1}-2 z_{2}\right)\left(\bar{z}_{1}-2 \bar{z}_{2}\right)=\left(2-z_{1} \bar{z}_{2}\right)\left(2-\bar{z}_{1} z_{2}\right) \quad\left[z \bar{z}=|z|^{2}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow\left|z_{1}\right|^{2}+4\left|z_{2}\right|^{2}-2 \bar{z}_{1} z_{2}-2 z_{1} \bar{z}_{2}{/tex} 4+|z1|2|z2|2- {tex}2\bar z_1 z_2{/tex}- {tex}2\bar z_1 z_2{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left(\left|z_{2}\right|^{2}-1\right)\left(\left|z_{1}\right|^{2}-4\right){/tex} = 0
{tex}\because \quad\left|z_{2}\right| \neq 1{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad\left|z_{1}\right|=2{/tex}
Let z1 = x + iy {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2+ y2 = (2)2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Point z1 lies on a circle of radius 2.

questions.question: If z is a complex number such that $|z| \geq 2$, then the minimum value of $\left|z+\frac{1}{2}\right|$
questions.question_raw: If z is a complex number such that {tex}|z| \geq 2{/tex}, then the minimum value of {tex}\left|z+\frac{1}{2}\right|{/tex}

options.text: is strictly greater than 3/2 but less than 5/2

questions.explanation:
|z| {tex}\geq{/tex} 2 is the region on or outside circle whose centre is
(0, 0) and radius is 2.
Minimum {tex}\left|z+\frac{1}{2}\right|{/tex} is distance of z, which lie on circle |z| = 2 from (-1/2, 0).
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Minimum {tex}\left|z+\frac{1}{2}\right|{/tex} = Distance of {tex}\left(-\frac{1}{2}, 0\right){/tex} from (-2, 0)
{tex}=\sqrt{\left(-2+\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}+0}=\frac{3}{2}=\sqrt{\left(\frac{-1}{2}+2\right)^{2}+0}=\frac{3}{2}{/tex}

Geometrically min {tex}\left|z+\frac{1}{2}\right|=A D{/tex}
Hence, minimum value of {tex}\left|z+\frac{1}{2}\right|{/tex} lies in the interval (1, 2).

questions.question: If $\alpha \beta \in$ C are the distinct roots of the equation x^2 - x + 1 = 0 , then $\alpha$^101 + $\beta$^107 is equal to
questions.question_raw: If  {tex}\alpha \beta \in{/tex} C are the distinct roots of the equation x2 - x + 1 = 0 , then {tex}\alpha{/tex}101 + {tex}\beta{/tex}107 is equal to
questions.explanation:
We have, {tex}\alpha, \beta{/tex} are the roots of x2 - x + 1 = 0
{tex}\because{/tex} Roots of x2 - x + 1 = 0 are {tex}-\omega,-\omega^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Let {tex}\alpha=-\omega{/tex} and {tex}\beta=-\omega^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \alpha^{101}+\beta^{107}=(-\omega)^{101}+\left(-\omega^{2}\right)^{107}=-\left(\omega^{101}+\omega^{214}\right){/tex}
{tex}=-\left(\omega^{2}+\omega\right){/tex}
= -1(-1) {tex}\left[\because 1+\omega+\omega^{2}=0\right]{/tex}
= 1

questions.question:
Let complex numbers $\alpha$ and $1 / \bar{\alpha}$ lies on circles (x - x_0)^2 + (y - y_0)^2 = r^2 and (x - x_0)^2 + (y - y_0)^2 = 4r^2, respectively.
If z_0 = x_0 + iy_0 satisfies the equation 2|z_0|^2 = r^2 + 2, then $|\alpha|$ is equal to
questions.question_raw:
Let complex numbers {tex}\alpha{/tex} and {tex}1 / \bar{\alpha}{/tex} lies on circles (x - x0)2 + (y - y0)2 = r2 and (x - x0)2 + (y - y0)2 = 4r2, respectively.
If z0 = x0 + iy0 satisfies the equation 2|z0|2 = r2 + 2, then {tex}|\alpha|{/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation:
Plan:   Intersection of circles the basic concept is to solve the equations simultaneously and using properties of modulus of complex numbers.
Formula used:   |z|2 = {tex}z\cdot\bar z{/tex}
and |z1 - z2|2 = (z1 - z2) ({tex}\bar z_1{/tex} - {tex}\bar z_2{/tex}) = |z1|2 - z1{tex}\bar z_2{/tex} - z2{tex}\bar z_1{/tex} + |z2|2
Here, (x - x0)2 + (y - y0)2 = r2
and (x - x0)2 + (y - y0)2 = 4r2 can be written as,
|z - z0|2 = r2 and | z - z0|2 = 4r2
Since, {tex}\alpha{/tex} and {tex}\frac{1}{\bar{\alpha}}{/tex} lies on first and second respectively.
{tex}\therefore \quad\left|\alpha-z_{0}\right|^{2}=r^{2} \text { and }\left|\frac{1}{\bar{\alpha}}-z_{0}\right|^{2}=4 r^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad\left(\alpha-z_{0}\right)\left(\bar{\alpha}-\bar{z}_{0}\right)=r^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad|\alpha|^{2}-z_{0} \bar{\alpha}-\bar{z}_{0} \alpha+\left|z_{0}\right|^{2}=r^{2}{/tex} ...(i)
and {tex}\left|\frac{1}{\bar{\alpha}}-z_{0}\right|^{2}=4 r^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad\left(\frac{1}{\bar{\alpha}}-z_{0}\right)\left(\frac{1}{\alpha}-\bar{z}_{0}\right)=4 r^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{|\alpha|^{2}}-\frac{z_{0}}{\alpha}-\frac{\bar{z}_{0}}{\bar{\alpha}}+\left|z_{0}\right|^{2}=4 r^{2}{/tex}
Since, {tex}|\alpha|^{2}=\alpha \cdot \alpha{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{|\alpha|^{2}}-\frac{z_{0} \cdot \bar{\alpha}}{|\alpha|^{2}}-\frac{\bar{z}_{0}}{|\alpha|^{2}} \cdot \alpha+\left|z_{0}\right|^{2}=4 r^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 1-z_{0} \bar{\alpha}-\bar{z}_{0} \alpha+|\alpha|^{2}\left|z_{0}\right|^{2}=4 r^{2}|\alpha|^{2}{/tex} ...(ii)
On subtracting Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get
(|{tex}\alpha{/tex}|2 - 1) + |z0|2(1 - |{tex}\alpha{/tex}|2) = r2 (1 - 4 |{tex}\alpha{/tex}|2)
({tex}\alpha{/tex}|2 - 1) (1 - |z0|2) = r2(1 - 4 |{tex}\alpha{/tex}|2)
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad\left(|\alpha|^{2}-1\right)\left(1-\frac{r^{2}+2}{2}\right)=r^{2}\left(1-4|\alpha|^{2}\right){/tex}
Given, {tex}\left|z_{0}\right|^{2}=\frac{r^{2}+2}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad\left(|\alpha|^{2}-1\right) \cdot\left(\frac{-r^{2}}{2}\right)=r^{2}\left(1-4|\alpha|^{2}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad|\alpha|^{2}-1=-2+8|\alpha|^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 7|\alpha|^2=1{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad|\alpha|=\frac {1 }{ \sqrt{7}}{/tex}

questions.question: For a positive integer n, if the quadratic equation, x(x + 1) + (x + 1) (x + 2) + ... + (x + n - 1) (x + n) = 10n has two consecutive integral solutions, then n is equal to
questions.question_raw: For a positive integer n, if the quadratic equation, x(x + 1) + (x + 1) (x + 2) + ... + (x + n - 1) (x + n) = 10n has two consecutive integral solutions, then n is equal to
questions.explanation:
Given quadratic equation is
x(x + 1) + (x + 1)(x + 2) +....+ (x + n - 1) (x + n) = 10n
{tex}\Rightarrow\left(x^{2}+x^{2}+\ldots+x^{2}\right)+[(1+3+5+\ldots+(2 n-1)] x{/tex} {tex}+[(1 \cdot 2+2 \cdot 3+\ldots+(n-1) n]=10 n{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad n x^{2}+n^{2} x+\frac{n\left(n^{2}-1\right)}{3}-10 n=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad x^{2}+n x+\frac{n^{2}-1}{3}-10=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 3 x^{2}+3 n x+n^{2}-31=0{/tex}
Let {tex}\alpha{/tex} and {tex}\beta{/tex} be the roots.
Since, {tex}\alpha{/tex} and {tex}\beta{/tex} are consecutive,
{tex}\therefore \quad|\alpha-\beta|=1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad(\alpha-\beta)^{2}=1{/tex}
Again, {tex}(\alpha-\beta)^{2}=(\alpha+\beta)^{2}-4 \alpha \beta{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 1=\left(\frac{-3 n}{3}\right)^{2}-4\left(\frac{n^{2}-31}{3}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 1=n^{2}-\frac{4}{3}\left(n^{2}-31\right){/tex}{tex}\Rightarrow 3=3 n^{2}-4 n^{2}+124{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad n^{2}=121 \Rightarrow n=\pm 11{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad n=11{/tex} {tex}[\because n>0]{/tex}

questions.question: Let z be a complex number such that the imaginary part of z is non-zero and a = z^2 + z + 1 is real. Then, a cannot take the value
questions.question_raw: Let z be a complex number such that the imaginary part of z is non-zero and a = z2 + z + 1 is real. Then, a cannot take the value
questions.explanation:
Plan: If ax2 + bx + c =0 has roots {tex}\alpha, \ \beta{/tex}, then
{tex}\alpha, \beta=\frac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^{2}-4 a c}}{2 a}{/tex}
For roots to be real b2 - 4ac {tex}\geq{/tex} 0.
Description of Situation: As imaginary part of z = x+ iy is non-zero.
{tex}\Rightarrow y \ne 0{/tex}
Method I: Let z = x + iy
{tex}\therefore{/tex} a = (x+ iy)2+ (x+ iy) + 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x2 - y2+ x + 1 - a) + i ( 2xy + y) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x2- y2+ x + 1 - a) + iy (2x + 1) = 0,
It is purely real, if y (2x+ 1) = 0
but imaginary part of z, i.e. y is non-zero.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x+ 1 = 0 or x = - 1/2
From Eq. (i), {tex}\frac{1}{4}-y^{2}-\frac{1}{2}{/tex} + l - a = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad a=-y^{2}+\frac{3}{4} \Rightarrow a<\frac{3}{4}{/tex}
Method II: Here, z2 + z + (1 - a) = 0
{tex}\therefore \quad z=\frac{-1 \pm \sqrt{1-4(1-a)}}{2 \times 1}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad z=\frac{-1 \pm \sqrt{4 a-3}}{2}{/tex}
For z do not have real roots, 4a - 3 < 0 {tex}\Rightarrow a<\frac{3}{4}{/tex}

questions.question: The sum of all real values of x satisfying the equation $ (x^2 - 5x +5)^{x^2 + 4x - 60}$ = 1 is
questions.question_raw: The sum of all real values of x satisfying the equation {tex} (x^2 - 5x +5)^{x^2 + 4x - 60}{/tex} = 1 is
questions.explanation:
Given, {tex}\left(x^{2}-5 x+5\right)^{x^{2}+4 x-60}=1{/tex}
Clearly, this is possible when

  1. x2 + 4x - 60 = 0 and {tex}x^{2}-5 x+5 \neq 0{/tex}
    or
  2. x2 - 5x + 5 = 0
    or
  3. x2 - 5x + 5 = -1 and x2 + 4x - 60 = Even integer.

Case I: When x2 + 4x - 60 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 + 10 - 6x - 60 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x(x + 10) - 6(x + 10) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x + 10) (x - 6) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = -10 or x = 6
Note that, for these two values of x,x2 - 5x + 5 {tex} \neq {/tex} 0
Case II: When x2 - 5x + 5 = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 - 5x + 4 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 - 4x - x + 4 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x(x - 4) - 1 (x - 4) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x - 4) (x - 1) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4 or x = 1
Case III: When x2 - 5x + 5 = -1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 - 5x + 6 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 - 2x - 3x + 6 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x(x - 2) -3(x - 2) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x - 2) (x - 3) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 2 or x = 3
Now, when x = 2, x2 + 4x - 60 = 4 + 8 - 60 = -48, which is an even integer.
When x = 3, x2 + 4x - 60 = 9 + 12 - 60 = -39, which is not an even integer.
Thus, in this case, we get x = 2
Hence, the sum of all real value of
x = -10 + 6 + 4 + 1 + 2 = 3

questions.question: Let z = x+ iy be a complex number where, x and y are integers. Then, the area of the rectangle whose vertices are the root of the equation $z \bar{z}^{3}+\bar{z} z^{3}=350$, is
questions.question_raw: Let z = x+ iy be a complex number where, x and y are integers. Then, the area of the rectangle whose vertices are the root of the equation {tex}z \bar{z}^{3}+\bar{z} z^{3}=350{/tex}, is
questions.explanation:
Since, {tex}z \bar{z}^{3}+\bar{z} z^{3}=350{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2(x2 + y2)(x2 - y2) = 350
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x2 + y2)(x2 - y2) = 175
Since, x, y {tex}\in{/tex} I, the only possible case which gives integral solution, is
x2+ y2 = 25 ...(i)
x2- y2 = 7 ...(ii)
From Eqs. (i) and (ii),
x2= 16, y2 = 9 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = ± 4 , y = ± 3
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of rectangle = 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 6 = 48

questions.question: If |z| = 1 and $z \neq \pm 1$, then all the values of $\frac{z}{1-z^{2}}$ lie on
questions.question_raw: If |z| = 1 and {tex}z \neq \pm 1{/tex}, then all the values of {tex}\frac{z}{1-z^{2}}{/tex} lie on

options.text: a line not passing through the origin

questions.explanation:
Let z = cos{tex}\theta{/tex} + i sin{tex}\theta{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{z}{1-z^{2}}=\frac{\cos \theta+i \sin \theta}{1-(\cos 2 \theta+i \sin 2 \theta)}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\cos \theta+i \sin \theta}{2 \sin ^{2} \theta-2 i \sin \theta \cos \theta}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\cos \theta+i \sin \theta}{-2 i\sin \theta( \cos \theta+ i \sin \theta)}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{i}{2sin\theta}{/tex}
Hence, {tex}\frac{z}{1-z^{2}}{/tex} lies on the imaginary axis i.e. Y-axis.
Alternate Solution:
Let {tex}E=\frac{z}{1-z^{2}}=\frac{z}{z \bar{z}-z^{2}}=\frac{1}{\bar{z}-z}{/tex} which is an imaginary.

questions.question: If $w=\alpha+i \beta$, where $\beta \neq 0$ and $z \neq 1$, satisfies the condition that $\left(\frac{w-\bar{w} z}{1-z}\right)$ is purely real, then the set of values of z is
questions.question_raw: If {tex}w=\alpha+i \beta{/tex}, where {tex}\beta \neq 0{/tex} and {tex}z \neq 1{/tex}, satisfies the condition that {tex}\left(\frac{w-\bar{w} z}{1-z}\right){/tex} is purely real, then the set of values of z is
questions.explanation:
Let z1 = {tex}\left(\frac{w-\bar{w} z}{1-z}\right){/tex} be purely real {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} z1 = {tex}=\bar{z}_{1}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad \frac{w-\bar{w} z}{1-z}=\frac{\bar{w}-w \bar{z}}{1-\bar{z}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad w-w \bar{z}-\bar{w} z+\bar{w} z \cdot \bar{z}=\bar{w}-z \bar{w}-w \bar{z}+w z \cdot \bar{z}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad(w-\bar{w})+(\bar{w}-w)|z|^{2}=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (w - {tex}\bar w{/tex}) (1 - |z|2) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |z|2 = 1 [as w - {tex}\bar w \ne 0{/tex}, since {tex}\beta \ne 0{/tex}]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |z| = 1 and {tex}z \neq 1{/tex}

questions.question: Let $-\frac{\pi}{6}<\theta<-\frac{\pi}{12}$. Suppose $\alpha_1$ and $\beta_1$ are the roots of the equation x^2 - 2xsec $\theta$ + 1, and $\alpha_{2} \ and \ \beta_{2}$ are the roots of the equation x^2 + 2x tan $\theta$ - 1 = 0. If $\alpha_{1}>\beta_{1}$ and $\alpha_{2}>\beta_{2}$ then $\alpha_{1}+\beta_{2}$ equals
questions.question_raw: Let {tex}-\frac{\pi}{6}<\theta<-\frac{\pi}{12}{/tex}. Suppose {tex}\alpha_1{/tex} and {tex}\beta_1{/tex} are the roots of the equation x2 - 2xsec {tex}\theta{/tex} + 1, and {tex}\alpha_{2} \ and \ \beta_{2}{/tex} are the roots of the equation x2 + 2x tan {tex}\theta{/tex} - 1 = 0. If {tex}\alpha_{1}>\beta_{1}{/tex} and {tex}\alpha_{2}>\beta_{2}{/tex} then {tex}\alpha_{1}+\beta_{2}{/tex} equals
questions.explanation:
Here, {tex}x^{2}-2 x \sec \theta+1=0{/tex} has root {tex}\alpha_{1}{/tex} and {tex}\beta_1{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad \alpha_{1}, \beta_{1}=\frac{2 \sec \theta \pm \sqrt{4 \sec ^{2} \theta-4}}{2 \times 1}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{2 \sec \theta \pm 2|\tan \theta|}{2}{/tex}
Since, {tex}\theta \in\left(-\frac{\pi}{6},-\frac{\pi}{12}\right){/tex}
i.e. {tex}\theta \in IV{/tex} quadrant {tex}=\frac{2 \sec \theta \mp 2 \tan \theta}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad \alpha_{1}=\sec \theta-\tan \theta \text { and } \beta_{1}{/tex} {tex}=\sec \theta+\tan \theta\left[\operatorname{as} \alpha_{1}>\beta_{1}\right]{/tex}
and {tex}x^{2}+2 x \tan \theta-1=0{/tex} has roots {tex}\alpha_{2}{/tex} and {tex}\beta_{2}{/tex}
i.e. {tex}\alpha_{2}, \beta_{2}=\frac{-2 \tan \theta \pm \sqrt{4 \tan ^{2} \theta+4}}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad \alpha_{2}=-\tan \theta+\sec \theta{/tex}
and {tex}\beta_{2}=-\tan \theta-\sec \theta \quad\left[\text { as } \alpha_{2}>\beta_{2}\right]{/tex}
Thus, {tex}\alpha_{1}+\beta_{2}=-2 \tan \theta{/tex}

Since, |z| = 1 and {tex}w=\frac{z-1}{z+1}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} z - 1 = wz + w {tex}\Rightarrow z=\frac{1+w}{1-w} \Rightarrow|z|=\frac{|1+w|}{|1-w|}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |1 - w| = |1 + w| [{tex}\because{/tex} |z| = 1]
On squaring both sides, we get
1 + |w|2 - 2|w|Re (w) = 1 + |w|2 + 2|w|Re (w)
[using |z1 ± z2|2 = |z1|2 + |z2|2 ± 2|z1||z2| Re ({tex}\bar z_1{/tex}z2)]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4 |w|Re |w| = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Re (w) = 0

questions.question: Let $\alpha$ and $\beta$ be the roots of equation x^2 - 6x - 2 = 0. If $a_n = \alpha^n-\beta^n$, for n $ \geq$ 1, then the value of $\frac{a_{10}-2 a_{8}}{2 a_{9}}$ is

questions.explanation:
Given, {tex}\alpha{/tex} and {tex}\beta{/tex} are the roots of the equation x2 - 6x - 2 = 0
{tex}\therefore a_{n}=a^{n}-\beta^{n}{/tex} for {tex}n \geq 1{/tex}
{tex}a_{10}=a^{10}-\beta^{10}{/tex}
{tex}a_{8}=a^{8}-\beta^{8}{/tex}
{tex}a_{9}=\alpha^{9}-\beta^{9}{/tex}
Now, consider
{tex}\frac{\alpha_{10}-2 \alpha_{8}}{2 \alpha_{9}}=\frac{\alpha^{10}-\beta^{10}-2\left(\alpha^{8}-\beta^{8}\right)}{2\left(\alpha^{9}-\alpha^{9}\right)}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\alpha^{8}\left(\alpha^{2}-2\right)-\beta^{8}\left(\beta^{2}-2\right)}{2\left(\alpha^{9}-\beta^{9}\right)}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{{{\alpha ^8} \cdot 6\alpha - {\beta ^8}6\beta }}{{2({\alpha ^9} - {\beta ^9})}} = \frac{{6{\alpha ^9} - 6{\beta ^9}}}{{2({\alpha ^9} - 6{\beta ^9})}} = \frac{6}{2} = 3{/tex}
{tex}\left. {\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{l}} {\because \alpha {\text{ and }}\beta {\text{ are the roots of }}} \\ {{x^2} - 6x - 2 = 0{\text{ or }}{x^2} = 6x + 2} \\ { \Rightarrow \quad {\alpha^2} = 6\alpha + 2 \Rightarrow {\alpha^2} - 2 = 6\alpha} \\ {{\text{ and }}\quad {\beta ^2} = 6\beta + 2 \Rightarrow {\beta ^2} - 2 = 6\beta } \end{array}} \right.} \right]{/tex}

questions.question: In the quadratic equation p(x) = 0 with real coefficients has purely imaginary roots. Then, the equation p[p(x)] = 0 has

neither real nor purely imaginary roots

questions.explanation:
If quadratic equation has purely imaginary roots, then coefficient of {tex}x{/tex} must be equal to zero.
Let {tex}p(x)=a x^2+b{/tex} with {tex}a, b{/tex} of same sign and {tex}a, b \in R{/tex}. Then,
{tex} p[p(x)]=\alpha\left(a x^2+b\right)^2+b{/tex}
{tex}p(x){/tex} has imaginary roots say i {tex}x{/tex}.
Then, also {tex}a x^2+b \in R{/tex} and {tex}\left(a x^2+b\right)^2>0{/tex}
{tex} \therefore a\left(\alpha x^2+b\right)^2+b \neq 0, \forall x {/tex}
{tex} \text { Thus, } p[p(x)] \neq 0, \forall x {/tex}

questions.question: If z_1, z_2 and z_3 are complex numbers such that $\left|z_{1}\right|=\left|z_{2}\right|=\left|z_{3}\right|=\left|\frac{1}{z_{1}}+\frac{1}{z_{2}}+\frac{1}{z_{3}}\right|=1$, then |z_1 + z_2 + z_3| is

Now, |z1| = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad\left|z_{1}\right|^{2}=1 \Rightarrow z_{1} \bar{z}_{1}=1{/tex}
Similarly, {tex}z_{2} \bar{z}_{2}=1, z_{3} \bar{z}_{3}{/tex} = 1
Again now, = {tex}\left|\frac{1}{z_{1}}+\frac{1}{z_{2}}+\frac{1}{z_{3}}\right|=1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow\left|\bar{z}_{1}+\bar{z}_{2}+\bar{z}_{3}\right|=1 \Rightarrow \overline{\left|z_{1}+z_{2}+z_{3}\right|}=1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |z1 + z2 + z3| = 1

questions.question: Let $\alpha$ and $\beta$ be the roots of equation px^2 + qx + r = 0, p $\ne$ 0. If p, q and r are in AP and $\frac{1}{\alpha}+\frac{1}{\beta}$ = 4, then the value of $|\alpha-\beta|$ is
questions.question_raw: Let {tex}\alpha{/tex} and {tex}\beta{/tex} be the roots of equation px2 + qx + r = 0, p {tex}\ne{/tex} 0. If p, q and r are in AP and {tex}\frac{1}{\alpha}+\frac{1}{\beta}{/tex} = 4, then the value of {tex}|\alpha-\beta|{/tex} is
questions.explanation:
Plan: If ax2 + bx + c = 0 has roots {tex}\alpha{/tex} and {tex}\beta{/tex}, then {tex}\alpha+\beta=-b / a{/tex} and {tex}\alpha \beta=\frac{c}{a}{/tex}. Find the value of {tex}\alpha+\beta{/tex} and {tex}\alpha \beta{/tex} and then put in {tex}(\alpha-\beta)^{2}=(\alpha+\beta)^{2}-4 \alpha \beta{/tex} to get required value.
Given, {tex}\alpha{/tex} and {tex}\beta{/tex} are roots of px2 + qx + r = 0, {tex}p \neq 0{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad \alpha+\beta=\frac{-q}{p}, \quad \alpha \beta=\frac{r}{p}{/tex} ..... (i)
Since, p, q and r are in AP.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 2q = p + r .... (ii)
Also, {tex}\frac{1}{\alpha}+\frac{1}{\beta}=4 \Rightarrow \frac{\alpha+\beta}{\alpha \beta}=4{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \alpha+\beta=4 \alpha \beta \Rightarrow \frac{-q}{p}=\frac{4 r}{p}{/tex} [from Eq. (i)]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} q = -4r
On putting the value of q in Eq. (ii), we get
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2(-4r) = p + r {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} p = -9r
Now, {tex}\alpha+\beta=\frac{-q}{p}=\frac{4 r}{p}=\frac{4 r}{-9 r}=-\frac{4}{9}{/tex}
and {tex}\alpha \beta=\frac{r}{p}=\frac{r}{-9 r}=\frac{1}{-9}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad(\alpha-\beta)^{2}=(\alpha+\beta)^{2}-4 \alpha \beta=\frac{16}{81}+\frac{4}{9}=\frac{16+36}{81}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad(\alpha-\beta)^{2}=\frac{52}{81}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad|\alpha-\beta|=\frac{2}{9} \sqrt{13}{/tex}

questions.question: For positive integers n_1, n_2 the value of expression $(1+i)^{n_{1}}+\left(1+i^{3}\right)^{n_{1}}+\left(1+i^{5}\right)^{n_{2}}+\left(1+i^{7}\right)^{n_{2}}$ here $i=\sqrt{-1}$ is a real number, if and only if
questions.question_raw: For positive integers n1, n2 the value of expression {tex}(1+i)^{n_{1}}+\left(1+i^{3}\right)^{n_{1}}+\left(1+i^{5}\right)^{n_{2}}+\left(1+i^{7}\right)^{n_{2}}{/tex} here {tex}i=\sqrt{-1}{/tex} is a real number, if and only if
questions.explanation:
{tex}(1+i)^{n_{1}}+\left(1-i\right)^{n_{1}}+\left(1+i\right)^{n_{2}}+\left(1-i\right)^{n_{2}}{/tex}
= {tex}\left[^{n_1} C_{0}+^{n_{1}} C_{1} i+^{n_{1}} C_{2} i^{2}+^{n_{1}} C_{3} i^{3}+\ldots\right]{/tex} + {tex}\left[^{n_1} C_{0}- \ ^{n_{1}} C_{1} i+\ ^{n_{1}} C_{2} i^{2}-\ ^{n_{1}} C_{3} i^{3}+\ldots\right]{/tex} + {tex}\left[^{n_2} C_{0}+ \ ^{n_{2}} C_{1} i+\ ^{n_{2}} C_{2} i^{2}+\ ^{n_{2}} C_{3} i^{3}+\ldots\right]{/tex} + {tex}\left[^{n_2} C_{0}- \ ^{n_{2}} C_{1} i+\ ^{n_{2}} C_{2} i^{2}-\ ^{n_{2}} C_{3} i^{3}+\ldots\right]{/tex}
= {tex}2\left[^{n_1} C_{0}+ \ ^{n_{1}} C_{2} i^2+\ ^{n_{1}} C_{4} i^{4}+\ldots\right]{/tex} + {tex}2\left[^{n_2} C_{0}+ \ ^{n_{2}} C_{2} i^2+\ ^{n_{2}} C_{4} i^{4}+\ldots\right]{/tex}
=  {tex}2\left[^{n_1} C_{0}- \ ^{n_{1}} C_{2}+\ ^{n_{1}} C_{4} -\ldots\right]{/tex} + {tex}2\left[^{n_2} C_{0}- \ ^{n_{2}} C_{2} +\ ^{n_{2}} C_{4} -\ldots\right]{/tex}
This is a real number irrespective of the values of n1 and n2.
Alternate Solution:
{(l + i)n1 + (l - i)n1} + {(1 + i)n2 + (1 - i)n2}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A real number for all n1 and n2 {tex}\in{/tex} R.
[{tex}\because{/tex} z + {tex}\bar z{/tex} = 2 Re (z) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (1 + i)n1 + (1 - i)n1 is real number for all n {tex}\in{/tex} R]

questions.question: Let $\alpha$ and $\beta$ be the roots of x^2 - 6x - 2 = 0, with $\alpha>\beta$. If a_n = $\alpha^{n}-\beta^{n}$ for $n \geq 1$, then the value of $\frac{a_{10}-2 a_{8}}{2 a_{9}}$ is
questions.question_raw: Let {tex}\alpha{/tex} and {tex}\beta{/tex} be the roots of x2 - 6x - 2 = 0, with {tex}\alpha>\beta{/tex}. If an = {tex}\alpha^{n}-\beta^{n}{/tex} for {tex}n \geq 1{/tex}, then the value of {tex}\frac{a_{10}-2 a_{8}}{2 a_{9}}{/tex} is
questions.explanation:
{tex}\frac{a_{10}-2 a_{8}}{2 a_{9}}=\frac{\left(\alpha^{10}-\beta^{10}\right)-2\left(\alpha^{8}-\beta^{8}\right)}{2\left(\alpha^{9}-\beta^{9}\right)}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\alpha^{8}\left(\alpha^{2}-2\right)-\beta^{8}\left(\beta^{2}-2\right)}{2\left(\alpha^{9}-\beta^{9}\right)}{/tex}
[ {tex}\because \alpha{/tex} is root of x2 - 6x - 2 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow \alpha^{2}-2=6 \alpha{/tex} and {tex}\beta{/tex} is root of x2 - 6x - 2 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \beta^{2}-2=6 \beta{/tex} ]
{tex}=\frac{\alpha^{8}(6 \alpha)-\beta^{8}(6 \beta)}{2\left(\alpha^{9}-\beta^{9}\right)}=\frac{6\left(\alpha^{9}-\beta^{9}\right)}{2\left(\alpha^{9}-\beta^{9}\right)}=3{/tex}

questions.question_raw: Let p and q be real numbers such that p {tex} e{/tex} 0, p3  {tex} e{/tex} q and p3 {tex} e{/tex} -q. If {tex}\alpha{/tex} and {tex} eta{/tex} are non-zero complex numbers satisfying {tex}\alpha + eta{/tex} = - p and {tex}\alpha^3 \ + eta^3{/tex} = q, then a quadratic equation having {tex} rac{\alpha}{ eta} ext { and } rac{ eta}{\alpha}{/tex} as its roots is
questions.explanation:
Sum of roots {tex}=\frac{\alpha^{2}+\beta^{2}}{\alpha \beta}{/tex} and product = 1
Given, {tex}\alpha+\beta=-p{/tex} and {tex}\alpha^{3}+\beta^{3}=q{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad(\alpha+\beta)\left(\alpha^{2}-\alpha \beta+\beta^{2}\right)=q{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad \alpha^{2}+\beta^{2}-\alpha \beta=\frac{-q}{p}{/tex} ..... (i)
and {tex}(\alpha+\beta)^{2}=p^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \alpha^{2}+\beta^{2}+2 \alpha \beta=p^{2}{/tex} ..... (ii)
From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get
{tex}\alpha^{2}+\beta^{2}=\frac{p^{3}-2 q}{3 p}{/tex} and {tex}\alpha \beta=\frac{p^{3}+q}{3 p}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Required equation is, {tex}x^{2}-\frac{\left(p^{3}-2 q\right) x}{\left(p^{3}+q\right)}+1={0}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow\left(p^{3}+q\right) x^{2}-\left(p^{3}-2 q\right) x+\left(p^{3}+q\right)=0{/tex}

questions.question: Let $\alpha, \beta$ be the roots of the equation x^2 -px + r = 0 and $\frac{\alpha}{2}$, 2$\beta$ be the roots of the equation x^2 - qx + r = 0. Then, the value of r is
questions.question_raw: Let {tex}\alpha, \beta{/tex} be the roots of the equation x2 -px + r = 0 and {tex}\frac{\alpha}{2}{/tex}, 2{tex}\beta{/tex} be the roots of the equation x2 - qx + r = 0. Then, the value of r is

{tex}\therefore \alpha+\beta{/tex} = p ...(i)
and {tex}\alpha \beta{/tex} = r ...(ii)
Also {tex}\frac{\alpha}{2}{/tex} and 2{tex}\beta{/tex} are the roots of x2 - qx + r = 0

Solving (i) and (iii) for {tex}\alpha{/tex} and {tex}\beta{/tex}, we get

questions.question: If $z=\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}+\frac{i}{2}\right)^{5}+\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}-\frac{i}{2}\right)^{5}$, then
questions.question_raw: If {tex}z=\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}+\frac{i}{2}\right)^{5}+\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}-\frac{i}{2}\right)^{5}{/tex}, then
questions.explanation:
Given, {tex}z=\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}+\frac{i}{2}\right)^{5}+\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}-\frac{i}{2}\right)^{5}{/tex} {tex}\left[\because \omega=\frac{-1+i \sqrt{3}}{2} \text { and } \omega^{2}=\frac{-1-i \sqrt{3}}{2}\right]{/tex}
Now, {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}+i}{2}=-i\left(\frac{-1+i \sqrt{3}}{2}\right)=-i \omega{/tex}
and {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}-i}{2}=i\left(\frac{-1-i \sqrt{3}}{2}\right)=i \omega^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad z=(-i \omega)^{5}+\left(i \omega^{2}\right)^{5}=-i \omega^{2}+i \omega{/tex}
{tex}=i\left(\omega-\omega^{2}\right)=i(i \sqrt{3})=-\sqrt{3}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Re(z) < 0 and lm (z) = 0
Alternate Solution:
We know that,  z + {tex}\bar{z}{/tex} =2 Re(z)
If {tex}z=\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}+\frac{i}{2}\right)^{5}+\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}-\frac{i}{2}\right)^{5}{/tex}, then z is purely real. i.e. Im (z) = 0

questions.question: If a,b,c are the sides of a triangle ABC such that x^2 -2 (a + b+ c) x+ 3$\lambda$ (ab + 6c+ ca) = 0 has real roots, then
questions.question_raw: If a,b,c are the sides of a triangle ABC such that x2 -2 (a + b+ c) x+ 3{tex}\lambda{/tex} (ab + 6c+ ca) = 0 has real roots, then
questions.explanation:
Since, roots are real, therefore {tex}D \geq 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 4(a+b+c)^{2}-12 \lambda(a b+b c+c a) \geq 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad(a+b+c)^{2} \geq 3 \lambda(a b+b c+c a){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad a^{2}+b^{2}+c^{2} \geq(a b+b c+c a)(3 \lambda-2){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 3 \lambda-2 \leq \frac{a^{2}+b^{2}+c^{2}}{a b+b c+c a}{/tex} .... (i)
Also, {tex}\cos A=\frac{b^{2}+c^{2}-a^{2}}{2 b c}<1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b2 + c2 - a2 < 2bc
Similarly, c2 + a2 - b2 < 2ca
and a2 + b2 - c2 < 2ab
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a2 + b2 + c2 < 2 (ab + bc + ca)
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{a^{2}+b^{2}+c^{2}}{a b+b c+c a}<2{/tex} .... (ii)
From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get
{tex}3 \lambda-2<2 \Rightarrow \lambda<\frac{4}{3}{/tex}

questions.question: If one root is square of the other root of the equation x^2 + px + q =0, then the relation between p and q is
questions.question_raw: If one root is square of the other root of the equation x2 + px + q =0, then the relation between p and q is
questions.explanation:
Let the roots of x2 + px + q = 0 be {tex}\alpha{/tex} and {tex}\alpha^2{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \alpha+\alpha^{2}=-p{/tex} and {tex}\alpha^{3}=q{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \alpha(\alpha+1)=-p{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \alpha^{3}\left\{\alpha^{3}+1+3 \alpha(\alpha+1)\right\}=-p^{3}{/tex} [cubing both sides]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} q(q + 1 - 3p) = -p3
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} p3 - (3p - 1)q + q2 = 0
