questions.question_raw: If z is a complex number of unit modulus and argument {tex}\theta{/tex}, then arg {tex}\left(\frac{1+z}{1+\bar{z}}\right){/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation:
Given, |z| = 1, arg z = {tex}\theta{/tex}
 {tex}\therefore{/tex} z = ei{tex}\theta{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\bar{z}=e^{-i \theta} \Rightarrow \bar{z}=\frac{1}{z}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} arg {tex}\left(\frac{1+z}{1+\bar{z}}\right)=\arg \left(\frac{1+z}{1+\frac{1}{z}}\right)=\arg (z)=\theta{/tex}

questions.question: The number of solutions of log_4(x - 1) = log_2(x - 3) is
questions.question_raw: The number of solutions of log4(x - 1) = log2(x - 3) is
questions.explanation:
Given, log4(x - 1) = log2(x - 3) = log41/2(x - 3)
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \log _{4}(x-1)=2 \log _{4}(x-3){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log4(x - 1) = log4(x - 3)2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x - 3)2 = x - 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 + 9 - 6x = x - 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 - 7x + 10 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x - 2)(x - 5) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 2, or x = 5
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 5        [{tex}\because{/tex} x = 2 makes log (x - 3) undefined]
Hence, one solution exists.

questions.question: For the equation 3x^2 + px + 3 = 0, p > 0, if one of the root is square of the other, then p is equal to
questions.question_raw: For the equation 3x2 + px + 3 = 0, p > 0, if one of the root is square of the other, then p is equal to
questions.explanation:
Let {tex}\alpha, \alpha^{2}{/tex} be the roots of 3x2 + px + 3 = 0
Now, {tex}S=\alpha+\alpha^{2}=-\frac {p}{3}{/tex}
{tex}P=\alpha^{3}=1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \alpha=1, \omega, \omega^{2}{/tex}
Now, {tex}\alpha + {\alpha ^2} = - \frac{p}{3}{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \omega + {\omega ^2} = - \frac{p}{3}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex} - 1 = -\frac{p}{3}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} p = 3

questions.question: If arg (z) < 0, then arg (-z) - arg (z) equals
questions.question_raw: If arg (z) < 0, then arg (-z) - arg (z) equals
questions.explanation:
Since, arg(z) < 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} arg (z) = - {tex}\theta{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} z - r cos (-{tex}\theta{/tex}) + i sin (-{tex}\theta{/tex})
= r (cos {tex}\theta{/tex} - i sin {tex}\theta{/tex})
and - z = - r [cos {tex}\theta{/tex} - i sin {tex}\theta{/tex}]
= r [cos ({tex}\pi{/tex} - {tex}\theta{/tex}) + i sin ({tex}\pi{/tex} - {tex}\theta{/tex})]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} arg (-z) = {tex}\pi{/tex} - {tex}\theta{/tex}
Thus, arg (-z) - arg (z) = {tex}\pi{/tex} - {tex}\theta{/tex} - ( - {tex}\theta{/tex}) = {tex}\pi{/tex}

questions.question: If $\alpha\ and\ \beta$ $(\alpha<\beta)$ are the roots of the equation x^2 + bx + c = 0, where c < 0 < b, then
questions.question_raw: If {tex}\alpha\ and\ \beta{/tex} {tex}(\alpha<\beta){/tex} are the roots of the equation x2 + bx + c = 0, where c < 0 < b, then
questions.explanation:
Given, c < 0 < b
Since, {tex}\alpha+\beta=-b{/tex} ... (i)
and {tex}\alpha \beta=c{/tex} ... (ii)
From Eq. (ii), c < 0 {tex}\Rightarrow \alpha \beta<0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Either {tex}\alpha{/tex} is -ve, {tex}\beta{/tex} is -ve or {tex}\alpha{/tex} is +ve, {tex}\beta{/tex} is -ve
From Eq. (i), {tex}b>0 \Rightarrow-b<0 \Rightarrow \alpha+\beta<0 \Rightarrow{/tex} the sum is negative.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Modulus of negative quantity is > modulus of positive quantity but {tex}\alpha<\beta{/tex} is given.
Therefore, it is clear that {tex}\alpha{/tex} is negative and {tex}\beta{/tex} is positive and modulus of {tex}\alpha{/tex} is greater than modulus of {tex}\beta{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \alpha<0<\beta<|\alpha|{/tex}

questions.question: The equation $\sqrt{x+1}-\sqrt{x-1}=\sqrt{4 x-1}$ has
questions.question_raw: The equation {tex}\sqrt{x+1}-\sqrt{x-1}=\sqrt{4 x-1}{/tex} has
questions.explanation:
Since, {tex}\sqrt{x+1}-\sqrt{x-1}=\sqrt{4 x-1}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad(x+1)+(x-1)-2 \sqrt{x^{2}-1}=4 x-1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 1-2 x=2 \sqrt{x^{2}-1} \Rightarrow 1+4 x^{2}-4 x=4 x^{2}-4{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 4 x=5 \Rightarrow x=\frac{5}{4}{/tex}
But it does not satisfy the given equation.
Hence, no solution exists.

questions.question: Let z and w be two complex numbers such that $|z| \leq 1$, $|w| \leq 1$ and $|z+i w|=|z-\overline{i w}|=2$, then z equals
questions.question_raw: Let z and w be two complex numbers such that {tex}|z| \leq 1{/tex}, {tex}|w| \leq 1{/tex} and {tex}|z+i w|=|z-\overline{i w}|=2{/tex}, then z equals
questions.explanation:
Given, |z + iw| = |z - {tex}\overline {iw}{/tex} | = 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |z - (-iw)| = |z - ({tex}\overline {iw}{/tex})| = 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |z - (-iw)| = |z - (- {tex}\overline {iw}{/tex})|
{tex}\therefore{/tex} z lies on the perpendicular bisector of the line joining - iw and - {tex}\overline {iw}{/tex}). Since, - {tex}\overline {iw}{/tex} is the mirror image of - iw in the X-axis, the locus of z is the X-axis.
Let z = x + iy and y = 0
Now, |z| {tex}\leq{/tex} 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 + 02 {tex}\leq{/tex} 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -1 {tex}\leq{/tex} x {tex}\leq{/tex} 1.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} z may take values given in option (c).
Alternate Solution:
|z + iw| {tex}\leq{/tex} |z| + |iw| = |z| + |w|
{tex}\leq{/tex} 1 + 1 =2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} |z + iw|{tex}\leq{/tex} 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |z + i w| = 2 holds when arg z - arg i w = 0,
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \arg \frac{z}{i w}=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{z}{i \ w}=0{/tex} is purely real.
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{z}{ w}{/tex} is purely imaginary.
Similarly, when |z - i {tex}\overline {w}{/tex}| = 2, then {tex}\frac{z}{\bar{w}}{/tex} is purely imaginary
Now, given relation |z + iw | = |z - {tex}\overline {iw}{/tex}| = 2
Put w = i, we get
Iz + i2| = |z + i2| = 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |z - 1| = 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} z = -1       [{tex}\because{/tex} |z| {tex}\leq{/tex}1]
Put w = -i, we get
|z - i2| = |z - i2| = 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |z + 1| = 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} z = 1       [{tex}\because{/tex} |z| {tex}\leq{/tex}1]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} z = 1 or -1 is the correct option.

questions.question: The equation $x^{\frac{3}{x^{4}}\left(\log _{2} x\right)^{2}+\log _{2} x-\frac{5}{4}}$ = $\sqrt2$ has
questions.question_raw: The equation {tex}x^{\frac{3}{x^{4}}\left(\log _{2} x\right)^{2}+\log _{2} x-\frac{5}{4}}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt2{/tex} has
questions.explanation:
Given, {tex}x^{\frac{3}{4}\left(\log _{2} x\right)^{2}+\log _{2} x-\frac{5}{4}}=\sqrt{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{3}{4}\left(\log _{2} x\right)^{2}+\log _{2} x-\frac{5}{4}=\log _{x} \sqrt{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{3}{4}\left(\log _{2} x\right)^{2}+\log _{2} x-\frac{5}{4}=\frac{1}{2 \log _{2} x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 3\left(\log _{2} x\right)^{3}+4\left(\log _{2} x\right)^{2}-5\left(\log _{2} x\right)-2=0{/tex}
Put log2 x = y
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 3y3 + 4y2 - 5y - 2 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (y - 1) (y + 2) (3y + 1) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 1, -2, {tex}\frac{{ - 1}}{3}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log2 x = 1, -2, {tex}\frac{{ - 1}}{3}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad x=2, \frac{1}{2^{1 / 3}}, \frac{1}{4}{/tex}

questions.question: If $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are the roots of x^2 + px + q = 0 and $\alpha^4, \ \beta^4$ are the roots of x^2 - rx + s = 0, then the equation x^2 - 4qx + 2q^2 - r = 0 has always
questions.question_raw: If {tex}\alpha{/tex} and {tex}\beta{/tex} are the roots of x2 + px + q = 0 and {tex}\alpha^4, \ \beta^4{/tex} are the roots of x2 - rx + s = 0, then the equation x2 - 4qx + 2q2 - r = 0 has always

options.text: One positive and one negative root

questions.explanation:
Since, {tex}\alpha, \beta{/tex} are the roots of x2 + px + q = 0 and {tex}\alpha^{4}, \beta^{4}{/tex} are the roots of x2 - rx + s = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow \alpha+\beta=-p ; \alpha \beta=q ; \alpha^{4}+\beta^{4}=r{/tex} and {tex}\alpha^{4} \beta^{4}=s{/tex}
Let roots of x2 - 4qx + (2q2 - r) = 0 be {tex}\alpha'{/tex} and {tex}\beta'{/tex}
Now, {tex}\alpha^{\prime} \beta^{\prime}=\left(2 q^{2}-r\right)=2(\alpha \beta)^{2}-\left(\alpha^{4}+\beta^{4}\right){/tex}
{tex}=-\left(\alpha^{4}+\beta^{4}-2 \alpha^{2} \beta^{2}\right)=-\left(\alpha^{2}-\beta^{2}\right)^{2}<0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Roots are real and of opposite sign.

questions.question: Let z and $\omega$ be two non-zero complex numbers such that |z| = |$\omega$| and arg (z) + arg ($\omega$) = $\pi$, then z equals
questions.question_raw: Let z and {tex}\omega{/tex} be two non-zero complex numbers such that |z| = |{tex}\omega{/tex}| and arg (z) + arg ({tex}\omega{/tex}) = {tex}\pi{/tex}, then z equals
questions.explanation:
Since, |z| = |{tex}\omega{/tex}| and arg (z) = {tex}\pi{/tex} - arg ({tex}\omega{/tex})
Let {tex}\omega{/tex} = rei{tex}\theta{/tex}, then {tex}\bar{\omega}{/tex} = rei{tex}\theta{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} z = rei({tex}\pi-\theta{/tex}) = {tex}re^{i \pi} \cdot e^{-i\theta}{/tex}= -re-i{tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}-\bar{\omega}{/tex}

questions.question: The equation x - $\frac{2}{x-1}=1-\frac{2}{x-1}$ has
questions.question_raw: The equation x - {tex}\frac{2}{x-1}=1-\frac{2}{x-1}{/tex} has
questions.explanation:
Given, {tex}x-\frac{2}{x-1}=1-\frac{2}{x-1} \Rightarrow x=1{/tex}
But at x = 1, the given equation is not defined.
Hence, no solution exist.

questions.question: If z_1 and z_2 are two non-zero complex numbers such that |z_1 + z_2| = |z_1| + |z_2l, then arg (z_1) - arg (z_2) is equal to
questions.question_raw: If z1 and z2 are two non-zero complex numbers such that |z1 + z2| = |z1| + |z2l, then arg (z1) - arg (z2) is equal to
questions.explanation:
Given, |z1 + z2l = Iz1| + |z2|
On squaring both sides, we get
|z1|2 + |z2l2 + 2Iz1||z2| cos (arg z1 - arg z2) = |z1|2 + |z2l2 + 2Iz1||z2|
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2|z1||z2l cos (arg z1 - arg z2) = 2|z1| |z2|
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cos (arg z1 - arg z2) = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} arg (z1) - arg (z2) = 0

questions.question: For real x, the function $\frac{(x-a)(x-b)}{(x-c)}$ will assume all real values provided
questions.question_raw: For real x, the function {tex}\frac{(x-a)(x-b)}{(x-c)}{/tex} will assume all real values provided
questions.explanation:
Let {tex}y=\frac{x^{2}-(a+b) x+a b}{x-c}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} yx - cy = x2 - (a + b)x + ab
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 - (a + b + y)x + (ab + cy) = 0
For real roots {tex}D \geq 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad(a+b+y)^{2}-4(a b+c y) \geq 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow(a+b)^{2}+y^{2}+2(a+b) y-4 a b-4 c y \geq 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y2 + 2(a + b - 2c)y + (a -b)2 {tex}\geq{/tex} 0
which is true for all real values of y.
{tex}\therefore \ D \ \leq \ 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad4(a+b-2c)^{2}-4(a-b)^2 \leq 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4(a + b - 2c + a - b)(a + b - 2c - a + b) {tex}\leq{/tex} 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (2a - 2c)(2b - 2c) {tex}\leq{/tex} 0
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad(a-c)(b-c) \leq 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad(c-a)(c-b) \leq 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c must lie between a and b
i.e. {tex}a \leq c \leq b \text { or } b \leq c \leq a{/tex}

questions.question: If a, b, c and u, v, w are the complex numbers representing the vertices of two triangles such that c = (1 - r) a + rb and w = (1 - r) u + rv, where r is a complex number, then the two triangles
questions.question_raw: If a, b, c and u, v, w are the complex numbers representing the vertices of two triangles such that c = (1 - r) a + rb and w = (1 - r) u + rv, where r is a complex number, then the two triangles
questions.explanation:
Since a, b, c and u, v, w are the vertices of two triangles.
Also, c = (1 - r) a + rb
and w = (1 - r) u + rv ...(i)
Consider {tex}\left|\begin{array}{lll}{a} & {u} & {1} \\ {b} & {v} & {1} \\ {c} & {w} & {1}\end{array}\right|{/tex}
Applying R3 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} R3 - {(1 - r) R1 + rR2}
= {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc}{a} & {u} & {1} \\ {b} & {v} & {1} \\ {c-(1-r) a-r b} & {w-(1-r) u-r v} & {1-(1-r)-r}\end{array}\right|{/tex}
= {tex}\left|\begin{array}{lll}{a} & {u} & {1} \\ {b} & {v} & {1} \\ {0} & {0} & {0}\end{array}\right|=0{/tex} [from Eq. (i)]

questions.question: Both the roots of the equation (x - b) (x - c) + (x - a) (x - c) + (x - a) (x - b) = 0 are always
questions.question_raw: Both the roots of the equation (x - b) (x - c) + (x - a) (x - c) + (x - a) (x - b) = 0 are always
questions.explanation:
(x - a) (x - b) + (x - b) (x - c) + (x - c) (x - a) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3x2 - 2(a + b + c)x + (ab + bc + ca) = 0
Now, discriminant = 4(a + b + c)2 - 12(ab + bc + ca)
= 4{a2 + b2 + c2 - ab - bc - ca}
= 2{(a - b)2 + (b - c)2 + (c - a)2}
Which is always positive.
Hence, both roots are real.

questions.question: Let a > 0, b > 0 and c > 0. Then, both the roots of the equation ax^2 + bx+ c = 0
questions.question_raw: Let a > 0, b > 0 and c > 0. Then, both the roots of the equation ax2 + bx+ c = 0
questions.explanation:
Since, a, b, c > 0 and ax2 + bx + c = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad x=\frac{-b}{2 a} \pm \frac{\sqrt{b^{2}-4 a c}}{2 a}{/tex}
Case I When b2 - 4ac > 0
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad x=\frac{-b}{2 a}-\frac{\sqrt{b^{2}-4 a c}}{2 a}{/tex}
and {tex}\frac{-b}{2 a}+\frac{\sqrt{b^{2}-4 a c}}{2 a}{/tex} both roots, are negative.
Case II When b2 - 4ac = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow x=\frac{-b}{2 a}{/tex}, i.e. both roots are equal and negative
Case III When b2 - 4ac < 0
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad x=\frac{-b}{2 a} \pm i \frac{\sqrt{4 a c-b^{2}}}{2 a}{/tex}
have negative real part.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} From above discussion, both roots have negative real parts.

questions.question: Let $z=\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}+\frac{i}{2}\right)^{5}+\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}-\frac{i}{2}\right)^{5}$. If R(z) and I(z) respectively denote the real and imaginary parts of z, then
questions.question_raw: Let {tex}z=\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}+\frac{i}{2}\right)^{5}+\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}-\frac{i}{2}\right)^{5}{/tex}. If R(z) and I(z) respectively denote the real and imaginary parts of z, then
questions.explanation:
Given, {tex}z=\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}+\frac{i}{2}\right)^{5}+\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}-\frac{i}{2}\right)^{5}{/tex}
{tex}\because{/tex} Euler's form of
{tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}+\frac{i}{2}=\left(\cos \frac{\pi}{6}+i \sin \frac{\pi}{6}\right)=e^{i(\pi(6)}{/tex}
and {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}-\frac{i}{2}=\cos \left(\frac{-\pi}{6}\right)+i \sin \left(-\frac{\pi}{6}\right)=e^{-i \pi 6}{/tex}
So, {tex}z=\left(e^{i \pi/ 6}\right)^{5}+\left(e^{-i \pi /6}\right)^{5}=e^{i \frac{5 \pi}{6}}+e^{-i \frac{5 \pi}{6}}{/tex}
{tex}=\left(\cos \frac{5 \pi}{6}+i \sin \frac{5 \pi}{6}\right)+\left(\cos \frac{5 \pi}{6}-i \sin \frac{5 \pi}{6}\right){/tex}
{tex}=2 \cos \frac{5 \pi}{6}\quad\left[\because e^{i \theta}=\cos \theta+i \sin \theta\right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} I(z) = 0 and R(z) = -2 cos{tex}\frac{\pi}{6}=-\sqrt{3}<0{/tex} {tex}\left[\because \cos \frac{5 \pi}{6}=\cos \left(\pi-\frac{\pi}{6}\right)=-\cos \frac{\pi}{6}\right]{/tex}

questions.question: If $\alpha,\ \beta \ and \ \gamma$ are three consecutive terms of a non-constant GP such that the equations $\alpha$x^2 + 2$\beta$x + $\gamma$ = 0 and x^2 + x - 1 = 0 have a common root, then, $\alpha$($\beta$ + $\gamma$) is equal to
questions.question_raw: If {tex}\alpha,\ \beta \ and \ \gamma{/tex} are three consecutive terms of a non-constant GP such that the equations {tex}\alpha{/tex}x2 + 2{tex}\beta{/tex}x + {tex}\gamma{/tex} = 0 and x2 + x - 1 = 0 have a common root, then, {tex}\alpha{/tex}({tex}\beta{/tex} + {tex}\gamma{/tex}) is equal to
questions.explanation:
Given, {tex}\alpha, \beta{/tex} and {tex}\gamma{/tex} are three consecutive terms of a non-constant GP.
Let {tex}\alpha=\alpha, \beta=\alpha r{/tex}, {tex}\gamma=\alpha r^{2},\{r \neq 0,1\}{/tex}
and given quadratic equation is
{tex}\alpha x^{2}+2 \beta x+\gamma=0{/tex} ..... (i)
On putting the values of {tex}\alpha, \beta, \gamma{/tex} in Eq. (i), we get
{tex}\alpha x^{2}+2 \alpha r x+\alpha r^{2}=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad x^{2}+2 r x+r^{2}=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad(x+r)^{2}=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = -r
{tex}\because{/tex} The quadratic equation {tex}\alpha x^{2}+2 \beta x+\gamma=0{/tex} and x2 + x - 1 = 0 have a common root, So x = -r must be root of equation x2 + x - 1 = 0, So
r2 - r - 1 = 0 ..... (ii)
Now, {tex}\alpha(\beta+\gamma)=\alpha\left(\alpha r+\alpha r^{2}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\alpha^{2}\left(r+r^{2}\right){/tex}
From the options,
{tex}\beta y=\alpha r \cdot \alpha r^{2}=\alpha^{2} r^{3}=\alpha^{2}\left(r+r^{2}\right){/tex}
{tex}\left[\because r^{2}-r-1=0 \Rightarrow r^{3}=r+r^{2}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad \alpha(\beta+\gamma)=\beta \gamma{/tex}

questions.question: A particle P starts from the point z_0 = 1 + 2i, where $i=\sqrt{-1}$. It moves first horizontally away from origin by 5 units and then vertically away from origin by 3 units to reach a point z_1. From z_1 the particle moves $\sqrt2$ units in the direction of the vector $\hat{i}+\hat{j}$ and then it moves through an angle $\frac{\pi}{2}$ in anti-clockwise direction on a circle with centre at origin, to reach a point z_2. The point z_2 is given by
questions.question_raw: A particle P starts from the point z0 = 1 + 2i, where {tex}i=\sqrt{-1}{/tex}. It moves first horizontally away from origin by 5 units and then vertically away from origin by 3 units to reach a point z1. From z1 the particle moves {tex}\sqrt2{/tex} units in the direction of the vector {tex}\hat{i}+\hat{j}{/tex} and then it moves through an angle {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} in anti-clockwise direction on a circle with centre at origin, to reach a point z2. The point z2 is given by
questions.explanation:
z2' = (6 + {tex}\sqrt 2{/tex} cos {tex}45^\circ{/tex}, 5 + {tex}\sqrt 2{/tex} sin {tex}45^\circ{/tex}) = (7, 6) = 7 + 6i
By rotation about (0, 0),
{tex}\frac{z_{2}}{z_{2}^{\prime}}=e^{i \pi 2} \Rightarrow z_{2}=z_{2}^{\prime}\left(e^{i \frac{\pi}{2}}\right){/tex}
= (7 + 6i) {tex}\left(\cos \frac{\pi}{2}+i \sin \frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} = (7 + 6i) (i) = - 6 + 7i

questions.question: If the equations x^2+ 2x + 3 =0 and ax^2+ bx + c = 0, a ,b ,c $\in$ R have a common root, then a : b : c is
questions.question_raw: If the equations x2+ 2x + 3 =0 and ax2+ bx + c = 0, a ,b ,c {tex}\in{/tex} R have a common root, then a : b : c is
questions.explanation:
Given equations are x2 + 2x + 3 = 0 .... (i)
and ax2 + bx + c = 0 ..... (ii)
Since, Eq. (i) has imaginary roots, so Eq. (ii) will also have both roots sam as Eq. (i).
Thus, {tex}\frac{a}{1}=\frac{b}{2}=\frac{c}{3}{/tex}
Hence, a : b : c is 1 : 2 : 3

questions.question: A man walks a distance of 3 units from the origin towards the North-East (N 45^o E) direction. From there, he walks a distance of 4 units towards the North-West (N 45^o W) direction to reach a point P. Then, the position of P in the Argand plane is
questions.question_raw: A man walks a distance of 3 units from the origin towards the North-East (N 45o E) direction. From there, he walks a distance of 4 units towards the North-West (N 45o W) direction to reach a point P. Then, the position of P in the Argand plane is
questions.explanation:
Let OA =3, so th at the complex number associated with A is 3ei{tex}\pi{/tex}/44. If z is the complex number associated with P,
then

 {tex}\frac{z-3 e^\frac {i \pi} {4}}{0-3 e^\frac {i \pi} {4}}=\frac{4}{3} e^{-i \pi 2}=-\frac{4 i}{3}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 3 z-9 e^\frac {i \pi} {4}=12 i e^\frac {i \pi} {4}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}z=(3+4 i) e^\frac {i \pi} {4}{/tex}

questions.question: Let $\alpha$, $\beta$ be the roots of the equation, (x - a )(x - b) = c, c $\ne$ 0. Then the roots of the equation (x - $\alpha$) (x - $\beta$) + c = 0 are
questions.question_raw: Let {tex}\alpha{/tex}, {tex}\beta{/tex} be the roots of the equation, (x - a )(x - b) = c, c {tex}\ne{/tex} 0. Then the roots of the equation (x - {tex}\alpha{/tex}) (x - {tex}\beta{/tex}) + c = 0 are
questions.explanation:
Given {tex}\alpha, \beta {/tex} are the roots of (x - a) (x - b) - c = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad(x-a)(x-b)-c=(x-\alpha)(x-\beta){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad(x-a)(x-b)=(x-\alpha)(x-\beta)+c{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a, b are the roots of equation {tex}(x-\alpha)(x-\beta)+c=0{/tex}

questions.question: A value of b for which the equations x^2 + bx -1 = 0, x^2 + x + b = 0 have one root in common is
questions.question_raw: A value of b for which the equations x2 + bx -1 = 0, x2 + x + b = 0 have one root in common is
questions.explanation:
If a1x2 + b1x + c1 = 0
and a2x2 + b2x + c2 = 0 have a common real root, then
{tex} \quad\left(a_{1} c_{2}-a_{2} c_{1}\right)^{2}=\left(b_{1} c_{2}-b_{2}c_1\right)\left(a_{1} b_{2}-a_{2} b_{1}\right){/tex}
{tex}\left.\therefore \begin{array}{l}{x^{2}+b x-1=0} \\ {x^{2}+x+b=0}\end{array}\right\}{/tex} have a common root.
{tex}\Rightarrow (1+b)^{2}=\left(b^{2}+1\right)(1-b){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow b^{2}+2 b+1=b^{2}-b^{3}+1-b{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow b^{3}+3 b=0{/tex}
{tex}\therefore b\left(b^{2}+3\right)=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow b=0, \pm \sqrt{3} i{/tex}

questions.question: The shaded region, where P = (-1, 0), $Q=(-1+\sqrt{2}, \sqrt{2})$ $R=(-1+\sqrt{2},-\sqrt{2})$, S = (1, 0) is represented by
questions.question_raw: The shaded region, where P = (-1, 0), {tex}Q=(-1+\sqrt{2}, \sqrt{2}){/tex} {tex}R=(-1+\sqrt{2},-\sqrt{2}){/tex}, S = (1, 0) is represented by
questions.explanation:
Since, |PQ| = |PS| = |PR| = 2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Shaded part represents the external part of circle having centre (-1,0) and radius 2.
As we know equation of circle having centre z0 and radius r, is | z - z0| = r
{tex}\therefore{/tex} z - (-1 + 0 i) > 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |z + 1| > 2
Also, argument of z + 1 with respect to positive direction of A-axis is {tex}\pi{/tex}/4.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} arg (z + 1) {tex}\leq \frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} ...(ii)
and argument of z + 1 in anticlockwise direction is - {tex}\pi{/tex}/4.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} -{tex}\pi{/tex}/4 < arg (z + 1) .. .(ii)
From Eqs. (i) and (ii),
| arg (z + 1) | {tex}\leq{/tex} {tex}\pi{/tex}/4

questions.question: Let P(4, - 4) and Q(9,6) be two points on the parabola, y^2 = 4x and let X be any point on the arc POQ of this parabola, where O is the vertex of this parabola, such that the area of $\Delta$PXQ is maximum. Then, this maximum area (in sq units) is
questions.question_raw: Let P(4, - 4) and Q(9,6) be two points on the parabola, y2 = 4x and let X be any point on the arc POQ of this parabola, where O is the vertex of this parabola, such that the area of {tex}\Delta{/tex}PXQ is maximum. Then, this maximum area (in sq units) is
questions.explanation:
Given parabola is y2 = 4x,
Since, X lies on the parabola, so let the coordinates of X be (t2, 2t). Thus, the coordinates of the vertices of the triangle PXQ are P(4, -4), X(t2, 2t) and Q(9, 6)

{tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of {tex}\Delta PXQ = \frac{1}{2}\left| {\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 4&{ - 4}&1 \\ {{t^2}}&{2t}&1 \\ 9&6&1 \end{array}} \right|} \right|{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{2}\left|\left[4(2 t-6)+4\left(t^{2}-9\right)+1\left(6 t^{2}-18 t\right) |\right.\right.{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{2}\left|\left[8 t-24+4 t^{2}-36+6 t^{2}-18 t\right]\right|{/tex}
{tex}=\left|5 t^{2}-5 t-30\right|=|5(t+2)(t-3)|{/tex}
Now, as X is any point on the arc POQ of the parabola, therefore ordinate of point X, {tex}2 t \in(-4,6) \Rightarrow t \in(-2,3){/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of {tex}\Delta P X Q{/tex} = -5(t + 2)(t - 3) = -5t2 + 5t + 30
{tex}[\because|x-a|{/tex} = -(x - a), if x < a]
The maximum area (in square units)
{tex}=-\left[\frac{25-4(-5)(30)}{4(-5)}\right]=\frac{125}{4}{/tex}
[{tex}\because{/tex} Maximum value of quadratic expression ax2 + bx + c, when a < 0 is {tex}-\frac{D}{4 a}{/tex}]

questions.question: Consider the quadratic equation, (c - 5 )x^2 - 2cx + (c - 4) = 0, c $\ne$ 5. Let S be the set of all integral values of c for which one root of the equation lies in the interval (0, 2) and its other root lies in the interval (2, 3). Then, the number of elements in S is
questions.question_raw: Consider the quadratic equation, (c - 5 )x2 - 2cx + (c - 4) = 0, c {tex}\ne{/tex} 5. Let S be the set of all integral values of c for which one root of the equation lies in the interval (0, 2) and its other root lies in the interval (2, 3). Then, the number of elements in S is
questions.explanation:
Let f(x) = (c - 5)x2 - 2cx + (c - 4) = 0
Then, according to problem, the graph of y = f(x) will be either of the two ways, shown below.

In both cases f(0). f(2) < 0 and f(2)f(3) < 0
Now, consider f(0) f(2) < 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (c - 4) [4(c - 5) - 4c + (c - 4)] < 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (c - 4) (c - 24) < 0
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad c \in(4,24){/tex} ...... (i)

Similarly, {tex}f(2) \cdot f(3)<0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} [4(c - 5) -4c + (c - 4)]
[9(c - 5) -6c + (c - 4)] < 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (c - 24) (4c - 49) < 0


{tex}\Rightarrow \quad c \in\left(\frac{49}{4}, 24\right){/tex} ........(ii)
From (i) and (ii) we get 
{tex} c \in\left(\frac{49}{4}, 24\right){/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Integral values of c are 13, 14, ...., 23.
Thus, 11  integral values of c are possible.

questions.question: If $0<\alpha<\frac{\pi}{2}$ is a fixed angle. If $P=(\cos \theta, \sin \theta)$ and $Q=\{\cos (\alpha-\theta), \sin (\alpha-\theta)\}$, then Q is obtained from P by
questions.question_raw: If {tex}0<\alpha<\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} is a fixed angle. If {tex}P=(\cos \theta, \sin \theta){/tex} and {tex}Q=\{\cos (\alpha-\theta), \sin (\alpha-\theta)\}{/tex}, then Q is obtained from P by

options.text: clockwise rotation around origin through an angle $\alpha$
reflection in the line through origin with slope tan $\alpha$
anti-clockwise rotation around origin through an angle $\alpha$
reflection in the line through origin with slope $\tan \frac{\alpha}{2}$

questions.explanation:
In the Argand plane, P is represented by ei{tex}\theta{/tex} and Q is represented by ei({tex}\alpha{/tex} - {tex}\theta{/tex})
Now, rotation about a line with angle a is given by e{tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} ei({tex}\alpha{/tex} - {tex}\theta{/tex}). Therefore, Q is obtained from P by reflection in the line making an angle {tex}\alpha{/tex}/2.

questions.question: If both the roots of the quadratic equation x^2 - mx + 4 = 0 are real and distinct and they lie in the interval [1, 5] then m lies in the interval
questions.question_raw: If both the roots of the quadratic equation x2 - mx + 4 = 0 are real and distinct and they lie in the interval [1, 5] then m lies in the interval
questions.explanation:
According to given information, we have the following graph

Now, the following conditions should satisfy

  1. {tex}D>0 \Rightarrow b^{2}-4 a c>0{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \quad m^{2}-4 \times 1 \times 4>0{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \quad m^{2}-16>0{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \quad(m-4)(m+4)>0{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \quad m \in(-\infty,-4) \cup(4, \infty){/tex}
  2. The vertex of the parabola should lie between x = 1 and x = 5
    {tex}\therefore \quad-\frac{b}{2 a} \in(1,5) \Rightarrow 1<\frac{m}{2}<5 \Rightarrow m \in(2,10){/tex}
  3. {tex}f(1)>0 \Rightarrow 1-m+4>0{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow m<5 \Rightarrow m \in(-\infty, 5){/tex}
  4. {tex}f(5)>0 \Rightarrow 25-5 m+4>0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 5 m<29 \Rightarrow m \in\left(-\infty, \frac{29}{5}\right){/tex}

From the value of m obtained in (i), (ii), (iii) and (iv), we get {tex}m \in(4,5){/tex}

questions.question: The complex numbers z_1, z_2 and z_3 satisfying $\frac{z_{1}-z_{3}}{z_{2}-z_{3}}=\frac{1-i \sqrt{3}}{2}$ are the vertices of a triangle which is
questions.question_raw: The complex numbers z1, z2 and z3 satisfying {tex}\frac{z_{1}-z_{3}}{z_{2}-z_{3}}=\frac{1-i \sqrt{3}}{2}{/tex} are the vertices of a triangle which is
questions.explanation:
{tex}\frac{z_{1}-z_{3}}{z_{2}-z_{3}}=\frac{1-i \sqrt{3}}{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{(1-i \sqrt{3})(1+i \sqrt{3})}{2(1+i \sqrt{3})}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1-i^{2} 3}{2(1+i \sqrt{3})}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{4}{2(1+i \sqrt{3})}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{2}{(1+i \sqrt{3})}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{z_{2}-z_{3}}{z_{1}-z_{3}}=\frac{1+i \sqrt{3}}{2}=\cos \frac{\pi}{3}+i \sin \frac{\pi}{3}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow\left|\frac{z_{2}-z_{3}}{z_{1}-z_{3}}\right|=1{/tex} and arg {tex}\left(\frac{z_{2}-z_{3}}{z_{1}-z_{3}}\right)=\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex} 
Hence, the triangle is an equilateral.
Alternate Solution:
{tex}\quad \frac{z_{1}-z_{3}}{z_{2}-z_{3}}=\frac{1-i \sqrt{3}}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{z_{2}-z_{3}}{z_{1}-z_{3}}=\frac{2}{1-i \sqrt{3}}=\frac{1+i \sqrt{3}}{2}=\cos \frac{\pi}{3}+i \sin \frac{\pi}{3}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \arg \left(\frac{z_{2}-z_{3}}{z_{1}-z_{3}}\right)=\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex} and also {tex}\left|\frac{z_{2}-z_{3}}{z_{1}-z_{3}}\right|=1{/tex}
Therefore, triangle is equilateral.

questions.question: If a $\in$ R and the equation -3 (x - [x])^2 + 2(x - [x])+ a^2= 0 (where, [x] denotes the greatest integer $\leq$ x) has no integral solution, then all possible values of a lie in the interval
questions.question_raw: If a {tex}\in{/tex} R and the equation -3 (x - [x])2 + 2(x - [x])+ a2= 0 (where, [x] denotes the greatest integer {tex}\leq{/tex} x) has no integral solution, then all possible values of a lie in the interval
questions.explanation:
Put t = x - [x] = {X}, which is a fractional part function and lie between {tex}0 \leq\{X\}<1{/tex} and then solve it.
Given, {tex}a \in R{/tex} and equation is
{tex}-3\{x-[x]\}^{2}+2\{x-[x]\}+a^{2}=0{/tex}
Let t = x - [x], then equation is
-3t2 + 2t + a2 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad t=\frac{1 \pm \sqrt{1+3 a^{2}}}{3}{/tex}
{tex}\because{/tex} t = x - [x] = {X} [fractional part]
{tex}\therefore \quad 0 \leq t \leq 1{/tex}
{tex}0 \leq \frac{1 \pm \sqrt{1+3 a^{2}}}{3} \leq 1{/tex}
Taking positive sign, we get
{tex}0 \leq \frac{1+\sqrt{1+3 a^{2}}}{3}<1{/tex}
{tex}[\because\{x\}>0]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \sqrt{1+3 a^{2}}<2 \Rightarrow 1+3 a^{2}<4{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad a^{2}-1<0 \Rightarrow(a+1)(a-1)<0{/tex}
{tex}\therefore a \in(-1,1){/tex}, for no integer solution of a, we consider {tex}(-1,0) \cup(0,1){/tex}

questions.question: If z and w are two complex numbers such that |zw| = 1 and arg(z) - arg(w) = $\frac{\pi}{2}$, then
questions.question_raw: If z and w are two complex numbers such that |zw| = 1 and arg(z) - arg(w) = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}, then
questions.explanation:
It is given that, there are two complex numbers z and w, such that | z w | = 1 and arg(z) - arg(w) = {tex}\pi{/tex}/2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} |z| |w| = 1 [{tex}\because{/tex} |z1 z2I = |z1| |z2I] and arg(z) = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} + arg(w)
Let |z| = r, then |w| = {tex}\frac{1}{r}{/tex} ...(i)
and let arg(w) = {tex}\theta{/tex}, then arg(z) = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}+\theta{/tex} ...(ii)
So, we can assume z = rei({tex}\pi/2 +\theta{/tex})
[{tex}\because{/tex} if z = x + ry is a complex number, then it can be written as z = rei{tex}\theta{/tex} where, r = |z| and {tex}\theta{/tex} = arg (z)]
and {tex}w=\frac{1}{r} e^{i \theta}{/tex} ...(iv)
Now, {tex}\bar{z} \cdot w=r e^{-i(\pi / 2+\theta)} \cdot \frac{1}{r} e^{i \theta}{/tex}
{tex}=e^{i(-\pi / 2-\theta+\theta)}=e^{-i(\pi / 2)}=-i{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} ei{tex}\theta{/tex} = cos{tex}\theta{/tex} - i sin{tex}\theta{/tex}]
and {tex}z \bar{w}=r e^{i(\pi / 2+\theta)} \cdot \frac{1}{r} e^{-i \theta}{/tex}
{tex}=e^{i(\pi / 2+\theta-\theta)}=e^{i(\pi / 2)}=i{/tex}

questions.question: For all x, x^2+ 2ax + 10 - 3a > 0, then the interval in which a lies is
questions.question_raw: For all x, x2+ 2ax + 10 - 3a > 0, then the interval in which a lies is
questions.explanation:
As we know, ax2 + bx + c > 0 for all {tex}x \in R{/tex}, if a > 0 and D < 0.
Given equation is x2 + 2ax + (10 - 3a) > 0, {tex}\forall x \in R{/tex}
Now, D < 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4a2 - 4(10 - 3a) < 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4(a2 + 3a - 10) < 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (a + 5) (a - 2) < 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a {tex}\in{/tex} (-5, 2)

questions.question: If $z=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}+\frac{i}{2}(i=\sqrt{-1})$, then (1 + iz + z^5 + iz^8)^9 is equal to
questions.question_raw: If {tex}z=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}+\frac{i}{2}(i=\sqrt{-1}){/tex}, then (1 + iz + z5 + iz8)9 is equal to
questions.explanation:
Given, {tex}z=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}+\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) i=\cos \frac{\pi}{6}+i \sin \frac{\pi}{6}=e^{i \frac{\pi}{6}}{/tex}
So, (1 +iz + z5 + iz8)9
{tex}=\left(1+i e^{i \frac{\pi}{6}}+e^{i \frac{5 \pi}{6}}+i e^{i \frac{8 \pi}{6}}\right)^{9}{/tex}
{tex}=\left(1+e^{i \frac{\pi}{2}} \cdot e^{i \frac{\pi}{6}}+e^{i \frac{5 \pi}{6}}+e^{i \frac{\pi}{2}} \cdot e^{i \frac{4 \pi}{3}}\right)^{9}{/tex} {tex}\left[\because i=e^{i \frac{\pi}{2}}\right]{/tex}
{tex}=\left(1+e^{i \frac{2 \pi}{3}}+e^{i \frac{5 \pi}{6}}+e^{i \frac{11 \pi}{6}}\right)^{9}{/tex}
{tex}=\left[1+\left(\cos \frac{2 \pi}{3}+i \sin \frac{2 \pi}{3}\right)+\left(\cos \frac{5 \pi}{6}+i \sin \frac{5 \pi}{6}\right)\right.{/tex}+ {tex}\left.\left(\cos \frac{11 \pi}{6}+i \sin \frac{11 \pi}{6}\right)\right]^{9}{/tex}
{tex}=\left(1-\frac{1}{2}+\frac{i \sqrt{3}}{2}-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}+\frac{1}{2} i+\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}-\frac{i}{2}\right)^{9}{/tex}
{tex}=\left(\frac{1}{2}+\frac{\sqrt{3} i}{2}\right)^{9}=\left(\cos \frac{\pi}{3}+i \sin \frac{\pi}{3}\right)^{9}{/tex} 
= cos 3{tex}\pi{/tex} + i sin 3{tex}\pi{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} for any natural number 'n', (cos{tex}\theta{/tex} + i sin{tex}\theta{/tex})n = cos(n{tex}\theta{/tex}) + i sin(n{tex}\theta{/tex})]
= -1

questions.question: If b > a, then the equation (x - a) (x - b) - 1 = 0 has

options.text: One root in (-$\infty$ ,a) and the other in (b, $\infty$)

questions.question: Let z_0 be a root of the quadratic equation, x^2 + x + 1 = 0, If $z=3+6 i z_{0}^{81}-3 i z_{0}^{93}$, then arg z is equal to
questions.question_raw: Let z0 be a root of the quadratic equation, x2 + x + 1 = 0, If {tex}z=3+6 i z_{0}^{81}-3 i z_{0}^{93}{/tex}, then arg z is equal to
questions.explanation:
Given, x2 + x + 1 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad x=\frac{-1 \pm \sqrt{3} i}{2}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} Roots of quadratic equation ax2 + bx + c = 0 are given by x = {tex}\frac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^{2}-4 a c}}{2 a}{/tex}]
{tex}\Rightarrow z_{0}=\omega, \omega^{2}{/tex} [where {tex}\omega=\frac{-1+\sqrt{3} i}{2}{/tex} and {tex}\omega^{2}=\frac{-1-\sqrt{3} i}{2}{/tex} are the cube roots of unity and {tex}\omega, \omega^{2} \neq 1{/tex}]
Now consider z = 3 + 6i {tex}z_{0}^{\mathrm{8} 1}-3 i \ z_{0}^{93}{/tex}
= 3 + 6i - 3i {tex}\left(\because \omega^{3 n}=\left(\omega^{2}\right)^{3 n}=1\right){/tex}
= 3 + 3i = 3(1 + i)
If '{tex}\theta{/tex}' is the argument of z, then
{tex}\tan \theta=\frac{\operatorname{Im}(z)}{\operatorname{Re}(z)}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} z is in the first quadrant]
{tex}=\frac{3}{3}=1 \Rightarrow \theta=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}

questions.question: If the roots of the equation x^2 - 2ax + a^2+ a - 3 = 0 are real and less than 3, then
questions.question_raw: If the roots of the equation x2 - 2ax + a2+ a - 3 = 0 are real and less than 3, then
questions.explanation:
Let f(x) = x2 - 2ax + a2 + a - 3

Since, both root are less than 3.
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \alpha<3, \beta<3{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Sum, {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \alpha<3, \beta<3{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{\alpha+\beta}{2}<3{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{2 a}{2}<3{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a < 3 .... (i)
Again, product, {tex}P=\alpha \beta{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad P<9 \Rightarrow \alpha \beta<9{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a2 + a - 3 < 9
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a2 + a - 12 < 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (a - 3) (a + 4) < 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -4 < a < 3 .... (ii)
Again, {tex}D=B^{2}-4 A C \geq 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow(-2 a)^{2}-4 \cdot 1\left(a^{2}+a-3\right) \geq 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 4 a^{2}-4 a^{2}-4 a+12 \geq 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad-4 a+12 \geq 0 \Rightarrow a \leq 3{/tex} ...... (iii)
Again a f (3) > 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1[(3)2 - 2a(3) + a2 + a - 3] > 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 9 - 6a + a2 + a - 3 > 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a2 - 5a + 6 > 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (a - 2) (a - 3) > 0
{tex}\therefore \quad a \in(-\infty, 2) \cup(3, \infty){/tex} .... (iv)
From Eqs. (i), (ii), (iii) and (iv) we get
{tex}a \in(-4,2){/tex}

questions.question: Let f(x) be a quadratic expression which is positive for all real values of x. If g(x) = f(x) + f' (x) + f" (x), then for any real x
questions.question_raw: Let f(x) be a quadratic expression which is positive for all real values of x. If g(x) = f(x) + f' (x) + f" (x), then for any real x
questions.explanation:
Let f(x) = ax2 + bx + c > 0, {tex}\forall x \in R{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a > 0
and b2 4ac < 0 .... (i)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} g(x) = f(x) + f' (x) + f" (x)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} g(x) = ax2 + bx + c + 2ax + b + 2a
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} g(x) = ax2 + x(b + 2a) + (c + b + 2a)
Whose discriminant
= (b + 2a)2 - 4a(c + b + 2a)
= b2 + 4a2 + 4ab - 4ac - 4ab - 8a2
= b2 - 4a2 - 4ac = (b2 - 4ac) - 4a2 < 0 [from Eq. (i)]
{tex}\therefore g(x)>0\; \forall x{/tex}, as a > 0 and disscriminant < 0
Thus, {tex}g(x)>0, \forall x \in R{/tex}

questions.question: Let $z=\cos \theta+i \sin \theta$. Then, the value of $\sum_\limits{m=1}^{15} \operatorname{Im}\left(z^{2 m-1}\right)$ at $\theta=2^{\circ}$ is
questions.question_raw: Let {tex}z=\cos \theta+i \sin \theta{/tex}. Then, the value of {tex}\sum_\limits{m=1}^{15} \operatorname{Im}\left(z^{2 m-1}\right){/tex} at {tex}\theta=2^{\circ}{/tex} is
questions.explanation:
Given that, z = cos{tex}\theta{/tex} + i sin{tex}\theta{/tex} = ei{tex}\theta{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \sum_\limits{\mu=1}^{15} I \mu\left(\zeta^{2 \mu-1}\right)=\sum_\limits{\mu=1}^{15} I \mu\left(\varepsilon^{1 \theta}\right)^{2 \mu-1}=\sum_\limits{\mu=1}^{15} I \mu \ \varepsilon^{\prime(2 \mu-1) \theta}{/tex}
= sin{tex}\theta{/tex} + sin 3{tex}\theta{/tex} + sin 5{tex}\theta{/tex} + ... + sin 29 {tex}\theta{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\sin \left(\frac{\theta+29 \theta}{2}\right) \sin \left(\frac{15 \times 2 \theta}{2}\right)}{\sin \left(\frac{2 \theta}{2}\right)}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\sin (15 \theta) \sin (15 \theta)}{\sin \theta}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \sin 2^{\circ}}{/tex}

questions.question: The number of real roots of the equation 5 + |2^x - 1|= 2^x(2^x - 2) is
questions.question_raw: The number of real roots of the equation 5 + |2x - 1|= 2x(2x - 2) is
questions.explanation:
Given equation {tex}5+\left|2^{x}-1\right|=2^{x}\left(2^{x}-2\right){/tex}
Case I:
If {tex}2^{x}-1 \geq 0 \Rightarrow x \geq 0{/tex}
then 5 + 2x - 1 = 2x(2x - 2)
Put 2x = t, then
5 + t - 1 = t2 - 2t {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} t2 - 3t - 4 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} t2 - 4t + t - 4 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} t(t - 4) + 1(t - 4) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} t = 4 or -1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} t = 4  ( {tex}\because{/tex} t = 2x > 0 )
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x = 4 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 2 > 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 2 is the solution
Case II:
If 2x - 1 < 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x < 0,
then 5 + 1 - 2x = 2x(2x - 2)
Put 2x = y, then 6 - y = y2 - 2y
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y2 - y - 6 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y2 - 3y + 2y - 6 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (y + 2) (y - 3) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 3 or -2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 3 (as y = 2x > 0) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x = 3
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = log2 3 > 0
So, x = log2 3 is not a solution.
Therefore, number of real roots is one.

questions.question: The minimum value of $\left|a+b \omega+c \omega^{2}\right|$, where a, b and c are all not equal integers and $\omega(\neq 1)$ is a cube root of unity, is
questions.question_raw: The minimum value of {tex}\left|a+b \omega+c \omega^{2}\right|{/tex}, where a, b and c are all not equal integers and {tex}\omega(\neq 1){/tex} is a cube root of unity, is
questions.explanation:
Let z = |a + b{tex}\omega{/tex} + c{tex}\omega{/tex}|2
{tex}\Rightarrow z^{2}=\left|a+b \omega+c \omega^{2}\right|^{2}{/tex} = (a2 + b2 + c2 - ab - bc - ca)
or {tex}z^{2}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {(a - b)2 + (b - c)2 + (c - a)2} ...(i)
Since, a, b, c are all integers but not all simultaneously equal.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} If a - b then a {tex}\ne{/tex} c and b {tex}\ne{/tex} c
Because difference of integers = integer
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (b - c)2 {tex}\geq{/tex} 1 {as minimum difference of two consecutive integers is (± 1)} also (c - a)2 {tex}\geq{/tex} 1
and we have taken a = b {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (a - b)2 = 0
From Eq. (i), {tex}z^{2} \geq \frac{1}{2}(0+1+1){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad z^{2} \geq 1{/tex}
Hence, minimum value of |z| is 1.

questions.question: All the pairs (x,y) that satisfy the inequality $2^{\sqrt{\sin ^{2} x-2 \sin x+5}} \cdot \frac{1}{4^{\sin ^{2} y}} \leq 1$ also satisfy the equation
questions.question_raw: All the pairs (x,y) that satisfy the inequality {tex}2^{\sqrt{\sin ^{2} x-2 \sin x+5}} \cdot \frac{1}{4^{\sin ^{2} y}} \leq 1{/tex} also satisfy the equation
questions.explanation:
Given, inequality is
{tex}2^{\sqrt{\sin ^{2} x-2 \sin x+5}} \cdot \frac{1}{4^{\sin ^{2} y}} \leq 1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 2^{\sqrt{(\sin x-1)^{2}+4}} \cdot 2^{-2 \sin ^{2} y} \leq 1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 2^{\sqrt{(\sin x-1)^{2}+4}} \leq 2^{2 \sin ^{2} y}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt{(\sin x-1)^{2}+4} \leq 2 \sin ^{2} y{/tex} [if a > 1 and {tex}a^{n} \Rightarrow m \leq n{/tex}]
{tex}\because{/tex} Range of {tex}\sqrt{(\sin x-1)^{2}+4} \text { is }[2,2 \sqrt{2}]{/tex}
and range of 2sin2y is [0, 2].
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The above inequality holds, iff
{tex}\sqrt{(\sin x-1)^{2}+4}=2=2 \sin ^{2} y{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \sin x=1 \text { and } \sin ^{2} y=1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \sin x=|\sin y|{/tex} [from the options]

questions.question: If $\omega(\neq 1)$ be a cube root of unity and $\left(1+\omega^{2}\right)^{n}=\left(1+\omega^{4}\right)^{n}$, then the least positive value of n is
questions.question_raw: If {tex}\omega(\neq 1){/tex} be a cube root of unity and {tex}\left(1+\omega^{2}\right)^{n}=\left(1+\omega^{4}\right)^{n}{/tex}, then the least positive value of n is
questions.explanation:
Given, (1 + {tex}\omega{/tex}2)n = (1 + {tex}\omega{/tex}4)n
( - {tex}\omega{/tex})n = ( - {tex}\omega{/tex}2)n   [{tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\omega{/tex}3 = 1 and 1 + {tex}\omega{/tex} + {tex}\omega{/tex}2= 0]
{tex}\omega{/tex}n = 1 
n = 3 is the least positive value of n.

questions.question: The sum of the solutions of the equation $|\sqrt{x}-2|+\sqrt{x}(\sqrt{x}-4)+2=0(x>0)$ is equal to
questions.question_raw: The sum of the solutions of the equation {tex}|\sqrt{x}-2|+\sqrt{x}(\sqrt{x}-4)+2=0(x>0){/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation:
Given equation is
{tex}|\sqrt{x}-2|+\sqrt{x}(\sqrt{x}-4)+2=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow|\sqrt{x}-2|+x-4 \sqrt{x}+4=2{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow|\sqrt{x}-2|+(|\sqrt{x}-2|)^{2}=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow(|\sqrt{x}-2|)^{2}+|\sqrt{x}-2|-2=0{/tex}
Let {tex}|\sqrt{x}-2|=y{/tex}, then above equation reduced to
y2 + y - 2 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y2 + 2y - y - 2 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y(y + 2) -1(y + 2) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (y + 2) (y - 1) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 1, -2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} y = 1 {tex}[\because y=|\sqrt{x}-2| \geq 0]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad|\sqrt{x}-2|=1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \sqrt{x-2}=\pm 1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \sqrt{x}=3 \text { or } 1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 9 or 1

{tex}\therefore{/tex} Sum of roots = 9 + 1 = 10

questions.question: Let $\omega=-\frac{1}{2}+i \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$, then value of the determinant $\left|\begin{array}{ccc}{1} & {1} & {1} \\ {1} & {-1-\omega^{2}} & {\omega^{2}} \\ {1} & {\omega^{2}} & {\omega}\end{array}\right|$ is
questions.question_raw: Let {tex}\omega=-\frac{1}{2}+i \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}{/tex}, then value of the determinant {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc}{1} & {1} & {1} \\ {1} & {-1-\omega^{2}} & {\omega^{2}} \\ {1} & {\omega^{2}} & {\omega}\end{array}\right|{/tex} is
questions.explanation:
Let {tex}\Delta = \left|\begin{array}{ccc}{1} & {1} & {1} \\ {1} & {-1-\omega^{2}} & {\omega^{2}} \\ {1} & {\omega^{2}} & {\omega}\end{array}\right|{/tex}
Applying R2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} R2 - R1;R3 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} R3 - R1 = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc}{1} & {1} & {1} \\ {0} & {-2-\omega^{2}} & {\omega^{2}-1} \\ {0} & {\omega^{2}-1} & {\omega-1}\end{array}\right|{/tex}
= {tex}\left(-2-\omega^{2}\right)(\omega-1)-\left(\omega^{2}-1\right)^{2}{/tex}
= {tex}-2 \omega+2-\omega^{3}+\omega^{2}-\left(\omega^{4}-2 \omega^{2}+1\right){/tex}
= {tex}3 \omega^{2}-3 \omega=3 \omega(\omega-1) \quad\left[\because \omega^{4}=\omega\right]{/tex}

questions.question: The real number k for which the equation, 2x^2 + 3x + k = 0 has two distinct real roots in [0, 1]
questions.question_raw: The real number k for which the equation, 2x2 + 3x + k = 0 has two distinct real roots in [0, 1]
questions.explanation:
Let f(x) = 2x3 + 3x + k
On differentiating w.r.t. x, we get
{tex}f^{\prime}(x)=6 x^{2}+3>0, \quad \forall x \in R{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(x) is strictly increasing function.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(x) = 0 has only one real root, so two roots are not possible.

questions.question: Let z_1 and z_2 be nth roots of unity which subtend a right angled at the origin, then n must be of the form (where, k is an integer)
questions.question_raw: Let z1 and z2 be nth roots of unity which subtend a right angled at the origin, then n must be of the form (where, k is an integer)
questions.explanation:
Since, arg {tex}\frac{z_{1}}{z_{2}}=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{z_{1}}{z_{2}}=\cos \frac{\pi}{2}+i \sin \frac{\pi}{2}=i{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad \frac{z_{1}^{n}}{z_{2}^{n}}=(i)^{n} \Rightarrow i^{n}=1 \quad\left[\because\left|z_{2}\right|=\left|z_{1}\right|=1\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad n=4 k{/tex}
Alternate Solution:
Since, arg {tex}\frac{z_{1}}{z_{2}}=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad \frac{z_{2}}{z_{1}}=\left|\frac{z_{2}}{z_{1}}\right| e^{i \frac{\pi}{2}}{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{z_{2}}{z_{1}}=i \quad\left[\because\left|z_{1}\right|=\left|z_{2}\right|=1\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad\left(\frac{z_{2}}{z_{1}}\right)^{n}=i^{n}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} z1 and z2 are nth roots of unity.
{tex}z_1^n=z_2^n=1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow\left(\frac{z_{2}}{z_{1}}\right)^n=1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} in = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} n = 4k, where k is an integer.

questions.question: Let a,b,c be real numbers, a $\ne$ 0. If $\alpha$ is a root of a^2x^2 + bx+c = 0, $\beta$ is the root of a^2x^2 - b x - c = 0 and $0<\alpha<\beta$, then the equation a^2x^2 + 2bx + 2c = 0 has a root $\gamma$ that always satisfies
questions.question_raw: Let a,b,c be real numbers, a {tex}\ne{/tex} 0. If {tex}\alpha{/tex} is a root of a2x2 + bx+c = 0, {tex}\beta{/tex} is the root of a2x2 - b x - c = 0 and {tex}0<\alpha<\beta{/tex}, then the equation a2x2 + 2bx + 2c = 0 has a root {tex}\gamma{/tex} that always satisfies
questions.explanation:
Since, {tex}\alpha{/tex} is a root of a2x2 + bx + c = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad a^{2} \alpha^{2}+b \alpha+c=0{/tex} ..... (i)
and {tex}\beta{/tex} is a root of a2x2 - bx - c = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad a^{2} \beta^{2}-b \beta-c=0{/tex} ..... (ii)
{tex}\therefore \quad f(\alpha)=a^{2} \alpha^{2}+2 b \alpha+2 c{/tex}
Let f(x) = a2x2 + 2bx + 2c
{tex}\therefore f(\alpha)=a^{2} \alpha^{2}+2 b \alpha+2 c{/tex}
{tex}=a^{2} \alpha^{2}-2 a^{2} \alpha^{2}=-a^{2} \alpha^{2}{/tex} [from Eq. (i)]
and {tex}f(\beta)=\alpha^{2} \beta^{2}+2 b \beta+2 c{/tex}
{tex}=a^{2} \beta^{2}+2 a^{2} \beta^{2}=3 a^{2} \beta^{2}{/tex} [from Eq. (ii)]
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad f(\alpha) f(\beta)<0{/tex}
f(x) must have a root lying in the open interval {tex}(\alpha, \beta){/tex}.
{tex}\therefore \quad \alpha<\gamma<\beta{/tex}

questions.question: If $i=\sqrt{-1}$, then $4+5\left(-\frac{1}{2}+\frac{i \sqrt{3}}{2}\right)^{334}+3\left(-\frac{1}{2}+\frac{i \sqrt{3}}{2}\right)^{365}$ is equal to
questions.question_raw: If {tex}i=\sqrt{-1}{/tex}, then {tex}4+5\left(-\frac{1}{2}+\frac{i \sqrt{3}}{2}\right)^{334}+3\left(-\frac{1}{2}+\frac{i \sqrt{3}}{2}\right)^{365}{/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation:
We know that, 
{tex}\omega=-\frac{1}{2}+\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} i{/tex}
{tex}\therefore 4+5\left(-\frac{1}{2}+\frac{i \sqrt{3}}{2}\right)^{334}+3\left(-\frac{1}{2}+\frac{i \sqrt{3}}{2}\right)^{365}{/tex}
= 4 + 5 {tex}\omega^{334}+3 \omega^{365}{/tex}
= 4 + 5 {tex}\cdot \left(\omega^{3}\right)^{111} \cdot \omega+3 \cdot\left(\omega^{3}\right)^{121} \cdot \omega^{2}{/tex}
= 4 + 5 {tex}\omega+3 \omega^{2}{/tex} {tex}\left[\because \omega^{3}=1\right]{/tex}
= 1 + 3 + 2 {tex}\omega+3 \omega+3 \omega^{2}{/tex}
= 1 + 2 {tex}\omega+3\left(1+\omega+\omega^{2}\right)=1+2 \omega+3 \times 0{/tex} {tex}\left[\because 1+\omega+\omega^{2}=0\right]{/tex}
= {tex}1+(-1+\sqrt{3} i)=\sqrt{3} i{/tex}

questions.question: If a + b + c = 0, then the quadratic equation 3ax^2 + 2bx + c = 0 has
questions.question_raw: If a + b + c = 0, then the quadratic equation 3ax2 + 2bx + c = 0 has

options.text: One root in (2, 3) and the other in (-2, -1)

questions.explanation:
Let f(x) = ax3 + bx2 + cx + d
{tex}\therefore{/tex} f(0) = d and f(1) = a + b + c + d = d [{tex}\because{/tex} a + b + c = 0]
{tex}\therefore{/tex}  f(0) = f(1)
f is continuous in the closed interval [0, 1] and f is derivable in the open interval (0, 1).
Also, f(0) = f(1)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} By Rolle's theorem {tex}f^{\prime}(\alpha)=0{/tex} for {tex}0<\alpha<1{/tex}
Now, f'(x) = 3ax2 + 2bx + c
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad f^{\prime}(\alpha)=3 a \alpha^{2}+2 b \alpha+c=0{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Eq. (i) has exist atleast one root in the interval (0, 1).
Thus, f'(x) must have root in the interval (0, 1) or 3ax2 + 2bx + c = 0 has root {tex}\in(0,1){/tex}

questions.question: The largest interval for which x^12 - x^9 + x^4 - x + 1 > 0 is
questions.question_raw: The largest interval for which x12 - x9 + x4 - x + 1 > 0 is
questions.explanation:
Given, x12 - x9 + x4 - x + 1 > 0
Here, three cases arises:
Case I When  {tex}x \leq 0 \Rightarrow x^{12}>0,-x^{9}>0, x^{4}>0,-x>0{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad x^{12}-x^{9}+x^{4}-x+1>0, \forall x \leq 0{/tex} .... (i)
Case II: When {tex}0<x \leq 1{/tex}
x9 < x4 and x < 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -x9 + x4 > 0 and 1 - x > 0
{tex}\therefore \quad x^{12}-x^{9}+x^{4}-x+1>0, \forall \;0<x \leq 1{/tex} .... (ii)
Case III: When x > 1 {tex}\Rightarrow x^{12}>x^{9}{/tex} and x4 > x
{tex}\therefore \quad x^{12}-x^{9}+x^{4}-x+1>0, \forall x>1{/tex} .... (iii)
From Eqs. (i), (ii) and (iii), the above equation holds for all {tex}x \in R{/tex}

questions.question: If $\omega$ is an imaginary cube root of unity, then $\left(1+\omega-\omega^{2}\right)^{7}$ is equal to
questions.question_raw: If {tex}\omega{/tex} is an imaginary cube root of unity, then {tex}\left(1+\omega-\omega^{2}\right)^{7}{/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation:
{tex}\left(1+\omega-\omega^{2}\right)^{7}{/tex} = {tex}\left(-\omega-\omega^{2}\right)^{7}{/tex} {tex}\left[\because 1+\omega+\omega^{2}=0\right]{/tex}
= {tex}\left(-2 \omega^{2}\right)^{7}=(-2)^{7} \omega^{14}=-128 \omega^{2}{/tex}

questions.question: If $\omega(\neq 1)$ is a cube root of unity and $(1+\omega)^{7}=A+B \omega$, then A and B are respectively
questions.question_raw: If {tex}\omega(\neq 1){/tex} is a cube root of unity and {tex}(1+\omega)^{7}=A+B \omega{/tex}, then A and B are respectively
questions.explanation:
{tex}(1+\omega)^{7}=(1+\omega)(1+\omega)^{6}{/tex}
= {tex}(1+\omega)\left(-\omega^{2}\right)^{6}=1+\omega{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad A+B \omega=1+\omega{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A = 1, B = 1

questions.question:
Let a,b,c be non-zero real numbers such that $\int_{0}^{1}\left(1+\cos ^{8} x\right)\left(a x^{2}+b x+c\right) d x$
= $\int_{0}^{2}\left(1+\cos ^{8} x\right)\left(a x^{2}+b x+c\right) d x$ Then, the quadratic equation ax^2 + bx + c = 0 has
questions.question_raw:
Let a,b,c be non-zero real numbers such that {tex}\int_{0}^{1}\left(1+\cos ^{8} x\right)\left(a x^{2}+b x+c\right) d x{/tex}
= {tex}\int_{0}^{2}\left(1+\cos ^{8} x\right)\left(a x^{2}+b x+c\right) d x{/tex} Then, the quadratic equation ax2 + bx + c = 0 has
questions.explanation:
Consider,
{tex}f(x)=\int_{0}^{1}\left(1+\cos ^{8} x\right)\left(a x^{2}+b x+c\right) d x{/tex}
Obviously, f(x) is continuous and differentiable in the interval [1, 2]
Also, f(1) = f(2) [given]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} By Rolle's theorem, there exist atleast one point {tex}k \in(1,2){/tex}, such that f'(k) = 0
Now, f'(x) = (1 + cos8x)(ax2 + bx + c)
f'(k) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (1 + cos8k)(ak2 + bk + c) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} ak2 + bk + c = 0       [as(1 + cos8k) {tex}\neq{/tex} 0] 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x = k is root of ax2 + bx + c = 0,
where {tex}k \in(1,2){/tex}

questions.question: The value of $\sum_\limits{k=1}^{6}\left(\sin \frac{2 \pi k}{7}-i \cos \frac{2 \pi k}{7}\right)$ is
questions.question_raw: The value of {tex}\sum_\limits{k=1}^{6}\left(\sin \frac{2 \pi k}{7}-i \cos \frac{2 \pi k}{7}\right){/tex} is
questions.explanation:
{tex}\sum_\limits{k=1}^{6}\left(\sin \frac{2 \pi k}{7}-i \cos \frac{2 \pi k}{7}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\sum_\limits{k=1}^{6}-i\left(\cos \frac{2 k \pi}{7}+i \sin \frac{2 k \pi}{7}\right){/tex}
= {tex}-i\left\{\sum_\limits{k=1}^{6} e^{\frac{i 2 k \pi}{7}}\right\}{/tex} {tex}=-i\left\{e^\frac {i 2 \pi }{7}+e^\frac{i 4 \pi}{7}+e^\frac{i 6 \pi }{7}\right. {/tex} {tex}\left.+e^\frac{i 8 \pi }{7}+e^\frac {i 10 \pi}{7}+e^\frac{i 12 \pi }{7}\right\}{/tex}
= {tex}-i\left\{e^\frac {i 2 \pi}{7} \frac{\left(1-e^\frac {i 12 \pi}{7}\right)}{1-e^\frac {i 2 \pi}{7}}\right\}{/tex}
= {tex}-i\left\{\frac{e^\frac {i 2 \pi }{7}-e^\frac {j 14 pi}{7}}{1-e^\frac {i 2 \pi }{7}}\right\} \quad\left[\because e^\frac {i 14 \pi}{7}=1\right]{/tex}
= {tex}-i\left\{\frac{e^\frac{i 2 \pi }{7}-1}{1-e^\frac{i 2 \pi }{7}}\right\}=i{/tex}

questions.question: 1 + i^2 + i^4 + i^6 + ... + i^2n is
questions.question_raw: 1 + i2 + i4 + i6 + ... + i2n is
questions.explanation: 1 + i2 + i4 + i6 + ... + i2n = 1 – 1 + 1 – 1 + ... (–1)n which can not be evaluated unless n is known.

questions.question: If the complex number z = x + iy satisfies the condition |z + 1| = 1, then z lies on
questions.question_raw: If the complex number z = x + iy satisfies the condition |z + 1| = 1, then z lies on
questions.explanation:
|z + 1| = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |(x + 1) + iy| = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x + 1)2 + y2 = 1
which is a circle with centre (–1, 0) and radius 1.

questions.question: The area of the triangle on the complex plane formed by the complex numbers z, – iz and z + iz is:
questions.question_raw: The area of the triangle on the complex plane formed by the complex numbers z, – iz and z + iz is:
questions.explanation:
Let z = x + iy. Then – iz = y – ix. Therefore,
z + iz = (x – y) + i (x + y)
Required area of the triangle = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} (x2 + y2) = {tex}\frac{|z|^{2}}{2}{/tex}

questions.question: The equation |z + 1 - i| = |z - 1 + i| represents a
questions.question_raw: The equation |z + 1 - i| = |z - 1 + i| represents a
questions.explanation:
|z + 1 - i| = |z - 1 + i|
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |z - (-1 + i)| = |z - (1 - i)|
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} PA = PB, where A denotes the point (–1, 1), B denotes the point (1, –1) and P denotes the point (x, y)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} z lies on the perpendicular bisector of the line joining A and B and perpendicular bisector is a straight line.

questions.question: Number of solutions of the equation z^2 + |z|^2 = 0 is
questions.question_raw: Number of solutions of the equation z2 + |z|2 = 0 is
questions.explanation:
z2 + |z|2 = 0, z {tex}\neq{/tex} 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 – y2 + i2xy + x2 + y2 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x2 + i2xy = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}2x (x + iy) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 0 or x + iy = 0 (not possible)
Therefore, x = 0 and z {tex}\neq{/tex} 0
So y can have any real value. Hence infinitely many solutions.

questions.question: The amplitude of sin $\frac{\pi}{5}$ + i (1 - cos $\frac{\pi}{5}$) is
questions.question_raw: The amplitude of sin {tex}\frac{\pi}{5}{/tex} + i (1 - cos {tex}\frac{\pi}{5}{/tex}) is
questions.explanation:
Here r cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = sin {tex}\left(\frac{\pi}{5}\right){/tex} and r sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = 1 - cos {tex}\frac{\pi}{5}{/tex}
Therefore, tan {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1-\cos \frac{\pi}{5}}{\sin \frac{\pi}{5}}=\frac{2 \sin ^{2}\left(\frac{\pi}{10}\right)}{2 \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{10}\right) \cdot \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{10}\right)}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} tan {tex}\theta{/tex} = tan {tex}\left(\frac{\pi}{10}\right){/tex} i.e., {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex} \frac{\pi}{10} {/tex}

questions.question: The real value of α for which the expression $\frac{1-i \sin \alpha}{1+2 i \sin \alpha}$ is purely real is:
questions.question_raw: The real value of α for which the expression {tex}\frac{1-i \sin \alpha}{1+2 i \sin \alpha}{/tex} is purely real is:

options.text: None of these, where n $\in$ N

questions.explanation:
{tex}z=\frac{1-i \sin \alpha}{1+2 i \sin \alpha}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{(1-i \sin \alpha)(1-2 i \sin \alpha)}{(1+2 i \sin \alpha)(1-2 i \sin \alpha)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1-i \sin \alpha-2 i \sin \alpha+2 i^{2} \sin ^{2} \alpha}{1-4 i^{2} \sin ^{2} \alpha}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{1-3 i \sin \alpha-2 \sin ^{2} \alpha}{1+4 \sin ^{2} \alpha}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1-2 \sin ^{2} \alpha}{1+4 \sin ^{2} \alpha}-\frac{3 i \sin \alpha}{1+4 \sin ^{2} \alpha}{/tex}
It is given that z is a purely real.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{-3 \sin \alpha}{1+4 \sin ^{2} \alpha}=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -3 sin {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sin {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\alpha{/tex} = n{tex}\pi{/tex}, n {tex}\in{/tex} I

questions.question: If z = x + iy lies in the third quadrant, then $\frac{\bar{z}}{z}$ also lies in the third quadrant if
questions.question_raw: If z = x + iy lies in the third quadrant, then {tex}\frac{\bar{z}}{z}{/tex} also lies in the third quadrant if
questions.explanation:
Since z = x + iy lies in the third quadrant we get
x < 0 and y < 0
Now, {tex}\frac{\bar{z}}{z}=\frac{x-i y}{x+i y}=\frac{(x-i y)(x-i y)}{(x+i y)(x-i y)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{x^{2}-y^{2}-2 i x y}{x^{2}+y^{2}}=\frac{x^{2}-y^{2}}{x^{2}+y^{2}}-\frac{2 i x y}{x^{2}+y^{2}}{/tex}
Since {tex}\frac{\overline z}{z}{/tex} also lies in third quadrant, we get
{tex}\frac{x^{2}-y^{2}}{x^{2}+y^{2}}<0 \text { and } \frac{-2 x y}{x^{2}+y^{2}}<0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 - y2 < 0 and -2xy < 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 < y2 and xy > 0
But x, y < 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y < x < 0

{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Ix = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 4n, n {tex}\in{/tex} N

questions.question: A real value of x satisfies the equation $\left(\frac{3-4 i x}{3+4 i x}\right)$ = $\alpha$ - i$\beta$ ($\alpha$,$\beta$ $\in$ R) if $\alpha$^2 + $\beta$^2 =
questions.question_raw: A real value of x satisfies the equation {tex}\left(\frac{3-4 i x}{3+4 i x}\right){/tex} = {tex}\alpha{/tex} - i{tex}\beta{/tex} ({tex}\alpha{/tex},{tex}\beta{/tex} {tex}\in{/tex} R) if {tex}\alpha{/tex}2 + {tex}\beta{/tex}2 =
questions.explanation:
Given that {tex}\left(\frac{3-4 i x}{3+4 i x}\right)=\alpha-i \beta{/tex}
{tex}8 \Rightarrow\left(\frac{3-4 i x}{3+4 i x} \times \frac{3-4 i x}{3-4 i x}\right)=\alpha-i \beta{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{9-12 i x-12 i x+16 i^{2} x^{2}}{9-16 i^{2} x^{2}}=\alpha-i \beta{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{9-24 i x-16 x^{2}}{9+16 x^{2}}=\alpha-i \beta{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{9-16 x^{2}}{9+16 x^{2}}-\frac{24 x}{9+16 x^{2}} i=\alpha-i \beta{/tex} ....(i)
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{9-16 x^{2}}{9+16 x^{2}}+\frac{24 x}{9+16 x^{2}} i=\alpha+i \beta{/tex} .....(ii)
multiplying eqn. (i) and (ii) we g et
{tex}\left(\frac{9-16 x^{2}}{9+16 x^{2}}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{24 x}{9+16 x^{2}}\right)^{2}=\alpha^{2}+\beta^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\left(9-16 x^{2}\right)^{2}+(24 x)^{2}}{\left(9+16 x^{2}\right)^{2}}=\alpha^{2}+\beta^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{81+256 x^{4}-288 x^{2}+576 x^{2}}{\left(9+16 x^{2}\right)^{2}}=\alpha^{2}+\beta^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{81+256 x^{4}+288 x^{2}}{\left(9+16 x^{2}\right)^{2}}=\alpha^{2}+\beta^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\left(9+16 x^{2}\right)^{2}}{\left(9+16 x^{2}\right)^{2}}=\alpha^{2}+\beta^{2}{/tex}
So, {tex}\alpha{/tex}2 + {tex}\beta{/tex}2 = 1
Hence, the correct option is (a)

questions.question: Which of the following is correct for any two complex numbers z_1 and z_2?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following is correct for any two complex numbers z1 and z2?
questions.explanation:
Let z1 = r1(cos {tex}\theta{/tex}1 + i sin {tex}\theta{/tex}1)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} |z1| = r1
 and z2 = r2(cos {tex}\theta{/tex}2 +i sin{tex}\theta{/tex}2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} |z2| = r2
z1z2 = r1(cos {tex}\theta{/tex}1 + isin {tex}\theta{/tex}1)⋅r2(cos {tex}\theta{/tex}2 + i sin {tex}\theta{/tex}2)
= r1r2(cos {tex}\theta{/tex}1 + i sin {tex}\theta{/tex}1)⋅(cos {tex}\theta{/tex}2 + i sin {tex}\theta{/tex}2)
= r1r2(cos {tex}\theta{/tex}1 cos {tex}\theta{/tex}2 + i sin {tex}\theta{/tex}2 cos {tex}\theta{/tex}1 + i sin {tex}\theta{/tex}1 cos {tex}\theta{/tex}2 + i2 sin {tex}\theta{/tex}1 sin {tex}\theta{/tex}2)
= r1r2[(cos {tex}\theta{/tex}1 cos{tex}\theta{/tex}2 − sin {tex}\theta{/tex}1 sin {tex}\theta{/tex}2) + i(sin {tex}\theta{/tex}1 cos {tex}\theta{/tex}2 + cos {tex}\theta{/tex}1 sin {tex}\theta{/tex}2)]
= r1 r2 [cos({tex}\theta{/tex}1 + {tex}\theta{/tex}2) + i sin({tex}\theta{/tex}1 + {tex}\theta{/tex}2)]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} |z1z2| = |z1||z2|

questions.question: The point represented by the complex number 2 – i is rotated about origin through an angle $\frac{\pi}{2}$in the clockwise direction, the new position of point is:
questions.question_raw: The point represented by the complex number 2 – i is rotated about origin through an angle {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}in the clockwise direction, the new position of point is:
questions.explanation:
Given that, z=2−i
It is rotated about origin through an angle {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} in the clockwise direction
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Now position = {tex}z e^{-i \pi / 2}=(2-i) e^{-i \pi / 2}{/tex}
= (2 - i) {tex}\left[\cos \left(\frac{-\pi}{2}\right)+i \sin \left(\frac{-\pi}{2}\right)\right]{/tex} = (2 - i) [0 - i]
= -2i - 1 - 2i

questions.question: If a + ib = c + id, then

questions.question: If z is a complex number, then
questions.question_raw: If z is a complex number, then
questions.explanation:
If z is a complex number, then z = x + iyz = x + iy
|z| = |x + iy| and |z|2 = |x + iy|2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |z|2 = x2 + y2 ...(i)
and z2 = (x + iy)2 = x2 + i2y2 + i2xy
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left|z^{2}\right|=\sqrt{\left(x^{2}-y^{2}\right)^{2}+(2 x y)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left|z^{2}\right|=\sqrt{x^{4}+y^{4}-2 x^{2} y^{2}+4 x^{2} y^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left|z^{2}\right|=\sqrt{x^{4}+y^{4}-2 x^{2} y^{2}}=\sqrt{\left(x^{2}+y^{2}\right)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |z2| = x2 + y2 ..(ii)
From Eqs. (i) and (ii)
|z|2 = |z|2

questions.question: |z_1 + z_2| = |z_1| + |z_2| is possible if
questions.question_raw: |z1 + z2| = |z1| + |z2| is possible if
questions.explanation:
Let z1 = r1 (cos {tex}\theta{/tex}1 + i sin {tex}\theta{/tex}1) and z2 = r2 (cos {tex}\theta{/tex}2 + i sin {tex}\theta{/tex}2)
Since |z1 + z2| = |z1| + |z2|
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} z1 + z2 = r1 cos {tex}\theta{/tex}1 + ir1 sin {tex}\theta{/tex}1+ r2 cos {tex}\theta{/tex}2 + ir2 sin {tex}\theta{/tex}2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |z1 + z2|
= {tex}\sqrt{\mathrm{r}_{1}^{2} \cos ^{2} \theta_{1}+\mathrm{r}_{2}^{2} \cos ^{2} \theta_{2}+2 \mathrm{r}_{1} \mathrm{r}_{2} \cos \theta_{1} \cos \theta_{2}+\mathrm{r}_{1}^{2} \sin ^{2} \theta_{1}+\mathrm{r}_{2}^{2} \sin ^{2} \theta_{2}+2 \mathrm{r}_{1} \mathrm{r}_{2} \sin \theta_{1} \sin \theta_{2}}{/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt{\mathrm{r}_{1}^{2}+\mathrm{r}_{2}^{2}+2 \mathrm{r}_{1} \mathrm{r}_{2} \cos \left(\theta_{1}-\theta_{2}\right)}{/tex}
But |z1 + z2| = |z1| + |z2|
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{\mathrm{r}_{1}^{2}+\mathrm{r}_{2}^{2}+2 \mathrm{r}_{1} \mathrm{r}_{2} \cos \left(\theta_{1}-\theta_{2}\right)}=\mathrm{r}_{1}+\mathrm{r}_{2}{/tex}
Squaring both sides,
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}r_{1}^{2}+r_{2}^{2}+2 r_{1} r_{2} \cos \left(\theta_{1}-\theta_{2}\right)=r_{1}^{2}+r_{2}^{2}+2 r_{1} r_{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2r1r2 – 2r1r2 cos ({tex}\theta{/tex}1 – {tex}\theta{/tex}2) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1 – cos ({tex}\theta{/tex}1 – {tex}\theta{/tex}2) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cos ({tex}\theta{/tex}1 – {tex}\theta{/tex}2) = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} ({tex}\theta{/tex}1 – {tex}\theta{/tex}2) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\theta{/tex}1 = {tex}\theta{/tex}2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} arg (z1) = arg (z2)

questions.question: The real value of $\theta$ for which an expression $\frac{1+i \cos \theta}{1-2 i \cos \theta}$ is a real number is:
questions.question_raw: The real value of {tex}\theta{/tex} for which an expression {tex}\frac{1+i \cos \theta}{1-2 i \cos \theta}{/tex} is a real number is:
questions.explanation:
Let z = {tex}\frac{1+i \cos \theta}{1-2 i \cos \theta}{/tex}
Rationalizing the denominator,
= {tex}\frac{1+i \cos \theta}{1-2 i \cos \theta} \times \frac{1+2 i \cos \theta}{1+2 i \cos \theta}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{1+2 i \cos \theta+i \cos \theta+2 i^{2} \cos ^{2} \theta}{1-4 i^{2} \cos ^{2} \theta}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{1-3 i \cos \theta-2 \cos ^{2} \theta}{1+4 \cos ^{2} \theta}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{1-2 \cos ^{2} \theta}{1+4 \cos ^{2} \theta}+\frac{3 \cos \theta}{1+4 \cos ^{2} \theta}{/tex}
If z is a real number,
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}{tex}\frac{3 \cos \theta}{1+4 \cos ^{2} \theta}=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3 cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = 0
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}2 n \pi \pm \frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}, n {tex}\in{/tex} N

questions.question: If f(z) = $\frac{7-z}{1-z^{2}}$, where z = 1 + 2i, then |f (z)| is
questions.question_raw: If f(z) = {tex}\frac{7-z}{1-z^{2}}{/tex}, where z = 1 + 2i, then |f (z)| is
questions.explanation:
Given f(z) = {tex}\frac{7-z}{1-z^{2}}{/tex} where z = 1 + 2i
{tex}\Rightarrow|z|=\sqrt{1^{2}+2^{2}}=\sqrt{5}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{z})=\frac{7-\mathrm{z}}{1-\mathrm{z}^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{7-(1+2 i)}{1-(1+2 i)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{7-(1+2 i)}{1-(1+2 i)^{2}}{/tex}
={tex}\frac{6-2 i}{4-4 i}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{3-i}{2-2 i}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{3-i}{2-2 i} \times \frac{2+2 i}{2+2 i}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{6+6 i-2 i-2 i^{2}}{4-4 i}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{6+4 i+2}{4+4}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{8+4 i}{8}{/tex}
= {tex}1+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{i}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow|\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{z})|=\sqrt{1^{2}+\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}}{/tex}
= {tex} \sqrt{1+\frac{1}{4}} {/tex}
= {tex}\frac{\sqrt{5}}{2}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{|z|}{2}{/tex}

{tex}\left(\frac{-17}{20}+\frac{11}{10}\right)+\left(\frac{17}{10}+\frac{11}{20}\right) i{/tex} = {tex}\left(\frac{5}{20}+\frac{45}{20} i\right){/tex} = {tex}\left(\frac{1}{4}+\frac{9}{4} i\right){/tex}

questions.question: If a, b are the roots of the equation x^2 + x + 1 = 0, then a^2 + b^2 =
questions.question_raw: If a, b are the roots of the equation x2 + x + 1 = 0, then a2 + b2 =
questions.explanation:
Given equation: x2 + x + 1 = 0
Also, a and b are the roots of the given equation.
Sum of the roots = a + b = {tex}\frac{-\text { Coefficientof } x}{\text { Coeficient of } x^{2}}{/tex} = -{tex}\frac{1}{1}{/tex} = -1
Product of the roots = ab = {tex}\frac{\text { Constant term }}{\text { Coefficient of } x^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{1}{/tex} = 1
{tex}\therefore{/tex} (a + b)2 = a2 + b2 + 2ab
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (-1)2 = a2 + b2 + 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1 - 2 = a2 + b2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a2 + b2 = -1

questions.question: If $\alpha, \beta$ are roots of the equation 4x^2 + 3 x + 7 = 0, then $\frac{1}{\alpha}+\frac{1}{\beta}$ is equal to

Also, {tex}\alpha{/tex} and {tex}\beta{/tex} are the roots of the equation.
Sum of the roots = {tex}\alpha+\beta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-\text { Coefficient of } x}{\text { Coefficient of } x^{2}}{/tex}= -{tex}\frac{3}{4}{/tex} 
Product of the roots = {tex}\alpha\beta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\text { Constant term }}{\text { Coefficient of } x^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{7}{4}{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\alpha}+\frac{1}{\beta}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\alpha+\beta}{\alpha \beta}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-\frac{3}{4}}{\frac{7}{4}}{/tex} = -{tex}\frac{3}{7}{/tex}

questions.question: The number of real roots of the equation (x^2 + 2x)^2 - (x + 1)^2 - 55 = 0 is
questions.question_raw: The number of real roots of the equation (x2 + 2x)2 - (x + 1)2 - 55 = 0 is
questions.explanation:
(x2 + 2x)2 - (x + 1)2 - 55 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x2 + 2x + 1 - 1)2 - (x + 1)2 - 55 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {(x + 1)2 - 1}2 - (x + 1)2 - 55 = 0

{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {(x + 1)2}2 - 3(x + 1)2 - 54 = 0
Let p = (x + 1)2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} p2 - 3p - 54 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} p2 - 9p + 6p - 54 = =0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (p + 6) (p - 9) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} p = 9 or p = -6
Rejecting p = -6
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x + 1)2 = 9
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 + 2x - 8 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 + 4x - 2x - 8 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x + 4)(x - 2) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 2, x = -4

questions.question: If $\alpha, \beta$ are the roots of the equation ax^2 + bx + c = 0, then $\frac{1}{a \alpha+b}+\frac{1}{a \beta+b}$ =
questions.question_raw: If {tex}\alpha, \beta{/tex} are the roots of the equation ax2 + bx + c = 0, then {tex}\frac{1}{a \alpha+b}+\frac{1}{a \beta+b}{/tex} =
questions.explanation:
Given equation:
ax2 + bx + c = 0
Also, {tex}\alpha{/tex} and {tex}\beta{/tex} are the roots of the given equation.
Then, sum of the roots = {tex}\alpha+\beta{/tex} = -{tex}\frac{b}{a}{/tex} 
Product of the roots = {tex}\alpha \beta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{c}{a}{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{a \alpha+b}+\frac{1}{a \beta+b}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{a \beta+b+a \alpha+b}{(a \alpha+b)(a \beta+b)}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{a(\alpha+\beta)+2 b}{a^{2} \alpha \beta+a b \alpha+a b \beta+b^{2}}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{a(\alpha+\beta)+2 b}{a^{2} \alpha \beta+a b(\alpha+\beta)+b^{2}}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{a\left(-\frac{b}{a}\right)+2 b}{a^{2}\left(\frac{c}{a}\right)+a b\left(-\frac{b}{a}\right)+b^{2}}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{b}{ac}{/tex}

questions.question: If $\alpha, \beta$ are the roots of the equation x^2 + px + 1 = 0; $\gamma$, $\delta$ the roots of the equation x^2 + qx + 1 = 0, then $(\alpha-\gamma)(\alpha+\delta)(\beta-\gamma)(\beta+\delta)$ =
questions.question_raw: If {tex}\alpha, \beta{/tex} are the roots of the equation x2 + px + 1 = 0; {tex}\gamma{/tex}, {tex}\delta{/tex} the roots of the equation x2 + qx + 1 = 0, then {tex}(\alpha-\gamma)(\alpha+\delta)(\beta-\gamma)(\beta+\delta){/tex} =
questions.explanation:
Given: {tex}\alpha{/tex} and {tex}\beta{/tex} are the roots of the equation x2 + px + 1 = 0
Also, {tex}\gamma{/tex} and {tex}\delta{/tex} are the roots of the equation x2 + qx + 1 =  0
Then, the sum and the product of the roots of the given equation are as follows:
{tex}\alpha+\beta{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{p}{1}{/tex} = -p
{tex}\alpha\beta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{1}{/tex} = 1
{tex}\gamma+\delta{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{q}{1}{/tex} = -q
{tex}\gamma\delta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{1}{/tex} = 1
Moreover, {tex}(\gamma+\delta)^{2}{/tex} = {tex}\gamma^{2}+\delta^{2}+2 \gamma \delta{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\gamma^{2}+\delta^{2}{/tex} = q2 - 2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}(\alpha-\gamma)(\alpha+\delta)(\beta-\gamma)(\beta+\delta){/tex} = {tex}(\alpha-\gamma)(\beta-\gamma)(\alpha+\delta)(\beta+\delta){/tex}
= {tex}\left(\alpha \beta-\alpha \gamma-\beta \gamma+\gamma^{2}\right){/tex} {tex}\left(\alpha \beta+\alpha \delta+\beta \delta+\delta^{2}\right){/tex}
= {tex}\left[\alpha \beta-\gamma(\alpha+\beta)+\gamma^{2}\right]{/tex} {tex}\left[\alpha \beta+\delta(\alpha+\beta)+\delta^{2}\right]{/tex}
= {tex}\left(1-\gamma(-p)+\gamma^{2}\right)\left(1+\delta(-p)+\delta^{2}\right){/tex}
= {tex}\left(1+\gamma p+\gamma^{2}\right)\left(1-\delta p+\delta^{2}\right){/tex}
= {tex}1-p \delta+\delta^{2}+p \gamma-p^{2} \gamma \delta+p \gamma \delta^{2}+\gamma^{2}-p \delta \gamma^{2}+\gamma^{2} \delta^{2}{/tex}
= {tex}1-p \delta+p \gamma+\delta^{2}-p^{2} \gamma \delta+p \gamma \delta^{2}+\gamma^{2}-p \delta \gamma^{2}+\gamma^{2} \delta^{2}{/tex}
= {tex}1-p(\delta-\gamma)-p^{2} \gamma \delta+p \gamma \delta(\delta-\gamma)+\left(\gamma^{2}+\delta^{2}\right)+1{/tex}
= {tex}1-p^{2} \gamma \delta+p \gamma \delta(\delta-\gamma)-p(\delta-\gamma)+\left(\gamma^{2}+\delta^{2}\right)+1{/tex}
= {tex}1-p^{2}+(\delta-\gamma) p(\gamma \delta-1)+q^{2}-2+1{/tex}
= {tex}-p^{2}+(\delta-\gamma) p(1-1)+q^{2}{/tex}
= q2 - p2
