questions.question_raw: The number of real solutions of |2x - x2 - 3| = 1 is
questions.explanation:
Given equation: |2x - x2 - 3| = 1

  1. 2x - x2 - 3 = 1
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x - x2 - 4 = 0
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 - 2x + 4 = 0
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x - 2)2 = 0
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 2, 2
  2. -2x + x2 + 3 = 1
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 - 2x + 2 = 0
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 - 2x + 1 + 1 = 0
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x - 1)2 - i2 = 0
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x - 1 + i) (x - 1 - i) = 0
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 1 - i , 1 + i
    Hence, the real solutions are 2, 2.

questions.question: The number of solutions of x^2 + Ix - 1I = 1 is
questions.question_raw: The number of solutions of x2 + Ix - 1I = 1 is
questions.explanation:
x2 + |x - 1| = x2 + x - 1, x {tex}\ge{/tex} 1 = x2 - x + 1, x < 1

  1. x2 + x - 1 = 1
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 + x - 2 = 0
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 + 2x - x - 2 = 0
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x(x + 2) - 1(x + 2) = 0
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x + 2 = 0 or, x - 1 = 0
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = -2 or x = 1
    Since -2 does not satisfy the condition x {tex}\ge{/tex} 1
  2. x2 - x + 1 = 1
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 - x = 0
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 - x = 0
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x(x - 1) = 0
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 0 or, (x - 1) = 0
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 0, x = 1
    x = 1 does not satisfy the condition x < 1
    So, there are two solutions.

questions.question: If x is real and k = $\frac{x^{2}-x+1}{x^{2}+x+1}$, then
questions.question_raw: If x is real and k = {tex}\frac{x^{2}-x+1}{x^{2}+x+1}{/tex}, then
questions.explanation:
k = {tex}\frac{x^{2}-x+1}{x^{2}+x+1}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} kx2 + kx + k = x2 - x + 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (k - 1)x2 + (k + 1)x + k - 1 = 0
For real values of x, the discriminant of 
(k - 1)x2 + (k + 1)x + k - 1 = 0 should be greater than or equal to zero.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} if k {tex}\ne{/tex} 1
(k + 1)2 - 4(k - 1) (k - 1) {tex}\ge{/tex} 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (k + 1)2 - {2(k - 1)}2 {tex}\ge{/tex} 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (k + 1 + 2k - 2)(k + 1 - 2k + 2) {tex}\ge{/tex} 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (3k - 1)(-k + 3) {tex}\ge{/tex} 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (3k - 1)(k - 3) {tex}\le{/tex} 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{3} \leq k \leq 3{/tex} i.e. {tex}k \in\left[\frac{1}{3}, 3\right]{/tex} - {1} ......(i) 
And, if k = 1, then,
x = 0, which is real .....(ii)
So, from (i) and (ii), we get
{tex}k \in\left[\frac{1}{3}, 3\right]{/tex}

questions.question: If the roots of x^2 - bx + c = 0 are two consecutive integers, then b^2 - 4c is
questions.question_raw: If the roots of x2 - bx + c = 0 are two consecutive integers, then b2 - 4c is
questions.explanation:
Given equation:
x2 - bx + c = 0
Let {tex}\alpha{/tex} and {tex}\alpha + 1{/tex} be the two consective roots of the equation.
Sum of the roots = {tex}\alpha+\alpha+1{/tex} = {tex}2 \alpha+1{/tex} 
Product of the roots = {tex}\alpha(\alpha+1){/tex} = {tex}\alpha(\alpha+1){/tex} 
So, sum of the roots = {tex}2 \alpha+1{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-\text { Coeffecient of } x}{\text { Coeffecient of } x^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{b}{1}{/tex} = b
Product of the roots = {tex}\alpha^{2}+\alpha{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\text { Constant term }}{\text { Coeffecient of } x^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{c}{1}{/tex} = c
Now, b2 - 4c = {tex}(2 \alpha+1)^{2}-4\left(\alpha^{2}+\alpha\right){/tex} = {tex}4 \alpha^{2}+4 \alpha+1-4 \alpha^{2}-4 \alpha{/tex} = 1

questions.question: The value of a such that x^2 - 11x + a = 0 and x^2 - 14 x + 2a = 0 may have a common root is
questions.question_raw: The value of a such that x2 - 11x + a = 0 and x2 - 14 x + 2a = 0 may have a common root is
questions.explanation:
Let {tex}\alpha{/tex} be the common roots of the equations x2 - 11x + a = 0 and x2 - 14x + 2a = 0.
Therefore,
{tex}\alpha^2{/tex} - 11{tex}\alpha{/tex} + a = 0 ... (i)
{tex}\alpha^2{/tex} - 14{tex}\alpha{/tex} + 2a = 0 ... (ii)
Solving (i) and (ii) by cross multiplication, we get,
{tex}\frac{\alpha^{2}}{-22 a+14 a}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\alpha}{a-2 a}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{-14+11}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\alpha^{2}=\frac{-22 a+14 a}{-14+11}{/tex}, {tex}\alpha=\frac{a-2 a}{-14+11}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\alpha^{2}=\frac{-8 a}{-3}=\frac{8 a}{3}{/tex}, {tex}\alpha=\frac{-a}{-3}=\frac{a}{3}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left(\frac{a}{3}\right)^{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{8a}{3}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a2 = 24a
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a2 - 24a = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a(a - 24) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = 0 or a = 24

questions.question: The values of k for which the quadratic equation for kx^2 +1 = kx + 3x - 11x^2 has real and equal roots are
questions.question_raw: The values of k for which the quadratic equation for kx2 +1 = kx + 3x - 11x2 has real and equal roots are
questions.explanation:
The given equation is kx2 + 1 = kx + 3x - 11x2 which can be written as.
kx2 + 11x2 - kx - 3x + 1 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (k + 11)x2 - (k + 3) x + 1 = 0
For equal and real roots, the discriminant of 
(k + 11)x2 - (k + 3)x + 1 = 0
{tex}\therefore{/tex} (k + 3)2 - 4(k + 11) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} k2 + 2k - 35 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (k - 5) (k + 7) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} k = 5, -7
Hence, the equation has real and equal roots when k = 5, -7.

questions.question: If the equations x^2 + 2x + 3$\lambda$ = 0 and 2x^2 + 3 x + 5$\lambda$ = 0 have a non-zero common roots, then $\lambda$ =
questions.question_raw: If the equations x2 + 2x + 3{tex}\lambda{/tex} = 0 and 2x2 + 3 x + 5{tex}\lambda{/tex} = 0 have a non-zero common roots, then {tex}\lambda{/tex} =
questions.explanation:
Let {tex}\alpha{/tex} be the common roots of the equations, x2 + 2x + 3{tex}\lambda{/tex} = 0 and 2x2 + 3x + 5{tex}\lambda{/tex} = 0
Therefore,
{tex}\alpha^{2}+2 \alpha+3 \lambda{/tex} = 0 .....(i)
{tex}\alpha^{2}+2 \alpha+3 \lambda{/tex} = 0  .....(ii)
Solving (i) and (ii) by cross multiplication, we get
{tex}\frac{\alpha^{2}}{10 \lambda-9 \lambda}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\alpha}{6 \lambda-5 \lambda}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{3-4}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\alpha^{2}=-\lambda, \alpha=-\lambda{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}-\lambda=\lambda^{2}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} = -1

questions.question: If one root of the equation x^2 + px + 12 = 0 is 4, while the equation x^2 + px + q = 0 has equal roots, the value of q is
questions.question_raw: If one root of the equation x2 + px + 12 = 0 is 4, while the equation x2 + px + q = 0 has equal roots, the value of q is
questions.explanation:
It is given that, 4 is the root of the equation x2 + px + 12 = 0.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 16 + 4p + 12 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} p = -7
It is also given that, the equation
x2 + px + q = 0 has equal roots. So, the discriminant of x2 + px + q = 0 will be zero.
{tex}\therefore{/tex}  p2 - 4q = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4q = (-7)2 = 49
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} q = {tex}\frac{49}{4}{/tex}

questions.question: The value of p and q (p $\ne$ 0, q $\ne$ 0) for which p, q are the roots of the equation x^2 + px + q ab = 0 are
questions.question_raw: The value of p and q (p {tex}\ne{/tex} 0, q {tex}\ne{/tex} 0) for which p, q are the roots of the equation x2 + px + q ab = 0 are
questions.explanation:
It is given that, p and q (p {tex}\ne{/tex} 0, q {tex}\ne{/tex} 0) are the roots of the equation x2 + px + q = 0.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Sum of roots = p + q = -p
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2p + q = 0 ... (i)
Product of roots = pq = q
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} qp - 1 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} p = 1, q = 0 but q {tex}\ne{/tex} 0
Now, substituting p = 1 in (i), we get,
2 + q = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} q = -2

questions.question: The set of all values of m for which both the roots of the equation x^2 - (m + 1) x + m + 4 = 0 are real and negative, is
questions.question_raw: The set of all values of m for which both the roots of the equation x2 - (m + 1) x + m + 4 = 0 are real and negative, is
questions.explanation:
The roots of the quadratic equation x2 - (m + 1)x + m + 4 = 0 will be real, if its discriminant is greater than or equal to zero.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} m + 12 - 4m + 4 {tex}\ge{/tex} 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} m - 5m + 3 {tex}\ge{/tex} 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} m {tex}\le{/tex} -3 or m {tex}\ge{/tex} 5 ... (i)
It is also given that, the roots of x2 - (m + 1)x + m + 4 = 0 are negative.
So, the sum of the roots will be negative.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Sum of the roots < 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} m + 1 < 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} m < -1 ... (ii) and product of zeros > 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} m + 4 > 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} m > -4 ...(iii)
From 1, 2 and 3, we get,
m ∈ (-4, -3]

questions.question: If $\alpha, \beta$ are the roots of the equation x^2 - p(x + 1) - c = 0, then ($\alpha$ + 1) ($\beta$ + 1) =
questions.question_raw: If {tex}\alpha, \beta{/tex} are the roots of the equation x2 - p(x + 1) - c = 0, then ({tex}\alpha{/tex} + 1) ({tex}\beta{/tex} + 1) =
questions.explanation:
Given equation:
x2 - p(x + 1) - c = 0
or x2 - px - p - c = 0
Also, {tex}\alpha{/tex} and {tex}\beta{/tex} are the roots of the equation.
Sum of the roots = {tex}\alpha+\beta{/tex} = p
Product of the roots = {tex}\alpha\beta{/tex} = -(c + p)
Then, {tex}(\alpha+1)(\beta+1){/tex} = {tex}\alpha \beta+\alpha+\beta+1{/tex} 
= -(c + p) + p + 1
= -c - p + p + 1
= 1 - c

questions.question: The value of (1 + i) (1 + i^2) (1 + i^3) (1 + i^4) is
questions.question_raw: The value of (1 + i) (1 + i2) (1 + i3) (1 + i4) is
questions.explanation:
0
(1 + i) (1 + i2) (1 + i3) (1 + i4)
= (1 + i) (1 - 1) (1 - i) (1 + 1) ({tex}\because{/tex} i2 = -1, i3 = -i and i4 = 1)
= (1 + i) (0) (1 - i) (2) = 0

questions.question: If $\frac{3+2 i \sin \theta}{1-2 i \sin \theta}$ is a real number and $0<\theta<2 \pi$, then $\theta$ =
questions.question_raw: If {tex}\frac{3+2 i \sin \theta}{1-2 i \sin \theta}{/tex} is a real number and {tex}0<\theta<2 \pi{/tex}, then {tex}\theta{/tex} =
questions.explanation:
{tex}\pi{/tex}
Given:
{tex}\frac{3+2 i \sin \theta}{1-2 i \sin \theta}{/tex} is a real number
On rationalising, we get,
{tex}\begin{aligned} &3+2 i \sin \theta\\ &1-2 i \sin \theta \end{aligned}{/tex} {tex}\times\begin{array}{l} 1+2 i \sin \theta \\ 1+2 i \sin \theta \end{array}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{(3+2 i \sin \theta)(1+2 i \sin \theta)}{(1)^{2}-(2 i \sin \theta)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{3+2 i \sin \theta+6 i \sin \theta+4 i^{2} \sin ^{2} \theta}{1+4 \sin ^{2} \theta}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{3-4 \sin ^{2} \theta+8 i \sin \theta}{1+4 \sin ^{2} \theta}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} i2 = -1]
{tex}=\begin{array}{l} 3-4 \sin ^{2} \theta \\ 1+4 \sin ^{2} \theta \end{array}+i \begin{array}{c} 8 \sin \theta \\ 1+4 \sin ^{2} \theta \end{array}{/tex} For the above term to be real, the imaginary part has to be zero.
{tex}\therefore \frac{8 \sin \theta}{1+4 \sin ^{2} \theta}=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 8 sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = 0
For this to be zero,
sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\theta{/tex} = 0,
{tex}\pi, 2 \pi, 3 \pi {/tex} ...
But
{tex}0<\theta<2 \pi{/tex}
Hence,
{tex}\theta=\pi{/tex}

questions.question: If (1 + i) (1 + 2 i) (1 + 3 i) ... (1 + n i) = a + i b, then 2 $\times$ 5 $\times$ 10 $\times$ ... $\times$ (1 + n^2) is equal to
questions.question_raw: If (1 + i) (1 + 2 i) (1 + 3 i) ... (1 + n i) = a + i b, then 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 {tex}\times{/tex} ... {tex}\times{/tex} (1 + n2) is equal to
questions.explanation:
a2 + b2
(1 + i) (1 + 2i) (1 + 3i) ... (1 + ni) = a + ib
Taking modulus on both the sides, we get:
{tex}[(1+i)(1+2 i)(1+3 i){/tex} ... {tex}(1+n i)|=| a+i b |{/tex}
{tex}|(1+i)(1+2 i)(1+3 i){/tex} ... {tex}(1+n i) |{/tex} can be written as
{tex}|(1+i)||(1+2 i)|{/tex} {tex}|(1+3 i)|{/tex} ... {tex}|(1+n i)|{/tex}
{tex}\sqrt{1^{2}+1^{2}} \times \sqrt{1^{2}+2^{2}} {/tex} {tex}\times \sqrt{1^{2}+3^{2}} \times{/tex} ... {tex}\times \sqrt{1+n^{2}}=\sqrt{a^{2}+b^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt{2} \times \sqrt{5} \times \sqrt{10} \times{/tex} ... {tex}\times \sqrt{1+n^{2}}=\sqrt{a^{2}+b^{2}}{/tex}
Squaring on both the sides, we get:
{tex}2 \times 5 \times 10 \times{/tex} ... {tex}\times{/tex} (1 + n2) = a2 + b2

questions.question: If $\sqrt{a+i b}$ = x + iy, then possible value of $\sqrt{a-i b}$ is
questions.question_raw: If {tex}\sqrt{a+i b}{/tex} = x + iy, then possible value of {tex}\sqrt{a-i b}{/tex} is
questions.explanation:
x - i y
{tex}\sqrt{a+i b}{/tex} = x + iy
Squaring on both the sides, we get,
a + ib = x2 + (iy)2 + 2ixy
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a + ib = (x2 - y2) + 2ixy
{tex}\therefore{/tex} a = (x2 - y2)
and b = 2xy
{tex}\therefore{/tex} a - ib (x2 - y2) - 2ixy
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a - ib = x2 + i2y2 - 2ixy [{tex}\because{/tex} i2 = -1]
Taking square root on both the sides, we get:
{tex}\sqrt{a-i b}{/tex} = x - iy

questions.question: If z = cos $\frac{\pi}{4}$ + i sin $\frac{\pi}{6}$, then
questions.question_raw: If z = cos {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} + i sin {tex}\frac{\pi}{6}{/tex}, then
questions.explanation:
{tex}z=\cos \frac{\pi}{4}+i \sin \frac{\pi}{6}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow z=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}+\frac{1}{2} i{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow|z|=\sqrt{\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^{2}+\frac{1}{4}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow|z|=\sqrt{\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{4}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow|z|=\sqrt{\frac{3}{4}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow|z|=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\tan \alpha=\left|\frac{\operatorname{Im}(z)}{\operatorname{Re}(z)}\right|{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \alpha=\tan ^{-1}\left(\begin{array}{c} 1 \\ \sqrt{2} \end{array}\right){/tex}
since, the point z lies in the first quadrant.
Therefore, arg (z) {tex}=\alpha=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right){/tex}

questions.question: If i^2 = -1, then sum i + i^2 + i^3 + ... upto 1000 terms is equal to
questions.question_raw: If i2 = -1, then sum i + i2 + i3 + ... upto 1000 terms is equal to
questions.explanation:
0
i + i2 + i3 + i4 ... i1000
i + i2 + i3 + i4 [{tex}\because{/tex} i2 = -1, i3 = -i and i4 = 1]
= i - 1 - i + 1
= 0
Similarly, the sum of the next four terms of the series will be equal to 0. This is because the powers of i follow a cyclicity of 4.
1000, will be zero.

questions.question: If z $=\frac{-2}{1+i \sqrt{3}}$, then the value of arg (z) is
questions.question_raw: If z {tex}=\frac{-2}{1+i \sqrt{3}}{/tex}, then the value of arg (z) is
questions.explanation:
{tex}\frac{2 \pi}{3}{/tex}
{tex}z=\frac{-2}{1+i \sqrt{3}}{/tex}
Rationalising z, we get
{tex}z=\frac{-2}{1+i \sqrt{3}}{/tex} {tex}\times \frac{1-i \sqrt{3}}{1-i \sqrt{3}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow z=\frac{-2+i 2 \sqrt{3}}{1+3}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow z=\frac{-1+i \sqrt{3}}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow z=\frac{-1}{2}+\frac{i \sqrt{3}}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\tan \alpha=\left|\frac{\operatorname{Im}(z)}{\operatorname{Re}(z)}\right|{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{3}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \alpha=\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex}
since, z lies in the second quadrant.
Therefore, arg (z) {tex}=\pi-\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{2 \pi}{3}{/tex}

questions.question: If a cos $\theta$ + i sin $\theta$, then $\frac{1+a}{1-a}=$
questions.question_raw: If a cos {tex}\theta{/tex} + i sin {tex}\theta{/tex}, then {tex}\frac{1+a}{1-a}={/tex}
questions.explanation:
{tex}i \cot \frac{\theta}{2}{/tex}
{tex}a=\cos \theta+i \sin \theta{/tex} (given)
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1+a}{1-a}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1+\cos \theta+i \sin \theta}{1-\cos \theta-i \sin \theta}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \begin{array}{l} 1+a \\ 1-a \end{array}{/tex} {tex}=\begin{array}{l} 1+\cos \theta+i \sin \theta \\ 1-\cos \theta-i \sin \theta \end{array}{/tex} {tex}\times\begin{array}{l} 1-\cos \theta+i \sin \theta \\ 1-\cos \theta+i \sin \theta \end{array}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1+a}{1-a}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{(1+i \sin \theta)^{2}-\cos ^{2} \theta}{(1-\cos \theta)^{2}-(i \sin \theta)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1+a}{1-a}{/tex}{tex}=\frac{1-\sin ^{2} \theta+2 i \sin \theta-\cos ^{2} \theta}{1+\cos ^{2} \theta-2 \cos \theta+\sin ^{2} \theta}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1+a}{1-a}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1-\left(\sin ^{2} \theta+\cos ^{2} \theta\right)+2 i \sin \theta}{1+\left(\sin ^{2} \theta+\cos ^{2} \theta\right)-2 \cos \theta}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1+a}{1-a}=\frac{2 i \sin \theta}{2(1-\cos \theta)}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1+a}{1-a}=\frac{2 i \sin \frac{\theta}{2} \cos \frac{\theta}{2}}{2 \sin ^{2} \frac{\theta}{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1+a}{1-a}=\frac{i \cos \frac{\theta}{2}}{\sin \frac{\theta}{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1+a}{1-a}=i \cot \frac{\theta}{2}{/tex}

questions.question: If (1 + i) (1 + 2i) (1 + 3i) ... (1 + ni) = a + ib, then 2.5.10.17 ... (1 + n^2) =
questions.explanation:
a2 + b2
(1 + i) (1 + 2i) (1 + 3i) ... (1 + ni) = a + ib
Taking modulus on both the sides, we get,
{tex}|(1+i)(1+2 i){/tex} {tex}(1+3 i) ... {/tex} {tex}(1+n i) |{/tex} = a + ib
{tex}|(1+i)(1+2 i)(1+3 i){/tex} {tex}...(1+n i) |{/tex} can be wriiten as {tex}|(1+i)||(1+2 i)|{/tex} {tex}|(1+3 i)| \ldots |(1+n i)|{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \sqrt{1^{2}+1^{2}} \times \sqrt{1^{2}+2^{2}}{/tex}{tex}\times \sqrt{1^{2}+3^{2}} \ldots \times{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{1^{2}+n^{2}}=\sqrt{a^{2}+b^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt{2} \times \sqrt{5} \times \sqrt{10}{/tex} {tex}\ldots \times \sqrt{1+n^{2}}=\sqrt{a^{2}+b^{2}}{/tex}
Squaring on both the sides, we get:
{tex}2 \times 5 \times 10 \ldots \times{/tex} (1 + n2) = a2 + b2

questions.question: If $\frac{\left(a^{2}+1\right)^{2}}{2 a-i}$ = x + iy, then x^2 + y^2 is equal to
questions.question_raw: If {tex}\frac{\left(a^{2}+1\right)^{2}}{2 a-i}{/tex} = x + iy, then x2 + y2 is equal to
questions.explanation:
{tex}\frac{\left(a^{2}+1\right)^{4}}{4 a^{2}+1}{/tex}
x + iy {tex}=\frac{\left(a^{2}+1\right)}{2 a-i}{/tex}
Taking modulus on both the sides, we get:
{tex}\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}}=\frac{\left(a^{2}+1\right)^{2}}{\sqrt{4 a^{2}+1}}{/tex}
x2 + y2 {tex}=\frac{\left(a^{2}+1\right)^{2}}{4 a^{2}+1}{/tex}

questions.question: The principal value of the amplitude of (1 + i) is
questions.question_raw: The principal value of the amplitude of (1 + i) is
questions.explanation:
{tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}
Let z = (1 + i)
{tex}\tan \alpha=\left|\frac{\operatorname{Im}(z)}{\operatorname{Re}(z)}\right|{/tex}
= 1
{tex}\Rightarrow \alpha=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}
since, z lies in the first quadrant.
Therefore, arg (z) {tex}=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}

questions.question: The least positive integer n such that $\left(\frac{2 i}{1+i}\right)^{n}$ is a positive integer, is
questions.question_raw: The least positive integer n such that {tex}\left(\frac{2 i}{1+i}\right)^{n}{/tex} is a positive integer, is
questions.explanation:
8
Let z {tex}=\left(\frac{2 i}{1+i}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow z=\frac{2 i}{1+i} \times \frac{1-i}{1-i}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow z=\frac{2 i(1-i)}{1-i^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow z=\frac{2 i(1-i)}{1+1}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} i2 = -1]
{tex}\Rightarrow z=\frac{2 i(1-i)}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} z = i - i2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} z = i + 1
Now, zn = (1 + i)n
For n = 2,
z2 = (1 + n)2
= 1 + i2 + 2i
= 1 - i + 2i
= 2i ... (i)
since this is not a positive integer
For n = 4
z4 = (1 + i)4
= [(1 + i)2]4
= (2i)2 [Using (i)]
= 4i2
= -4 ... (ii)
This is a negative integer.
For n = 8,
z8 = (1 + i)8
= [(1 + i)4]2
= (-4)2 Using (ii)]
= 16
This is a positive integer.
Thus, z {tex}=\left(\frac{2 i}{1+i}\right)^{n}{/tex} is positive for n = 8.
Therefore, 8 is the least positive integer such that {tex}\left(\frac{2 i}{1+i}\right)^{n}{/tex} is a positive integer

questions.question: If z is a non-zero complex number, then $\left|\frac{|\bar{z}|^{2}}{z \bar{z}}\right|$ is equal to
questions.question_raw: If z is a non-zero complex number, then {tex}\left|\frac{|\bar{z}|^{2}}{z \bar{z}}\right|{/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation:
{tex}\left|\frac{\bar{z}}{z}\right|{/tex}
{tex}\left|\frac{|\bar{z}|^{2}}{z \bar{z}}\right|=\left|\frac{|\bar{z}|^{2}}{|z|^{2}}\right|{/tex} {tex}\left(\because z \bar{z}=|z|^{2}\right){/tex}
Let z = a - ib
{tex}\Rightarrow|z|=\sqrt{a^{2}+b^{2}}{/tex}
Let {tex}\bar{z}{/tex} = a - ib
{tex}\Rightarrow|z|=\sqrt{a^{2}+b^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore\left|\frac{|\bar{z}|^{2}}{z \bar{z}}\right|=\left|\frac{|\bar{z}|^{2}}{|z|^{2}}\right|{/tex}
{tex}=\left|\frac{\bar{z}}{z}\right|{/tex}

questions.question: If (x + iy)^$\frac {1} {3}$ = a + ib, then $\frac{x}{a}+\frac{y}{b}=$
questions.question_raw: If (x + iy){tex}\frac {1} {3}{/tex} = a + ib, then {tex}\frac{x}{a}+\frac{y}{b}={/tex}
questions.explanation:
(x + iy){tex}\frac {1} {3}{/tex} = a + ib
Cubing on both the sides, we get :
x + iy = (a + ib){tex}\frac {1} {3}{/tex}
x + iy = (a + ib)3
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x + iy a3 (ib)3 + 3a2bi + a(ib)2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x + iy = a3 + i3b3 + 3a2ib + 3i2ab2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x + iy = a3 - ib3 + 3a2ib - 3ab2 ({tex}\because{/tex} i2 = -1, i3 = -i)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x + iy = a3 - .3ab2 + i(-b3 + 3a2b)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x = a3 - 3ab2 and y = 3a2b - b3
or {tex}\frac{x}{a}{/tex} = a2 - 3b2 and {tex}\frac{y}{b}{/tex} = 3a2 - b2
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{x}{a}+\frac{y}{b}{/tex} = a2 - 3b2 + 3a2 - b2
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{x}{a}+\frac{y}{b}{/tex} = 4a2 - 4b2

questions.question: The argument of $\frac{1-i \sqrt{3}}{1+i \sqrt{3}}$ is
questions.question_raw: The argument of {tex}\frac{1-i \sqrt{3}}{1+i \sqrt{3}}{/tex} is
questions.explanation:
240°
{tex}\frac{1-i \sqrt{3}}{1+i \sqrt{3}}{/tex}
Rationalising the denominator,
{tex}\frac{1-i \sqrt{3}}{1+i \sqrt{3}} \times \frac{1-i \sqrt{3}}{1-i \sqrt{3}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1+3 i^{2}-2 \sqrt{3} i}{1-3 i^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{-2-2 \sqrt{3} i}{4}{/tex} ({tex}\because{/tex} i2 = -1)
{tex}=\frac{-1}{2}-i \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\tan \alpha=\left|\begin{array}{l} \operatorname{Im}(z) \\ \operatorname{Re}(z) \end{array}\right|{/tex}
Thus, {tex}\tan \alpha=\left|\frac{\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}}{\frac{-1}{2}}\right|{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{3}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \alpha=60^{\circ}{/tex}
Since the points {tex}\left(\frac{-1}{2}, \frac{-\sqrt{3}}{2}\right){/tex} lie in the third quadrant, the argument is given by :
{tex}\theta{/tex} = 180° + 60°
= 240°

questions.question: If z $=\left(\frac{1+i}{1-i}\right)$, then z^4 equals.
questions.question_raw: If z {tex}=\left(\frac{1+i}{1-i}\right){/tex}, then z4 equals.
questions.explanation:
1
Let z {tex}=\frac{1+i}{1-i}{/tex}
{tex}z=\frac{1+i}{1-i} \times \frac{1+i}{1+i}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow z=\frac{1+i^{2}+2 i}{1-i^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow z=\frac{2 i}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} z = i
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} z4 = i4
Since i2 = -1, we have:
{tex}\Rightarrow z^{4}=i^{2} \times i^{2}{/tex}
 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} z4 = 1

questions.question: If z $=\frac{1+2 i}{1-(1-i)^{2}}$, then arg (z) equals
questions.question_raw: If z {tex}=\frac{1+2 i}{1-(1-i)^{2}}{/tex}, then arg (z) equals
questions.explanation:
0
Let z {tex}=\frac{1+2 i}{1-(1-i)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow z=\frac{1+2 i}{1-\left(1+i^{2}-2 i\right)}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow z=\frac{1+2 i}{1-(1-1-2 i)}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow z=\frac{1+2 i}{1+2 i}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} z = 1
Since point (1, 0) lies on the positive direction of real axis, we have:
arg (z) = 0

questions.question: If z $=\frac{1}{(2+3 i)^{2}}$, then |z| =
questions.question_raw: If z {tex}=\frac{1}{(2+3 i)^{2}}{/tex}, then |z| =
questions.explanation:
{tex}\frac{1}{13}{/tex}
Let z {tex}=\frac{1}{(2+3 i)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow z=\frac{1}{4+9 i^{2}+12 i}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow z=\frac{1}{-5+12 i}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow z=\frac{1}{-5+12 i}{/tex} {tex}\times\frac{-5-12 i}{-5-12 i}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow z=\frac{-5-12 i}{25+144}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow z=\frac{-5}{169}-\frac{12 i}{169}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow|z|=\sqrt{\frac{25}{169^{2}}+\frac{144}{169^{2}}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow|z|=\frac{1}{\sqrt{169}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow|z|=\frac{1}{13}{/tex}

questions.question: If z $=\frac{1}{(1-i)(2+3 i)}$, then |z| =
questions.question_raw: If z {tex}=\frac{1}{(1-i)(2+3 i)}{/tex}, then |z| =
questions.explanation:
{tex}1 / \sqrt{26}{/tex}
Let z {tex}=\frac{1}{(1-i)(2+3 i)}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow z=\frac{1}{2+i-3 i^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow z=\frac{1}{2+i+3}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow z=\frac{1}{5+i} \times \frac{5-i}{5-i}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow z=\frac{5-i}{25-i^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow z=\frac{5-i}{25+1}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow z=\frac{5-i}{26}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow z=\frac{5}{26}-\frac{i}{26}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow|z|=\sqrt{\frac{25}{676}+\frac{1}{676}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow z=\frac{1}{\sqrt{26}}{/tex}

questions.question: If z = 1 - cos $\theta$ + i sin $\theta$, then |z| =
questions.question_raw: If z = 1 - cos {tex}\theta{/tex} + i sin {tex}\theta{/tex}, then |z| =
questions.explanation:
{tex}2\left|\sin \frac{\theta}{2}\right|{/tex}
{tex}\because z=1-\cos \theta+i \sin \theta{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow|z|=\sqrt{(1-\cos \theta)^{2}+\sin ^{2} \theta}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow|z|=\sqrt{1+\cos ^{2} \theta-2 \cos \theta+\sin ^{2} \theta}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow|z|=\sqrt{1+1-2 \cos \theta}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow|z|=\sqrt{2(1-\cos \theta)}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow|z|=\sqrt{4 \sin ^{2} \frac{\theta}{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow|z|=2\left|\sin \frac{\theta}{2}\right|{/tex}

questions.question: If x + iy = (1 + i) (1 + 2i) (1 + 3i), then x^2 + y^2 =
questions.question_raw: If x + iy = (1 + i) (1 + 2i) (1 + 3i), then x2 + y2 =
questions.explanation:
100
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x + iy = (1 + i) (1 + 2i) (1 + 3i), then x2 + y2 = 
Taking modulus on both the sides :
{tex}|x+i y|=|(1+i){/tex} {tex}(1+2 i)(1+3 i) |{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow|x+i y|=|1+i|{/tex} {tex}\times|1+2 i| \times|1+3 i|{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}}=\sqrt{1^{2}+1^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{1^{2}+2^{2}} \sqrt{1^{2}+3^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}}=\sqrt{2} \sqrt{5} \sqrt{10}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}}=\sqrt{100}{/tex}
Squaring both the sides
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 + y2 = 100

questions.question: If z $=\frac{1}{1-\cos \theta-i \sin \theta}$, then Re (z) =
questions.question_raw: If z {tex}=\frac{1}{1-\cos \theta-i \sin \theta}{/tex}, then Re (z) =
questions.explanation:
{tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}z=\frac{1}{1-\cos \theta-i \sin \theta}{/tex}
{tex}z=\frac{1}{1-\cos \theta-i \sin \theta}{/tex} {tex}\times \frac{1-\cos \theta+i \sin \theta}{1-\cos \theta+i \sin \theta}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow z=\frac{1-\cos \theta+i \sin \theta}{(1-\cos \theta)^{2}-(i \sin \theta)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow z=\frac{1-\cos \theta+i \sin \theta}{1+\cos ^{2} \theta-2 \cos \theta+\sin ^{2} \theta}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow z=\frac{1-\cos \theta+i \sin \theta}{1+1-2 \cos \theta}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow z=\frac{1-\cos \theta+i \sin \theta}{2(1-\cos \theta)}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Re (z) {tex}=\frac{(1-\cos \theta)}{2(1-\cos \theta)}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}

questions.question: If x + iy $=\frac{3+5 i}{7-6 i}$, then y =

On comparing both the sides:

questions.question: If $\frac{1-i x}{1+i x}$ = a + ib, then a^2 + b^2 =
questions.question_raw: If {tex}\frac{1-i x}{1+i x}{/tex} = a + ib, then a2 + b2 =

1
{tex}\frac{1-i x}{1+i x}{/tex} = a + ib
Taking modulus on both the sides, we get:
{tex}\left|\frac{1-i x}{1+i x}\right|=|a+i b|{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\sqrt{1^{2}+x^{2}}}{\sqrt{1^{2}+x^{2}}}=\sqrt{a^{2}+b^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt{a^{2}+b^{2}}=1{/tex}
Squaring both the sides, we get:

questions.question: If $\theta$ is the amplitude of $\frac{a+i b}{a-i b}$, then tan $\theta$ =

{tex}\frac{2 a b}{a^{2}-b^{2}}{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow z=\frac{a^{2}+i^{2} b^{2}+2 a b i}{a^{2}-i^{2} b^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow z=\frac{a^{2}-b^{2}+2 a b i}{a^{2}+b^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow z=\frac{a^{2}-b^{2}}{a^{2}+b^{2}}{/tex} {tex}+i \frac{2 a b}{a^{2}+b^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Re (z) {tex}=\frac{a^{2}-b^{2}}{a^{2}+b^{2}}{/tex},  Im (z) {tex}=\frac{2 a b}{a^{2}+b^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\tan \alpha=\left|\frac{\operatorname{Im}(z)}{\operatorname{Re}(z)}\right|{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{2 a b}{a^{2}-b^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\alpha=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 a b}{a^{2}-b^{2}}\right){/tex}
since, z lies in the first quadrant. Therefore,
arg (z) {tex}=\alpha=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 a b}{a^{2}-b^{2}}\right){/tex}
{tex}\tan \theta=\frac{2 a b}{a^{2}-b^{2}}{/tex}

questions.question: If z $=\frac{1+7 i}{(2-i)^{2}}$, then
questions.question_raw: If z {tex}=\frac{1+7 i}{(2-i)^{2}}{/tex}, then
questions.explanation:
amp (z) {tex}=\frac{3 \pi}{4}{/tex}
{tex}z=\frac{1+7 i}{(2-i)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow z=\frac{1+7 i}{4+i^{2}-4 i}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow z=\frac{1+7 i}{4-1-4 i}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} i2 = -1]
{tex}\Rightarrow z=\frac{1+7 i}{3-4 i}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow z=\frac{1+7 i}{3-4 i} \times \frac{3+4 i}{3+4 i}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow z=\frac{3+4 i+21 i+28 i^{2}}{9-16 i^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow z=\frac{3-28+25 i}{9+16}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow z=\frac{-25+25 i}{25}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} = -1 + i

= 1
{tex}\Rightarrow \alpha=\frac{\pi}4{/tex}
since, z lies in the second quadrant.
Therefore amp (z) {tex}=\pi-a{/tex}
{tex}=\pi-\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{3 \pi}{4}{/tex}

questions.question: The amplitude of $\frac{1}{i}$ is equal to
questions.question_raw: The amplitude of {tex}\frac{1}{i}{/tex} is equal to

{tex}-\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}
Let z {tex}=\frac{1}{i}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow z=\frac{1}{i} \times \frac{i}{i}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow z = \frac{i}{i^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} z = -i
Since, z(0, -1) lies on the negative imaginary axis.
Therefore, arg (z) {tex}=\frac{-\pi}{2}{/tex}

questions.question: The argument of $\frac{1-i}{1+i}$ is
questions.question_raw: The argument of {tex}\frac{1-i}{1+i}{/tex} is

{tex}-\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}
Let z {tex}=\frac{1-i}{1+i}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow z=\frac{1-i}{1+i} \times \frac{1-i}{1-i}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow z=\frac{1+i^{2}-2 i}{1-i^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow z=\frac{1-1-2 i}{1+1}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow z=\frac{-2 i}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} z = -i
Since, z lies on negative direction of imaginary axis.
Therefore, arg (z) {tex}=\frac {-\pi} {2}{/tex}

questions.question: The amplitude of $\frac{1+i \sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{3}+i}$ is
questions.question_raw: The amplitude of {tex}\frac{1+i \sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{3}+i}{/tex} is
questions.explanation:
{tex}\frac{\pi}{6}{/tex}
Let z {tex}=\frac{1+i \sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{3}+i}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow z=\frac{1+i \sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{3}+i} \times \frac{\sqrt{3}-i}{\sqrt{3}-i}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow z=\frac{\sqrt{3}+2 i-\sqrt{3} i^{2}}{3-i^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow z=\frac{\sqrt{3}+\sqrt{3}+2 i}{4}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow z=\frac{2 \sqrt{3}+2 i}{4}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow z=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}+\frac{1}{2} i{/tex}
{tex}\tan \alpha=\left|\frac{\operatorname{Im}(z)}{\operatorname{Re}(z)}\right|{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \alpha=\frac{\pi}{6}{/tex}
since, z lies in the first quadrant.
Therefore, arg (z) {tex}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right)=\frac{\pi}{6}{/tex}

questions.question: The value of $\frac {\left(i^{5}+i^{6}+i^{7}+i^{8}+i^{9}\right)} {(1 + i)}$ is
questions.question_raw: The value of {tex}\frac {\left(i^{5}+i^{6}+i^{7}+i^{8}+i^{9}\right)} {(1 + i)}{/tex} is

{tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} (1 + i)
{tex}\frac {i^{5}+i^{6}+i^{2}+i^{8}+i^{9}} {1 + i}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{i-1-i+1+i}{1+i}{/tex} [As, i5 = i, i6 = -1, i7 = -i, i8 = 1, i9 = i
{tex}=\frac {i} {i + 1}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{i}{i+1} \times \frac{i-1}{i-1}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{i(i-1)}{i^{2}-1}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{i^{2}-i}{-2}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} (1 + i)

questions.question: $\frac{1+2 i+3 i^{2}}{1-2 i+3 i^{2}}$ equals.
questions.question_raw: {tex}\frac{1+2 i+3 i^{2}}{1-2 i+3 i^{2}}{/tex} equals.

-i
Let z {tex}=\frac{1+2 i+3 i^{2}}{1-2 i+3 i^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow z=\frac{1+2 i-3}{1-2 i-3}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow z=\frac{-2+2 i}{-2-2 i}{/tex} {tex}\times\frac{-2+2 i}{-2+2 i}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow z=\frac{(-2+2 i)^{2}}{(-2)^{2}-(2 i)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow z=\frac{4+4 i^{2}-8 i}{4+4}{/tex}

questions.question: The value of $\frac{i^{592}+i^{590}+i^{588}+i^{586}+i^{584}}{i^{582}+i^{580}+i^{578}+i^{576}+i^{574}}-1$ is

-2

{tex}=\frac{1+i^{2}+1+i^{2}+1}{i^{2}+1+i^{2}+1+i^{2}}-1{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{-1}-1{/tex}

questions.question: The value of (1 + i)^4 + (1 - i)^4 is
questions.question_raw: The value of (1 + i)4 + (1 - i)4 is
questions.explanation:
-8
Using a4 + b4 = (a2 + b2) - 2a2b2
(1 + i)4 + (1 - i)4
= ((1 + i)2 + (1 - i)2)2 - 2(1 + i)2 (1 - i)2
= (1 + i2 + 2i + 1 + i2 - 2i)2 - 2(1 + i2 + 2i) (1 + i2 - 2i)
= (1 - 1 + 2i + 1 - 1 - 2i)2 - 2(1 - 1 + 2i) (1 - 1 - 2i)
= (0) - 2(2i) (-2i) ({tex}\because{/tex} i2 = -1)
= 8i2
= -8

questions.question: If z = a + ib lies in third quadrant, then $\frac{\bar{z}}{z}$ also lies in the third quadrant if
questions.question_raw: If z = a + ib lies in third quadrant, then {tex}\frac{\bar{z}}{z}{/tex} also lies in the third quadrant if

since, z = a + ib lies in third quadrant.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a< 0 and b < 0 ... (i)
Now,
{tex}\frac{z}{z}=\frac{a+i b}{a+i b}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{a-i b}{a+i b}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{a-i b}{a+i b} \times \frac{a-i b}{a-i b}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{a^{2}+i^{2} b^{2}-2 a b i}{a^{2}-i^{2} b^{2}}{/tex}
Since,
{tex}\frac{z}{z}{/tex} also lies in third quadrant.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a2 - b2 < 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (a - b) (a + b) < 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a - b > 0 and a + b < 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a > b ... (ii)
From (i) and (ii)
b < a < 0

questions.question: z_1 and z_2 are two distinct points in an argand plane. If a|z_1| = b|z_2|, (where a, b $\in$ R) then the point $\frac{a z_{1}}{b z_{2}}+\frac{b z_{2}}{a z_{1}}$ is a point on the:
questions.question_raw: z1 and z2 are two distinct points in an argand plane. If a|z1| = b|z2|, (where a, b {tex}\in{/tex} R) then the point {tex}\frac{a z_{1}}{b z_{2}}+\frac{b z_{2}}{a z_{1}}{/tex} is a point on the:

options.text: line segment [- 2, 2] of the real axis
the line with arg z = tan^-1 2
line segment [- 2, 2] of the imaginary axis

questions.explanation:
Let us assume that,
arg(z1) = {tex}\theta{/tex} and arg(z2) = {tex}\theta + \alpha{/tex}
Now, {tex}\frac {az_1}{bz_2} = \frac {a|z_1|e^{i\theta}}{b|z_2|e^{i(\theta + \alpha)}}=e^{-i\alpha}{/tex}
and, {tex}\frac {bz_2}{az_1} = \frac {b|z_2|e^{i(\theta + \alpha)}}{a|z_1|e^{i\theta}}=e^{i\alpha}{/tex}
Thus, {tex}\frac {az_1} {bz_2} + \frac {bz_2} {az_1}{/tex} = {tex}e^{-i\alpha} + e^{i \alpha}{/tex} = 2 cos({tex}\alpha{/tex})
Hence the point lies on [−2, 2]

questions.question: If ax^2 + bx + 6 = 0 does not have two distinct real roots, $a \in R, b \in R$ then the least value of (3a + b) is:
questions.question_raw: If ax2 + bx + 6 = 0 does not have two distinct real roots, {tex}a \in R, b \in R{/tex} then the least value of (3a + b) is:
questions.explanation:
As the quadratic equation does not have two distinct real roots, so b2 = 4ac
b2 = 4a {tex}\times{/tex} 6 = 24a
b2 = 24a
3a = {tex}\frac{b^{2}}{8}{/tex} 
3a + b = {tex}\frac{b^{2}}{8}+b=\frac{b^{2}+8 b}{8}=\frac{(b+4)^{2}-16}{8 b}{/tex} 
Least value of (b + 4)2 = 0
Hence least value of {tex}\frac{(b+4)^{2}-16}{8 b}=\frac{0-16}{8}{/tex} = -2

questions.question: If the roots of the equation x^2 + bx + c = 0 and x^2 - cx + b = 0 differ by the same quantity, then the value of (b + c), is:
questions.question_raw: If the roots of the equation x2 + bx + c = 0 and x2 - cx + b = 0 differ by the same quantity, then the value of (b + c), is:
questions.explanation:
Let the roots are {tex}\alpha, \beta{/tex} of x2 - bx + c = 0 and {tex}\alpha^{\prime}, \beta^{\prime}{/tex} be roots of x2 - cx + b = 0
Now {tex}\alpha-\beta{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{(\alpha+\beta)^{2}-4 \alpha \beta}=\sqrt{b^{2}-4 c}{/tex} ...(i)
and {tex}\alpha^{\prime}-\beta^{\prime}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{(\alpha+\beta)^{2}-4 \alpha^{\prime} \beta^{\prime}}=\sqrt{c^{2}-4 b}{/tex}
But {tex}\alpha-\beta=\alpha^{\prime}-\beta^{\prime}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{b^{2}-4 c}=\sqrt{c^{2}-4 b}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b2 - 4c = c2 - 4b
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b2 - c2 = 4c - 4b
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (b + c)(b - c) = 4(c - b) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b + c = -4

z = 1 + cos {tex}\left(\frac{11 \pi}{9}\right)+\sin \left(\frac{11 \pi}{9}\right){/tex}
= 2 cos2 {tex}(\frac {11\pi}{18}){/tex} + i {tex}\times{/tex} 2 sin {tex}\left(\frac{11 \pi}{18}\right)+cos \left(\frac{11 \pi}{18}\right){/tex}
= 2 cos {tex}\left(\frac{11 \pi}{18}\right)\left[\cos \frac{11 \pi}{18}+i \sin \frac{11 \pi}{18}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |z| = 2 cos {tex}(\frac {11\pi}{18}){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 180° < {tex}\frac {11\pi}{18}{/tex} < 270°
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Principle argument is {tex}\frac{11 \pi}{18}-\pi = \frac{-7\pi}{18}{/tex}
Hence, the {tex}\frac{-7 \pi}{18}, \ 2 \cos( \frac{11\pi}{18}){/tex}

Given quadratic equations
x2 − cx + d = 0 ...(i)
Let {tex}\alpha, \beta{/tex} be the roots of equation (i)
x2 − ax + b = 0 ...(ii)
Let {tex}\alpha, \alpha{/tex} be the roots of equation (ii)
2{tex}\alpha{/tex} = a
{tex}\alpha=\frac{a}{2}{/tex} 
Also, {tex}\alpha^{2}=b{/tex} 
Since, {tex}\alpha{/tex} is a root of (i)
{tex}\alpha^{2}-c \alpha+d{/tex} = 0
b + d = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{ac}}{2}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2(b + d) = ac

questions.question: If z_1 $\ne$ 0 and z_2 be two complex numbers such that $\frac{z_{2}}{z_{1}}$ is a purely imaginary number, then $\left|\frac{2 z_{1} \ + \ 3 z_{2}}{2 z_{1} \ - \ 3 z_{2}}\right|$ is equal to:
questions.question_raw: If z1 {tex}\ne{/tex} 0 and z2 be two complex numbers such that {tex}\frac{z_{2}}{z_{1}}{/tex} is a purely imaginary number, then {tex}\left|\frac{2 z_{1} \ + \ 3 z_{2}}{2 z_{1} \ - \ 3 z_{2}}\right|{/tex} is equal to:
questions.explanation:
Let z1 = 1 + i and z2 = 1 − i
{tex}\frac{Z_{2}}{z_{1}}=\frac{1 \ - \ i}{1 \ + \ i}=\frac{(1 \ - \ i)(1 \ - \ i)}{(1 \ + \ i)(1 \ - \ i)}{/tex} = i
{tex}\frac{2 z_{1} \ + \ 3 z_{2}}{2 z_{1} \ - \ 3 z_{2}}=-\frac{2 \ + \ 3\left(\frac{z_{2}}{z_{1}}\right)}{2 \ - \ 3\left(\frac{z_{2}}{z_{1}}\right)}=\frac{2 \ - \ 3 i}{2 \ + \ 3 i}{/tex}
{tex}\left|\frac{2 z_{1} \ + \ 3 z_{2}}{2 z_{1} \ - \ 3 z_{2}}\right|=\left|\frac{2 \ - \ 3 i}{2 \ + \ 3 i}\right|=\left|\frac{2 \ - \ 3 i}{2 \ + \ 3 i}\right|{/tex} {tex}\left[\because\left|\frac{z_{1}}{z_{2}}\right|=\frac{\left|z_{1}\right|}{\left|z_{2}\right|}\right]{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{\sqrt{4 \ + \ 9}}{\sqrt{4 \ + \ 9}}{/tex} = 1

questions.question: z_1, z_2, z_3, z_4 are distinct complex numbers representing the vertices of a quadrilateral ABCD taken in order. If z_1 - z_4 = z_2 - z_3 and arg $\frac{z_{4} \ - \ z_{1}}{z_{2} \ - \ z_{1}}=\frac{\pi}{2}$, then the quadrilateral is:
questions.question_raw: z1, z2, z3, z4 are distinct complex numbers representing the vertices of a quadrilateral ABCD taken in order. If z1 - z4 = z2 - z3 and arg {tex}\frac{z_{4} \ - \ z_{1}}{z_{2} \ - \ z_{1}}=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}, then the quadrilateral is:
questions.explanation:
Let
A = z1​
B = z2​
C = z3​
D = z​4
Hence
z1​ − z4 ​= z2 ​− z3​
Implies AD||BC
Hence opposites sides are parallel. Therefore it's a type of parallelogram.
Now
arg{tex}\frac{z_{4} \ - \ z_{1}}{z_{2} \ - \ z_{1}}=\angle A{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}
Hence,
All the angles are 90o
Therefore, it is a rectangle.

questions.question: If z_1, z_2 and z_3, z_4 are 2 pairs of complex conjugate numbers, then arg $\left(\frac{z_{1}}{z_{4}}\right)+\arg \left(\frac{z_{2}}{z_{3}}\right)$ equals:
questions.question_raw: If z1, z2 and z3, z4 are 2 pairs of complex conjugate numbers, then arg {tex}\left(\frac{z_{1}}{z_{4}}\right)+\arg \left(\frac{z_{2}}{z_{3}}\right){/tex} equals:
questions.explanation:
Given z1, z2 & z3, z4 are conjugate complete no;
{tex}\therefore{/tex} z2 = {tex}\overline z_1{/tex} & z4 = {tex}\overline z_3{/tex}
arg{tex}\left(\frac{z_{1}}{z_{4}}\right)+\arg \left(\frac{z_{2}}{z_{3}}\right){/tex} = arg{tex}\left(\frac{z_{1}}{z_{4}}\right)\left(\frac{z_{2}}{z_{3}}\right){/tex}
= arg{tex}\left(\frac{z_{1}}{z_{4}}\right)\left(\frac{\overline z_{2}}{z_{3}}\right){/tex}
= arg{tex}\left(\frac{{z}_{1} \overline{{z}_{1}}}{{z}_{3} \overline{{z}_{3}}}\right){/tex}
= arg{tex}\left(\frac{\left|{z}_{1}\right|^{2}}{\left|{z}_{3}\right|^{2}}\right){/tex}
= 0 ({tex}\frac{\left|z_{1}\right|^{2}}{\left|z_{3}\right|^{2}} \Rightarrow{/tex} purely real)

questions.question: The number of ordered pair of the form (a, b) for which a(x - 1)^2 + b(x - 3x + 2) + x - a^2 = 0 becomes an identity are:
questions.question_raw: The number of ordered pair of the form (a, b) for which a(x - 1)2 + b(x - 3x + 2) + x - a2 = 0 becomes an identity are:
questions.explanation:
Now, let's simplify this expression:
a(x - 1)2 + b(x - 3x + 2) + x - a2 = 0
a(x2 - 2x + 1) + b(-2x + 2) + x - a2 = 0
ax2 - 2ax + a + (-2bx + 2b) + x - a2 = 0
ax2 + (-2a - 2b + 1)x + (a - a2 + 2b) = 0
For this expression to be an identity, the coefficients of x^2 and x terms must be zero:
a - 2a - 2b + 1 = 0 (coefficient of x2 term)
a - a2 + 2b = 0 (coefficient of x term)
Let's solve these two equations:
a - 2a - 2b + 1 = 0
-a - 2b + 1 = 0
-a = 2b - 1
a = 1 - 2b (Equation 1)
a - a2 + 2b = 0
Substitute the value of a from Equation 1:
1 - 2b - (1 - 2b)2 + 2b = 0
1 - 2b - (1 - 4b + 4b2) + 2b = 0
1 - 2b - 1 + 4b - 4b2 + 2b = 0
-4b2 + 2b + 1 = 0
4b2 - 2b - 1 = 0
(2b - 1)(2b + 1) = 0
Two possible values for b:
2b - 1 = 0
2b = 1
b = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
2b + 1 = 0
2b = -1
b = {tex}-\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
Now that we have two possible values for b (b = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}or b = {tex}-\frac{1}{2}{/tex}), let's find the corresponding values of a using Equation 1:
If b = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}:
a = 1 - 2({tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}) = 1 - 1 = 0
So, one ordered pair is (a, b) = (0, {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}).
If b = {tex}-\frac{1}{2}{/tex}:
a = 1 - 2({tex}-\frac{1}{2}{/tex}) = 1 + 1 = 2
So, another ordered pair is (a, b) = (2, {tex}-\frac{1}{2}{/tex}).
Therefore, the two ordered pairs (a, b) for which the given expression becomes an identity are (0, {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}) and (2, {tex}-\frac{1}{2}{/tex}).

questions.question: The value of m for which the equations 3x^2 - 2mx - 4 = 0 and x^2 - 4mx + 2 = 0 have a common root is:
questions.question_raw: The value of m for which the equations 3x2 - 2mx - 4 = 0 and x2 - 4mx + 2 = 0 have a common root is:
questions.explanation:
3x2 - 2mx - 4 = 0 and x2 - 4mx + 2 = 0 have a common root.
Let {tex}\alpha{/tex} be the common root.
{tex}3 \alpha^{2}-2 m \alpha-4=0{/tex} 
{tex}\alpha^{2}-4 m \alpha+2=0{/tex} 
By Cramer's rule,
{tex}\frac{\alpha^{2}}{-4 m-16 m}=\frac{\alpha}{-4-6}=\frac{1}{-12 m+2 m}{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{\alpha^{2}}{-20 m}=\frac{\alpha}{-10}=\frac{1}{-10 m}{/tex} 
{tex}\alpha=2 \mathrm{~m}, \alpha=\frac{1}{\mathrm{~m}}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2m = {tex}-\frac{1}{m}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} m2 = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} m = {tex}\pm \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}

questions.question: If a complex number z satisfies the equation z + $\sqrt 2$ |z + 1| + i = 0, where i = $\sqrt {-1}$, then |z| is equal to:
questions.question_raw: If a complex number z satisfies the equation z + {tex}\sqrt 2{/tex} |z + 1| + i = 0, where i = {tex}\sqrt {-1}{/tex}, then |z| is equal to:
questions.explanation:
Put z = x + iy, we get (x + {tex}\sqrt{2} \cdot \sqrt{(x+1)^{2}+y^{2}}{/tex}) + i(y + 1) = 0 + i0
{tex}\therefore{/tex} On equating real and imaginary parts, we get x + {tex}\sqrt{2} \cdot \sqrt{(x+1)^{2}+y^{2}}{/tex} = 0 ...(i)
and y + 1 = 0 ...(ii)
So, on solving (i) and (ii), we get
x = −2, y = −1
{tex}\therefore{/tex} z = −2 − i {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} ∣z∣ = {tex}\sqrt 5{/tex}

questions.question: For all complex numbers z of the form 1 + i$\alpha$, $\alpha \ \in$ R, if z^2 = x + iy then:
questions.question_raw: For all complex numbers z of the form 1 + i{tex}\alpha{/tex}, {tex}\alpha \ \in{/tex} R, if z2 = x + iy then:
questions.explanation:
Let z = 1 + i{tex}\alpha{/tex}, {tex}\alpha \ \in{/tex} R
z2 = (1 + i{tex}\alpha{/tex})(1 + i{tex}\alpha{/tex})
x + iy = (1 + 2i{tex}\alpha{/tex} − α2)
On comparing real and imaginary parts, we get x = 1 − {tex}\alpha{/tex}2, y = 2{tex}\alpha{/tex}
y2 + 4x − 4 = 0
LHS: y2 + 4x − 4
= (2{tex}\alpha{/tex})2 + 4(1 − {tex}\alpha{/tex}2) − 4
= 4{tex}\alpha{/tex}2 + 4 − 4{tex}\alpha{/tex}2 − 4
= 0 = R.H.S

questions.question: A monic cubic polynomial P(x) satisfying the condition P (1) = 1, P(3) = 17 and P(5) = 49 then the value of is:
questions.question_raw: A monic cubic polynomial P(x) satisfying the condition P (1) = 1, P(3) = 17 and P(5) = 49 then the value of is:
questions.explanation: Assuming the question asks for P(2), we model P(x) as a monic cubic polynomial: P(x) = x³ + ax² + bx + c. Using the given conditions P(1) = 1, P(3) = 17, and P(5) = 49, we set up equations: 1) a + b + c = 0, 2) 9a + 3b + c = -10, 3) 25a + 5b + c = -76. Solving these, we find a = -7, b = 23, c = -16. Thus, P(x) = x³ - 7x² + 23x - 16. Calculating P(2): 8 - 28 + 46 - 16 = 10.

questions.question: If $a \cos \theta+b \sin \theta=c$ have roots $\alpha$ and $\beta$. Then, what will be the value of $\sin \alpha$ $+\sin \beta$ ?
questions.question_raw: If {tex}a \cos \theta+b \sin \theta=c{/tex} have roots {tex}\alpha{/tex} and {tex}\beta{/tex}. Then, what will be the value of {tex}\sin \alpha{/tex} {tex}+\sin \beta{/tex} ?
questions.explanation:
{tex}2 b c /\left(a^2+b^2\right){/tex}
Given, {tex}a \cos \theta+b \sin \theta=c{/tex}
So, this implies {tex}a \cos \theta=c-b \sin \theta{/tex}
Now squaring both the sides we get,
{tex} (a \cos \theta)^2=(c-b \sin \theta)^2 {/tex}
{tex} a^2 \cos ^2 \theta=c^2+b^2 \sin ^2 \theta-2 b c \sin \theta {/tex}
{tex} a^2\left(1-\sin ^2 \theta\right)=c^2+b^2 \sin ^2 \theta-2 b c \sin \theta {/tex}
{tex} a^2-a^2 \sin ^2 \theta=c^2+b^2 \sin ^2 \theta-2 b c \sin \theta {/tex}
Now rearranging the elements,
{tex} \left(a^2+b^2\right) \sin ^2 \theta-2 b c \sin \theta+\left(c^2-a^2\right)=\theta{/tex}
So, as sum of the roots are in the form -b/a if there is a quadratic equation {tex}a x^2+b x+c=0{/tex}
Now, we can conclude that
{tex} \sin \alpha+\sin \beta=2 b c /\left(a^2+b^2\right){/tex}

questions.question: If p and q are the roots of the equation x^2 + px + q =0 then, what are the values of p and q?
questions.question_raw: If p and q are the roots of the equation x2 + px + q =0 then, what are the values of p and q?
questions.explanation:
p = 1, q = -2
Explanation: Since, {tex}p{/tex} and {tex}q{/tex} are the roots of the equation {tex}x^2+p x+q=0{/tex}
So, {tex}p+q=-p{/tex} and {tex}p q=q{/tex}
So, {tex}p q=q{/tex}
And, {tex}q=0{/tex} or {tex}p=1{/tex}
If, {tex}q=0{/tex} then, {tex}p=0{/tex} and if {tex}p=1{/tex} then {tex}q=-2{/tex}.

questions.question: Let a, b $\in$ R, a $\neq$ 0 be such that the equation ax^2 - 2bx + 5 = 0 has a repeated root $\alpha$, which is also a root of the equation, x^2 - 2bx - 10 = 0. If $\beta$ is the other root of this equation, then $\alpha^{2}+\beta^{2}$ is equal to:
questions.question_raw: Let a, b {tex}\in{/tex} R, a {tex}\neq{/tex} 0 be such that the equation ax2 - 2bx + 5 = 0 has a repeated root {tex}\alpha{/tex}, which is also a root of the equation, x2 - 2bx - 10 = 0. If {tex}\beta{/tex} is the other root of this equation, then {tex}\alpha^{2}+\beta^{2}{/tex} is equal to:
questions.explanation:
ax2 - 2bx + 5 = 0, If {tex}\alpha{/tex} and {tex}\alpha{/tex} are roots of equations, then sum of roots
{tex}2 \alpha=\frac{2 b}{a}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \alpha=\frac{b}{a}{/tex}
and product of roots = {tex}\alpha^{2}=\frac{5}{a}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{b^{2}}{a^{2}}=\frac{5}{a}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b2 = 5a (a {tex}\neq{/tex} 0) ...(i)
For x2 - 2bx - 10 = 0
{tex}\alpha+\beta{/tex} = 2b ...(ii)
and {tex}\alpha \beta{/tex} = -10 ...(iii)
{tex}\alpha=\frac{b}{a}{/tex} is also root of x2 - 2bx - 10 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b2 - 2ab2 - 10a2 = 0
By eqn. (i) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 5a- 10a2 - 10a2 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 20a2 = 5a {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = {tex}\frac {1}{4}{/tex} and b2 = {tex}\frac {5}{4}{/tex}
{tex}\alpha^{2}{/tex} = 20 and {tex}\beta^{2}{/tex} = 5
Now, {tex}\alpha^{2}+\beta^{2}{/tex} = 5 + 20 = 25

questions.question: If z = $\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}+\frac{i}{2}(i=\sqrt{-1})$, then (1 + iz + z^5 + iz^8)^9 is equal to:
questions.question_raw: If z = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}+\frac{i}{2}(i=\sqrt{-1}){/tex}, then (1 + iz + z5 + iz8)9 is equal to:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}+\frac{i}{2}{/tex} = {tex}-i\left(-\frac{1}{2}+\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} i\right)=-i \omega{/tex}
where {tex}\omega{/tex} is imaginary cube root of unity.
Now, (1 + iz + z5 + iz8)9
= (1 + {tex}\omega{/tex} - i{tex}\omega^2{/tex} + i{tex}\omega^2{/tex})9 = (1 + {tex}\omega{/tex})9
= ({tex}-\omega^2{/tex})9 = {tex}-\omega^{18}{/tex} = -1 ({tex}\because{/tex} 1 + {tex}\omega{/tex} + {tex}\omega^2{/tex} = 0)

We have
f(x) = x2 + 2bx + 2c2
and g(x) = -x2 - 2cx + b2, (x {tex}\in{/tex} R)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(x) - (x + b)2 + 2c2 - b2
and g(x) = -(x + c)2 + b2 + c2
Now, fmin = 2c2 - b2 and gmax = b2 + c2
Given: mm f(x) > max g(x)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2c2 - b2 > b2 + c2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c2 > 2b2 {tex}\Rightarrow|c|>|b| \sqrt{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{|c|}{|b|}>\sqrt{2} \Rightarrow\left|\frac{c}{b}\right|>\sqrt{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\left|\frac{c}{b}\right| \in(\sqrt{2}, \infty){/tex}

questions.question: The least positive integer n for which $\left(\frac{1+i \sqrt{3}}{1-i \sqrt{3}}\right)^{n}$ = 1, is
questions.question_raw: The least positive integer n for which {tex}\left(\frac{1+i \sqrt{3}}{1-i \sqrt{3}}\right)^{n}{/tex} = 1, is
questions.explanation:
Let l = {tex}\left(\frac{1+i \sqrt{3}}{1-i \sqrt{3}}\right){/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} l = {tex}\left(\frac{1+i \sqrt{3}}{1-i \sqrt{3}}\right) \times\left(\frac{1+i \sqrt{3}}{1+i \sqrt{3}}\right){/tex}
= {tex}\left(\frac{-2+i 2 \sqrt{3}}{4}\right){/tex} = {tex}\left(\frac{1-i \sqrt{3}}{-2}\right){/tex}
Also, l = {tex}\left(\frac{1+i \sqrt{3}}{1-i \sqrt{3}}\right) \times\left(\frac{1-i \sqrt{3}}{1-i \sqrt{3}}\right){/tex}
= {tex}\left(\frac{4}{-2-i 2 \sqrt{3}}\right)=\left(\frac{-2}{1+i \sqrt{3}}\right){/tex}
Now, {tex}\left(\frac{1+i \sqrt{3}}{1-i \sqrt{3}}\right)^{3}{/tex} = {tex}\left(\frac{1+i \sqrt{3}}{1-i \sqrt{3}}\right) \times\left(\frac{1+i \sqrt{3}}{1-i \sqrt{3}}\right) \times\left(\frac{1+i \sqrt{3}}{1-i \sqrt{3}}\right){/tex}
= {tex}\left(\frac{1+i \sqrt{3}}{1-i \sqrt{3}}\right) \times\left(\frac{-2}{1+i \sqrt{3}}\right){/tex}{tex}\times\left(\frac{1-i \sqrt{3}}{-2}\right){/tex} = 1
{tex}\therefore{/tex} least positive integer n is 3

questions.question: If a, b, c, d and p are distinct real numbers such that (a^2 + b^2 + c^2)p^2 - 2(ab + bc + cd)p + (b^2 + c^2 + d^2) $\leq$ 0 then a, b, c, d
questions.question_raw: If a, b, c, d and p are distinct real numbers such that (a2 + b2 + c2)p2 - 2(ab + bc + cd)p + (b2 + c2 + d2) {tex}\leq{/tex} 0 then a, b, c, d
questions.explanation:
Given: a, b, c, d, p are real and distinct numbers such that
(a2 + b2 + c2)p2 - 2(ab + bc + cd)p + (b2 + c2 + d2) {tex}\leq{/tex} 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (a2p2 + b2p2 + c2p2) - (2abp + 2bcp + 2cdp) + (b2 + c2 + d2) {tex}\leq{/tex} 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (a2p2 - 2abp + b2) + (b2p2 - 2bcp + c2) + (c2p2 - 2cdp + d2) {tex}\leq{/tex} 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (ap - b)2 + (bp - c)2 + (cp - d)2 {tex}\leq{/tex} 0
Since LHS is the sum of perfect squares, therefore LHS can never be -ve.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} (ap - b)2 + (bp - c)2 + (cp - d)2 = 0
Which is possible only when each term is zero individually i.e.
ap - b = 0; bp - c = 0; cp - d = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{b}{a}=p ; \frac{c}{b}=p ; \frac{d}{c}=p{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{b}{a}=\frac{c}{b}=\frac{d}{c}=p{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} a, b, c, d are in G.P.

questions.question: Let z_1 and z_2 be any two non-zero complex numbers such that 3$\left|z_{1}\right|$ = 4 $\left|z_{2}\right|$. If z = $\frac{3 z_{1}}{2 z_{2}}+\frac{2 z_{2}}{3 z_{1}}$ then:
questions.question_raw: Let z1 and z2 be any two non-zero complex numbers such that 3{tex}\left|z_{1}\right|{/tex} = 4 {tex}\left|z_{2}\right|{/tex}. If z = {tex}\frac{3 z_{1}}{2 z_{2}}+\frac{2 z_{2}}{3 z_{1}}{/tex} then:
questions.explanation:
{tex}{3}\left|{z}_{1}\right|={4}\left|{z}_{2}\right|{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\left|\mathbf{z}_{1}\right|}{\left|\mathbf{z}_{2}\right|}=\frac{\mathbf{4}}{\mathbf{3}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\left|\mathbf{3} \mathbf{z}_{1}\right|}{\left|\mathbf{2 z}_{2}\right|}{/tex} = 2
Let {tex}\frac{3 \mathbf{z}_{1}}{2 \mathbf{z}_{2}}{/tex} = a = 2 cos{tex}\theta{/tex} + 2i sin{tex}\theta{/tex}
z = {tex}\frac{3 \mathbf{z}_{1}}{2 \mathbf{z}_{2}}+\frac{2 \mathrm{z}_{2}}{3 \mathrm{z}_{1}}{/tex} = a + {tex}\frac {1}{a}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{5}{2} \cos \theta+\frac{3}{2} i \sin \theta{/tex}
{tex}|\mathbf{z}|=\sqrt{\frac{17}{2}}{/tex}

questions.question: If $\alpha$ and $\beta$ be two roots of the equation x^2 - 64x + 256 = 0. Then the value of $\left(\frac{\alpha^{3}}{\beta^{5}}\right)^{\frac{1}{8}}+\left(\frac{\beta^{3}}{\alpha^{5}}\right)^{\frac{1}{8}}$ is:
