Here,
{tex}|\bar{z} \omega|=|\bar{z}||\omega|=|z||\omega|{/tex}
= |z{tex}\omega{/tex}| = 1
and arg {tex}(\bar{z} \omega){/tex} = arg{tex}(\bar{z}){/tex} + arg{tex}(\omega){/tex}
= arg{tex}(\omega){/tex} - arg(z)
= {tex}-\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \bar{z} \omega=|\bar{z} \omega|{/tex} [cos{tex}\left(-\frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} + i sin{tex}\left(-\frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex}]
= 1(0 - i)
= -i

questions.question: If the roots of the equation 12x^2 - mx + 5 = 0 are in the ratio 2 : 3, then m =
questions.question_raw: If the roots of the equation 12x2 - mx + 5 = 0 are in the ratio 2 : 3, then m =
questions.explanation:
Here, a = 12, b = -m, c = 5
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 {tex}\times{/tex} (-m)2 = 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 5(2 + 3)2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 6m2 = 1500
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} m2 = 250
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} m = {tex}5 \sqrt{10}{/tex}

questions.question: If 1, $\omega, \omega^{2}, \omega^{3}, \ldots, \omega^{n-1}$ are then n, n^th roots of unity, then (1 - $\omega$) (1 - $\omega^2$) ... (1 - $\omega^{n-1}$) equals
questions.question_raw: If 1, {tex}\omega, \omega^{2}, \omega^{3}, \ldots, \omega^{n-1}{/tex} are then n, nth roots of unity, then (1 - {tex}\omega{/tex}) (1 - {tex}\omega^2{/tex}) ... (1 - {tex}\omega^{n-1}{/tex}) equals
questions.explanation:
Since, {tex}\omega, \omega^{2}, \omega^{3}, \ldots, \omega^{n-1}{/tex} are the nth roots of unity
{tex}\therefore{/tex} xn - 1 = (x - 1) (x - {tex}\omega{/tex}) (x - {tex}\omega^2{/tex}) ... (x - {tex}\omega^{n-1}{/tex})
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x - {tex}\omega{/tex}) (x - {tex}\omega^2{/tex}) ... (x - {tex}\omega^{n-1}{/tex}) = {tex}\frac{x^{n}-1}{x-1}{/tex}
= xn-1 + xn-2 + ... + x + 1
Putting x = 1 on both sides, we get
(1 - {tex}\omega{/tex}) (1 - {tex}\omega^2{/tex}) ... (1 - {tex}\omega^{n-1}{/tex}) = n

questions.question: If one root of x^2 - x - k = 0 other, then k =
questions.question_raw: If one root of x2 - x - k = 0 other, then k =
questions.explanation:
Here, a = 1, b = -1, c = -k
{tex}\therefore{/tex} (1)2 (-k) + (-k)2 + (-1)3 = 3(1)(-1)(-k)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -k + k2 - 1 = 3K
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} k2 - 4k - 1 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} k = {tex}2 \pm \sqrt{5}{/tex}

questions.question: Let f(n) = (1 + $\omega$ + $\omega^2$ + ... + $\omega^n$)^n, n $\in$ N and $\omega \neq$ 1 is a cube root of unity. The number of distinct values of f(n) is
questions.question_raw: Let f(n) = (1 + {tex}\omega{/tex} + {tex}\omega^2{/tex} + ... + {tex}\omega^n{/tex})n, n {tex}\in{/tex} N and {tex}\omega \neq{/tex} 1 is a cube root of unity. The number of distinct values of f(n) is
questions.explanation:
f(n) = (1 + {tex}\omega{/tex} + {tex}\omega^2{/tex} + ... + {tex}\omega^n{/tex})n
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(n) = 0, n = 3k + 2
= {tex}\omega^2{/tex}, n = 3k + 1, k is odd
= -{tex}\omega^2{/tex}, n = 3k + 1, k is even
= 1, n = 3k
f(n) takes four values.

questions.question: The sum of the roots of a quadratic equation is 2 and sum of their cubes is 98, then the equation is:
questions.question_raw: The sum of the roots of a quadratic equation is 2 and sum of their cubes is 98, then the equation is:
questions.explanation:
Let the roots be {tex}\alpha{/tex} and {tex}\beta{/tex}.
Then, {tex}\alpha{/tex} + {tex}\beta{/tex} = 2 and {tex}\alpha^3{/tex} + {tex}\beta^3{/tex} = 98 ...(i)
{tex}\alpha^3{/tex} + {tex}\beta^3{/tex} = ({tex}\alpha{/tex} + {tex}\beta{/tex})3 - 3{tex}\alpha \beta{/tex}({tex}\alpha{/tex} + {tex}\beta{/tex})
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} 98 = (2)3 - 3{tex}\alpha \beta{/tex}(2) ...[From (i)]
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} 6{tex}\alpha \beta{/tex} = 8 - 98
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\alpha \beta{/tex} = -15
The required equation is
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 - 2x - 15 = 0

questions.question: Let $\omega$ be a non real cube root of unity. The least degree of a polynomial with real coefficients, having 2$\omega$, 2 + 3$\omega$, 2 + 3$\omega^2$ and 2 - $\omega$ - $\omega^2$ as its zeroes, is
questions.question_raw: Let {tex}\omega{/tex} be a non real cube root of unity. The least degree of a polynomial with real coefficients, having 2{tex}\omega{/tex}, 2 + 3{tex}\omega{/tex}, 2 + 3{tex}\omega^2{/tex} and 2 - {tex}\omega{/tex} - {tex}\omega^2{/tex} as its zeroes, is
questions.explanation:
2 - {tex}\omega-\omega^{2}{/tex} = 2 - {tex}\left(\omega+\omega^{2}\right){/tex}
 = 2 - (-1) = 3, a real root
2 + 3{tex}\omega{/tex} and  2 + 3{tex}\omega^2{/tex} are complex conjugates of each other
The complex roots of a polynomial equation having real coefficients appear in pair, i.e., if {tex}\alpha+i \beta{/tex} is a root then {tex}\alpha-i \beta{/tex} is also a root
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} If 2{tex}\omega{/tex} is a root then 2{tex}\omega^2{/tex} is also a root
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Least degree = 5

questions.question: If $\alpha$, $\alpha^2$ be the roots of x^2 + x + 1 = 0, then the equation whose roots are $\alpha^{31}$, $\alpha^{62}$ is:
questions.question_raw: If {tex}\alpha{/tex}, {tex}\alpha^2{/tex} be the roots of x2 + x + 1 = 0, then the equation whose roots are {tex}\alpha^{31}{/tex}, {tex}\alpha^{62}{/tex} is:
questions.explanation:
Since, {tex}\alpha{/tex} and {tex}\alpha^2{/tex} are the roots of x2 + x + 1 = 0
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\alpha{/tex} + {tex}\alpha^2{/tex} = -1 ... (i)
and {tex}\alpha^3{/tex} = 1 ... (ii)
Now, {tex}\alpha^{31}{/tex} + {tex}\alpha^{62}{/tex} = {tex}\alpha^{31}{/tex}(1 + {tex}\alpha^{31}{/tex})
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\alpha^{31}{/tex} + {tex}\alpha^{62}{/tex} = {tex}\alpha^{30} \cdot \alpha\left(1+\alpha^{30} \cdot \alpha\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\alpha^{31}+\alpha^{62}=\left(\alpha^{3}\right)^{10} \cdot \alpha\left(1+\left(\alpha^{3}\right)^{10} \cdot \alpha\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\alpha^{31}+\alpha^{62}=\alpha(1+\alpha){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\alpha^{31}+\alpha^{62}=-1{/tex}
Also, {tex}\alpha^{31} \cdot \alpha^{62}=\alpha^{93}=\left(\alpha^{3}\right)^{31}=1{/tex}
The required equation is
{tex}x^{2}-\left(\alpha^{31}+\alpha^{62}\right) x+\alpha^{31} \cdot \alpha^{62}=0{/tex}
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} x2 + x + 1 = 0

questions.question:
The value of the product (1 - $\omega$) (1 - $\omega^2$) (1 - $\omega^4$) (1 - $\omega^5$) is
($\omega$ : an imaginary cube root of unity)
questions.question_raw:
The value of the product (1 - {tex}\omega{/tex}) (1 - {tex}\omega^2{/tex}) (1 - {tex}\omega^4{/tex}) (1 - {tex}\omega^5{/tex}) is
({tex}\omega{/tex} : an imaginary cube root of unity)
questions.explanation:
(1 - {tex}\omega{/tex}) (1 - {tex}\omega^2{/tex}) (1 - {tex}\omega^4{/tex}) (1 - {tex}\omega^5{/tex})
= (1 - {tex}\omega{/tex}) (1 - {tex}\omega^2{/tex}) (1 - {tex}\omega{/tex}) (1 - {tex}\omega^2{/tex})
= [(1 - {tex}\omega{/tex}) (1 - {tex}\omega^2{/tex})]2
Now 1 + z + z2 = (z - {tex}\omega{/tex}) (z - {tex}\omega^2{/tex}), for all z
substitute z = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1 + 1 + 1 =(1 - {tex}\omega{/tex}) (1 - {tex}\omega^2{/tex})
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (1 - {tex}\omega{/tex}) (1 - {tex}\omega^2{/tex}) = 3
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Required product = 9

questions.question: If $\alpha^2$ = 5$\alpha^2$ - 3 and $\beta^2$ = 5$\beta$ - 3, where $\alpha \neq \beta$, then the equation whose roots are $\frac{\alpha}{\beta}$ and $\frac{\beta}{\alpha}$ is:
questions.question_raw: If {tex}\alpha^2{/tex} = 5{tex}\alpha^2{/tex} - 3 and {tex}\beta^2{/tex} = 5{tex}\beta{/tex} - 3, where {tex}\alpha \neq \beta{/tex}, then the equation whose roots are {tex}\frac{\alpha}{\beta}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{\beta}{\alpha}{/tex} is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\alpha{/tex}, {tex}\beta{/tex} are roots of x2 - 5x + 3 = 0
{tex}\alpha^{2}+\beta^{2}=(\alpha+\beta)^{2}-2 \alpha \beta{/tex}
= 25 - 2(3) = 19
The required equation is
{tex}x^{2}-\left(\frac{\alpha^{2}+\beta^{2}}{\alpha \beta}\right) x+1=0{/tex}
{tex}\Leftrightarrow x^{2}-\frac{19}{3} x+1=0{/tex}
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} 3x2 -19x + 3 = 0

questions.question: If x = a + b, y = a$\omega$ + b$\omega^2$, z = a$\omega^2$ + b$\omega$ then x^3 + y^3 + z^3, in terms of a and b is
questions.question_raw: If x = a + b, y = a{tex}\omega{/tex} + b{tex}\omega^2{/tex}, z = a{tex}\omega^2{/tex} + b{tex}\omega{/tex} then x3 + y3 + z3, in terms of a and b is
questions.explanation:
x + y + z = a(1 + {tex}\omega+\omega^{2}{/tex}) + b(1 + {tex}\omega+\omega^{2}{/tex}) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x3 + y3 + z3 = 3xyz
= 3(a + b) (a{tex}\omega{/tex} + b{tex}\omega^2{/tex}) (a{tex}\omega^2{/tex} + b{tex}\omega{/tex})
= 3{tex}\omega^2{/tex}(a + b) (a + b{tex}\omega{/tex}) (a{tex}\omega{/tex} + b)
= 3{tex}\omega^2{/tex} (a + b) [(a2 + b2) {tex}\omega{/tex} + ab(1 + {tex}\omega^2{/tex})]
= 3{tex}\omega^2{/tex} (a + b) (a2 + b2 - ab){tex}\omega{/tex}
= 3(a + b) (a2 + b2 - ab)
= 3(a3 + b3)

questions.question: Let $\alpha \neq \beta$ satisfy $\alpha^2$ + 1 = 6$\alpha$, $\beta^2$ + 1 = 6$\beta$. Then, the quadratic equation whose roots are $\frac{\alpha}{\alpha+1}, \frac{\beta}{\beta+1}$ is:
questions.question_raw: Let {tex}\alpha \neq \beta{/tex} satisfy {tex}\alpha^2{/tex} + 1 = 6{tex}\alpha{/tex}, {tex}\beta^2{/tex} + 1 = 6{tex}\beta{/tex}. Then, the quadratic equation whose roots are {tex}\frac{\alpha}{\alpha+1}, \frac{\beta}{\beta+1}{/tex} is:
questions.explanation:
Let {tex}\frac{\alpha}{\alpha+1}=t{/tex}
Then {tex}\frac{\alpha+1}{\alpha}=\frac{1}{t} \Leftrightarrow 1+\frac{1}{\alpha}=\frac{1}{t}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1-t}{t}=\frac{1}{\alpha} \Leftrightarrow \alpha=\frac{t}{1-t}{/tex}
{tex}\alpha{/tex} satisfies x2 - 6x + 1 = 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\alpha{/tex}t2 (1 + 6 + 1) + t(-6 -2) + 1 = 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} 8t2 - 8t + 1 = 0 (A quadratic equation whose roots are {tex}\frac{\alpha}{\alpha+1}, \frac{\beta}{\beta+1}{/tex})

questions.question: a is a non zero real and $\omega$ is an imaginary cube root of unity. The value of [a$\omega^2$ + (a + 1)$\omega$ + a]^90 is
questions.question_raw: a is a non zero real and {tex}\omega{/tex} is an imaginary cube root of unity. The value of [a{tex}\omega^2{/tex} + (a + 1){tex}\omega{/tex} + a]90 is
questions.explanation:
[a{tex}\omega^2{/tex} + (a + 1){tex}\omega{/tex} + a]90 = [a(1 + {tex}\omega+\omega^{2}{/tex}) + {tex}\omega{/tex}]90
= {tex}\omega^{90}=\left(\omega^{3}\right)^{30}{/tex} = 1

questions.question: Let $\omega$ be a complex number such that 2$\omega$ + 1 = z where z = $\sqrt{-3}$. If $\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -\omega^{2}-1 & \omega^{2} \\ 1 & \omega^{2} & \omega^{7} \end{array}\right|$ = 3k, then k is equal to
questions.question_raw: Let {tex}\omega{/tex} be a complex number such that 2{tex}\omega{/tex} + 1 = z where z = {tex}\sqrt{-3}{/tex}. If {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -\omega^{2}-1 & \omega^{2} \\ 1 & \omega^{2} & \omega^{7} \end{array}\right|{/tex} = 3k, then k is equal to
questions.explanation:
R1 {tex}\to{/tex} R1 + R2 + R3 gives (using {tex}\omega^{7}=\omega{/tex} and 1 + {tex}\omega+\omega^{2}{/tex} = 0),
{tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 3 & 0 & 0 \\ 1 & -\omega^{2}-1 & \omega^{2} \\ 1 & \omega^{2} & \omega \end{array}\right|{/tex} = 3 {tex}\left(-\omega^{3}-\omega-\omega^{4}\right){/tex} = 3 (-1 {tex}-\omega-\omega{/tex})
Given that 3k = 3(-1 - 2{tex}\omega{/tex}) = -3z
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} k = -z

questions.question: If ax^2 + bx + c = 0 and bx^2 + cx + a = 0 have a common root, a $\neq$ 0, then $\frac{a^{3}+b^{3}+c^{3}}{a b c}$ =
questions.question_raw: If ax2 + bx + c = 0 and bx2 + cx + a = 0 have a common root, a {tex}\neq{/tex} 0, then {tex}\frac{a^{3}+b^{3}+c^{3}}{a b c}{/tex} =
questions.explanation:
The condition for common roots gives:
(bc - a2)2 = (ca - b2)(ab - c2)
On simplification, we get a3 + b3 + c3 = 3abc
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{a^{3}+b^{3}+c^{3}}{a b c}{/tex} = 3

questions.question: Suppose z_1, z_2, z_3 are the vertices of an equilateral triangle inscribed in the circle |z| = 2. If z_1 = 1 + $i \sqrt{3}$, then values of z_3 and z_2 are respectively
questions.question_raw: Suppose z1, z2, z3 are the vertices of an equilateral triangle inscribed in the circle |z| = 2. If z1 = 1 + {tex}i \sqrt{3}{/tex}, then values of z3 and z2 are respectively
questions.explanation:
One of the numbers must be a conjugate of
z1 = 1 + {tex}i \sqrt{3}{/tex}  i.e., z2 = 1 - {tex}i \sqrt{3}{/tex}
Also, z3 = z1 {tex}e^{\frac{2 i \pi}{3}}{/tex} and z2 = z1 {tex}e^{\frac{-2 i \pi}{3}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} z3 = (1 + {tex}i \sqrt{3}{/tex}) [cos{tex}\left(\frac{2 \pi}{3}\right){/tex} + i sin{tex}\left(\frac{2 \pi}{3}\right){/tex}]
= (1 + {tex}i \sqrt{3}{/tex}) {tex}\left(\frac{-1}{2}+i \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right){/tex} = -2

questions.question: $\alpha$, $\beta$, $\gamma$ are the roots of x^3 + 7x^2 + ax + b = 0 and $\alpha$, $\beta$, $\delta$ are roots of x^3 + 5x^2 + ax + c = 0. The equation whose roots are $\gamma$ and $\delta$ is px^2 + qx + r = 0 then p + q + r is:
questions.question_raw: {tex}\alpha{/tex}, {tex}\beta{/tex}, {tex}\gamma{/tex} are the roots of x3 + 7x2 + ax + b = 0 and {tex}\alpha{/tex}, {tex}\beta{/tex}, {tex}\delta{/tex} are roots of x3 + 5x2 + ax + c = 0. The equation whose roots are {tex}\gamma{/tex} and {tex}\delta{/tex} is px2 + qx + r = 0 then p + q + r is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\alpha{/tex}, {tex}\beta{/tex}, {tex}\gamma{/tex} are the roots of x3 + 7x2 + ax + b = 0 ...(i)
and {tex}\alpha{/tex}, {tex}\beta{/tex}, {tex}\delta{/tex} are roots of x3 + 5x2 + ax + c = 0 ...(ii)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\alpha{/tex}, {tex}\beta{/tex} are roots of an equation which is a combination equation (i) and (ii) i.e.,
c1 {tex}\times{/tex} (i) + c2 {tex}\times{/tex} (ii), where c1 and c2 are some constants.
(i) - (ii) gives 2x2 + b - c = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\alpha{/tex} + {tex}\beta{/tex} = 0
Also, we have {tex}\alpha{/tex} + {tex}\beta{/tex} + {tex}\gamma{/tex} = -7
and {tex}\alpha{/tex} + {tex}\beta{/tex} + {tex}\gamma{/tex} = -5
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\gamma{/tex} = -7 and {tex}\delta{/tex} = -5
The required equation is x2 - ({tex}\gamma{/tex} + {tex}\delta{/tex}) x + {tex}\gamma \delta{/tex} = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 + 12x + 35 = 0
p = 1, q = 12, r = 35
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} p + q + r = 48

questions.question: The equation 3x^2 + 5x + 7 = 0, 5x^2 + 7x + 3 = 0 and 7x^2 + 3x + 5 = 0 have a common root $\alpha$. Then $\alpha^{2018}$ + $\alpha^{2017}$ + 1 equals:
questions.question_raw: The equation 3x2 + 5x + 7 = 0, 5x2 + 7x + 3 = 0 and 7x2 + 3x + 5 = 0 have a common root {tex}\alpha{/tex}. Then {tex}\alpha^{2018}{/tex} + {tex}\alpha^{2017}{/tex} + 1 equals:
questions.explanation:
Adding all given equations, we get
15(x2 + x + 1) = 0.
This equation is satisfied by x = {tex}\alpha{/tex}.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\alpha^2{/tex} + {tex}\alpha{/tex} + 1 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a2 = -({tex}\alpha{/tex} + 1)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\alpha^3{/tex} = -({tex}\alpha^2{/tex} + {tex}\alpha{/tex}) = -(-1)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\alpha^3{/tex} = 1
Required expression
= {tex}\left(\alpha^{3}\right)^{672} \cdot \alpha^{2}+\left(\alpha^{3}\right)^{672} \cdot \alpha+1{/tex}
= {tex}\alpha^{2}+\alpha+1{/tex} = 0

questions.question: $\sum_\limits{p=1}^{32}$ (3p + 2) $\left[\sum_\limits{q=1}^{10}\left(\sin \frac{2 q \pi}{11}-i \cos \frac{2 q \pi}{11}\right)\right]^{P}$=
questions.question_raw: {tex}\sum_\limits{p=1}^{32}{/tex} (3p + 2) {tex}\left[\sum_\limits{q=1}^{10}\left(\sin \frac{2 q \pi}{11}-i \cos \frac{2 q \pi}{11}\right)\right]^{P}{/tex}=
questions.explanation:
{tex}\sum_\limits{q=1}^{10}{/tex} (sin{tex}\frac{2 q \pi}{11}{/tex} - i cos{tex}\frac{2 q \pi}{11}{/tex})
= -{tex}\sum_\limits{q=1}^{10}{/tex} (cos{tex}\frac{2 q \pi}{11}{/tex} + i sin{tex}\frac{2 q \pi}{11}{/tex})
= -i {tex}\sum_\limits{q=1}^{10}\left(e^{i \frac{2 \pi}{11}}\right)^{q}{/tex}
= -i {tex}\sum_\limits{q=1}^{10} \alpha^{q}{/tex}, where {tex}\alpha=e^{\frac{2 \pi \pi}{11}}{/tex} = -i (-1) ... [the sum of all non real ten 11th roots of unity is -1]
= i
Required sum = {tex}\sum_\limits{p=1}^{32}{/tex} p ip + 2 {tex}\sum_\limits{p=1}^{32}{/tex} ip
= 3A + 2B,
where A = i + 2i2 + 3i3 + ... + 32i32
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Ai = i2 + 2i3 + ... + 31i32 + 32i33
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A - Ai = (i + i2 + i3 + ... + i32) - 32i33
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A(1 - i) = {tex}\frac{i\left(1-i^{32}\right)}{1-i}{/tex} - 32i ...[{tex}\because{/tex} i33 = i32 {tex}\cdot{/tex} i =(i4)8 {tex}\cdot{/tex} i = i]
{tex}=\frac{i(1-1)}{1-i}{/tex} - 32i = -32i
{tex}\therefore{/tex} A = {tex}\frac{-32 i}{1-i}=\frac{-32 i(1+i)}{2}{/tex}
= -16(i - 1) = 16(1 - i) and B = i + i2 + ... + i32 = {tex}\frac{i\left(1-i^{32}\right)}{1-i}{/tex} = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Required sum = 48(1 - i) + 0 = 48(1 - i)

questions.question: If $\frac{x-a}{x^{2}-6 x+5}$ is real for x$\in$R. then a satisfies:
questions.question_raw: If {tex}\frac{x-a}{x^{2}-6 x+5}{/tex} is real for x{tex}\in{/tex}R. then a satisfies:
questions.explanation:
Let {tex}\frac{x-a}{x^{2}-6 x+5}{/tex} = y
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} yx - a = y(x2 - 6x + 5)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2y - (6y + 1)x + 5y + a = 0
x is real {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} Discriminant D {tex}\ge{/tex} 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (6y + 1)2 - 4[y(5y + 9)] {tex}\ge{/tex} 0, for all y
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} 36y2 + 12y + 1 - [20y2 + 4ay] {tex}\ge{/tex} 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} 16y2 + (12 - 4a)y + 1 {tex}\ge{/tex} 0 for all y
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} Coefficient of y2 (leading coefficient) is positive and D {tex}\le{/tex} 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (12 - 4a)2 - 4(16) {tex}\le{/tex} 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} a2 - 6a + 5 {tex}\le{/tex} 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (a - 5) (a - 1) {tex}\le{/tex} 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} 1 {tex}\le{/tex} a {tex}\le{/tex} 5

questions.question: If $\alpha,\beta$ are roots of the equation x^2 - 4x + 8 = 0, then for any n $\in$ N, $\alpha^{2 n}+\beta^{2 n}$ =
questions.question_raw: If {tex}\alpha,\beta{/tex} are roots of the equation x2 - 4x + 8 = 0, then for any n {tex}\in{/tex} N, {tex}\alpha^{2 n}+\beta^{2 n}{/tex} =
questions.explanation:
x2 - 4x + 8 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x - 2)2 = -4
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 2 {tex}\pm{/tex} 2i
{tex}\alpha^{2 n}+\beta^{2 n}{/tex} = 22n (1 + i)2n + 22n(1 - i)2n
= 22n {tex}\cdot{/tex} 2n {tex}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}+\frac{i}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^{2 n}{/tex} + 22n {tex}\cdot{/tex} 2n {tex}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}-\frac{i}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^{2 n}{/tex}
= 23n (cos{tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} + i sin{tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex})2n + 23n (cos{tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} - i sin{tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex})2n
= 23n {tex}\cdot{/tex} 2cos{tex}\frac{n \pi}{2}{/tex} = 23n+1 cos{tex}\frac{n \pi}{2}{/tex}

questions.question: The values of sin $\frac{\pi}{14}$ sin$\frac{3 \pi}{14}$ ... sin$\frac{13 \pi}{14}$ is
questions.question_raw: The values of sin {tex}\frac{\pi}{14}{/tex} sin{tex}\frac{3 \pi}{14}{/tex} ... sin{tex}\frac{13 \pi}{14}{/tex} is
questions.explanation:
Let P = sin{tex}\frac{\pi}{14}{/tex} sin{tex}\frac{3\pi}{14}{/tex} ... sin{tex}\frac{3\pi}{14}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\sin \frac{\pi}{14} \sin \frac{2 \pi}{14} \sin \frac{3 \pi}{14} \sin \frac{4 \pi}{14} \ldots \sin \frac{12 \pi}{14} \sin \frac{13 \pi}{14}}{\sin \frac{2 \pi}{14} \sin \frac{4 \pi}{14} \ldots \sin \frac{12 \pi}{14}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\sin \frac{\pi}{14} \sin \frac{2 \pi}{14} \sin \frac{3 \pi}{14} \ldots \sin \frac{13 \pi}{14}}{\sin \frac{\pi}{7} \sin \frac{2 \pi}{7} \ldots \sin \frac{6 \pi}{7}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\frac{14}{2^{14-1}}}{\frac{7}{2^{7-1}}}=\frac{2^{6}}{2^{13}} \times{/tex} 2 = {tex}\frac{1}{2^{6}}{/tex}

questions.question: The value of b for which the quadratic expression x^2 - 2bx + 7 has a minimum value of 6 is:
questions.question_raw: The value of b for which the quadratic expression x2 - 2bx + 7 has a minimum value of 6 is:
questions.explanation:
Given expression = (x - b)2 + (7 - b2) {tex}\ge{/tex} 7 - b2
Least value of the expression = 7 - b2 = 6
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} b2 = 1
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} b = {tex}\pm 1{/tex}

questions.question: Let $\alpha_{1}, \alpha_{2}, \ldots, \alpha_{6}$ be non-real 7^th roots of unity. Then Im$\left(\alpha_{1}\right)$ + Im$\left(\alpha_{2}\right)$ + Im$\left(\alpha_{4}\right)$ is
questions.question_raw: Let {tex}\alpha_{1}, \alpha_{2}, \ldots, \alpha_{6}{/tex} be non-real 7th roots of unity. Then Im{tex}\left(\alpha_{1}\right){/tex} + Im{tex}\left(\alpha_{2}\right){/tex} + Im{tex}\left(\alpha_{4}\right){/tex} is
questions.explanation:
{tex}\alpha_{1}, \alpha_{2}, \ldots, \alpha_{6}{/tex} are cos{tex}\frac{2 k \pi}{7}{/tex} + i sin {tex}\frac{2 k \pi}{7}{/tex} where k = 1, 2, ..., 6
Let z1 = {tex}\alpha_{1}+\alpha_{2}+\alpha_{4}{/tex} and z2 = {tex}\alpha_{3}+\alpha_{5}+\alpha_{6}{/tex}
{tex}=\alpha_{1}+\alpha_{1}^{2}+\alpha_{1}^{4}{/tex} {tex}=\alpha_{1}^{3}+\alpha_{1}^{5}+\alpha_{1}^{6}{/tex}
z1 + z2 = -1 = {tex}\alpha_{1}+\alpha_{1}^{2}+\alpha_{1}^{3}+\alpha_{1}^{4}+\alpha_{1}^{5}+\alpha_{1}^{6}{/tex} and z1z2 = {tex}\left(\alpha_{1}+\alpha_{1}^{2}+\alpha_{1}^{4}\right)\left(\alpha_{1}^{3}+\alpha_{1}^{5}+\alpha_{1}^{6}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\alpha_{1}^{4}+\alpha_{1}^{6}+\alpha_{1}^{7}+\alpha_{1}^{5}+\alpha_{1}^{7}+\alpha_{1}^{8}+\alpha_{1}^7+\alpha_{1}^{9}+\alpha_{1}^{10}{/tex}
{tex}\alpha_{1}^{7}{/tex} = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} z1z2 = 3 + {tex}\alpha_{1}^{4}+\alpha_{1}^{6}+\alpha_{1}^{5}+\alpha_{1}+\alpha_{1}^{2}+\alpha_{1}^{3}{/tex}
= 3 - 1 = 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} z1, z2 are roots of z2 + z + 2= 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} z1, z2 = {tex}\frac{-1 \pm \sqrt{1-8}}{2}=\frac{-1 \pm \sqrt{7} i}{2}{/tex}
Im(z1) = sin{tex}\frac{2\pi}{7}{/tex} + sin {tex}\frac{4\pi}{7}{/tex} + sin{tex}\frac{8\pi}{7}{/tex}
=(sin{tex}\frac{2 \pi}{7}{/tex} - sin{tex}\frac{ \pi}{7}{/tex}) + sin{tex}\frac{4\pi}{7}{/tex} > 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Im {tex}\alpha_{1}{/tex} + Im {tex}\alpha_{2}{/tex} + Im {tex}\alpha_{4}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{7}}{2}{/tex} as Im (z1) > 0

questions.question: For what value of p, the curve x = y^2 + py + 36 touches the y axis?
questions.question_raw: For what value of p, the curve x = y2 + py + 36 touches the y axis?
questions.explanation:
If x = y2 + py + 36 represents a parabola, that touches the y-axis then x = (y {tex}\pm6{/tex})2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} The values of p should be {tex}\pm12{/tex}

questions.question: The graph of quadratic expression y = ax^2 + bx + c has the vertex at (-1, 5) and the point (- 2, 3) lies on it. The expression is:
questions.question_raw: The graph of quadratic expression y = ax2 + bx + c has the vertex at (-1, 5) and the point (- 2, 3) lies on it. The expression is:
questions.explanation:
For any expression ax2 + bx + c; a, b, c{tex}\in{/tex}R and a {tex}\neq{/tex} 0, the graph represents a parabola with vertex at 
{tex}\left(\frac{-b}{2 a}, \frac{4 a c-b^{2}}{4 a}\right){/tex}
Here {tex}\left(\frac{-b}{2 a}, \frac{4 a c-b^{2}}{4 a}\right){/tex} = (-1, 5)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{-b}{2 a}{/tex} = -1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b = 2a
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{4 a c-b^{2}}{4 a}=5 \Rightarrow \frac{4 a c-4 a^{2}}{4 a}=5{/tex} 
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} c- a = 5 {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} c = 5 + a
(-2, 3) lies on y = ax2 + bx + c
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a(-2)2 + b(-2) + c = 3
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} 4a - 2(2a) + 5 + a = 3
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} a = -2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 8 = - 4 and c = 3
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Required expression f(x) = -2x3 - 4x + 3

questions.question: The solution set of the equation ix^2 - x + 12i = 0 is
questions.question_raw: The solution set of the equation ix2 - x + 12i = 0 is
questions.explanation:
x = {tex}\frac{-(-1) \pm \sqrt{(-1)^{2}-4 . i .(12 i)}}{2 i}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{1 \pm 7}{2 i}=\frac{4}{i},-\frac{3}{i}{/tex} = -4i, 3i

questions.question: x$\in$R. The least value of the expression x(x + 1) (x + 2) (x + 3) is:
questions.question_raw: x{tex}\in{/tex}R. The least value of the expression x(x + 1) (x + 2) (x + 3) is:
questions.explanation:
Given expression = x(x + 3) (x + 1) (x + 2)
= (x2 + 3x) (x2 + 3x + 2)
= (x2 + 3x)2 + 2(x2 + 3)
Add and subtract 1 = (x2 + 3x + 1)2 - 1 {tex}\ge{/tex} -1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Least value of the expression = -1

questions.question: If $\sum_\limits{n=0}^{\infty}\left(\frac{2 i}{3}\right)^{n}$ = a + ib, then 13 (a + b)
questions.question_raw: If {tex}\sum_\limits{n=0}^{\infty}\left(\frac{2 i}{3}\right)^{n}{/tex} = a + ib, then 13 (a + b)
questions.explanation:
{tex}\sum_\limits{n=0}^{\infty}\left(\frac{2 i}{3}\right)^{n}{/tex} = 1 + {tex}\frac{2 i}{3}+\left(\frac{2 i}{3}\right)^{2}{/tex} + ... infinite terms
{tex}=\frac{1}{1-\frac{2 i}{3}}=\frac{3}{3-2 i} \times \frac{3+2 i}{3+2 i}=\frac{9+6 i}{13}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} a = {tex}\frac{9}{13}{/tex}; b = {tex}\frac{6}{13}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 13(a + b) = 15

questions.question: 1 + (1 + i) + (1 + i)^2 + (1 + i)^3 + ... + upto 100 terms is
questions.question_raw: 1 + (1 + i) + (1 + i)2 + (1 + i)3 + ... + upto 100 terms is
questions.explanation:
Sum of 100 terms in G.P
{tex}\frac{1\left[(1+i)^{100}-1\right]}{1+i-1}=\frac{\left\{(1+i)^{2}\right\}^{50}-1}{i}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{(2 i)^{50}-1}{i}{/tex}
= 250 (i)49 + i = i(250 + 1) is purely imaginary.

questions.question: If $z_1=2+3 i$ and $z_2=5+2 i$, then find $z_1-z_2$.
questions.question_raw: If {tex}z_1=2+3 i{/tex} and {tex}z_2=5+2 i{/tex}, then find {tex}z_1-z_2{/tex}.
questions.explanation:
{tex}-3+1 i{/tex}
In subtracting one complex number from other, difference of corresponding parts of two complex numbers is calculated. So, {tex}z_1-z_2=(2-5)+(3-2) i=-3+1 i{/tex} .

questions.question: If z = x + iy, u = $\frac{z+2 i}{z-3}$ then the path of (x, y), when u is purely imaginary, is
questions.question_raw: If z = x + iy, u = {tex}\frac{z+2 i}{z-3}{/tex} then the path of (x, y), when u is purely imaginary, is
questions.explanation:
u = {tex}\frac{x+i y+2 i}{x+i y-3}=\frac{x+i(y+2)}{(x-3)+i y} \times \frac{(x-3)-i y}{(x-3)-i y}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{x(x-3)+y(y+2)+i[(y+2)(x-3)-x y]}{(x-3)^{2}+y^{2}}{/tex}
u is purely imaginary
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{x(x-3)+y(y+2)}{(x-3)^{2}+y^{2}}{/tex} = 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} x2 + y2 - 3x + 2y = 0 which is equation of a circle.

questions.question: For real x, the least value of the expression $\frac{x^{2}-6 x+5}{x^{2}+2 x+1}$, x $\neq$ -1, is:
questions.question_raw: For real x, the least value of the expression {tex}\frac{x^{2}-6 x+5}{x^{2}+2 x+1}{/tex}, x {tex}\neq{/tex} -1, is:
questions.explanation:
Let y = {tex}\frac{x^{2}-6 x+5}{x^{2}+2 x+1}{/tex}
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (1 - y)x2 - (6 + 2y) x + 5 - y = 0
Since x is real, therefore
(6+2y)2 - 4(1 - y)(5 - y) {tex}\ge{/tex} 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} 9 + y2 + 6y - (y2 - 6y + 5) {tex}\ge{/tex} 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} 12y + 4 {tex}\ge{/tex} 0 {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} y {tex}\ge{/tex} {tex}-\frac{1}{3}{/tex}

questions.question: $1 / {z}$ is ________ for complex number z.
questions.question_raw: {tex}1 / {z}{/tex} is ________ for complex number z.
questions.explanation:
multiplicative inverse
On multiplying reciprocal of complex number (1/z) to complex number {tex}z{/tex}, we get multiplying inverse one i.e. {tex}z* 1=z{/tex}.

questions.question: $-z$ is _________ for complex number $z$.
questions.question_raw: {tex}-z{/tex} is _________ for complex number {tex}z{/tex}.
questions.explanation:
additive inverse
On adding negative of complex number (-z) to complex number {tex}z{/tex}, we get additive identity element zero i.e. {tex}z+(-z)=0{/tex}.

questions.question: Let p, q, r be the roots of the equation az^3 + bz^2 + cz + d = 0. Then in terms of the coefficients a, b, c, d, the expression (4 + p^2) (4 + q^2) (4 + r^2), is
questions.question_raw: Let p, q, r be the roots of the equation az3 + bz2 + cz + d = 0. Then in terms of the coefficients a, b, c, d, the expression (4 + p2) (4 + q2) (4 + r2), is
questions.explanation:
p, q, r are roots of the equation az3 + bz2 + cz + d = 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} az3 + bz2 + cz + d = (z - p) (z - q) (z - r) for all z ...(i)
Substitute z = 2i in (i) to get
-8ai - 4b + 2ci + d = (2i - p) (2i - q) (2i - r)
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (d - 4b) + 2i(c - 4a) = (2i - p) (2i - q) (2i - r) ...(ii)
Substitute z = -2i in (i) to get
8ai - 4b - 2ci + d = (-2i - p) (-2i - q) (-2i - r)
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (d - 4b) - 2i(c - 4a) = (-2i - p) (-2i - q) (-2i - r) ...(iii)
Multiply (ii) and (iii) to get
(d - 4b)2 - (2i)2 (c - 4a)2
= [(-p)2 - (2i)2] [(-q)2 - (2i)2] [(-r)2 - (2i)2]
= (p2 + 4) (q2 + 4) (r2 + 4)
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (4 + p2) (4 + q2) (4 + r2) = (d - 4b)2 + 4(c - 4a)2

questions.question: If $\theta$ is real and z_1, z_2 are connected by $z_{1}^{2}+z_{2}^{2}$ + 2z_1z_2 cos$\theta$ = 0, then triangle with vertices 0, z_1, z_2 is
questions.question_raw: If {tex}\theta{/tex} is real and z1, z2 are connected by {tex}z_{1}^{2}+z_{2}^{2}{/tex} + 2z1z2 cos{tex}\theta{/tex} = 0, then triangle with vertices 0, z1, z2 is
questions.explanation:
{tex}z_{1}^{2}+z_{2}^{2}{/tex} + 2z1z2 cos{tex}\theta{/tex} = 0
{tex}\left(\frac{z_{1}}{z_{2}}\right)^{2}{/tex} + 2{tex}\left(\frac{z_{1}}{z_{2}}\right){/tex} cos{tex}\theta{/tex} + 1 = 0
{tex}\therefore \frac{z_{1}}{z_{2}}=\frac{2 \cos \theta \pm \sqrt{4 \cos ^{2} \theta-4}}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{z_{1}}{z_{2}}{/tex} = cos{tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\pm{/tex} i sin{tex}\theta{/tex}
{tex}\frac{\left|z_{1}\right|}{\left|z_{2}\right|}{/tex} = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |z1| = |z2|
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} The triangle is an isosceles triangle.

questions.question: The number of solutions of the equation $\sqrt{2}$ |z + 1| = z - i is (where z = x + iy)
questions.question_raw: The number of solutions of the equation {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} |z + 1| = z - i is (where z = x + iy)
questions.explanation:
{tex}\sqrt{2} \sqrt{(x+1)^{2}+y^{2}}{/tex} = x + i(y - 1) ...[L.H.S. {tex}\geq{/tex} 0]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y - 1 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 1
{tex}\sqrt{2} \sqrt{(x+1)^{2}+y^{2}}{/tex} = x ...(i)
y = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt{2} \sqrt{(x+1)^{2}+1^{2}}{/tex} = x
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 + 4x + 4 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} R.H.S. is negative {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} No solution exists.

questions.question: If |z_1 - 1| < 2; |z_2 - 2| < 1 then |z_1 + z_2| is
questions.question_raw: If |z1 - 1| < 2; |z2 - 2| < 1 then |z1 + z2| is
questions.explanation:
|z1 + z2| = |z1 - 1 + z2 - 2 + 3| {tex}\leq{/tex} |z1 - 1| + |z2 - 2| + 3 < 2 + 1 + 3
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} |z1 + z2| < 6

questions.question: If $z_1=2+3 i$ and $z_2=5+2 i$, then find sum of two complex numbers.
questions.question_raw: If {tex}z_1=2+3 i{/tex} and {tex}z_2=5+2 i{/tex}, then find sum of two complex numbers.
questions.explanation:
In addition of two complex numbers, corresponding parts of two complex numbers are added i.e. real parts of both are added and imaginary parts of both are added.
So, sum {tex}=(2+5)+(3+2) i=7+5 i{/tex}.

questions.question: If for the complex numbers z_1 and z_2 |1 - $\bar{z}_1$z_2|^2 - |z_1 - z_2|^2 = k (1 - |z_1|^2) (1 - |z_2|^2), then k is equal to
questions.question_raw: If for the complex numbers z1 and z2 |1 - {tex}\bar{z}_1{/tex}z2|2 - |z1 - z2|2 = k (1 - |z1|2) (1 - |z2|2), then k is equal to
questions.explanation:
|1 - {tex}\bar{z}_1{/tex} z2|2 - |z1 - z2|2
L.H.S. = (1 - {tex}\bar{z}_1{/tex} z2) (1 - z1 {tex}\bar{z}_2{/tex}) - (z1 - z2) {tex}\left(\bar{z}_{1}-\bar{z}_{2}\right){/tex}
= 1 - {tex}\bar{z}_1{/tex} z2 - z1 {tex}\bar{z}_2{/tex} + |z1|2 |z2|2 - (|z1|2 + |z2|2 - z2 {tex}\bar{z}_1{/tex} - z1 {tex}\bar{z}_2{/tex})
= 1 + |z1|2 |z2|2 - |z1|2 - |z2|2
= (1 - |z1|2) (1 - |z2|2)
R.H.S. = k(1 - |z1|2) (1 - |z2|2) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} k = 1

questions.question: If ${p}({x}, {y})$ denotes ${z}={x}+{iy}$ in Argand plane and $\left|\frac{z-1}{z+2 i}\right|=1$ the locus of P is ${a} / {an}$
questions.question_raw: If {tex}{p}({x}, {y}){/tex} denotes {tex}{z}={x}+{iy}{/tex} in Argand plane and {tex}\left|\frac{z-1}{z+2 i}\right|=1{/tex} the locus of P is {tex}{a} / {an}{/tex}
questions.explanation: Straight line

questions.question: For a complex number z, $\log _{\frac{1}{3}}\left[\frac{3+|z-1|}{(1-|z-1|)(|z-1|-2)}\right]$ < 1 then z can be
questions.question_raw: For a complex number z, {tex}\log _{\frac{1}{3}}\left[\frac{3+|z-1|}{(1-|z-1|)(|z-1|-2)}\right]{/tex} < 1 then z can be
questions.explanation:
Let |z - 1| = t then
{tex}\log _{\frac{1}{3}}\left[\frac{3+t}{(1-t)(t-2)}\right]{/tex} < 1
{tex}\frac{3+t}{(1-t)(t-2)}>\frac{1}{3}{/tex}
{tex}\Leftrightarrow \frac{3+t}{(t-1)(t-2)}+\frac{1}{3}{/tex} < 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow \frac{9+3 t+t^{2}-3 t+2}{(t-1)(t-2)}{/tex} < 0 {tex}\Leftrightarrow \frac{t^{2}+11}{(t-1)(t-2)}{/tex} < 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (t - 1) (t - 2) < 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} 1 < t < 2 {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} 1 < |z - 1| < 2
The only complex number which lies in
1 < |z - 1| < 2 is 2 - i

questions.question: A sequence of complex numbers is recursively given as z_1 = 0, z_n+1 - $z_{n}^{2}$ + i = 0. Then [|z_2017|], where [x] stands for greatest integer involved in x, is
questions.question_raw: A sequence of complex numbers is recursively given as z1 = 0, zn+1 - {tex}z_{n}^{2}{/tex} + i = 0. Then [|z2017|], where [x] stands for greatest integer involved in x, is
questions.explanation:
z1 = 0, z2 = {tex}z_{1}^{2}{/tex} - i = -i, z3 = {tex}z_{2}^{2}{/tex} - i = - 1 - i,
z4 = {tex}z_{3}^{2}{/tex} - i = i, z5 = {tex}z_{4}^{2}{/tex} - i = - i - 1,
z6 = {tex}z_{5}^{2}{/tex} - i = -i
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} z2017 = - i - 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |z2017| = {tex}\sqrt{2} \Rightarrow{/tex} [|z2017|] = 1

questions.question: t is real and t^2 < 3. A complex number z is such that z = 2 + t + $i \sqrt{3-t^{2}}$. Then $\left|\frac{z+1}{z-1}\right|$ is
questions.question_raw: t is real and t2 < 3. A complex number z is such that z = 2 + t + {tex}i \sqrt{3-t^{2}}{/tex}. Then {tex}\left|\frac{z+1}{z-1}\right|{/tex} is
questions.explanation:
z + 1 = 3 + t + {tex}i \sqrt{3-t^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |z + 1| = {tex}\sqrt{(3+t)^{2}+3-t^{2}}=\sqrt{12+6 t}{/tex} and
|z - 1| = {tex}\sqrt{(1+t)^{2}+3-t^{2}}=\sqrt{4+2 t}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow\left|\frac{z+1}{z-1}\right|=\sqrt{3}{/tex}

questions.question: A complex number satisfies $\left|z^{3}+\frac{1}{z^{3}}\right| \leq$ 2. The maximum value of $\left|z+\frac{1}{z}\right|$ is
questions.question_raw: A complex number satisfies {tex}\left|z^{3}+\frac{1}{z^{3}}\right| \leq{/tex} 2. The maximum value of {tex}\left|z+\frac{1}{z}\right|{/tex} is
questions.explanation:
{tex}\left|z^{3}+\frac{1}{z^{3}}\right|=\left|\left(z+\frac{1}{z}\right)^{3}-3\left(z+\frac{1}{z}\right)\right|\geq\left|z+\frac{1}{z}\right|^{3}-3\left|z+\frac{1}{z}\right|{/tex}
Let {tex}\left|z+\frac{1}{z}\right|{/tex} = r so that
2 {tex}\geq{/tex} r3 - 3r {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} r3 - 3r - 2 {tex}\leq{/tex} 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (r + 1) (r2 - r - 2) {tex}\leq{/tex} 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (r + 1)2 (r - 2) {tex}\leq{/tex} 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} r - 2 {tex}\leq{/tex} 0 {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} r {tex}\leq{/tex} 2
Equality occurs when z = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} MAximum value of {tex}\left|z+\frac{1}{z}\right|{/tex} = 2

questions.question: If a, b, $\alpha, \beta$ are real and (a + bi)^9 = $\alpha+i \beta$ then (b + ia)^9 =
questions.question_raw: If a, b, {tex}\alpha, \beta{/tex} are real and (a + bi)9 = {tex}\alpha+i \beta{/tex} then (b + ia)9 =
questions.explanation:
(a + bi)9 = {tex}\alpha+i \beta \Rightarrow i(\alpha+i \beta){/tex} = i(a + bi)9
{tex}\Leftrightarrow i \alpha-\beta{/tex} = (ia - b)9 ...[i = i9]
Take conjugate
{tex}-i \alpha-\beta{/tex} = (-ia - b)9 = -(ia + b)9
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (b + ia)9 = {tex}i \alpha+\beta{/tex}

questions.question: (x+3) + i(y-2) = 5+i2, find the values of x and y.
questions.question_raw: (x+3) + i(y-2) = 5+i2, find the values of x and y.
questions.explanation:
If two complex numbers are equal, then corresponding parts are equal i.e. real parts of both are equal and imaginary parts of both are equal.{tex} x+3=5 \text { and } y-2=2 {/tex}
{tex} x=5-3 \text { and } y=2+2 {/tex}
{tex} x=2 \text { and } y=4 . {/tex}

questions.question: z_1 and z_2 are non-zero complex numbers such that z_1 = -z_2 and $\frac{z_{1}+z_{2}}{z_{1}}=\frac{z_{2}}{z_{1}+z_{2}}$. Then which of the following is NOT correct?
questions.question_raw: z1 and z2 are non-zero complex numbers such that z1 = -z2 and {tex}\frac{z_{1}+z_{2}}{z_{1}}=\frac{z_{2}}{z_{1}+z_{2}}{/tex}. Then which of the following is NOT correct?
questions.explanation:
{tex}\frac{z_{1}+z_{2}}{z_{1}}=\frac{z_{2}}{z_{1}+z_{2}} \Leftrightarrow{/tex} 1 + {tex}\frac{z_{2}}{z_{1}}=\frac{1}{\frac{z_{1}}{z_{2}}+1}{/tex}

questions.question: If x = $e^{i \theta}$, y = $e^{\mathrm{i} \phi}$, then $\frac{x-y}{x+y}$ =
questions.question_raw: If x = {tex}e^{i \theta}{/tex}, y = {tex}e^{\mathrm{i} \phi}{/tex}, then {tex}\frac{x-y}{x+y}{/tex} =
questions.explanation:
{tex}\frac{x-y}{x+y}=\frac{e^{i \theta}-e^{i \phi}}{e^{1 \theta}+e^{i \phi}}=\frac{\cos \theta+i \sin \theta-\cos \phi-i \sin \phi}{\cos \theta+i \sin \theta+\cos \phi+i \sin \phi}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{-2 \sin \left(\frac{\theta+\phi}{2}\right) \sin \left(\frac{\theta-\phi}{2}\right)+i 2 \sin \left(\frac{\theta-\phi}{2}\right) \cos \left(\frac{\theta+\phi}{2}\right)}{2 \cos \left(\frac{\theta+\phi}{2}\right) \cos \left(\frac{\theta-\phi}{2}\right)+i 2 \sin \left(\frac{\theta+\phi}{2}\right) \cos \left(\frac{\theta+\phi}{2}\right)}{/tex}
= tan {tex}\left(\frac{\theta-\phi}{2}\right)\left[\frac{-\sin \left(\frac{\theta+\phi}{2}\right)+i \cos \left(\frac{\theta+\phi}{2}\right)}{\cos \left(\frac{\theta+\phi}{2}\right)+i \sin \left(\frac{\theta+\phi}{2}\right)}\right]{/tex}
= i tan {tex}\left(\frac{\theta-\phi}{2}\right){/tex}

questions.question: A complex number z satisfies $\left|z+\frac{1}{z}\right|$ = 2. If z is expressed as z = $r e^{i \theta}$ and r_1 and r_2 are two value of r for a given $\theta$, then for $\theta=\frac{\pi}{6}$, r_1 + r_2 is
questions.question_raw: A complex number z satisfies {tex}\left|z+\frac{1}{z}\right|{/tex} = 2. If z is expressed as z = {tex}r e^{i \theta}{/tex} and r1 and r2 are two value of r for a given {tex}\theta{/tex}, then for {tex}\theta=\frac{\pi}{6}{/tex}, r1 + r2 is
questions.explanation:
z + {tex}\frac{1}{z}{/tex} = 2 {tex}\Rightarrow\left|z+\frac{1}{z}\right|=\left|r e^{i \theta}+\frac{1}{r} e^{-i \theta}\right|{/tex} = 2
Squaring {tex}\Rightarrow\left(r+\frac{1}{r}\right)^{2}{/tex} cos2{tex}\theta{/tex} + {tex}\left(r-\frac{1}{r}\right)^{2}{/tex} sin2{tex}\theta{/tex} = 4
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} r2 + {tex}\frac{1}{r^{2}}{/tex} - 2 cos2{tex}\theta{/tex} = 4
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} r4 - (4 - 2 cos2{tex}\theta{/tex}) r2 + 1 = 0
(This is a quadratic equation in r2 having roots {tex}r_{1}^{2}{/tex} and {tex}r_{2}^{2}{/tex})
{tex}\Rightarrow r_{1}^{2}+r_{2}^{2}{/tex} = 4 - 2 cos2{tex}\theta{/tex} and (r1r2)2 = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (r1 + r2)2 = 4 - 2 cos2{tex}\theta{/tex} + 2 = 6 - 2 cos2{tex}\theta{/tex}
At {tex}\theta=\frac{\pi}{6}{/tex}, (r1 + r2)2 = 6 - 2 {tex}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right){/tex} = 5

questions.question: If $\omega$ ($\ne$ 1) is a cube root of unity, then $\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 1+i+\omega^{2} & \omega^{2} \\ 1-i & -1 & \omega^{2}-1 \\ -i & -i+\omega-1 & -1 \end{array}\right|$ is equal to

Let {tex}\Delta=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 1+i+\omega^{2} & \omega^{2} \\ 1-i & -1 & \omega^{2}-1 \\ -i & -i+\omega-1 & -1 \end{array}\right|{/tex}
Applying R1 {tex}\to{/tex} R1 + R3, we get
{tex}\Delta=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 1-i & -1 & \omega^{2}-1 \\ 1-i & -1 & \omega^{2}-1 \\ -i & -i+\omega-1 & -1 \end{array}\right|{/tex}
= 0 ...[{tex}\because{/tex} R1 {tex}\equiv{/tex} R2]

questions.question: The value of the expression 1(2 - $\omega$) (2 - $\omega^2$) + 2(3 - $\omega$) (3 - $\omega^2$) + ... + (n - 1) (n - $\omega$) (n - $\omega^2$), where $\omega$ is an imaginary cube root of unity, is
questions.question_raw: The value of the expression 1(2 - {tex}\omega{/tex}) (2 - {tex}\omega^2{/tex}) + 2(3 - {tex}\omega{/tex}) (3 - {tex}\omega^2{/tex}) + ... + (n - 1) (n - {tex}\omega{/tex}) (n - {tex}\omega^2{/tex}), where {tex}\omega{/tex} is an imaginary cube root of unity, is
questions.explanation:
rth term of the given series
= r[(r + 1) - {tex}\omega{/tex}] [(r + 1) - {tex}\omega^2{/tex}]
= r[(r + 1)2 - {tex}\left(\omega+\omega^{2}\right){/tex} (r + 1) + {tex}\omega^3{/tex}]
= r[(r + 1)2 - (-1) (r + 1) + 1]
= r(r2 + 3r + 3)
= r3 + 3r2 + 3r
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Sum of the given series = {tex}\sum_\limits{r=1}^{n-1}{/tex} (r3 + 3r2 + 3r)
= {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex}(n - 1)2 n2 + 3{tex}\cdot{/tex}{tex}\frac{1}{6}{/tex} (n - 1) (n) (2n - 1) + 3{tex}\cdot{/tex}{tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} (n - 1) n
= {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} (n - 1) n(n2 + 3n + 4)

questions.question: The value of sin$\frac{\pi}{18}$ sin$\frac{5\pi}{18}$ sin$\frac{7\pi}{18}$ is
questions.question_raw: The value of sin{tex}\frac{\pi}{18}{/tex} sin{tex}\frac{5\pi}{18}{/tex} sin{tex}\frac{7\pi}{18}{/tex} is

{tex}=\frac{\sin \frac{\pi}{18} \sin \frac{2 \pi}{18} \sin \frac{3 \pi}{18} \sin \frac{4 \pi}{18} \ldots \sin \frac{17 \pi}{18}}{(\mathrm{~A})(\mathrm{B})(\mathrm{C})(\mathrm{D})}{/tex}
where,
A = sin{tex}\frac{2\pi}{18}{/tex} sin{tex}\frac{3\pi}{18}{/tex} sin{tex}\frac{4\pi}{18}{/tex} sin{tex}\frac{6\pi}{18}{/tex}

D = sin{tex}\frac{15\pi}{18}{/tex} sin{tex}\frac{16\pi}{18}{/tex} sin{tex}\frac{17\pi}{18}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} P = {tex}\frac{\sin \frac{\pi}{18} \cdot \sin \frac{2 \pi}{18} \sin \frac{3 \pi}{18} \ldots \sin \frac{17 \pi}{18}}{\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2} \cdot \mathrm{P} \cdot \sin \frac{\pi}{9} \sin \frac{2 \pi}{9} \ldots \sin \frac{8 \pi}{9}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} P2 = {tex}\frac{4\left(\frac{18}{2^{17}}\right)}{\left(\frac{9}{2^{8}}\right)}=\frac{1}{2^{6}} \Rightarrow{/tex} P = {tex}\frac{1}{8}{/tex}

questions.question: If the roots of z^3 + iz^2 + 2i = 0 represent the vertices of a $\triangle$ABC in the Argand plane, then the area of the triangle is

z3 + iz2 + 2i = 0
We observe that z = i satisfies the equation
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} z - i is a factor of f(z) = z3 + iz2 + 2i
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(z) = (z - i) (z2 + 2iz - 2)
= (z - i) [(z + i)2 - 1]
= (z - i) (z + i + 1) (z + i - 1)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} The vertices are i, -1 -i, -i + 1
Area = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} (2) (2)
= 2 sq. units

ABCD parallelogram, diagonals bisect each other at M. Let B represent x + iy. Then
2 - i = {tex}\frac{1+i+x+i y}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} B represents 3 - 3i
Similarly, C represents 1 - {tex}\frac{3i}{2}{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} BD = 2AC, as |BD| = 2{tex}\sqrt{5}{/tex} and |A| = {tex}\sqrt{5}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} The diagonals are not equal
AB = |(3 - {tex}\frac{i}{2}{/tex}) - (3 - 3i)| = {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex}
AD = |3 - {tex}\frac{i}{2}{/tex} - (1 + i)| = {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Adjacent sides are equal
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} The parallelogram is a rhombus.

questions.question: Let z and w be two complex numbers. If |z| = 3 and w = $\frac{2 z+2}{z-1}$, z $\neq$ 1 and point P represents w. Then
questions.question_raw: Let z and w be two complex numbers. If |z| = 3 and w = {tex}\frac{2 z+2}{z-1}{/tex}, z {tex}\neq{/tex} 1 and point P represents w. Then

options.text: point P moves on a line parallel to imaginary axis
point P moves on a circle
point P moves on the imaginary axis
point P moves on a line parallel to the real axis

questions.explanation:
w = {tex}\frac{2 z+2}{z-1} \Leftrightarrow{/tex} (w - 2)z = 2 + w
{tex}\Leftrightarrow z=\frac{2+w}{w-2}{/tex}
|z| = 3 {tex}\Leftrightarrow\left|\frac{2+w}{w-2}\right|=\frac{3}{1}{/tex}
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} P moves on a circle on MN as a diameter.
Reason:

  1. PM is the internal angle bisector of {tex}\angle{/tex}P of {tex}\triangle{/tex}PAB where A and B represent complex numbers z = -2 and z = 2 respectively. PM divides AB internally in the ratio of PA : PB i.e., {tex}\frac{|w+2|}{|w-2|}=\frac{3}{1}{/tex}
  2. PN is the external angle bisector of {tex}\angle{/tex}P of {tex}\triangle{/tex}PAB, PN divides AB externally in the ratio {tex}\frac{\mathrm{AN}}{\mathrm{BN}}{/tex} i.e., 3 : 1
  3. The angle between PM and PN is a right angle {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} P moves in such a way that MN subtends a right angle at P i.e., P moves on a circle, with MN as a diameter.

A = {z : z = $\frac{t}{t+1}+i\left(\frac{1+t}{t}\right)$, t $\in$ R, t $\neq$ 0, -1} and B = {z : z = $\sqrt{2}$ cos (sin^-1t) + i cos (cos^-1t), 0 $\leq$ t $\leq$ 1}

Eliminating t, xy = 1 (a rectangular hyperbola)

say, then x = {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} cos (sin-1t)
= {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} cos [cos-1{tex}\left(\sqrt{1-t^{2}}\right){/tex}]
x = {tex}\sqrt{2\left(1-t^{2}\right)}{/tex} ...(i)
and y = {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex}t ...(ii)
Eliminating t, x2 + y2 = 2 (a circle)
The number of common elements = The number of points of intersection
= 2

{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} z4 = -4 + 3i
Vector OC represents i + (-1 + 3i) i.e., -1 + 4i = z3
|z3|2 + |z4|2 = 1 + 16 + 16 + 9 = 42

questions.question: a, b, c and d are distinct complex numbers. Given that $\frac{a-d}{b-c}$ and $\frac{b-d}{c-a}$ are purely imaginary. Then $\frac{c-d}{a-b}$ is
questions.question_raw: a, b, c and d are distinct complex numbers. Given that {tex}\frac{a-d}{b-c}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{b-d}{c-a}{/tex} are purely imaginary. Then {tex}\frac{c-d}{a-b}{/tex} is

options.text: x + iy where x and y are integers
x + iy, where x and y are real numbers

questions.explanation:
Let points A, B, C, D represent complex numbers a, b, c, d. Then {tex}\frac{a-d}{b-c}{/tex} is purely imaginary
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} arg {tex}\left(\frac{a-d}{b-c}\right)=\pm \frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AD {tex}\perp{/tex} BC
Similarly, BD {tex}\perp{/tex} AC
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} D is the orthocentre of {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} CD {tex}\perp{/tex} AB {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} arg {tex}\left(\frac{c-d}{a-b}\right)=\pm \frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{c-d}{a-b}{/tex} is purely imaginary.

questions.question: z_1, z_2 and z_3 are non zero and distinct. If |z_1| = a, |z_2| = b, |z_3| = c and $\left|\begin{array}{lll} a & b & c \\ c & a & b \\ b & c & a \end{array}\right|$ = 0 then arg $\left(\frac{z_{j}}{z_{2}}\right)$ is
questions.question_raw: z1, z2 and z3 are non zero and distinct. If |z1| = a, |z2| = b, |z3| = c and {tex}\left|\begin{array}{lll} a & b & c \\ c & a & b \\ b & c & a \end{array}\right|{/tex} = 0 then arg {tex}\left(\frac{z_{j}}{z_{2}}\right){/tex} is
questions.explanation:
{tex}\left|\begin{array}{lll} a & b & c \\ c & a & b \\ b & c & a \end{array}\right|{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} a3 + b3 + c3 - 3abc = 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (a + b + c) (a2 + b2 + c2) - ab - bc - ca) = 0
a, b , c positive
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a2 + b2 + c2 -ab - bc - ca = 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow \frac{1}{2}{/tex} [(a - b)2 + (b - c)2 + (c - a)2] = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = b = c
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} z1, z2, z3 lie on the circle having radius = a = b = c and centre at the origin
If {tex}\angle{/tex}BOC = {tex}\alpha{/tex} and {tex}\angle{/tex}BAC = {tex}\beta \Rightarrow \alpha{/tex} = 2{tex}\beta{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} arg{tex}\left(\frac{z_{3}}{z_{2}}\right){/tex} = 2 arg{tex}\left(\frac{z_{3}-z_{1}}{z_{2}-z_{1}}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} arg {tex}\left(\frac{z_{3}}{z_{2}}\right){/tex} = arg {tex}\left(\frac{z_{3}-z_{1}}{z_{2}-z_{1}}\right)^{2}{/tex}

questions.question: Find mirror image of point representing x+ iy on imaginary axis.
questions.question_raw: Find mirror image of point representing x+ iy on imaginary axis.
questions.explanation:
Mirror image of point (x, y) on imaginary axis is (-x, y).
Since imaginary axis is acting as mirror y-coordinate remains same whereas x-coordinate gets inverted. So, (-x, y) is mirror image of (x, y) on imaginary axis.

questions.question: Find multiplicative inverse of $3+5 i$.
questions.question_raw: Find multiplicative inverse of {tex}3+5 i{/tex}.
questions.explanation:
We know, {tex}z^{\star} \bar{z}=|z|^2{/tex}. 
{tex} (1 / z)=(\bar{z}) /\left(|z|^2\right) {/tex}
{tex} z^{-1}=(3-5 i) /\left(3^2+5^2\right)=(3-5 i) / 34 {/tex}

questions.question: L is a line whose complex slope is m. The complex slope of the reflection of line L in the origin, is
questions.question_raw: L is a line whose complex slope is m. The complex slope of the reflection of line L in the origin, is
questions.explanation:
Let L1 be the reflection of line L in the origin. Let P and Q be the points on line L, representing z1 and z2 respectively. Then points, say R and S, on line L1 representing -z1 and -z2 are images of P and Q respectively.
Complex slope of line L1 = {tex}\frac{-z_{1}-\left(-z_{2}\right)}{-\bar{z}_{1}-\left(-\bar{z}_{2}\right)}{/tex} = m

questions.question: The complex equation of straight line L is (3 + 2i)z + (3 - 2i) $\bar{z}$ = 12. The area of the triangle formed by L and the real and imaginary axes is
questions.question_raw: The complex equation of straight line L is (3 + 2i)z + (3 - 2i) {tex}\bar{z}{/tex} = 12. The area of the triangle formed by L and the real and imaginary axes is
questions.explanation:
Let P and Q be the points on the real and imaginary axis respectively where line L meets the axes. Let P and Q represent the complex numbers z0 and z1 respectively.

Substitute z0 = {tex}\bar{z}_{0}{/tex} in the equation
(3 + 2i) z0 + (3 - 2i) z0 = 12
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 6 z0 = 12 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} z0 = 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |OP| = 2
Substitute {tex}\bar{z}_{1}{/tex} = -z1
(3 + 2i) z1 - (3 - 2i) z1 = 12
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} z1 = {tex}\frac{12}{4i}{/tex} = -3i {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |OQ| = 3
Required area = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} (2) (3) = 3

questions.question: Which axis is known as real axis in argand plane?
questions.question_raw: Which axis is known as real axis in argand plane?
questions.explanation:
x-axis
The plane having a complex number assigned to each of its point is called the complex plane or the Argand plane. When (x + y i) is plotted in argand plane, x-axis is real axis.

questions.question: A complex number z saisfies |z - i| = |z| and arg $\left(\frac{z}{z-i}\right)=\frac{\pi}{3}$. Then Re(2z - i) equals
questions.question_raw: A complex number z saisfies |z - i| = |z| and arg {tex}\left(\frac{z}{z-i}\right)=\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex}. Then Re(2z - i) equals
questions.explanation:
|z - i| = |z| {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |OP| = |PQ| and arg{tex}\left(\frac{z}{z-i}\right)=\frac{\pi}{3} \Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}QPO = {tex}\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex}

Im(z) = |QM| = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} and Re(z) = |PM| = |QM| cot{tex}\frac{\pi}{6}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} z = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}+\frac{i}{2} \Rightarrow{/tex} 2z - i = {tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex}

questions.question: In $z=4+i$, what is imaginary part?
questions.question_raw: In {tex}z=4+i{/tex}, what is imaginary part?
questions.explanation:
In {tex}{z}={a}+{bi}, {a}{/tex} is real part and b is imaginary part.
So, in 4+i, imaginary part is 1 .

questions.question: Three distinct points P, Q, R in Argand plane represent complex numbers z_1, z_2, z_3. If $\angle$PQR = $\angle$PRQ = $\frac{1}{2}(\pi-\alpha)$, then $\frac{\left(z_{3}-z_{1}\right)\left(z_{1}-z_{2}\right)}{\left(z_{3}-z_{2}\right)^{2}}$ equals
questions.question_raw: Three distinct points P, Q, R in Argand plane represent complex numbers z1, z2, z3. If {tex}\angle{/tex}PQR = {tex}\angle{/tex}PRQ = {tex}\frac{1}{2}(\pi-\alpha){/tex}, then {tex}\frac{\left(z_{3}-z_{1}\right)\left(z_{1}-z_{2}\right)}{\left(z_{3}-z_{2}\right)^{2}}{/tex} equals
questions.explanation:
{tex}\triangle{/tex}PQR is an isosceles triangle with |PQ| = |PR|
{tex}\frac{z_{1}-z_{2}}{z_{3}-z_{2}}{/tex} = k [cos{tex}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\frac{\alpha}{2}\right){/tex} + i sin {tex}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\frac{\alpha}{2}\right){/tex}] ...(i)
and {tex}\frac{z_{3}-z_{1}}{z_{3}-z_{2}}{/tex} = k [(cos{tex}(\left.-\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\frac{\alpha}{2}\right)\right){/tex} + i sin{tex}\left.\left(-\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\frac{\alpha}{2}\right)\right)\right]{/tex} ...(ii)
where k = {tex}\frac{\left|z_{1}-z_{2}\right|}{\left|z_{3}-z_{2}\right|}=\frac{\left|z_{1}-z_{3}\right|}{\left|z_{2}-z_{3}\right|}{/tex}
(ii) {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{z_{3}-z_{1}}{z_{3}-z_{2}}{/tex} = k [cos{tex}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\frac{\alpha}{2}\right){/tex} - i sin{tex}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\frac{\alpha}{2}\right){/tex}] ...(iii)
(i) and (ii) {tex}\Rightarrow\left(\frac{z_{1}-z_{2}}{z_{3}-z_{2}}\right)\left(\frac{z_{3}-z_{1}}{z_{3}-z_{2}}\right){/tex} = k2 ...(iv)
{tex}\frac{\left|z_{3}-z_{2}\right|}{2}{/tex} = |QM| = |RM| and |PQ| = |z1 - z2| = |PR| = |z1 - z3|
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cos{tex}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\frac{\alpha}{2}\right)=\frac{1}{2} \frac{\left|z_{3}-z_{2}\right|}{\left|z_{1}-z_{3}\right|}{/tex}
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} 2sin{tex}\left(\frac{\alpha}{2}\right)=\frac{\left|z_{3}-z_{2}\right|}{\left|z_{1}-z_{3}\right|}=\frac{1}{k}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} k = {tex}\frac{1}{2 \sin \left(\frac{\alpha}{2}\right)}{/tex} ...(v)
(iv) and (v) give
{tex}\left(\frac{z_{1}-z_{2}}{z_{3}-z_{2}}\right) \cdot\left(\frac{z_{3}-z_{1}}{z_{3}-z_{2}}\right)=\frac{1}{4 \sin ^{2}\left(\frac{\alpha}{2}\right)}{/tex}

questions.question: The equation(s) of the line(s) making an angle of 45^o with the line (2 - i) z + (2 + i) $\bar{z}$ + 3 = 0 and passing through (-1, 4) is (are)
questions.question_raw: The equation(s) of the line(s) making an angle of 45o with the line (2 - i) z + (2 + i) {tex}\bar{z}{/tex} + 3 = 0 and passing through (-1, 4) is (are)
questions.explanation:
(2 - i)z + (2 + i) {tex}\bar{z}{/tex} + 3 = 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (2 - i) (x + iy) + (2 + i) (x - iy) + 3 = 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} 4x + 2y + 3 = 0
Let m be the slope of the required line. Then
tan 45o = {tex}\left|\frac{m+2}{1-2 m}\right|{/tex}
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} m - 2 = {tex}\pm{/tex} (1 - 2m)
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} m = {tex}-\frac{1}{3}{/tex} or m = 3
Required line is given by
3y + x - 11 = 0 or y - 3x - 7 = 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow \frac{z+\bar{z}}{2}{/tex} + 3{tex}\left(\frac{z-\bar{z}}{2 i}\right){/tex} - 11 = 0 or
{tex}\left(\frac{z-\bar{z}}{2 i}\right){/tex} - 3{tex}\left(\frac{z+\bar{z}}{2}\right){/tex} - 7 = 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (1 - 3i) z + (1 + 3i) {tex}\bar{z}{/tex} - 22 = 0 or
(3 - i) {tex}\bar{z}{/tex} + (3 + i) z + 14 = 0

questions.question: If z_1, z_2 and z_3 are complex numbers, not all zeroes, are represented by points A, B and C respectively, in Argand diagram and $\triangle$ABC is an equilateral triangle then
questions.question_raw: If z1, z2 and z3 are complex numbers, not all zeroes, are represented by points A, B and C respectively, in Argand diagram and {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC is an equilateral triangle then
questions.explanation:
(z3 - z2) (cos{tex}\frac{2\pi}{3}{/tex} + i sin{tex}\frac{2\pi}{3}{/tex}) = z1 - z3
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} z1 - z3 = {tex}\omega{/tex}(z3 - z2)
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} z1 + {tex}\omega{/tex}z2 - (1 + {tex}\omega{/tex}) z3 = 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} z1 + {tex}\omega{/tex}z2 + {tex}\omega^{2}{/tex}z3 = 0 ...(i)
We know that a2 + b2 + c2 - ab - bc - ca
= (a + b{tex}\omega{/tex} + c{tex}\omega{/tex})2) (a + b{tex}\omega^{2}{/tex} + c{tex}\omega{/tex})
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} If a + b{tex}\omega{/tex} + c{tex}\omega^{2}{/tex} = 0 then
a2 + b2 + c2 = ab + bc + ca
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}z_{1}^{2}+z_{2}^{2}+z_{3}^{2}{/tex} = z1z2 + z2z3 + z3z1

x {tex}\neq{/tex} 1 and x > 0 ...(i)

questions.question: The equation (a^3 - 7a + 6)x^2 + (a^2 + 3a - 4) + x + a(a - 1) = 0 is satisfied by all real x iff a =
questions.question_raw: The equation (a3 - 7a + 6)x2 + (a2 + 3a - 4) + x + a(a - 1) = 0 is satisfied by all real x iff a =
questions.explanation:
Given equation is satisfied by all real x if
a3 - 7a + 6 = 0 and a2 + 3a - 4 = 0 and a(a - 1) = 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (a - 1) (a2 + a - 6) = 0 and (a - 1)(a + 4) = 0 and a(a - 1) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = 1

questions.question: How many solutions do the equations x(x + y) = 9, y(x + y) = 16 have?
questions.question_raw: How many solutions do the equations x(x + y) = 9, y(x + y) = 16 have?
questions.explanation:
x(x + y) = 9 ... (i)
y(x + y) = 16 ... (ii)
L.H.S. of (i) and (ii) have homogeneous expressions of x and y, so substitute y = mx.
{tex}\frac{(\mathrm{ii})}{(\mathrm{i})} \Rightarrow m=\frac{16}{9}{/tex}
(i) {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} x2(1 + m) = 9, substitute m = {tex}\frac{16}{9}{/tex}
x2 = {tex}\frac{9}{1+\frac{16}{9}}=\frac{81}{25}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\pm \frac{9}{5}{/tex}
x = {tex}\frac{9}{5}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = {tex}\frac{16}{9} \times \frac{9}{5}=\frac{16}{5}{/tex}
x = {tex}-\frac{9}{5}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = {tex}\frac{16}{9} \times\left(-\frac{9}{5}\right)=-\frac{16}{5}{/tex}
{tex}\left(\frac{9}{5}, \frac{16}{5}\right){/tex} and {tex}\left(-\frac{9}{5},-\frac{16}{5}\right){/tex} are the solutions.

questions.question: If $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are roots of the equation x^2 + px + 7 = 0 and $\alpha^2$ + $\beta^2$ = 22 then |p| equals:
questions.question_raw: If {tex}\alpha{/tex} and {tex}\beta{/tex} are roots of the equation x2 + px + 7 = 0 and {tex}\alpha^2{/tex} + {tex}\beta^2{/tex} = 22 then |p| equals:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\alpha{/tex} and {tex}\beta{/tex} are roots of the equation
x2 + px + 7 = 0
{tex}\alpha^2{/tex} + p{tex}\alpha{/tex} + 7 = 0 and {tex}\beta^2{/tex} + p{tex}\beta{/tex} + 7 = 0
Adding {tex}\left(\alpha^{2}+\beta^{2}\right){/tex} + p({tex}\alpha{/tex} + {tex}\beta{/tex}) + 14 = 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} 22 + p(-p) + 14 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} p2 = 36 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} p = {tex}\pm 6{/tex}

questions.question: Given that pr = 2(q + s), p, q, r, s $\in$ R Consider equations A: x^2 + px + q = 0, B: x^2 + rx + s = 0. Then which of the following is correct?
questions.question_raw: Given that pr = 2(q + s), p, q, r, s {tex}\in{/tex} R Consider equations A: x2 + px + q = 0, B: x2 + rx + s = 0. Then which of the following is correct?

options.text: Equation A has non-real roots and equation B has real roots.
Equation A and B both have non-real roots.
At least one of the equations A, B has real roots.
Equation A has real roots and equation B has non-real roots.

questions.explanation:
Let equations A and B not have real roots then
p2 < 4q and r2 < 4s
Adding p2 + r2 < 4(q + s)
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} p2 + r2 < 2pr {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (p - r)2 < 0 (A contradiction)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} At least one of the equations has real roots.

questions.question: For all positive integers n, let f(n) = log_2002(n)^2. Let N = f(11) + f(13) + f(14). Then:
questions.question_raw: For all positive integers n, let f(n) = log2002(n)2. Let N = f(11) + f(13) + f(14). Then:
