N = f(11) + f(13) + f(14)
= 2(Iog2002 11 + Iog2002 13 + Iog2002 14)
= 2 log2002 (11 {tex}\times{/tex} 13 {tex}\times{/tex} 14) = 2 log2002(2002) = 2

questions.question: The roots of the equation (x - 2) (x - 4) + k(x - 3)(x - 5) = 0
questions.question_raw: The roots of the equation (x - 2) (x - 4) + k(x - 3)(x - 5) = 0
questions.explanation:
Let P(x) = (x - 2)(x - 4) + k(x - 3)(x - 5)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} P(2) > 0, P(3) < 0, P(4) < 0, P(5) > 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} The graph of y = P(x) meets the x-axis at two points; one point in (2, 3) and other in (4, 5).

questions.question: Consider the equation log_1 - x(3 - x) = log_3 - x(1 - x). Then the equation has:
questions.question_raw: Consider the equation log1 - x(3 - x) = log3 - x(1 - x). Then the equation has:
questions.explanation:
log1 - x(3 - x) = log3 - x(1 - x)
1 - x {tex}\neq{/tex} 1, 3 - x {tex}\neq{/tex} 1, 1 - x > 0 and 3 - x > 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} x {tex}\neq{/tex} 0 and x < 1 (feasible region)
Given equation {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} t = {tex}\frac{1}{t}{/tex} where t = log1 - x(3 - x)
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} t = {tex}\pm{/tex}1
t = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1 - x = 3 - x (absurd)
t = -1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{1-x}{/tex} = 3 - x
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (x - 2)2 = 2
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} x = 2 {tex}\pm{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex}
x = 2 - {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} is the only solution (as x = 2 + {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} does not lie in the feasible region.)

questions.question: The solution of the equation (0.125) (4^2x - 8) = $\left(\frac{0.25}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^{-x}$ is x = $\frac{p}{q}$, where H.C.F. of p, q = 1, then:
questions.question_raw: The solution of the equation (0.125) (42x - 8) = {tex}\left(\frac{0.25}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^{-x}{/tex} is x = {tex}\frac{p}{q}{/tex}, where H.C.F. of p, q = 1, then:
questions.explanation:
Given equation
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{8}\left(2^{2}\right)^{2 x-8}=\left(\frac{1}{4 \sqrt{2}}\right)^{-x}{/tex}
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{8}\left(2^{4 x-16}\right)=\left(2^{\frac{-5}{2}}\right)^{-x}{/tex}
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} 24x - 19 = {tex}2^{\frac{5}{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} 4x - 19 = {tex}\frac{5 x}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\frac{38}{3}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} p = 38, q = 3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} p = 13q - 1

questions.question:
Consider the equation $x^{\frac{3}{4}\left(\log _{2} x\right)^{2}+\log _{2} x-\frac{5}{4}}=\sqrt{2}$
 The equation has at least one real solution.
 The equation has three real solutions.
 The equation has exactly one irrational solution.
Which of the following is correct?
questions.question_raw:
Consider the equation {tex}x^{\frac{3}{4}\left(\log _{2} x\right)^{2}+\log _{2} x-\frac{5}{4}}=\sqrt{2}{/tex}

  1. The equation has at least one real solution.
  2. The equation has three real solutions.
  3. The equation has exactly one irrational solution.

Which of the following is correct?
questions.explanation:
Let log2x = t then
Given equation {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} {tex}2^{t\left(\frac{1}{4} t^{2}+t-\frac{5}{4}\right)}=2^{\frac{1}{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} 3t3 + 4t2 - 5t = 2
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} 3t3 + 4t2 - 5t -2 = 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (t -1) (3t2 + 7t + 2) = 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (t - 1)(3t + 1) (t + 2) = 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} t = 1, {tex}-\frac{1}{3}{/tex}, -2
t = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 2
t = {tex}-\frac{1}{3}{/tex}{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt[3]{2}}{/tex}
t = -2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex}

questions.question: The sum of integer values of k for which the equation (2k + 1)(x^4 + x^2 + 1) - (k - 1) (x^2 + x + 1) has exactly two real roots, is:
questions.question_raw: The sum of integer values of k for which the equation (2k + 1)(x4 + x2 + 1) - (k - 1) (x2 + x + 1) has exactly two real roots, is:
questions.explanation:
(2k + 1) (x4 + x2 + 1) = (k - 1) (x2 + x + 1)
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (2k + 1) [(x2 + 1)2 - x2)] = (k - 1) (x2 + x + 1)
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (2k + 1)[(x2 + x + 1) (x2 - x + 1)] = (k - 1) (x2 + x + 1)
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (x2 + x + 1)[(2k + 1) (x2 - x + 1) - (k - 1)] = 0
Real roots of the given equation, if any, are the real roots of
(2k + 1)(x2 - x + 1) - (k - 1) = 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (2k + 1)x2 - (2k + 1)x + k + 2 = 0
For real roots, (2k + 1)2 - 4(2k + 1)(k + 2) {tex}\ge{/tex} 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (2k + 1) (2k + 1 - 4k - 8) {tex}\ge{/tex} 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (2k + 1) (2k + 7) {tex}\le{/tex} 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} {tex}-\frac{7}{2}{/tex} {tex}\le{/tex} k {tex}\le{/tex} {tex}-\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
Integer k = -1, -2, -3

questions.question: If p = sum of the values x and q = product of the values of x that satisfy the equation $(\sqrt{4-\sqrt{15}})^{x}+(\sqrt{4+\sqrt{15}})^{x}$ = 8, then p - q =
questions.question_raw: If p = sum of the values x and q = product of the values of x that satisfy the equation {tex}(\sqrt{4-\sqrt{15}})^{x}+(\sqrt{4+\sqrt{15}})^{x}{/tex} = 8, then p - q =
questions.explanation:
Let {tex}(\sqrt{4-\sqrt{15}})^{x}{/tex} = t, then {tex}(\sqrt{4+\sqrt{15}})^{x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{t}{/tex} as {tex}(4-\sqrt{15})(4+\sqrt{15}){/tex} = 1
Given equation {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} t2 - 8t + 1 = 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} t = {tex}\frac{8 \pm \sqrt{64-4}}{2}{/tex} = {tex}4 \pm \sqrt{15}{/tex}
t = {tex}4 + \sqrt{15}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}(\sqrt{4-\sqrt{15}})^{x}{/tex} = {tex}4 \pm \sqrt{15}{/tex}
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} {tex}(\sqrt{4+\sqrt{15}})^{x}{/tex} = {tex}(\sqrt{4-\sqrt{15}})^{-1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = -1
t = 4 + {tex}\sqrt{15}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}(\sqrt{4-\sqrt{15}})^{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4 - {tex}\sqrt{15}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} p - 9 = -1 + 1 - (-1) = 1

questions.question: If the equation x^2 + ax + 8a = 0 has integer roots then the number of values of a is:
questions.question_raw: If the equation x2 + ax + 8a = 0 has integer roots then the number of values of a is:
questions.explanation:
Let the equation x2 + ax + 8a = 0 have two roots {tex}\alpha{/tex}, {tex}\beta{/tex}
Then {tex}\alpha{/tex} + {tex}\beta{/tex} = -a
and {tex}\alpha \beta{/tex} = 8a
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\alpha{/tex} + {tex}\beta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-\alpha \beta}{8}{/tex}
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} 8{tex}\alpha{/tex} + 8{tex}\beta{/tex} + {tex}\alpha \beta{/tex} = 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} ({tex}\alpha{/tex} + 8) ({tex}\beta{/tex} + 8) = 64
Let {tex}\alpha{/tex} + 8 = t then {tex}\beta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{64}{t}{/tex} - 8 and {tex}\alpha{/tex} = t - 8
For {tex}\alpha{/tex}, {tex}\beta{/tex} to be integers, 64 must be an integral multiple of t.
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} t = {tex}\pm{/tex}1, {tex}\pm{/tex}2, {tex}\pm{/tex}4, {tex}\pm{/tex}8
(Note that t can be take values {tex}\pm{/tex}16, {tex}\pm{/tex}32, {tex}\pm{/tex}64 but the roots so obtained are the same as what we obtain for t = {tex}\pm{/tex}4, {tex}\pm{/tex}2, {tex}\pm{/tex}1 respectively. So we ignore these values of t)

questions.question: Without direct multiplication; the value of $\sqrt{x}$ where x = (28)(29)(30)(31) + 1, is:
questions.question_raw: Without direct multiplication; the value of {tex}\sqrt{x}{/tex} where x = (28)(29)(30)(31) + 1, is:
questions.explanation:
x = 1 + n(n + 1)(n + 2)(n + 3), where n = 28
= 1 + (n2 + 3n) (n2 + 3n + 2)
= 1 + (n2 + 3n)2 + 2(n2 + 3n)
= (n2 + 3n + 1)2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{x}{/tex} = n2 + 3n + 1 = 869

questions.question: x, y, z, w $\ge$ 0. Given that x + 2y + z = 1 and 2x - 3y - w = 2. The number of quadraples (x, y, z, w) satisfying the equation is:
questions.question_raw: x, y, z, w {tex}\ge{/tex} 0. Given that x + 2y + z = 1 and 2x - 3y - w = 2. The number of quadraples (x, y, z, w) satisfying the equation is:
questions.explanation:
x + 2y + z = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 1 - 2y - z
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x {tex}\le{/tex} 1 ... (i)
2x - 3y - w = 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x = 2 + 3y + w {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x {tex}\ge{/tex} 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x {tex}\ge{/tex} 1 ... (ii)
(i) and (ii) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 1
Substitute x = 1 in given equations to get 2y + z = 0 and 3y + w = 0
y, z, w are non negative {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = z = w = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (1, 0, 0, 0) is the solution.

questions.question: a $\neq$ 0. The number of integral values of a so that the equation a^2 |a + x| + |a^2x + 1| = |a^3 - 1| has a unique solution, is
questions.question_raw: a {tex}\neq{/tex} 0. The number of integral values of a so that the equation a2 |a + x| + |a2x + 1| = |a3 - 1| has a unique solution, is
questions.explanation:
a2|a + x| + |a2x + 1| = |a3 - 1|
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} Ia3 + axI + |-a2x - 1| = |a3 - 1|
(i) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Equality occurs in triangle inequality
|a3 + ax| + |a2x - 1| {tex}\ge{/tex} |a3- 1|
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (a3 + a2x) (-a2x - 1) {tex}\ge{/tex} 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} -a2(a + x) (a2x + 1) {tex}\ge{/tex} 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (x + a) (a2x + 1) {tex}\le{/tex} 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} -a {tex}\le{/tex} x {tex}\le{/tex} {tex}-\frac{1}{a^{2}}{/tex} or {tex}-\frac{1}{a^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\le{/tex} x {tex}\le{/tex} -a
For unique solution, a = {tex}\frac{1}{a^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} The number of integral values of a = 1

questions.question: Let a and b be integers. The equations x^3 + ax^2 + 11x + 6 = 0 and x^3 + bx^2 + 14x + 8 = 0 have two common roots. Then a + b is:
questions.question_raw: Let a and b be integers. The equations x3 + ax2 + 11x + 6 = 0 and x3 + bx2 + 14x + 8 = 0 have two common roots. Then a + b is:
questions.explanation:
Let the two common roots be the zeroes of the polynomial x2 + px + q. Then
x3 + ax2 + 11x + 6 = (x2 + px + q)(x + r) ... (i)
and x3 + bx2 + 14x + 8 = (x2 + px + q)(x + s) ... (ii)
where (x + r) and (x + s) are the factors corresponding to the third zero of two polynomials respectively.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} qr = 6 and qs = 8
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} q = 1 or 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 6 or 3 and s = 8 or 4
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x + 6 or x + 3 is a factor of the cubic polynomial in (i),
Substitute x = -6 in x3 + ax2 + 11x + 6 = 0 to get -216 + 36a - 66 + 6 = 0 which does not give integer a.
Substitute x = -3 in x3 + ax2 + 11x + 6 = 0 to get -27 + 9a - 33 + 6 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = 6
Substitute x = -4 in x3 + bx2 + 14x + 8 = 0 to get
-64 + 16b - 56 + 8 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b = 7 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a + b = 13

questions.question: The range of values of m so that the equation x^2 + x + m = 0 has one root < 1 and other root > 1, is:
questions.question_raw: The range of values of m so that the equation x2 + x + m = 0 has one root < 1 and other root > 1, is:
questions.explanation:
The roots of the equation are real
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} 1 - 4m > 0 {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} m < {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} ... (i)
Let {tex}\alpha{/tex}, {tex}\beta{/tex} be the smaller and bigger roots respectively. Then
{tex}\alpha{/tex} < 1 {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{-1-\sqrt{1-4 m}}{2}{/tex} < 1 {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} {tex}-\sqrt{1-4 m}{/tex} < 3 (true)
{tex}\beta{/tex} > 1 {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{-1+\sqrt{1-4 m}}{2}{/tex} > 1 {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{1-4 m}{/tex} > 3
Squaring on bith sides
1 - 4m > 9
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} m < -2 ... (ii)
(i) and (ii) The range of m = ({tex}-\infty{/tex}, -2)

questions.question: Consider equations 3^x - 2^y = 1 and log_2x + log_3y = 2. The product of all values of x and y that satisfy the equation, is:
questions.question_raw: Consider equations 3x - 2y = 1 and log2x + log3y = 2. The product of all values of x and y that satisfy the equation, is:
questions.explanation:
3x - 2y = 1 ...(i) and
log2x + log3x = 2 ...(ii)
x = 2, y = 3 satisfy (i) and (ii) ...[by observation]
(x = 2, y = 3) is a solution.
If x < 2, y < 3 then L.H.S of (ii) < 2
If x > 2, y > 3 then L.H.S of (ii) > 2
If x < 2, y > 3 then L.H.S of (i) < 1
If x > 2, y < 3 then L.H.S of (i) > 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x = 2, y = 3) is the only solution.

questions.question: If 2x^2 + 3x + 5$\gamma$ = 0 and x^2 + 2x + 3$\gamma$ = 0 have a non-zero common root then $\gamma$ has the value:
questions.question_raw: If 2x2 + 3x + 5{tex}\gamma{/tex} = 0 and x2 + 2x + 3{tex}\gamma{/tex} = 0 have a non-zero common root then {tex}\gamma{/tex} has the value:
questions.explanation:
Let the roots of 2x2 + 3x + 5{tex}\gamma{/tex} = 0 be a, b and the roots of x2 + 2x + 3y = 0 be a and c.
Then a + b = {tex}\frac{-3}{2}{/tex}, ab = {tex}\frac{5 \gamma}{2}{/tex}
and a + c = -2, ac = 3{tex}\gamma{/tex}
{tex}\frac{a b}{a c}=\frac{\frac{5 \gamma}{2}}{3 \gamma} \Rightarrow \frac{b}{c}=\frac{5}{6} \Rightarrow b=\frac{5}{6} c{/tex}
a + {tex}\frac{5}{6} c{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-3}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 6a + 5c = -9
and 6a + 6c = -12 c = - 3
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a + (-3) = -2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = 1
ac = 3{tex}\gamma{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\gamma{/tex} = -1

questions.question: If the roots of the equation x^2 + bx - c = 0 are tan 15^o and tan 30^o then the value of 5 - c - b is:
questions.question_raw: If the roots of the equation x2 + bx - c = 0 are tan 15o and tan 30o then the value of 5 - c - b is:
questions.explanation:
Roots of equation x2 + bx - c = 0 are tan 15o and tan 30o
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} tan 15o + tan 30o = -b = sum of the roots
Also, tan 15o tan 30o = c = product of the roots
Now,
tan(30o + 15o) = {tex}\frac{\tan 30^{\circ}+\tan 15^{\circ}}{1-\tan 30^{\circ} \cdot \tan 15^{\circ}}{/tex}
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} tan(45o) = {tex}\frac{-b}{1-(-c)} \Leftrightarrow 1=\frac{-b}{1+c}{/tex}
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} 1 + c = -b {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} b + c + 1 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 5 - (c + b) = 5 - (-1) = 6

questions.question: If p, q, r $\in$ R and equations px^2 + qx + r = 0 and x^2 + 3x + 12 = 0 have a common root, then which of the following is true
questions.question_raw: If p, q, r {tex}\in{/tex} R and equations px2 + qx + r = 0 and x2 + 3x + 12 = 0 have a common root, then which of the following is true
questions.explanation:
Equations x2 + 3x + 12 = 0 ... (i)
and px2 + qx + r = 0 ... (ii)
have a common root.
Discriminant of the equation x2 + 3x + 12 = 0 is negative.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Roots are imaginary and when roots are imaginary, they are conjugate of each other.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Equation (i) and (ii) have both common roots.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} The two equations are same (or one equation is constant multiple of the other
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{p}{1}=\frac{q}{3}=\frac{r}{12}{/tex}
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} p : q : r = 1 : 3 : 12

questions.question: The values of p and q such that the equation x^4 - px^2 + q = 0 has 4 roots common with the equation x^2 - 6|x| + 5 = 0, are
questions.question_raw: The values of p and q such that the equation x4 - px2 + q = 0 has 4 roots common with the equation x2 - 6|x| + 5 = 0, are
questions.explanation:
x2 - 6|x| + 5 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 - 6x + 5 = 0 or x2 + 6x + 5 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x2 - 6x + 5) (x2 + 6x + 5) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x2 + 5)2 - (6x)2 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x4 - 10x2 + 25- 36x2 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x4 - 26x2 + 25 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} p = 26, q = 25

questions.question: The number of integral values of a for which the quadratic equation (x - a) (x - 10) + 1 = 0 has integral roots are:
questions.question_raw: The number of integral values of a for which the quadratic equation (x - a) (x - 10) + 1 = 0 has integral roots are:
questions.explanation:
(x - a) (x - 10) = -1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x - a) (x -10) = -1 {tex}\times{/tex} 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} either x - a = -1, x - 10 = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 11, a = 12
or x - 10 = -1, x - a = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 9, a = 8
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a takes two integral values.

questions.question: Two candidates attempt to solve a quadratic equation of the form x^2 + ax + b = 0, one starts with wrong value of a and his roots are -4, 3. The other starts with the wrong value of b and his roots are 3 and 1, then the correct roots of the equation are:
questions.question_raw: Two candidates attempt to solve a quadratic equation of the form x2 + ax + b = 0, one starts with wrong value of a and his roots are -4, 3. The other starts with the wrong value of b and his roots are 3 and 1, then the correct roots of the equation are:
questions.explanation:
First candidate gets roots as -4, 3, of the equation x2 + px + q = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Sum = (-4 + 3) = -p
Product q = -12 (correct product of the roots)
Second candidate gets roots as 3, 1
(3 + 1) = -p (sum of roots)
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} p = - 4 (correct sum of the roots)
The correct equation is x2 + px + q = 0
x2 - 4x - 12 = 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (x - 6) (x + 2) = 0
x = 6, x = -2

questions.question: If the equation 2x^2 - 27x + 2 = 0 has roots as p and q then the value of (2p - 27)^5(2q - 27)^5 is equal to:
questions.question_raw: If the equation 2x2 - 27x + 2 = 0 has roots as p and q then the value of (2p - 27)5(2q - 27)5 is equal to:
questions.explanation:
The given equation is 2x2 - 27x + 2 = 0 and the roots are p and q
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} p + q = {tex}\frac{27}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2p - 27 = -2q
2q - 27 = -2p and pq = 1
Now (2p - 27)5 (2q - 27)5 = [(2p - 27)(2q - 27)]5
= [(2p - 27)(2q - 27)]5
= [(-2q)(-2p)]5
= 45(pq)5
= 45 = 1024

questions.question: If x = $\sqrt{3+\sqrt{3+\sqrt{3+\ldots+\infty}}}$, then x is equal to:
questions.question_raw: If x = {tex}\sqrt{3+\sqrt{3+\sqrt{3+\ldots+\infty}}}{/tex}, then x is equal to:

options.text: an irrational number lying between 2 and 3

questions.explanation:
Given, x = {tex}\sqrt{3+\sqrt{3+\sqrt{3+\ldots}}}{/tex}
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\sqrt{3+x}{/tex} (feasible region: x {tex}\ge{/tex} 0)
Squaring, x2 - x - 3 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\frac{1 \pm \sqrt{13}}{2}{/tex} (Reject x = {tex}\frac{1-\sqrt{13}}{2}{/tex})
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\frac{1+\sqrt{13}}{2}{/tex}, which is an irrational number lying between 2 and 3.

questions.question: The value of $2+\frac{1}{2+\frac{1}{2+\ldots \infty}}$ is:
questions.question_raw: The value of {tex}2+\frac{1}{2+\frac{1}{2+\ldots \infty}}{/tex} is:
questions.explanation:
Let x = {tex}2+\frac{1}{2+\frac{1}{2+\ldots \infty}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}2+\frac{1}{x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 - 2x - 1 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}1 \pm \sqrt{2}{/tex}
But the value of the given expression cannot be less than 2.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}1+\sqrt{2}{/tex}

questions.question: The roots of |x - 2|^2 + |x - 2| - 6 = 0 are:
questions.question_raw: The roots of |x - 2|2 + |x - 2| - 6 = 0 are:
questions.explanation:
Let |x - 2| = t ... ({tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} t {tex}\ge{/tex} 0)
Given equation {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} t2 + t - 6 = 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\underbrace{(t+3)}_{\neq 0}(t-2){/tex} = 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} t = 2 {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} |x - 2| = 2
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} x - 2 = {tex}\pm2{/tex}
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} x = 0, 4

questions.question: If $12^{4+2 x^{2}}=(24 \sqrt{3})^{3 x^{2}-2}$, then x =
questions.question_raw: If {tex}12^{4+2 x^{2}}=(24 \sqrt{3})^{3 x^{2}-2}{/tex}, then x =
questions.explanation:
{tex}12^{4+2 x^{2}}=(24 \sqrt{3})^{3_{x}^{2}-2}{/tex}
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} {tex}12^{4+2 x^{2}}=(12)^{\frac{3}{2}\left(3 x^{2}-2\right)}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4 + 2x2 = {tex}\frac{3}{2}\left(3 x^{2}-2\right){/tex}
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} 5x2 = 14 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 = {tex}\frac{14}{5}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\pm \sqrt{\frac{14}{5}}{/tex}

questions.question: A real root of the equation log_4 {log_2 $(\sqrt{x+8}-\sqrt{x})$} = 0 is:
questions.question_raw: A real root of the equation log4 {log2 {tex}(\sqrt{x+8}-\sqrt{x}){/tex}} = 0 is:
questions.explanation:
x {tex}\ge{/tex} 0 and x + 8 > 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x {tex}\ge{/tex} 0 ... (i)
Also {tex}\sqrt{x+8}-\sqrt{x}>2^{0}{/tex} = 1
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{x+8}>1+\sqrt{x}{/tex}
Squaring {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x + 8 > 1 + x + {tex}2 \sqrt{x}{/tex}
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} x < {tex}\frac{49}{4}{/tex} ... (ii)
(i) and (ii)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Feasible region = {tex}\left[0, \frac{49}{4}\right){/tex}
{tex}\log _{4}\left\{\log _{2}(\sqrt{x+8}-\sqrt{x})\right\}{/tex} = 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\log _{2}(\sqrt{x+8}-\sqrt{x}){/tex} = 4o
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{x+8}-\sqrt{x}=2^{1}{/tex}
Squaring on both sides, we get
x + 8 + x - {tex}2 \sqrt{x^{2}+8 x}{/tex} = 4
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}2 \sqrt{x^{2}+8 x}{/tex} = 2x + 4
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{x^{2}+8 x}{/tex} = x + 2
Again squaring on both sides, we get
x2 + 8x = x2 + 4 + 4x
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4x = 4
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 1

questions.question: The number of roots of the quadratic equation 8 sec^2$\theta$ - 6 sec $\theta$ + 1 = 0 is:
questions.question_raw: The number of roots of the quadratic equation 8 sec2{tex}\theta{/tex} - 6 sec {tex}\theta{/tex} + 1 = 0 is:
questions.explanation:
8 sec2{tex}\theta{/tex} - 6 sec{tex}\theta{/tex} + 1 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sec {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} or sec {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex},
But sec {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\ge{/tex} 1 or sec {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\le{/tex} -1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} The given equation has no solution.

questions.question: If $\alpha , \beta$ are the roots of x^2 - 2x + 4 = 0, then $\alpha^5$ + $\beta^5$ is equal to:
questions.question_raw: If {tex}\alpha , \beta{/tex} are the roots of x2 - 2x + 4 = 0, then {tex}\alpha^5{/tex} + {tex}\beta^5{/tex} is equal to:
questions.explanation:
Here, {tex}\alpha{/tex} + {tex}\beta{/tex} = 2 and {tex}\alpha \beta{/tex} = 4
{tex}\alpha^2{/tex} + {tex}\beta^2{/tex} = ({tex}\alpha{/tex} + {tex}\beta{/tex})2 - 2{tex}\alpha \beta{/tex} = -4
and {tex}\alpha^3{/tex} + {tex}\beta^3{/tex} = ({tex}\alpha{/tex} + {tex}\beta{/tex})3 - 3{tex}\alpha \beta{/tex}({tex}\alpha{/tex} + {tex}\beta{/tex})
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\alpha^3{/tex} + {tex}\beta^3{/tex} = -16
Now,
{tex}\alpha^5{/tex} + {tex}\beta^3{/tex} = ({tex}\alpha^3{/tex} + {tex}\beta^2{/tex}) ({tex}\alpha^3{/tex} + {tex}\beta^3{/tex}) - {tex}\alpha^2 \beta^2{/tex}({tex}\alpha{/tex} + {tex}\beta{/tex})
= (-4) (-16) - (16) (2)
= 32

questions.question: If the sum of the roots of the quadratic equation ax^2 + bx + c = 0 is equal to the sum of the squares of their reciprocals, then $\frac{b^{2}}{a c}+\frac{b c}{a^{2}}$ =
questions.question_raw: If the sum of the roots of the quadratic equation ax2 + bx + c = 0 is equal to the sum of the squares of their reciprocals, then {tex}\frac{b^{2}}{a c}+\frac{b c}{a^{2}}{/tex} =
questions.explanation:
Let the roots be {tex}\alpha{/tex} and {tex}\beta{/tex}
Then, {tex}\alpha{/tex} + {tex}\beta{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{b}{a}{/tex} and {tex}\alpha \beta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{c}{a}{/tex}
According to the given condition,
{tex}\alpha{/tex} + {tex}\beta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\alpha^{2}}+\frac{1}{\beta^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}-\frac{b}{a}=\frac{\alpha^{2}+\beta^{2}}{\alpha^{2} \beta^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}-\frac{b}{a} \times \frac{c^{1}}{a^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}(\alpha+\beta)^{2}-2 \alpha \beta{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}-\frac{b c^{2}}{a^{3}}=\frac{b^{2}}{a^{2}}-\frac{2 c}{a}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{2}{a}=\frac{b^{2}}{a^{2} c}+\frac{b c}{a^{3}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2 = {tex}\frac{b^{2}}{a c}+\frac{b c}{a^{2}}{/tex}

questions.question: If $\alpha$, $\beta$ be the roots of the equation 2x^2 - 35x + 2 = 0, then the value of (2$\alpha$ - 35)^3 (2$\beta$ - 35)^3 is equal to
questions.question_raw: If {tex}\alpha{/tex}, {tex}\beta{/tex} be the roots of the equation 2x2 - 35x + 2 = 0, then the value of (2{tex}\alpha{/tex} - 35)3 (2{tex}\beta{/tex} - 35)3 is equal to
questions.explanation:
Since, {tex}\alpha{/tex}, {tex}\beta{/tex} are the roots of the equation
2x2 - 35x + 2 = 0
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 2{tex}\alpha^2{/tex} - 35{tex}\alpha{/tex} = -2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2{tex}\alpha{/tex} - 35 = {tex}-\frac{2}{\alpha}{/tex}
and {tex}2 \beta^{2}-35 \beta{/tex} = -2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2{tex}\beta{/tex} - 35 = {tex}-\frac{2}{\beta}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}(2 \alpha-35)^{3}(2 \beta-35)^{3}{/tex} = {tex}\left(\frac{-2}{\alpha}\right)^{3}\left(\frac{-2}{\beta}\right)^{3}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{64}{\alpha^{3} \beta^{3}}=\frac{64}{1^{3}}{/tex}
= 64

questions.question: If one root of the quadratic equation ax^2 + bx + c = 0 is equal to the n^th power of the other root, then the value of $\left(a c^{n}\right)^{\frac{1}{n+1}}+\left(a^{n} c\right)^{\frac{1}{n+1}}$ =
questions.question_raw: If one root of the quadratic equation ax2 + bx + c = 0 is equal to the nth power of the other root, then the value of {tex}\left(a c^{n}\right)^{\frac{1}{n+1}}+\left(a^{n} c\right)^{\frac{1}{n+1}}{/tex} =
questions.explanation:
Let the roots be {tex}\alpha{/tex} and {tex}\alpha^n{/tex}.
Then, {tex}\alpha{/tex} + {tex}\alpha^n{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{b}{a}{/tex} ... (i)
and {tex}\alpha{/tex}({tex}\alpha^n{/tex}) = {tex}\frac{c}{a}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\alpha{/tex} = {tex}\left(\frac{c}{a}\right)^{\frac{1}{n+1}}{/tex}
Substituting {tex}\alpha{/tex} in (i), we get
{tex}\left(\frac{c}{a}\right)^{\frac{1}{n+1}}+\left(\frac{c}{a}\right)^{\frac{n}{n+1}}=-\frac{b}{a}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}a \cdot a^{-\frac{1}{n+1}} \cdot c^{\frac{1}{n+1}}+a \cdot a^{-\frac{n}{n+1}} \cdot c^{\frac{n}{n+1}}{/tex} = -b
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left(a^{n} c\right)^{\frac{1}{n+1}}+\left(a c^{n}\right)^{\frac{1}{n+1}}{/tex} = -b

questions.question: If $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are the roots of 6x^2 - 6x + 1 = 0, then the value of $\frac{1}{2}\left[a+b \alpha+c \alpha^{2}+d \alpha^{3}\right]$ + $\frac{1}{2}\left[a+b \beta+c \beta^{2}+d \beta^{3}\right]$ is:
questions.question_raw: If {tex}\alpha{/tex} and {tex}\beta{/tex} are the roots of 6x2 - 6x + 1 = 0, then the value of {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left[a+b \alpha+c \alpha^{2}+d \alpha^{3}\right]{/tex} + {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left[a+b \beta+c \beta^{2}+d \beta^{3}\right]{/tex} is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\alpha{/tex} + {tex}\beta{/tex} = 1 and {tex}\alpha \beta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{6}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left[a+b \alpha+c \alpha^{2}+d \alpha^{3}\right]{/tex} + {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left[a+b \beta+c \beta^{2}+d \beta^{3}\right]{/tex}
= {tex}a+\frac{1}{2} b(\alpha+\beta)+\frac{1}{2} c\left(\alpha^{2}+\beta^{2}\right)+\frac{1}{2} d\left(\alpha^{3}+\beta^{3}\right){/tex}
= {tex}a+\frac{1}{2} b+\frac{1}{2} c\left[(\alpha+\beta)^{2}-2 \alpha \beta\right]{/tex} + {tex}\frac{1}{2} d\left[(\alpha+\beta)^{3}-3 \alpha \beta(\alpha+\beta)\right]{/tex}
= {tex}a+\frac{b}{2}+\frac{1}{2} c\left[(1)^{2}-2 \cdot \frac{1}{6}\right]+\frac{1}{2} d\left[(1)^{3}-3 \cdot \frac{1}{6}\right]{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{a}{1}+\frac{b}{2}+\frac{c}{3}+\frac{d}{4}{/tex}

questions.question: If $\alpha$, $\beta$ are the roots of (x - a)(x - b) = c, c $\neq$ 0, then the roots of (x - $\alpha$)(x - $\beta$) + c = 0 shall be:
questions.question_raw: If {tex}\alpha{/tex}, {tex}\beta{/tex} are the roots of (x - a)(x - b) = c, c {tex}\neq{/tex} 0, then the roots of (x - {tex}\alpha{/tex})(x - {tex}\beta{/tex}) + c = 0 shall be:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\alpha{/tex}, {tex}\beta{/tex} are the roots of (x - a) (x - b) = c
i.e., x2 - (a + b)x + ab - c = 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (x - a) (x - b) - c = (x - {tex}\alpha{/tex}) (x - {tex}\beta{/tex})
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (x - a) (x - b) = (x - {tex}\alpha{/tex}) (x - {tex}\beta{/tex}) + c
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = a, b are the roots of (x - {tex}\alpha{/tex}) (x - {tex}\beta{/tex}) + c = 0

questions.question: If $\alpha$, $\beta$ are the roots of x^2 - px + 1 = 0 and $\gamma$ is a root of x^2 + px + 1 = 0, then ($\alpha$ + $\gamma$) ($\beta$ + $\gamma$) is:
questions.question_raw: If {tex}\alpha{/tex}, {tex}\beta{/tex} are the roots of x2 - px + 1 = 0 and {tex}\gamma{/tex} is a root of x2 + px + 1 = 0, then ({tex}\alpha{/tex} + {tex}\gamma{/tex}) ({tex}\beta{/tex} + {tex}\gamma{/tex}) is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\alpha{/tex}, {tex}\beta{/tex} are the roots of x2 - px + 1 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\alpha{/tex} + {tex}\beta{/tex} = p and {tex}\alpha \beta{/tex} = 1
Also, {tex}\gamma{/tex} is a root of x2 + px + 1 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\gamma^2{/tex} + p{tex}\gamma{/tex} + 1 = 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\gamma^2{/tex} + ({tex}\alpha{/tex} + {tex}\beta{/tex}){tex}\gamma{/tex} + {tex}\alpha \beta{/tex} = 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\gamma^2{/tex} + {tex}\alpha \gamma{/tex} + {tex}\beta \gamma{/tex} + {tex}\alpha \beta{/tex} = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} ({tex}\alpha{/tex} + {tex}\gamma{/tex}) ({tex}\beta{/tex} + {tex}\gamma{/tex}) = 0

questions.question: If a root of the equation ax^2 + bx + c = 0 be reciprocal of a root of the equation a'x^2 + b'x + c' = 0, then
questions.question_raw: If a root of the equation ax2 + bx + c = 0 be reciprocal of a root of the equation a'x2 + b'x + c' = 0, then
questions.explanation:
Let {tex}\alpha{/tex} be the root of ax2 + bx + c = 0. Then {tex}\frac{1}{\alpha}{/tex} is the root of a'x2 + b'x + c' = 0
{tex}\therefore{/tex} a{tex}\alpha^2{/tex} + ba + c = 0 ... (i)
and {tex}a^{\prime} \frac{1}{\alpha^{2}}+b^{\prime} \frac{1}{\alpha}+c^{\prime}{/tex} = 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow c^{\prime} \alpha^{2}+b^{\prime} \alpha+a^{\prime}{/tex} ... (ii)
From (i) and (ii), we get
{tex}\frac{\alpha^{2}}{b a^{\prime}-b^{\prime} c}=\frac{\alpha}{c c^{\prime}-a a^{\prime}}=\frac{1}{a b^{\prime}-b c^{\prime}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} (cc' - aa')2 = (ba' - cb')(ab' - bc')

questions.question: The roots of the quadratic equation 2x^2 + 3x + 1 = 0 are:
questions.question_raw: The roots of the quadratic equation 2x2 + 3x + 1 = 0 are:
questions.explanation:
Discriminant {tex}\Delta{/tex} = 9 - 8 = 1 which is an integer square.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} The roots are rational.

questions.question: If k $\in$ $(-\infty,-2) \cup(2, \infty)$, then the roots of the equation x^2 + 2kx + 4 = 0 are:
questions.question_raw: If k {tex}\in{/tex} {tex}(-\infty,-2) \cup(2, \infty){/tex}, then the roots of the equation x2 + 2kx + 4 = 0 are:
questions.explanation:
k < -2 or k > 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} k2 > 4 ... (i)
Discriminant = 4(k2 - 4) > 0 ... [From (i)]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} The roots are real and unequal.

questions.question: If x is real, the expression $\frac{x+2}{2 x^{2}+3 x+6}$ takes all values in the interval:
questions.question_raw: If x is real, the expression {tex}\frac{x+2}{2 x^{2}+3 x+6}{/tex} takes all values in the interval:
questions.explanation:
Let y = {tex}\frac{x+2}{2 x^{2}+3 x+6}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x2y + (3y - 1)x + (6y - 2) = 0
For real x, D {tex}\ge{/tex} 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (3y - 1)2 - 4(2y)(6y - 2) {tex}\ge{/tex} 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} -39y2 + 10y + 1 {tex}\ge{/tex} 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (13y + 1)(3y - 1) {tex}\le{/tex} 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} {tex}-\frac{1}{13} \leq y \leq \frac{1}{3}{/tex}

questions.question: If the roots of the equation bx^2 + cx + a = 0 be imaginary, then for all real values of x, the expression 3b^2x^2 + 6bcx + 2c^2 is:
questions.question_raw: If the roots of the equation bx2 + cx + a = 0 be imaginary, then for all real values of x, the expression 3b2x2 + 6bcx + 2c2 is:
questions.explanation:
Since, the roots of bx2 + cx + a = 0 are imaginary (non-real), therefore, c2 - 4ab < 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c2 < 4ab
3b2x2 + 6bcx + 2c2
= {tex}3 b^{2}\left[x^{2}+2\left(\frac{c}{b}\right) x\right]+2 c^{2}{/tex}
= {tex}3 b^{2}\left(x+\frac{c}{b}\right)^{2}+2 c^{2}-3 b^{2}\left(\frac{c^{2}}{b^{2}}\right){/tex}
= {tex}3 b^{2}\left(x+\frac{c}{b}\right)^{2}-c^{2} \geq-c^{2}>-4 a b{/tex} ...[{tex}\because{/tex} c2 < 4ab {tex}\therefore{/tex} -c2 > -4ab]

questions.question: If x^2 - 3x + 2 be a factor of x^4 - px^2 + q, then (p, q) =
questions.question_raw: If x2 - 3x + 2 be a factor of x4 - px2 + q, then (p, q) =
questions.explanation:
x2 - 3x + 2 is a factor of x4 - px2 + q
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} The roots of x2 - 3x + 2 = 0 are also the roots of x4 - px2 + q = 0
x2 - 3x + 2 = 0 {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (x - 2)(x -1) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 2, 1
Putting these values in x4 - px + q = 0, we get
4p - q - 16 = 0 ... (i)
and p - q - 1 = 0 ... (ii)
Solving (i) and (ii), we get (p, q) = (5, 4)

questions.question: The quadratic equation in terms of t, such that A.M. of its roots is A and G.M. is G, is:
questions.question_raw: The quadratic equation in terms of t, such that A.M. of its roots is A and G.M. is G, is:
questions.explanation:
Let the roots be {tex}\alpha{/tex} and {tex}\beta{/tex}. Then,
A = {tex}\frac{\alpha+\beta}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\alpha{/tex} + {tex}\beta{/tex} = 2A
and G = {tex}\sqrt{\alpha \beta}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\alpha \beta{/tex} = G2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The required equation is t2 - 2At + G2 = 0

questions.question: If the harmonic mean between roots of (5 + $\sqrt{2}$)x^2 - bx + (8 + $2\sqrt{5}$) = 0 is 4, then b is equal to:
questions.question_raw: If the harmonic mean between roots of (5 + {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex})x2 - bx + (8 + {tex}2\sqrt{5}{/tex}) = 0 is 4, then b is equal to:
questions.explanation:
Given equation is
(5 + {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex})x2 - bx + (8 + {tex}2 \sqrt{5}{/tex}) = 0
Here, {tex}\alpha+\beta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{b}{5+\sqrt{2}}{/tex}
and {tex}\alpha \beta=\frac{8+2 \sqrt{5}}{5+\sqrt{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Harmonic mean = {tex}\frac{2 \alpha \beta}{\alpha+\beta}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4 = {tex}\frac{2\left(\frac{8+2 \sqrt{5}}{5+\sqrt{2}}\right)}{\frac{b}{5+\sqrt{2}}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b = 4 + {tex}\sqrt{5}{/tex}

questions.question: Let two numbers have arithmetic mean 9 and geometric mean 4. Then these numbers are the roots of the quadratic equation:
questions.question_raw: Let two numbers have arithmetic mean 9 and geometric mean 4. Then these numbers are the roots of the quadratic equation:
questions.explanation:
Let the two numbers be {tex}\alpha{/tex} and {tex}\beta{/tex}.
Then, {tex}\frac{\alpha+\beta}{2}{/tex} = 9 and {tex}\sqrt{\alpha \beta}{/tex} = 4
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\alpha{/tex} + {tex}\beta{/tex} = 18 and {tex}\alpha \beta{/tex} = 16
The required equation is
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 - 18x + 16 = 0

questions.question: If a, b, c are in G.P., then the equations ax^2 + 2bx + c = 0 and dx^2 + 2ex + f = 0 have a common root if $\frac{d}{a}, \frac{e}{b}, \frac{f}{c}$ are in:
questions.question_raw: If a, b, c are in G.P., then the equations ax2 + 2bx + c = 0 and dx2 + 2ex + f = 0 have a common root if {tex}\frac{d}{a}, \frac{e}{b}, \frac{f}{c}{/tex} are in:
questions.explanation:
According to the given condition, b2 = ac
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Equation ax2 + 2bx + c = 0 can be written as
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} ax2 + {tex}2 \sqrt{a c}{/tex} x + c = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}(\sqrt{a} x+\sqrt{c})^{2}=0 \Rightarrow{/tex} x = -{tex}\sqrt{\frac{c}{a}}{/tex} (repeated root)
This must be the common root by hypothesis. So it must satisfy the equation dx2 + 2ex + f = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow d\left(\frac{c}{a}\right)-2 e \sqrt{\frac{c}{a}}+f{/tex} = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d}{a}+\frac{f}{c}=\frac{2 e}{c} \sqrt{\frac{c}{a}}=\frac{2 e}{b}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d}{a}, \frac{e}{b}, \frac{f}{c}{/tex} are in A.P.

questions.question: If the roots of the equation $\frac{p q}{x+p}+\frac{p q}{x+q}=\frac{1}{r}$ are equal in magnitude but opposite in sign, then:
questions.question_raw: If the roots of the equation {tex}\frac{p q}{x+p}+\frac{p q}{x+q}=\frac{1}{r}{/tex} are equal in magnitude but opposite in sign, then:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\frac{p q}{x+p}+\frac{p q}{x+q}=\frac{1}{r}{/tex}
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} x + p + x + q = {tex}\frac{(x+p)(x+q)}{p q r}{/tex}
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} 2pqrx + p2qr + pq2r = x2 + (p + q)x + pq
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} x2 + (p + q - 2pqr)x + p2qr - pq2r - pq = 0
Let the roots be {tex}\alpha{/tex} and {tex}-\alpha{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sum of the roots = 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} p + q = 2pqr

questions.question: The smallest positive integer a for which $\frac{2 x^{2}+2 x+5}{x^{2}+x+1} \leq a$ holds true is:
questions.question_raw: The smallest positive integer a for which {tex}\frac{2 x^{2}+2 x+5}{x^{2}+x+1} \leq a{/tex} holds true is:
questions.explanation:
Let P = {tex}\frac{2 x^{2}+2 x+5}{x^{2}+x+1}=\frac{2\left(x^{2}+x+1\right)+3}{x^{2}+x+1}{/tex}
= {tex}2+\frac{3}{x^{2}+x+1}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} P is maximum when x2 + x + 1 is minimum.
But x2 + x + 1 = {tex}\left(x+\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}+\frac{3}{4} \geq \frac{3}{4}{/tex},
Equality occurs only when x = {tex}-\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Minimum value of x2 + x + 1 = {tex}\frac{3}{4}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Maximum value of P = {tex}2+\frac{3}{\frac{3}{4}}{/tex} = 2 + 4 = 6
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Smallest integer value of a must be 6.

questions.question: If the expression (a - 1)x^2 + (a + 1)x + (a - 1) is positive for all x $\in$ R, then:
questions.question_raw: If the expression (a - 1)x2 + (a + 1)x + (a - 1) is positive for all x {tex}\in{/tex} R, then:
questions.explanation:
(a - 1)x2 + (a + 1)x + (a - 1) > 0 for all x
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} < 0 and coefficient of x2 > 0 ... (i)
{tex}\Delta{/tex} < 0 {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (a + 1)2 - 4(a - 1)2 < 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (3a - 1) (-a + 3) < 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} a > 3 or a < {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex} ... (ii)
Also, a - 1 > 0 {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} a > 1 ... (iii)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} The values of a satisfying both (ii) and (iii) lie in (3, {tex}\infty{/tex})

questions.question: If p $\in$ R and the equation px^2 - x + p = 0, p $\neq$ 0 possesses two distinct real roots $\alpha$ and $\beta$ such that $\alpha^{2}+\beta^{2}>\frac{1}{P}$. Then:
questions.question_raw: If p {tex}\in{/tex} R and the equation px2 - x + p = 0, p {tex}\neq{/tex} 0 possesses two distinct real roots {tex}\alpha{/tex} and {tex}\beta{/tex} such that {tex}\alpha^{2}+\beta^{2}>\frac{1}{P}{/tex}. Then:
questions.explanation:
px2 - x + p = 0
Roots are real and distinct {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} > 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (-1)2 - {tex}4p \cdot p{/tex} > 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} p2 < {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex}
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} {tex}-\frac{1}{2}<p<\frac{1}{2}{/tex} ... (i)
{tex}\alpha^{2}+\beta^{2}>\frac{1}{p} \Leftrightarrow(\alpha+\beta)^{2}-2 \alpha \beta>\frac{1}{p}{/tex}
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{p^{2}}-2>\frac{1}{p} \Leftrightarrow \frac{1}{p^{2}}-\frac{1}{p}-2>0{/tex}
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left(\frac{1}{p}-2\right)\left(\frac{1}{p}+1\right)>0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{p}<-1{/tex} or {tex}\frac{1}{p}>2{/tex}
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left(\frac{1+p}{p}<0\right){/tex} or {tex}\left(\frac{1-2 p}{p}>0 \Leftrightarrow \frac{2 p-1}{p}<0\right){/tex}
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} -1 < p < 0 or 0 < p < {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} ... (ii)
(i) and (ii) give
{tex}-\frac{1}{2}<p<\frac{1}{2}{/tex}, p {tex}\neq{/tex} 0

questions.question: Let S = {x $\in$ R : x $\ge$ 0 and 2|$\sqrt{x}$ - 3| + $\sqrt{x}$ ($\sqrt{x}$ - 6) + 6 = 0}. Then S
questions.question_raw: Let S = {x {tex}\in{/tex} R : x {tex}\ge{/tex} 0 and 2|{tex}\sqrt{x}{/tex} - 3| + {tex}\sqrt{x}{/tex} ({tex}\sqrt{x}{/tex} - 6) + 6 = 0}. Then S
questions.explanation:
Case I: 0 {tex}\le{/tex} x {tex}\le{/tex} 9
{tex}2(3-\sqrt{x})+\sqrt{x}(\sqrt{x}-6)+6{/tex} = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}(\sqrt{x})^{2}-8 \sqrt{x}{/tex} = 12 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{x}{/tex} = 6, 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 36, 4
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 4
Case II: x {tex}\ge{/tex} 9
{tex}2(3-\sqrt{x})+\sqrt{x}(\sqrt{x}-6)+6{/tex} = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}(\sqrt{x})^{2}-4 \sqrt{x}{/tex} = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{x}{/tex} = 0, 4
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 0, 16
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 16
{tex}\therefore{/tex} S contains exactly two elements.

questions.question: The number of real solutions for the equation x^2 + 4x + 6 = $\sqrt x$
questions.question_raw: The number of real solutions for the equation x2 + 4x + 6 = {tex}\sqrt x{/tex}
questions.explanation:
x2 + 4x + 6 = {tex}\sqrt x{/tex}
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (x + 2)2 + 2 = {tex}\sqrt x{/tex}
squaring {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}[(x + 2)2 + 2]2 = {tex}(\sqrt x)^2{/tex}
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (x + 2)4 + 4(x + 2)2 + 4 = x
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (x + 2)4 + 4x2 + 16x + 16 + 4 = x
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (x + 2)4 + 4x2 + 15x + 20 = 0 ...(i)
Now, (x + 2)4 {tex}\ge{/tex} 0, to have a solution to (i), 4x2 + 15x + 20 {tex}\le{/tex} 0 for all x.
But {tex}\Delta{/tex} < 0 for 4x2 + 15x + 20,
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4x2 + 15x + 20 is always positive for all x.
Thus, no real value of x will give
(x + 2)4 + 4x2 +15x + 20 = 0

questions.question: Consider the equation $\sqrt{x+3-4 \sqrt{x-1}}+\sqrt{x+8-6 \sqrt{x-1}}$ = 1. The values of x that satisfy the equation:
questions.question_raw: Consider the equation {tex}\sqrt{x+3-4 \sqrt{x-1}}+\sqrt{x+8-6 \sqrt{x-1}}{/tex} = 1. The values of x that satisfy the equation:
questions.explanation:
Before solving the equation, feasible region needs to be obtained.
x + 3 {tex}\ge{/tex} 4{tex}\sqrt{x-1}{/tex} ... (i)
x + 8 {tex}\ge{/tex} 6{tex}\sqrt{x-1}{/tex} ... (ii) and
x {tex}\ge{/tex} 1 ... (iii)
Squaring (i),
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 + 6x + 9 {tex}\ge{/tex} 16(x - 1)
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} x2 - 10x + 25 {tex}\ge{/tex} 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (x - 5)2 {tex}\ge{/tex} 0 (true for all x)
Squaring (ii),
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 + 16x + 64 {tex}\ge{/tex} 36(x - 1)
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} x2 - 20x + 100 {tex}\ge{/tex} 0
(x - 10)2 {tex}\ge{/tex} 0 (true for all x)
Also,
{tex}\sqrt{x+3-4 \sqrt{x-1}}=1-\sqrt{x+8-6 \sqrt{x-1}}{/tex}
LHS {tex}\ge{/tex} 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{x+8-6 \sqrt{x-1}} \leq 1{/tex}
Squaring, x + 8 - {tex}6 \sqrt{x-1} \leq 1{/tex}
Squaring, x2 - 22x + 85 {tex}\le{/tex} 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (x - 17) (x - 5) {tex}\le{/tex} 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} 5 {tex}\le{/tex} x {tex}\le{/tex} 17 ... (iv)
Similarly,
{tex}\sqrt{x+8-6 \sqrt{x-1}}=1-\sqrt{x+3-4 \sqrt{x-1}}{/tex}
LHS {tex}\ge{/tex} 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{x+3-4 \sqrt{x-1}} \leq 1{/tex}
Squaring, {tex}x+3-4 \sqrt{x-1} \leq 1{/tex}
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} {tex}x+2 \leq 4 \sqrt{x-1}{/tex}
Squaring, x2 -12x + 20 {tex}\le{/tex} 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (x - 2) (x - 10) {tex}\le{/tex} 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} 2 {tex}\le{/tex} x {tex}\le{/tex} 10 ... (v)
(iii), (iv), (v) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} feasible region = 5 {tex}\le{/tex} x {tex}\le{/tex} 10
Now, let us solve the given equation.
Squaring
{tex}\sqrt{x+3-4 \sqrt{x-1}}=1-\sqrt{x+8-6 \sqrt{x-1}}{/tex}, we get
{tex}x+3-4 \sqrt{x-1}=1+x+8-6 \sqrt{x-1}{/tex} - {tex}2 \sqrt{x+8-6 \sqrt{x-1}}{/tex}
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} {tex}-6+2 \sqrt{x-1}=-2 \sqrt{x+8-6 \sqrt{x-1}}{/tex}
(R.H.S {tex}\le{/tex} 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{x-1}-3 \leq 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x {tex}\le{/tex} 10)
Squaring, 
{tex}x-1+9-6 \sqrt{x-1}=x+8-6 \sqrt{x-1}{/tex} true for all x
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Solution set is same as the feasible region, i.e. Solution set = [5,10]

questions.question:
Consider the equation $\sqrt{x+8+2 \sqrt{x+7}}+\sqrt{x+1-\sqrt{x+7}}$ = 4 and the following statements:
 Feasible region consists of exactly one point.
 Feasible region consists of more than one point and has finite length.
 Feasible region has infinite length.
 The equation has a unique solution.
 The equation has more than one solution.
Which of these are correct?
questions.question_raw:
Consider the equation {tex}\sqrt{x+8+2 \sqrt{x+7}}+\sqrt{x+1-\sqrt{x+7}}{/tex} = 4 and the following statements:

  1. Feasible region consists of exactly one point.
  2. Feasible region consists of more than one point and has finite length.
  3. Feasible region has infinite length.
  4. The equation has a unique solution.
  5. The equation has more than one solution.

Which of these are correct?
questions.explanation:
{tex}x+8+2 \sqrt{x+7} \geq 0{/tex} ... (i)
and {tex}x+1 \geq \sqrt{x+7}{/tex} ...(ii)
(ii) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x+1 \geq \sqrt{x+7} \Rightarrow x \geq-1{/tex} ...(iii)
Squaring, x2 + 2x + 1 {tex}\ge{/tex} x + 7
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} x2 + x - 6 {tex}\ge{/tex} 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (x + 3) (x - 2) (x + 3) (x - 2) {tex}\ge{/tex} 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x {tex}\ge{/tex} 2 ... (iv)
(iii) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (i) is always true.
Given equation
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} {tex}4-\sqrt{x+8+2 \sqrt{x+7}}=\sqrt{x+1-\sqrt{x+7}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}4 \geq \sqrt{x+8+2 \sqrt{x+7}}{/tex}
Squaring, 16 {tex}\ge{/tex} x + 8 + {tex}2 \sqrt{x+7}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 8 - x {tex}\ge{/tex} {tex}2 \sqrt{x+7}{/tex} ... [x {tex}\le{/tex} 8]
Squaring, 64 + x2 - 16x {tex}\ge{/tex} 4 (x + 7)
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} x2 - 20x + 36 {tex}\ge{/tex} 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (x -2) (x - 18) {tex}\ge{/tex} 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} x {tex}\le{/tex} 2 or x {tex}\ge{/tex} 1 8
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x {tex}\le{/tex} 2
(iv) and (v) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Feasible region: {2}
x = 2 satisfies the given equation
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} The equation has a unique solution.

questions.question: The equation x^2 + |x| - 20 = 0, has
questions.question_raw: The equation x2 + |x| - 20 = 0, has
questions.explanation:
x2 + |x| - 20 = 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} |x|2 + |x| - 20 = 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (|x| + 5) (|x| - 4) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |x| = 4 ... [{tex}\because{/tex} |x| + 5 > 0]
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\pm{/tex}4

questions.question: The sum of the roots of the equation 3^2x - 10$\cdot$3^x + 9 = 0 is:
questions.question_raw: The sum of the roots of the equation 32x - 10{tex}\cdot{/tex}3x + 9 = 0 is:
questions.explanation:
32x - 10{tex}\cdot{/tex}3x + 9 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (3x)2 - 10(3x) + 9 = 0
Let a = 3x, then
a2 - 10a + 9 = 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (a - 9)(a - 1) = 0
a = 9, 1
a = 3x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 9 = 3x {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} 32 = 3x {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} x = 2
and 1 = 3x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 30 = 3x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 0
The roots are 0, 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} The sum of the roots = 2

questions.question: The roots of the equation $2^{x+2}{27}^{\frac{x}{(x-1)}}$= 9 are given by:
questions.question_raw: The roots of the equation {tex}2^{x+2}{27}^{\frac{x}{(x-1)}}{/tex}= 9 are given by:
questions.explanation:
{tex}2^{x+2} 3^{\frac{3 x}{(x-1)}}{/tex} = 9
Taking log on both sides, we get
(x + 2) log 2 + {tex}\left(\frac{3 x}{x-1}\right){/tex} log 3 = 2 log 3
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (x + 2) log 2 + {tex}\left(\frac{3 x}{x-1}\right) \log 3{/tex} - 1 log 3 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x + 2) log 2 + {tex}\frac{(x+2)}{x-1} \log 3{/tex} = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}(x+2)\left(\log 2+\frac{1}{x-1} \log 3\right){/tex} = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = - 2 or {tex}\frac{1}{1-x}=\frac{\log 2}{\log 3}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = -2 or x = 1 - {tex}\frac{\log 3}{\log 2}{/tex}

questions.question: The sum of the values of x that satisfy 12^5x - 2 = $(24 \sqrt{3})^{3-x^{2}}$ is:
questions.question_raw: The sum of the values of x that satisfy 125x - 2 = {tex}(24 \sqrt{3})^{3-x^{2}}{/tex} is:
questions.explanation:
125x - 2 = {tex}(24 \sqrt{3})^{3-x^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} {tex}3^{5 x-2} \cdot 2^{10 x-4}{/tex} = {tex}2^{9-3 x^{2}} \cdot(3)^{\frac{3}{2}\left(3-x^{2}\right)}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 5x - 2 = {tex}\frac{3}{2}\left(3-x^{2}\right){/tex} {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} 3x2 + 10x - 13 = 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (3x + 13) (x - 1) = 0
x = {tex}-\frac{13}{3}{/tex}, 1
The sum of the values of x = {tex}-\frac{13}{3}{/tex} + 1 = {tex}-\frac{10}{3}{/tex}

questions.question: The product of all the value of x that satisfy the equation $\sqrt{7^{2 x^{2}-5 x-6}}=(\sqrt{2})^{3 \log _{2} 49}$ is:
questions.question_raw: The product of all the value of x that satisfy the equation {tex}\sqrt{7^{2 x^{2}-5 x-6}}=(\sqrt{2})^{3 \log _{2} 49}{/tex} is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\sqrt{7^{2 x^{2}-5 x-6}}=(\sqrt{2})^{3 \log _{2} \cdot \sqrt{9}}{/tex}
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} {tex}(7)^{\frac{1}{2}\left(2 x^{2}-5 x-6\right)}=2^{\frac{3}{2} \log _{2}(7)^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}(2)^{3 \log _{2} 7}=2^{\log _{2}(7)^{3}}{/tex}
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} {tex}7^{\frac{1}{2}\left(2 x^{2}-5 x-6\right)}{/tex} = 73
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} 2x2 - 5x - 6 = 6 {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} 2x2 - 5x - 12= 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (2x + 3) (x - 4) = 0
x = {tex}-\frac{3}{2}{/tex}, 4
The required product = -6

questions.question: How many solutions does the equation $2^{2 x-x^{2}}$ + 2x^2 - 3 = x^4 have?
questions.question_raw: How many solutions does the equation {tex}2^{2 x-x^{2}}{/tex} + 2x2 - 3 = x4 have?
questions.explanation:
{tex}2^{2 x-x^{2}}{/tex} + 2x2 - 3 = x4
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} {tex}2^{2 x-x^{2}}{/tex} = x4 - 2x2 + 3
R.H.S. = (x2 - 1)2 + 2 {tex}\ge{/tex} 2
L.H.S. = {tex}2^{-(x-1)^{2}+1}{/tex} {tex}\le{/tex} 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} L.H.S. = R.H.S = 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 1

questions.question: The solution set of the inequation $2^{x}<3^{\frac{1}{x}}$ is:
questions.question_raw: The solution set of the inequation {tex}2^{x}<3^{\frac{1}{x}}{/tex} is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}2^{x}<3^{\frac{1}{x}}{/tex}
Taking log(with base 2) on both sides we get
{tex}x \log _{2} 2<\frac{1}{x} \log _{2} 3{/tex}
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} {tex}x-\frac{1}{x} \log _{2} 3{/tex} < 0 {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{x^{2}-\log _{2} 3}{x}{/tex} < 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{\left(x-\sqrt{\log _{2} 3}\right)\left(x+\sqrt{\log _{2} 3}\right)}{x}{/tex} < 0

{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} {tex}x \in\left(-\infty,-\sqrt{\log _{2} 3}\right) \cup\left(0, \sqrt{\log _{2} 3}\right){/tex}

questions.question: Let m represent the sum of the values of x that satisfy the equation log_3(log_2x - 9) = 2 + log_3(1 - 4 log_x4). Then the digital sum of m is:
questions.question_raw: Let m represent the sum of the values of x that satisfy the equation log3(log2x - 9) = 2 + log3(1 - 4 logx4). Then the digital sum of m is:
questions.explanation:
We first find the feasible region.
log2x - 9 > 0 ... (i) and
1 - 4 logx4 > 0 ... (ii)
(i) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x > 29 and 1 - 4 logx4 = 1 - logx(28)
And {tex}\log _{2^{9}}\left(2^{8}\right){/tex} < 1 as 28 < 29
i.e., (ii) is satisfied x > 29
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Feasible region = {tex}\left(2^{9}, \infty\right){/tex}
Given equation
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} log3(logxx - 9) = log3(32) + log3(1 - 4logx4)
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} log3(log2 x - 9) = log3 [9 (1 - 4logx 4)]
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} log2x - 9 = 9(1 - 4logx4) = {tex}\left(1-8 \frac{\log _{2} 2}{\log _{2} x}\right){/tex}
Let log2x = t then t - 9 = {tex}9\left(1-\frac{8}{t}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} t2 - 18t + 72 = 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (t - 6) (t - 12) = 0
t = 6 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 26 (rejected)
t= 12 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 212 = 4096
There is only value of x, hence m = 4096
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} The digital sum of m = 19

questions.question: The greatest negative integer satisfying the inequation $\log _{\frac{1}{3}}\left(\frac{3 x-1}{x+2}\right)$ < 1 is:
questions.question_raw: The greatest negative integer satisfying the inequation {tex}\log _{\frac{1}{3}}\left(\frac{3 x-1}{x+2}\right){/tex} < 1 is:
questions.explanation:
We first find the feasible region,

{tex}\frac{3 x-1}{x+2}{/tex} > 0 {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} x < -2 or x > {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex}
Feasible region = {tex}(-\infty,-2) \cup\left(\frac{1}{3}, \infty\right){/tex}
Given inequation {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{3 x-1}{x+2}>\frac{1}{3}{/tex}
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{9 x-3-x-2}{3(x+2)}{/tex} > 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{8 x-5}{3(x+2)}{/tex} > 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} x < -2 or x > {tex}\frac{5}{8}{/tex}

The solution set is {tex}(-\infty,-2) \cup\left(\frac{5}{8}, \infty\right){/tex}
The greatest negative integer is -3.

questions.question: How many integer solutions does the equation $|x|^{x^{2}-x-2}<1$ have?
questions.question_raw: How many integer solutions does the equation {tex}|x|^{x^{2}-x-2}<1{/tex} have?

options.text: more than one but less than four

questions.explanation:
{tex}|x|^{\mid x^{2}-x-2}<1{/tex} ... (i)

Case I: |x| < 1 {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} -1 < x < 1
(i) {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} x2 - x - 2 > 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (x - 2) (x + 1) > 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} x < -1 or x > 2
No real value x < -1 or x > 2 satisfies -1 < x < 1
Case II: |x| > 1 {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} x < -1 or x > 1
(i) {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} x2 - x - 2 < 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (x - 2) (x + 1) < 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} -1 < x < 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1 < x < 2 is a solution. No integer lies in (1, 2)

questions.question: The interval of p for which the equation 3 cos^2x + p cos x + 1 = 0 has exactly two roots in $(-\pi, \pi)$, is:
questions.question_raw: The interval of p for which the equation 3 cos2x + p cos x + 1 = 0 has exactly two roots in {tex}(-\pi, \pi){/tex}, is:
questions.explanation:
3 cos2x + p cosx + 1 - 0, {tex}x \in(-\pi, \pi){/tex}
Put cos x = t ... t {tex}\in{/tex}(-1, 1)
3t2 + pt + 1 = 0 ... (Discriminant = D)
For roots to be real
D {tex}\ge{/tex} 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} p2 - 4(3) {tex}\ge{/tex} 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} p2 - 12 {tex}\ge{/tex} 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} {tex}p \in(-\infty,-2 \sqrt{3}] \cup[2 \sqrt{3}, \infty){/tex} ... (i)

Also f(-1) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3 - p + 1 > 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} p < 4 ... (ii)
f(1) > 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3 + p + 1 > 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} p > -4 ... (iii)
3t2 + pt + 1 = {tex}3\left(t+\frac{p}{6}\right)^{2}-\frac{p^{2}}{12} \geq-\frac{p^{2}}{12}{/tex} (Equality occurs at t = {tex}-\frac{p}{6}{/tex})
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -1 < {tex}-\frac{p}{6}{/tex} < 1
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} p {tex}\in{/tex} (-6, 6) ... (iv)
Considering (i), (ii), (iii) and (iv)
p {tex}\in{/tex} {tex}(-4,-2 \sqrt{3}] \cup[2 \sqrt{3}, 4){/tex}

questions.question: The equation e^x - x - 1 = 0 has:
questions.question_raw: The equation ex - x - 1 = 0 has:
questions.explanation:
The graphs of y = ex and y = x + 1 meet only at x = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 0 is the only root

questions.question: It is known that the equation (12x - 1) (6x - 1) (4x - 1) (3x - 1) = 5 has precisely two rational roots, $\alpha$ and $\beta$. The value of $\alpha$ + $\beta$ is:
questions.question_raw: It is known that the equation (12x - 1) (6x - 1) (4x - 1) (3x - 1) = 5 has precisely two rational roots, {tex}\alpha{/tex} and {tex}\beta{/tex}. The value of {tex}\alpha{/tex} + {tex}\beta{/tex} is:
questions.explanation:
Let 12x = y then
Given equation,
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} {tex}(y-1)\left(\frac{y}{2}-1\right)\left(\frac{y}{3}-1\right)\left(\frac{y}{4}-1\right){/tex} = 5
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (y - 1)(y - 2)(y - 3)(y - 4) = 5(4)(3)(2) ... [(y - 1)(y - 2)(y - 3)(y - 4) = product of four consecutive numbers]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y - 1 = 5 or y - 1 = -2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 6 or -1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}-\frac{1}{12}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\alpha{/tex} + {tex}\beta{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{1}{12}+\frac{1}{2}=\frac{5}{12}{/tex}

questions.question: If $\sqrt{3 x^{2}-7 x-30}+\sqrt{2 x^{2}-7 x-5}$ = x + 5, then x is equal to:
questions.question_raw: If {tex}\sqrt{3 x^{2}-7 x-30}+\sqrt{2 x^{2}-7 x-5}{/tex} = x + 5, then x is equal to:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\sqrt{3 x^{2}-7 x-30}+\sqrt{2 x^{2}-7 x-5}{/tex} = x + 5
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{3 x^{2}-7 x-30}=(x+5)-\sqrt{2 x^{2}-7 x-5}{/tex}
Squaring on both sides, we get
{tex}\sqrt{2 x^{2}-7 x-5}{/tex} = 5
Again squaring on both sides, we get
2x - 7x - 30 = 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (x - 6) (2x + 5) = 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} x = 6 or x = {tex}-\frac{5}{2}{/tex}

questions.question: The sum of all possible solutions of the equation 8x(2x^2 - 1) (8x^4 - 8x^2 + 1) = 1 is:
questions.question_raw: The sum of all possible solutions of the equation 8x(2x2 - 1) (8x4 - 8x2 + 1) = 1 is:
questions.explanation:
8x(2x2 - 1) (8x4 - 8x2 + 1) = 1
Observe that if x > 1 then L.H.S. > 1 and
for x < 1, L.H.S. < 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -1 {tex}\le{/tex} x {tex}\le{/tex} 1 (feasible region)
Let x = cos {tex}\theta{/tex} then given equation reduces to
8 cos {tex}\theta{/tex} cos 2{tex}\theta{/tex} [2(2 cos2 - 1)2 - 1] = 1
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} 8 cos {tex}\theta{/tex} cos 2{tex}\theta{/tex} (2 cos22{tex}\theta{/tex} - 1) = 1
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} 8 cos{tex}\theta{/tex} cos 2{tex}\theta{/tex} cos 4{tex}\theta{/tex} = 1
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{2 \sin \theta}(2 \sin \theta \cos \theta) \cos 2 \theta \cos 4 \theta=\frac{1}{8}{/tex}
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{2 \sin \theta} \cdot \frac{1}{2}(\sin 4 \theta \cos 4 \theta)=\frac{1}{8}{/tex}
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{8 \sin \theta}(\sin 8 \theta)=\frac{1}{8}{/tex}
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} sin 8{tex}\theta{/tex} = sin{tex}\theta{/tex}
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} sin 8{tex}\theta{/tex} - sin{tex}\theta{/tex} = 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} {tex}2 \cos \frac{9 \theta}{2} \sin \frac{7 \theta}{2}{/tex} = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{90}{2}=(2 n+1) \frac{\pi}{2} \text { or } \frac{7 \theta}{2}=n \pi{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\theta=(2 n+1) \frac{\pi}{9} \text { or } \theta=\frac{2 n \pi}{7}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\theta=2 m \pi+\frac{\pi}{3} \text { or } 2 p \pi{/tex} (Rest all values of {tex}\theta{/tex} give non real x)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} or 1
The required sum = {tex}\frac{3}{2}{/tex}

questions.question:
In the adjoining figure |$\alpha$| > $\beta$. Consider the following statements.
b > 0
c < 0
b^2 > 4c
The true statement(s) is/are:
questions.question_raw:
In the adjoining figure |{tex}\alpha{/tex}| > {tex}\beta{/tex}. Consider the following statements.

  1. b > 0
  2. c < 0
  3. b2 > 4c

The true statement(s) is/are:
questions.explanation:
|{tex}\alpha{/tex}| > {tex}\beta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\alpha{/tex} + {tex}\beta{/tex} < 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b > 0
To get c, substitute x = 0 in x2 + bx + c
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} c is y-intercept of the curve
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (From graph) c < 0
The graph meets the x-axis at two distinct points
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} b2 - 4c > 0 {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} b2 > 4c

questions.question:
The graph of y - x^2 + bx + c is cut by the lines y = x and y = 2x as shown. The arcs AB, CD are projected on the x-axis.
The lengths of their projections are p and q respectively. Then:
questions.question_raw:
The graph of y - x2 + bx + c is cut by the lines y = x and y = 2x as shown. The arcs AB, CD are projected on the x-axis.

The lengths of their projections are p and q respectively. Then:
questions.explanation:
Let A' B' and C’ D' be the projections of arcs AB and CD respectively. Let x1, x2, x3, x4 be x-coordinates of points A, B, C, D respectively then (x1, 0), (x2, 0), (x3, 0), (x4, 0) are points A’, B', C', D' respectively.
Solving y = 2x and y = x2 + bx + c we have x2 + bx + c = 2x {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} x2 + (b - 2)x + c = 0
x1 and x4 are the roots.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x1 + x4 = 2 - b ... (i)
Similarly solving y = x and y = x2 + bx + c we have
x2 + bx + c
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} x2 + (b - 1)x + c = 0
x2 and x3 are the roots. x2 + x3 = 1 - b ... (ii)
​​​​​​p = x2 - x1 and q = x4 - x3
p - q = (x2 + x3) - (x1 + x4)
= 1 - b - (2 - b) ... [from (i) and (ii)]
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} p + 1 = q

questions.question: For x, y$\in$R. The least value of the expression x^2 + 2xy + 3y^2 - 6x + 6y is
questions.question_raw: For x, y{tex}\in{/tex}R. The least value of the expression x2 + 2xy + 3y2 - 6x + 6y is
questions.explanation:
Given expression
= {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}[2x2 + 4xy + 6y2 - 12x + 12y]
= {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}[(x + 2y)2 + 2(y + 3)2 + (x - 6)2] - 27
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Given expression {tex}\ge{/tex} -27 (Equality occurs only when x = 6, y = -3 which also satisfies x + 2y = 0)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} The least value of the given expression = -27

questions.question: Two points P and Q in the Argand diagram represent z and 2z + 3 + i respectively. If P moves on a circle with centre at origin and radius 4, then the locus of Q is a circle with centre at
questions.question_raw: Two points P and Q in the Argand diagram represent z and 2z + 3 + i respectively. If P moves on a circle with centre at origin and radius 4, then the locus of Q is a circle with centre at
questions.explanation:
Let 2z + 3 + i = zi, then
2z = z1 - (3 + i) and |z| = 4
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |2z| = |z1 - (3 + i)|
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 8 = |z1 - (3 + i)|
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Locus of z1 is a circle with centre at 3 + i

questions.question: The number of real roots of the equation e^sinx - e^-sinx - 4 = 0 are:
questions.question_raw: The number of real roots of the equation esinx - e-sinx - 4 = 0 are:
questions.explanation:
esinx - e-sinx - 4 = 0
esinx - {tex}\frac 1{e^{\sin x}}{/tex}- 4 = 0
Let esinx = t
t - {tex}\frac 1t{/tex} - 4 = 0
{tex}\frac {t^2 - 1 - 4t}t{/tex} = 0
t2 - 4t - 1 = 0
by using quadratic formula,
we set
t = 2 - {tex}\sqrt 5{/tex}, t = 2 + {tex}\sqrt 5{/tex}
But t can not be 2 - {tex}\sqrt 5{/tex} as
t = esinx which is always positive
{tex}\therefore{/tex} t = 2 + {tex}\sqrt 5{/tex}
on resubstituting t = esinx
taking loge both side
sin x = loge (2 + {tex}\sqrt 5{/tex})
But 2 + {tex}\sqrt 5{/tex} > e
so, log 2 + {tex}\sqrt 5{/tex} > 1
hence, sin x > 1 which is not possible.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} the given equation has no real solution

questions.question: The number of values of x that satisfy the equation $\log _{2 x-1}\left(\frac{x^{4}+2}{2 x+1}\right)$ = 1 is:
questions.question_raw: The number of values of x that satisfy the equation {tex}\log _{2 x-1}\left(\frac{x^{4}+2}{2 x+1}\right){/tex} = 1 is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\log _{2 x-1}\left(\frac{x^{4}+2}{2 x+1}\right){/tex} = 1
log function is defined when
2x - 1 {tex}\ne{/tex} 1
x {tex}\ne{/tex} 1
2x - 1 > 0
x > {tex}\frac 12{/tex}
{tex}\left(\frac{x^{4}+2}{2 x+1}\right){/tex} > 0
{tex}\left(\frac{x^{4}+2}{2 x+1}\right){/tex} = (2x - 1)
(x4 + 2) = (2x - 1)(2x - 1)
x4 + 2 = (2x)2 - 1
x4 + 2 = 4x2 - 1
x4 - 4x2 + 3 = 0
Let x2 = t
t2 - 4t + 3 = 0
t2 - 3t - t + 3 = 0
t(t - 3) = 1(t - 3) = 0
t = 1, 3
Now, resubstitute value of and x2 = 1
x = {tex}\pm{/tex}1
and x2 = 3
x = {tex}\pm \sqrt 3{/tex}
But -1, 1 and -{tex}\sqrt 3{/tex} is not possible as function is not defined for these value.
Hence, x = {tex}\sqrt 3{/tex} is the only solution.

questions.question: Let w_1 be the point obtained by the rotation of z_1 = 5 + 4i about the origin through a right angle in the anticlockwise direction, and w_2 be the point obtained by the rotation of z_2 = 3 + 5i about the origin through a right angle in the clockwise direction. Then the principal argument of w_1 - w_2 is equal to
questions.question_raw: Let w1 be the point obtained by the rotation of z1 = 5 + 4i about the origin through a right angle in the anticlockwise direction, and w2 be the point obtained by the rotation of z2 = 3 + 5i about the origin through a right angle in the clockwise direction. Then the principal argument of w1 - w2 is equal to
questions.explanation:
W1 = z1i = (5 + 4i)i = -4 + 5i ...(i)
W2 = z2(- i) = (3 + 5i)(- i) = 5 - 3i ...(i)
Subtract equation (i) from (ii)
W1 - W2 = (-4 + 5i) - (5 - 3i) = -9 + 8i
Principle argument = {tex}\pi{/tex} -tan-1{tex}\left(\frac{8}{9}\right){/tex}

questions.question: If for z = $\alpha + i\beta$,|z + 2| = z + 4(l + i), then $\alpha + \beta$ and $\alpha\beta$ are the roots of the equation
questions.question_raw: If for z = {tex}\alpha + i\beta{/tex},|z + 2| = z + 4(l + i), then {tex}\alpha + \beta{/tex} and {tex}\alpha\beta{/tex} are the roots of the equation
questions.explanation:
|z + 2| = |{tex}\alpha{/tex} + i{tex}\beta{/tex} + 2|
= {tex}\alpha{/tex} + i{tex}\beta{/tex} + 4 + 4i
{tex}\sqrt{(\alpha+2)^2+\beta^2}=(\alpha+4)+i(\beta+4){/tex}
Comparing real imaginary part
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\beta{/tex} + 4 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\beta{/tex} = -4
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} ({tex}\alpha{/tex} + 2)2 + 16 = ({tex}\alpha{/tex} + 4)2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\alpha{/tex}2 + 4 + 4{tex}\alpha{/tex} + 16 = {tex}\alpha{/tex}2 + 16 + 8{tex}\alpha{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 1, {tex}\beta{/tex} = -4, So, {tex}\alpha{/tex} + {tex}\beta{/tex} = -3, {tex}\alpha{/tex}{tex}\beta{/tex} = -4
Sum of roots = -7, Product of roots = 12
So, equation will be x2 + 7x + 12 = 0

questions.question: If the center and radius of the circle $\left|\frac{z-2}{z-3}\right|$ = 2 are respectively $(\alpha, \beta)$ and $\gamma$, then 3$(\alpha+\beta+\gamma)$ is equal to
questions.question_raw: If the center and radius of the circle {tex}\left|\frac{z-2}{z-3}\right|{/tex} = 2 are respectively {tex}(\alpha, \beta){/tex} and {tex}\gamma{/tex}, then 3{tex}(\alpha+\beta+\gamma){/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation:
Let z = x + iy
{tex}\sqrt{(x-2)^2+y^2}{/tex} {tex}=2 \sqrt{(x-3)^2+y^2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x^2+y^2-4 x+4{/tex} {tex}=4 x^2+4 y^2-24 x+36{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 3 x^2+3 y^2{/tex} - 20x + 32 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow x^2+y^2-\frac{20}{3} x+\frac{32}{3}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow(\alpha, \beta)=\left(\frac{10}{3}, 0\right){/tex}
{tex}\gamma=\sqrt{\frac{100}{9}-\frac{32}{3}}=\sqrt{\frac{4}{9}}=\frac{2}{3} \text {; }{/tex}
{tex}3(\alpha+\beta+\gamma){/tex} {tex}=3\left(\frac{10}{3}+\frac{2}{3}\right){/tex} = 12

questions.question: For all z $\in$ C on the curve C_1 : |z| = 4, let the locus of the 1 point z + $\frac 1z$ be the curve C_2. Then
questions.question_raw: For all z {tex}\in{/tex} C on the curve C1 : |z| = 4, let the locus of the 1 point z + {tex}\frac 1z{/tex} be the curve C2. Then

options.text: the curves C_1 and C_2 intersect at 2 points
the curves C_1 lies inside C_2
the curves C_2 lies inside C_1
the curves C_1 and C_2 intersect at 4 points

questions.explanation:
Let w = z + {tex}\frac{1}{z}=4 e^{i \theta}+\frac{1}{4} e^{-i \theta}{/tex}
Since, z = {tex}4 e ^{ i \theta}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow w =(4 \cos \theta+ i \sin \theta){/tex} {tex}+\left(\frac{\cos \theta- i \sin \theta}{4}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow w =\frac{17}{4} \cos \theta+ i \frac{15}{4} \sin \theta{/tex}
So, locus of w is ellipse {tex}\frac{x^2}{\left(\frac{17}{4}\right)^2}+\frac{y^2}{\left(\frac{15}{4}\right)^2}{/tex} = 1
since, |z| = 4
So, locus of z is circle x2 + y2 = 16
thus intersect at 4 points.

questions.question: The complex number z = $\frac{i-1}{\cos \frac{\pi}{3}+i \sin \frac{\pi}{3}}$ is equal to:
questions.question_raw: The complex number z = {tex}\frac{i-1}{\cos \frac{\pi}{3}+i \sin \frac{\pi}{3}}{/tex} is equal to:
questions.explanation:
Let z1 = i - 1 = -1 + i
r = {tex}\sqrt{1+1}=\sqrt{2} ;{/tex} {tex}\theta=\frac{3 \pi}{4}{/tex}
= {tex}\pi-\tan ^{-1}(1)=\pi-\frac{\pi}{4}=\frac{3 \pi}{4}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore z =\frac{ i -1}{\cos \frac{\pi}{3}+ i \sin \frac{\pi}{3}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\sqrt{2}\left(\cos \frac{3 \pi}{4}+ i \sin \frac{3 \pi}{4}\right)}{\cos \frac{\pi}{3}+ i \sin \frac{\pi}{3}}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{2}\left[\cos \left(\frac{3 \pi}{4}-\frac{\pi}{3}\right)+i \sin \left(\frac{3 \pi}{4}-\frac{\pi}{3}\right)\right]{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{2}\left(\cos \frac{5 \pi}{12}+ i \sin \frac{5 \pi}{12}\right){/tex}

questions.question: Let p, q $\in$ R and (1- $\sqrt 3i$)^200 = 2^199(p + iq), i = $\sqrt {-1}$. Then p + q + q^2 and p - q + q^2 are roots of the equation.
questions.question_raw: Let p, q {tex}\in{/tex} R and (1- {tex}\sqrt 3i{/tex})200 = 2199(p + iq), i = {tex}\sqrt {-1}{/tex}. Then p + q + q2 and p - q + q2 are roots of the equation.
questions.explanation:
Given, {tex}(1-\sqrt{3} i)^{200}=2^{199}(p+i q){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 2^{200}\left(\frac{1}{2}-\frac{\sqrt{3} i}{2}\right)^{200}{/tex} {tex}=2^{199}( p + iq ){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 2^{200}\left(\cos \frac{\pi}{3}-i \sin \frac{\pi}{3}\right)^{200}{/tex} {tex}=2^{199}(p+i q){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 2\left(-\frac{1}{2}- i \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right){/tex} = p + iq {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex}  p = -1, q = {tex}-\sqrt{3}{/tex}
Now, {tex}\alpha{/tex} = p + q + q2 = 2 - {tex}\sqrt 3{/tex}, {tex}\beta{/tex} = p - q + q2 = 2 + {tex}\sqrt 3{/tex}
So, {tex}\alpha{/tex} + {tex}\beta{/tex} = 4, {tex}\alpha \cdot \beta{/tex} = 1
Thus the equation x2 - 4x + 1 = 0

= 2(32 + 10 {tex}\times{/tex} 8(-9) + 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 81) = 244

questions.question: Let S = {z = x + iy : |z - 1 + i| $\geq$ |z|, |z| < 2, |z + i| = |z - 1|}. Then the set of all values of, for which w = 2x + iy $\in$ S for some y $\in$ $\mathbb{R}$, is
questions.question_raw: Let S = {z = x + iy : |z - 1 + i| {tex}\geq{/tex} |z|, |z| < 2, |z + i| = |z - 1|}. Then the set of all values of, for  which w = 2x + iy {tex}\in{/tex} S for some y {tex}\in{/tex} {tex}\mathbb{R}{/tex}, is
questions.explanation:
Given expression is
|z - 1 + i| {tex}\geq{/tex}|z| ; |z| < 2; |z + i| = |z - 1|




Hence, w = 2x + iy {tex}\in{/tex} S
{tex}2 x \leq \frac{1}{2} {/tex} {tex}\therefore x \leq \frac{1}{4}{/tex}
Now, (2x)2 + (2x)2 < 4

questions.question: If z $\neq$ 0 be a complex number such that $\left|z-\frac{1}{z}\right|$ = 2, then the maximum value of |z| is:
questions.question_raw: If z {tex}\neq{/tex} 0 be a complex number such that {tex}\left|z-\frac{1}{z}\right|{/tex} = 2, then the maximum value of |z| is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}|| z\left|-\frac{1}{|z|}\right| \leq\left|z-\frac{1}{z}\right| \leq|z|+\frac{1}{|z|}{/tex}
Let |z| = t
{tex}\left|| t|-\frac{1}{|t|}\right| \leq 2 \leq t+\frac{1}{t}{/tex}
{tex}\left| | t|-\frac{1}{|t|}\right| \leq 2 {/tex} & {tex}t+\frac{1}{t} \geq 2{/tex}
Modulus can be negative or positive
{tex}t-\frac{1}{t} \geq-2 {/tex} & {tex}t-\frac{t}{t} \leq 2{/tex}
{tex}t^2-1 \leq 2 t{/tex}
{tex}t^2-2 t \leq 1{/tex}
{tex}(t-1)^2 \leq 2{/tex}
{tex}t-1 \leq \sqrt{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore|z|_{\max }=1+\sqrt{2}{/tex}

questions.question:
Let a complex number be w = 1 - $\sqrt 3$i. Let another complex number z be such that |zw| = 1 and arg(z) - arg(w) = $\frac{\pi}{2} $.
Then the area of the triangle with vertices origin, z and w is equal to:
questions.question_raw:
Let a complex number be w = 1 - {tex}\sqrt 3{/tex}i. Let another complex number z be such that |zw| = 1 and arg(z) - arg(w) = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2} {/tex}.
Then the area of the triangle with vertices origin, z and w is equal to:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\because w=1-\sqrt{3} i {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow|w|=2{/tex}
{tex}|w z|=1 \Rightarrow|w \| z|{/tex}{tex}=1 \Rightarrow|z|=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
Since, arg(z) - arg(w) = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}, then angle between z and w
is {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}; Area of triangle = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \cdot|w||z|=\frac{1}{2} \cdot 2 \times \frac{1}{2}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}

questions.question: a, b, c are integers, not all simultaneously equal and $\omega$ is cube root of unity ($\omega \neq$ 1), then minimum value of $\left|a+b \omega+c \omega^2\right|$ is
questions.question_raw: a, b, c are integers, not all simultaneously equal and {tex}\omega{/tex} is cube root of unity ({tex}\omega \neq{/tex} 1), then minimum value of {tex}\left|a+b \omega+c \omega^2\right|{/tex} is
questions.explanation:
Given that a, b, c are integers not all equal and {tex}\omega{/tex} is cube root of unity {tex}\ne{/tex} 1, then {tex}\left|a+b \omega+c \omega^2\right|{/tex}
= {tex}\left|a+b\left(\frac{-1+i \sqrt{3}}{2}\right)+c\left(\frac{-1-i \sqrt{3}}{2}\right)\right|{/tex}
= {tex}\left|\left(\frac{2 a-b-c}{2}\right)+i\left(\frac{b \sqrt{3}-c \sqrt{3}}{2}\right)\right|{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{(2 a-b-c)^2+3(b-c)^2}{/tex}
{tex}\sqrt{\frac{1}{2}\left[(a-b)^2+(b-c)^2+(c-a)^2\right]}{/tex}
R.H.S. will be minimum when a = b = c, but according to the question, we cannot take a = b = c.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The minimum value is obtained when any two are zero and third is a minimum magnitude integer i.e. 1.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} b = c = 0, a = 1; gives us the minimum value 1 .

questions.question: Let z be a complex number such that $\left|\frac{z-2 i}{z+i}\right|=2, z \neq-i$. Then z lies on the circle of radius 2 and centre
questions.question_raw: Let z be a complex number such that {tex}\left|\frac{z-2 i}{z+i}\right|=2, z \neq-i{/tex}. Then z lies on the circle of radius 2 and centre
questions.explanation:
Given a expression {tex}\left|\frac{z-2 i}{z+i}\right|{/tex} = 2
Take square both sides and apply z{tex}\bar z{/tex} = |z|2
(z - 2i) ({tex}\bar z{/tex} + 2i) = 4(z + i)({tex}\bar z{/tex} - i)
z{tex}\bar z{/tex} + 4 + 2i(z - {tex}\bar z{/tex}) = 4(z{tex}\bar z{/tex} + 1 + i)({tex}\bar z{/tex} - z)
3z{tex}\bar z{/tex} -6i(z - {tex}\bar z{/tex}) = 0
Put z = x + iy
x2 + y2 - 2i(2iy) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 + y2 + 4y = 0
So, here g ≡ 0, f ≡ 2  
Centre ≡ (0, –2)

questions.question: Let S1 = $\left\{z_1 \in C:\left|z_1-3\right|=\frac{1}{2}\right\}$ and S2 = $\left\{z_2 \in C:\left|z_2-\right| z_2+1||=\left|z_2+\right| z_2-1||\right\} $. Then, for z_1 $\in$ S_1 and z_2 $\in$ S_2, the least value of |z_2 - z_1| is:
questions.question_raw: Let S1 = {tex}\left\{z_1 \in C:\left|z_1-3\right|=\frac{1}{2}\right\}{/tex} and S2 = {tex}\left\{z_2 \in C:\left|z_2-\right| z_2+1||=\left|z_2+\right| z_2-1||\right\} {/tex}. Then, for z1 {tex}\in{/tex} S1 and z2 {tex}\in{/tex} S2, the least value of |z2 - z1| is:
questions.explanation:
|z2 +|z2 - 1||2 = |z2 - |z2 + 1||2
{tex}\Rightarrow\left(z_2+\left|z_2-1\right|\right)\left(\bar{z}_2+\left|z_2-1\right|\right){/tex} = {tex}\left(z_2-\left|z_2+1\right|\right)\left(\bar{z}_2-\left(z_2+1\right)\right){/tex}
z2{tex}\left[\left(\bar{z}_2+\left|z_2-1\right|\right)-\left(\bar{z}_2-\left|z_2+1\right|\right)\right]{/tex} + {tex}\bar{z}_2\left(\left|z_2-1\right|+\left|z_2+1\right|\right){/tex}
= |z2 + 1|2 - |z2 - 1|2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (z2 + {tex}\bar z_2{/tex})(|z2 - 1 + |z2 + 1|) = 2(z2 + {tex}\bar z_2{/tex})
{tex}\Rightarrow\left( z _2+\overline{ z }_2\right){/tex}{tex}\left(\left| z _2-1\right|+\left| z _2+1\right|-2\right){/tex} = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} z2 + {tex}\bar z_2{/tex} = 0 or |z2 - 1| + |z2 + 1| - 2 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} z2 lie on imaginary axis. Or on real axis with in (-1, 1)
Also we have |z1 - 3| = {tex}\frac 12{/tex} lie on circle having centre 3 and radius {tex}\frac 12{/tex}.

Now, min|z2 - z1| {tex}=\frac{5}{2}-1=\frac{3}{2}{/tex}

questions.question: The locus of z which lies in shaded region (excluding the boundaries) is best represented by

In the figure, we see that.
AB = AC = AD = 2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} BCD is an arc of a circle with centre at A and radius 2.
Shaded region is exterior part of this sector ABCDA.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} For any point represented by z on arc BCD we should have
