questions.question: Let $\alpha ,\beta$ be the roots of the equation $x^2-\sqrt{2} x$ + 2 = 0. Then $\alpha^{14}+\beta^{14}$ is equal to
questions.question_raw: Let {tex}\alpha ,\beta{/tex} be the roots of the equation {tex}x^2-\sqrt{2} x{/tex} + 2 = 0. Then {tex}\alpha^{14}+\beta^{14}{/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation:
Given, x2 - {tex}\sqrt{2} x{/tex} + 2 = 0
x = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{2} \pm \sqrt{2-8}}{2}=\frac{\sqrt{2} \pm \sqrt{6} i}{2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \pm \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} i{/tex}
{tex}\alpha=\frac{1}{2}+\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} i{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{2} e^{\frac{i \pi}{3}} \& \beta=\sqrt{2} e^{\frac{-i \pi}{3}}{/tex}
{tex}\alpha^{14}=2^7 e^{\frac{i 14 \pi}{3}}{/tex} {tex}=128\left[e^{\frac{i 2 \pi}{3}}\right] ; \beta^{14}=128\left[e^{\frac{-i 2 \pi}{3}}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\alpha^{14}+\beta^{14}=128(2) \cos \left(\frac{2 \pi}{3}\right){/tex} = -128

questions.question: Let $\alpha, \beta$ be the roots of the quadratic equation x^2 + $\sqrt 6x$ + 3 = 0. Then $\frac{\alpha^{23}+\beta^{23}+\alpha^{14}+\beta^{14}}{\alpha^{15}+\beta^{15}+\alpha^{10}+\beta^{10}}$ is equal to
questions.question_raw: Let {tex}\alpha, \beta{/tex} be the roots of the quadratic equation x2 + {tex}\sqrt 6x{/tex} + 3 = 0. Then {tex}\frac{\alpha^{23}+\beta^{23}+\alpha^{14}+\beta^{14}}{\alpha^{15}+\beta^{15}+\alpha^{10}+\beta^{10}}{/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation:
Given, x2 + {tex}\sqrt{6}{/tex}x + 3 = 0 
{tex}\alpha, \beta=\frac{-\sqrt{6} \pm \sqrt{6-12}}{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-\sqrt{6} \pm \sqrt{6} i }{2}=\sqrt{3} e ^{ \pm \frac{3 \pi i }{4}}{/tex}
Put value of {tex}\alpha, \beta{/tex} in required expression.
= {tex}\frac{(\sqrt{3})^{23}\left(2 \cos \frac{69 \pi}{4}\right)+(\sqrt{3})^{14}\left(2 \cos \frac{42 \pi}{4}\right)}{(\sqrt{3})^{15}\left(2 \cos \frac{45 \pi}{4}\right)+(\sqrt{3})^{10}\left(2 \cos \frac{30 \pi}{4}\right)}{/tex}
= {tex}(\sqrt{3})^8{/tex} = 81

questions.question: The sum of all the roots of the equation |x^2 - 8x + 15| - 2x + 7 = 0 is:
questions.question_raw: The sum of all the roots of the equation |x2 - 8x + 15| - 2x + 7 = 0 is:
questions.explanation:
|x2 - 8x - 15| = {tex}\left\{\begin{array}{l} x^2-8 x+15, x \leq 3 \text { or } x \geq 5 \\ -\left(x^2-8 x+15\right), 3<x<5 \end{array}\right.{/tex}
Case 1: x {tex}\le{/tex} 3 or x {tex}\ge{/tex} 5
x2 - 8x + 15 - 2x + 7 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 5 + {tex}\sqrt3{/tex}
Case 2: 3 < x < 5, x2 - 8x + 15 + 2x - 7 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 4
Hence sum = 9 + {tex}\sqrt3{/tex}

questions.question: The number of real roots of the equation $\sqrt{x^2-4 x+3}$ + $\sqrt{x^2-9}$ = $\sqrt{4 x^2-14 x+6}$, is:
questions.question_raw: The number of real roots of the equation {tex}\sqrt{x^2-4 x+3}{/tex} + {tex}\sqrt{x^2-9}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{4 x^2-14 x+6}{/tex}, is:
questions.explanation:
Since {tex}\sqrt{x^2-4 x+3}{/tex} + {tex}\sqrt{x^2-9}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{4 x^2-14 x+6}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt{(x-1)(x-3)}+\sqrt{(x-3)(x+3)}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt{4\left(x-\frac{12}{4}\right)\left(x-\frac{2}{4}\right)}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt{x-3}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 3 which is in domain
or {tex}\sqrt{x-1}+\sqrt{x+1}=\sqrt{4 x-2}{/tex}
Squaring on both sides we get
{tex}2 \sqrt{(x-1)(x-3)}{/tex} = 2x - 4
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 + 2x - 3 = x2 - 4x + 4 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 6x = 7
x = {tex}\frac 76{/tex} not in domain.

questions.question: Let $\alpha$ and $\beta$ be the roots of the equation x^2 + (2i - 1) = 0. Then, the value of |$\alpha^8+\beta^8$| is equal to:
questions.question_raw: Let {tex}\alpha{/tex} and {tex}\beta{/tex} be the roots of the equation x2 + (2i - 1) = 0. Then, the value of |{tex}\alpha^8+\beta^8{/tex}| is equal to:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\because{/tex} x2 + (2i - 1) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} X2 = 1 - 2i
Since, {tex}\alpha, \beta{/tex} are the roots of x2 + (2i - 1) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow \alpha^2{/tex} = 1 - 2i, {tex}\beta{/tex}2 = 1 - 2i
{tex}\left|\alpha^8+\beta^8\right|=\left|2 \alpha^8\right|=2\left|\alpha^2\right|^4{/tex} = 2{tex}\sqrt{5}^4{/tex} = 50

questions.question: Let $\alpha$ be a root of the equation 1 + x^2 + x^4 = 0. Then the value of $\alpha^{1011}+\alpha^{2022}-\alpha^{3033}$ is equal to:
questions.question_raw: Let {tex}\alpha{/tex} be a root of the equation 1 + x2 + x4 = 0. Then the value of {tex}\alpha^{1011}+\alpha^{2022}-\alpha^{3033}{/tex} is equal to:
questions.explanation:
x4 + x2 + 1 = 0 can be written as
(x2 + x + 1) (x2 - x + 1) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow x= \pm \omega, \pm \omega^2{/tex} where {tex}\omega=1^\frac {1}{3}{/tex} and imaginary.
So {tex}\alpha^{1011}+\alpha^{2022}-\alpha^{3033}{/tex} = 1 + 1 - 1 = 1

questions.question: Let $\alpha, \beta$ be two roots of the equation x^2 + (20)^$^\frac {1}2$ x + (5)^$^\frac 12$ = 0. Then $\alpha^8+\beta^8$ is equal to
questions.question_raw: Let {tex}\alpha, \beta{/tex} be two roots of the equation x2 + (20){tex}^\frac {1}2{/tex} x + (5){tex}^\frac 12{/tex} = 0. Then {tex}\alpha^8+\beta^8{/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation:
{tex}x^2+5^{\frac{1}{2}}=-(20)^{\frac{1}{4}} x {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\left(x^2+\sqrt{5}\right)^2=\sqrt{20} x^2{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x^4+5+2 \sqrt{5} x^2=2 \sqrt{5} x^2 {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x^4=-5 \Rightarrow x^8{/tex} = 25
So, {tex}\alpha^8+\beta^8{/tex} = 50

questions.question: The number of real solutions of the equation, x^2 - |x| - 12 = 0 is:
questions.question_raw: The number of real solutions of the equation, x2 - |x| - 12 = 0 is:
questions.explanation:
|x|2 - |x| - 12 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (|x| + 3) (|x| - 4) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |x| = 4 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\pm{/tex}2 {tex}[\because|x|+3 \neq 0]{/tex}

questions.question: If $\alpha, \beta \in R$ are such that 1 - 2i (here i^2 = -1) is a root of z^2 + $\alpha z+\beta$ = 0, then ($\alpha-\beta$) is equal to:
questions.question_raw: If {tex}\alpha, \beta \in R{/tex} are such that 1 - 2i (here i2 = -1) is a root of z2 + {tex}\alpha z+\beta{/tex} = 0, then ({tex}\alpha-\beta{/tex}) is equal to:
questions.explanation:
We have {tex}\alpha, \beta \in R{/tex} roots are 1 - 2i and 1 + 2i
Sum of roots = -{tex}\alpha \Rightarrow{/tex} -{tex}\alpha{/tex} = 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\alpha{/tex} = -2
Since, product of roots = {tex}\beta{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \beta{/tex} = (1)2 - (2i)2 = 5 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\alpha{/tex} - {tex}\beta{/tex} = -7

questions.question: If the set $\left\{\operatorname{Re}\left(\frac{z-\bar{z}+z \bar{z}}{2-3 z+5 \bar{z}}\right): z \in C , \operatorname{Re}(z)=3\right\}$ is equal to the interval $(\alpha, \beta],$ then 24$(\beta-\alpha)$ is equal to
questions.question_raw: If the set {tex}\left\{\operatorname{Re}\left(\frac{z-\bar{z}+z \bar{z}}{2-3 z+5 \bar{z}}\right): z \in C , \operatorname{Re}(z)=3\right\}{/tex} is equal to the interval {tex}(\alpha, \beta],{/tex} then 24{tex}(\beta-\alpha){/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation:
Let z1 = {tex}\left(\frac{z-\bar{z}+z \bar{z}}{2-3 z+5 \bar{z}}\right){/tex}
Let z = 3 + iy & {tex}\bar{z}{/tex} = 3 - iy
{tex}\Rightarrow z_1=\frac{2 i y+\left(9+y^2\right)}{2-3(3+i y)+5(3-i y)}=\frac{9+y^2+i(2 y)}{8-8 i y} \\ {/tex}
= {tex}\frac{\left(9+y^2\right)+i(2 y)}{8(1-i y)}{/tex}
So, Re(z1) = {tex}\frac{\left(9+y^2\right)-2 y^2}{8\left(1+y^2\right)}=\frac{9-y^2}{8\left(1+y^2\right)}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{1}{8}\left[\frac{10}{1+y^2}-1\right]{/tex}
1 + y2 {tex}\in{/tex} [1, {tex}\infty{/tex}] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{10}{1+y^2}-1 \in(-1,9]{/tex}
{tex}\operatorname{Re}\left(z_1\right) \in\left(\frac{-1}{8}, \frac{9}{8}\right){/tex}; {tex}\alpha=\frac{-1}{8}, \beta=\frac{9}{8}{/tex}
{tex}24(\beta-\alpha) {/tex} = 24{tex}\left(\frac{9}{8}+\frac{1}{8}\right){/tex} = 30

questions.question: Let $S=z=i\left(z^2+\operatorname{Re}(z)\right\}$. Then, $\sum\limits_{z \in S}|z|^2$ is equal to:
questions.question_raw: Let {tex}S=z=i\left(z^2+\operatorname{Re}(z)\right\}{/tex}. Then, {tex}\sum\limits_{z \in S}|z|^2{/tex} is equal to:
questions.explanation:
Let Z = x + iy, x, y {tex}\in{/tex} R
x - iy = i(x2 - y2 + (2xy)i + x)
x = -2xy ...(i)
-y = -y2 + x2 + x ...(ii)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 0, y = -{tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} (from (i))
If x {tex}\neq{/tex} 0, then y = 0, 1
If y = -{tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}, then x = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}, -{tex}\frac{3}{2}{/tex}
then Z = 0 + i0, 0 + i, {tex}\frac{1}{2}-\frac{i}{2},-\frac{3}{2}-\frac{i}{2}{/tex}
So, {tex}\sum_\limits{ z \in S}|z|^2{/tex} = 0 + 1 + {tex}\frac{1}{2}+\frac{10}{4}{/tex} = 4

questions.question: Let the complex number z = x + iy be such that $\frac{2 z-3 i}{2 z+i}$ is purely imaginary. If x + y^2 = 0, then y^4 + y^2 - y is equal to:
questions.question_raw: Let the complex number z = x + iy be such that {tex}\frac{2 z-3 i}{2 z+i}{/tex} is purely imaginary. If x + y2 = 0, then y4 + y2 - y is equal to:
questions.explanation:
Given, {tex}\frac{2 z-3 i}{2 z+i}{/tex} is purely imaginary
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{2 z-3 i}{2 z+i}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{2 \bar{z}+3 i}{2 \bar{z}-i}{/tex} = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4x2 + 4y2 - 4y - 3 = 0 ({tex}\because{/tex} z = x + iy)
Given that x + y2 = 0
So, y4 + y2 - y = {tex}\frac 34{/tex}

questions.question: Let S = {$z=x+i y: \frac{2 z-3 i}{4 z+2 i}$ is a real number}. Then which of the following is NOT correct?
questions.question_raw: Let S = {{tex}z=x+i y: \frac{2 z-3 i}{4 z+2 i}{/tex} is a real number}. Then which of the following is NOT correct?
questions.explanation:
{tex}\frac{2 z-3 i}{4 z+2 i} \in{/tex} R [{tex}\because{/tex} Put z = x + iy]
{tex}\frac{2(x+i y)-3 i}{4(x+i y)+2 i}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 x+(2 y-3) i}{4 x+(4 y+2) i} {/tex} {tex}\times \frac{4 x-(4 y+2) i}{4 x-(4 y+2) i}{/tex}
As imaginary part = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4x(2y -3) -2x(4y + 2) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 0, y {tex}\neq-\frac{1}{2}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} 16x2 + (4y + 2)2 {tex}\neq{/tex} 0]

questions.question: Let A = {$\theta \in(0,2 \pi): \frac{1+2 i \sin \theta}{1-i \sin \theta}$ is purely imaginary}. Then the sum of the elements in A is
questions.question_raw: Let A = {{tex}\theta \in(0,2 \pi): \frac{1+2 i \sin \theta}{1-i \sin \theta}{/tex} is purely imaginary}. Then the sum of the elements in A is
questions.explanation:
{tex}z=\frac{1+2 i \sin \theta}{1-i \sin \theta} \times \frac{1+i \sin \theta}{1+i \sin \theta}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} Rationalization]
z = {tex}\frac{1-2 \sin ^2 \theta+ i (3 \sin \theta)}{1+\sin ^2 \theta}{/tex}
As Re(z) = 0 [{tex}\because{/tex} Given z is purely imaginary]
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1-2 \sin ^2 \theta}{1+\sin ^2 \theta}=0 \Rightarrow \sin \theta=\frac{ \pm 1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}
So, A = {tex}\left\{\frac{\pi}{4}, \frac{3 \pi}{4}, \frac{5 \pi}{4}, \frac{7 \pi}{4}\right\}{/tex}
Sum of the elements in A = 4{tex}\pi{/tex}

Let {tex}9^{\tan ^2 x}= P \Rightarrow \frac{9}{ P }{/tex} + P = 10
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} P2 - 10P + 9 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (P - 9)(P - 1) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} P = 1, 9

tan2x = 0 or tan2x = 1
x = 0, {tex}\pm \frac{\pi}{4} {/tex} {tex}\therefore x \in\left(-\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex}

Then, {tex}\frac 16{/tex}(14 - 8{tex}\sqrt 3{/tex} - 14)2 = 32

questions.question:
For two non-zero complex number z_1 and z_2, if Re(z_1z_2) = 0 and Re(z_1 + z_2) = 0, then which of the following are possible?
 Im(z_1) > 0 and Im(z_2) > 0
 Im(z_1) < 0 and Im(z_2) > 0
 lm(z_1) > 0 and lm(z_2) < 0
 Im(z_1) < 0 and Im(z_2) < 0
questions.question_raw:
For two non-zero complex number z1 and z2, if Re(z1z2) = 0 and Re(z1 + z2) = 0, then which of the following are possible?

  1. Im(z1) > 0 and Im(z2) > 0
  2. Im(z1) < 0 and Im(z2) > 0
  3. lm(z1) > 0 and lm(z2) < 0
  4. Im(z1) < 0 and Im(z2) < 0
questions.explanation:
Let z1 = x1 + iy1 and z2 = x2 + iy2
Since, given Re(z1z2) = x1x2 - y1y2 = 0
and Re(z1 + z2) = x1 + x2 = 0
So, x1 and x2 are of opposite sign and y1 and y2 are of opposite sign.

questions.question: Let z_1 = 2 + 3i and z_2 = 3 + 4i. The set S = {z $\in$ C :|z - z_1|^2-|z - z_2|^2=|z_1 - z_2|^2} represents a

options.text: hyperbola with the length of the transverse axis 7
straight line with sum of its intercepts on the coordinate axes equals 14
straight line with the sum of its intercepts on the coordinate axes equals -18

questions.explanation:
|(x - 2) + i(y - 3)|2 - |(x - 3) + i(y - 4)|2
= |(2 + 3i - 3 - 4i)|2
((x - 2)2 + (y - 3)2 - ((x - 3)2 - (y - 4)2 = 1 + 1
After solving above eq., we get

questions.question: The number of integral values of k, for which one root of the equation 2x^2 - 8x+ k = 0 lies in the interval (1, 2) and its other root lies in the interval (2, 3), is:
questions.question_raw: The number of integral values of k, for which one root of the equation 2x2 - 8x+ k = 0 lies in the interval (1, 2) and its other root lies in the interval (2, 3), is:
questions.explanation:
Given equation is 2x2 - 8x + k = 0
Now, use the graph method of the given parabolic equation.

Here., f(1).f(2) < 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (k - 6)(k - 8) < 0
k {tex}\in{/tex} (6, 8)
Similarly, f(2).f(3) < 0
k {tex}\in{/tex} (6, 8)
So, integral value of k = 7

questions.question: Let $\lambda \neq$ 0 be a real number. Let $\alpha, \beta$ be the roots of the equation 14x^2 - 31x + 3 $ \lambda$= 0 and $\alpha, \gamma$ be the roots of the equation 35x^2 - 53x + 4$ \lambda$ = 0. Then $\frac{3 \alpha}{\beta} $ and $\frac{4 \alpha}{\gamma}$ are the roots of the equation:
questions.question_raw: Let {tex}\lambda \neq{/tex} 0 be a real number. Let {tex}\alpha, \beta{/tex} be the roots of the equation 14x2 - 31x + 3 {tex} \lambda{/tex}= 0 and {tex}\alpha, \gamma{/tex} be the roots of the equation 35x2 - 53x + 4{tex} \lambda{/tex} = 0. Then {tex}\frac{3 \alpha}{\beta} {/tex} and {tex}\frac{4 \alpha}{\gamma}{/tex} are the roots of the equation:
questions.explanation:
Since, 14x2 - 31x + 3{tex}\lambda{/tex} = 0
Now, {tex}\alpha+\beta=\frac{31}{14}{/tex} ...(i)
and {tex}\alpha \beta=\frac{3 \lambda}{14}{/tex} ...(ii)
35x2 - 53x + 4{tex}\lambda{/tex} = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow \alpha+\gamma=\frac{53}{35}{/tex} ...(iii)
and {tex}\alpha \gamma=\frac{4 \lambda}{35}{/tex} ...(iv)
{tex}\frac{\text { (ii) }}{\text { (iv) }} \Rightarrow \frac{\beta}{\gamma}=\frac{3 \times 35}{4 \times 14}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{15}{8} \Rightarrow \beta=\frac{15}{8} \gamma{/tex} and
{tex}\text { (i) }- \text { (iii) } \Rightarrow \beta-\gamma=\frac{31}{14}-\frac{53}{35}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{155-106}{70}=\frac{7}{10}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{15}{8} \gamma-\gamma=\frac{7}{10} \Rightarrow \gamma=\frac{4}{5}{/tex} and {tex}\beta=\frac{15}{8} \times \frac{4}{5}=\frac{3}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \alpha=\frac{31}{14}-\beta{/tex} {tex}=\frac{31}{14}-\frac{3}{2}=\frac{5}{7}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \lambda=\frac{14}{3} \alpha \beta=\frac{14}{3} \times \frac{5}{7} \times \frac{3}{2}{/tex} = 5
So, sum of roots {tex}\frac{3 \alpha}{\beta}+\frac{4 \alpha}{\gamma}=\left(\frac{3 \alpha \gamma+4 \alpha \beta}{\beta \gamma}\right){/tex}
= {tex}\frac{\left(3 \times \frac{4 \lambda}{35}+4 \times \frac{3 \lambda}{14}\right)}{\beta \gamma}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{12 \lambda(14+35)}{14 \times 35 \beta \gamma}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{49 \times 12 \times 5}{490 \times \frac{3}{2} \times \frac{4}{5}}{/tex} = 5
and product of roots
{tex}=\frac{3 \alpha}{\beta} \times \frac{4 \alpha}{\gamma}=\frac{12 \alpha^2}{\beta \gamma}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{12 \times \frac{25}{49}}{\frac{3}{2} \times \frac{4}{5}}=\frac{250}{49}{/tex}
So, required equation is {tex}x^2-5 x+\frac{250}{49}{/tex} = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 49x2 - 245x + 250 = 0

questions.question: The equation x^2 - 4x + [x] + 3 = x[x], where [x] denotes the greatest integer function, has:
questions.question_raw: The equation x2 - 4x + [x] + 3 = x[x], where [x] denotes the greatest integer function, has:
questions.explanation:
Since, x2 - 4x + [x] + 3 = x[x]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 - 4x + 3 + [x] - x[x] = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x - 1)(x - 3) - [x] (x - 1) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x - 1)(x - [x] - 3) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x - 1)({x} - 3) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 1, {x} - 3 {tex}\neq{/tex} 0
Only one solution x = 1 in (-{tex}\infty{/tex}, {tex}\infty{/tex})

questions.question: Let S be the set of all ($\alpha, \beta$), $\pi<\alpha, \beta<2 \pi$, for which the complex number $\frac{1-i \sin \alpha}{1+2 i \sin \alpha}$ is purely imaginary and $\frac{1-i \cos \beta}{1+2 i \sin \beta}$ is purely real. Let Z$\alpha \beta$ = sin 2$\alpha+i \cos 2 \beta$, ($\alpha, \beta$) $\in$ S. Then $\sum_\limits{(\alpha, \beta) \in S }\left(i Z_{\alpha \beta}+\frac{1}{i \bar{Z} \alpha \beta}\right)$ is equal to
questions.question_raw: Let S be the set of all ({tex}\alpha, \beta{/tex}), {tex}\pi<\alpha, \beta<2 \pi{/tex}, for which the complex number {tex}\frac{1-i \sin \alpha}{1+2 i \sin \alpha}{/tex} is purely imaginary and {tex}\frac{1-i \cos \beta}{1+2 i \sin \beta}{/tex} is purely real. Let Z{tex}\alpha \beta{/tex} = sin 2{tex}\alpha+i \cos 2 \beta{/tex}, ({tex}\alpha, \beta{/tex}) {tex}\in{/tex} S. Then {tex}\sum_\limits{(\alpha, \beta) \in S }\left(i Z_{\alpha \beta}+\frac{1}{i \bar{Z} \alpha \beta}\right){/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation:
Given interval is {tex}\pi<\alpha, \beta<2 \pi{/tex} and expression {tex}\frac{1-i \sin \alpha}{1+2 i \sin \alpha}{/tex}
Now, {tex}\frac{(1-i \sin \alpha)(1-2 i \sin \alpha)}{1+4 \sin ^2 \alpha}{/tex} = Purely imaginary
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1-2 \sin ^2 \alpha}{1+4 \sin ^2 \alpha}=0 \Rightarrow \sin ^2 \alpha{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow \alpha=\left[\frac{5 \pi}{4}, \frac{7 \pi}{4}\right]{/tex}
Take, {tex}\frac{1-i \cos \beta}{1+2 i \sin \beta)}{/tex} = Purely real
{tex}\frac{(1-i \cos \beta)(1-2 i \cos \beta)}{1+4 \cos ^2 \beta}{/tex} = Purely real
{tex}\Rightarrow 3 \cos \beta=0 {/tex} so, {tex}\beta=\frac{2 \pi}{2}{/tex}
Take, Z{tex}\alpha \beta=\sin \frac{5 \pi}{2}+i \cos 5 \pi{/tex} = 1 - i
Required values
{tex}=\left[i(1-i)+\frac{1}{i(1+i)}\right]+{/tex} {tex}\left[i(-1-i)+\frac{1}{i(-1+i)}\right]{/tex}
= i(-2i) + {tex}\frac{1}{1} \frac{2 i}{(-2)} \Rightarrow{/tex} 2 - 1 = 1

questions.question: Let $\alpha , \beta$ be the roots of the equation x^2 - $\sqrt{2} x+\sqrt{6}$ = 0 and $\frac{1}{\alpha^2}+1, \frac{1}{\beta^2}$+1 be the roots of the equation x^2 + ax + b = 0. Then the roots of the equation x^2 - (a + b - 2)x + (a + b + 2) = 0 are:
questions.question_raw: Let {tex}\alpha , \beta{/tex} be the roots of the equation x2 - {tex}\sqrt{2} x+\sqrt{6}{/tex} = 0 and {tex}\frac{1}{\alpha^2}+1, \frac{1}{\beta^2}{/tex}+1 be the roots of the equation x2 + ax + b = 0. Then the roots of the equation x2 - (a + b - 2)x + (a + b + 2) = 0 are:

options.text: real and exactly one of them is positive

questions.explanation:
For x2 + ax + b = 0
Sum of roots = a = {tex}\frac{-1}{\alpha^2}-\frac{1}{\beta^2}{/tex} - 2
Product of roots = b = {tex}\frac{1}{\alpha^2}+\frac{1}{\beta^2}+1+\frac{1}{\alpha^2 \beta^2}{/tex}
a + b = {tex}\frac{1}{(\alpha \beta)^2}-1=\frac{1}{6}-1=-\frac{5}{6}{/tex} {tex}[\because \alpha \beta=\sqrt{6}]{/tex}
x2 - {tex}\left(-\frac{5}{6}-2\right) x+\left(2-\frac{5}{6}\right){/tex} = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 6x2 + 17x + 7 = 0
x = -{tex}\frac{7}{3}{/tex}, x = -{tex}\frac 12{/tex} are the roots
Both roots are real and negative.

questions.question:
If $\alpha, \beta$ are the roots of the equation x^2 - $\left(5+3^{\sqrt{\log _3 5}}-5^{\sqrt{\log _5 3}}\right)$ + $3\left(3^{\left(\log _3 5\right)^{\frac{1}{3}}}-5^{(\log 3)^{\frac{2}{3}}}-1\right)$ = 0
Then the equation, whose roots are $\alpha+\frac{1}{\beta}$ and $\beta+\frac{1}{\alpha}$,
questions.question_raw:
If {tex}\alpha, \beta{/tex} are the roots of the equation x2 - {tex}\left(5+3^{\sqrt{\log _3 5}}-5^{\sqrt{\log _5 3}}\right){/tex} + {tex}3\left(3^{\left(\log _3 5\right)^{\frac{1}{3}}}-5^{(\log 3)^{\frac{2}{3}}}-1\right){/tex} = 0
Then the equation, whose roots are {tex}\alpha+\frac{1}{\beta}{/tex} and {tex}\beta+\frac{1}{\alpha}{/tex},
questions.explanation:
Given expression is x2 -{tex}\left(5+3^{\sqrt{\log _3 5}}-5^{\sqrt{\log _3 5}}\right){/tex} + 3{tex}\left(3^{\left(\log _3 5\right)^{\frac{1}{3}}}-5^{\left(\log _5 3\right)^{\frac{2}{3}}}-1\right){/tex} = 0
Take, {tex}3^{\sqrt{\log _3 5}}=3^{\sqrt{\log _3 5}}\cdot \sqrt{\log _3 5} \sqrt{\log _3 5}{/tex} {tex}=3^{\sqrt{\log _3 5}} \cdot \sqrt{\log _3 5}{/tex}
= 3{tex}{\left(\log _3 5\right)^{\sqrt{\log _5 3}}}=5^{\sqrt{\log _5 3}}{/tex}
{tex}3^{\sqrt[3]{\log _3 5}}=3^{\log _3 5 \cdot \sqrt[3]{\left(\log _5 3\right)^2}}{/tex} = {tex}\left(3^{\log _3 5}\right)^{\left(\log _5 3\right)^\frac{2}{3}}{/tex}
{tex}=5^{\left(\log _5 3\right)^\frac{2}{3}}{/tex}
After putting the value the equation is x2 - 5x - 3 - 0 and roots are {tex}\alpha{/tex} & {tex}\beta{/tex}.
Then, {tex}\alpha{/tex} + {tex}\beta{/tex} = 5; {tex}\alpha{/tex}{tex}\beta{/tex} = -3.
New roots are {tex}\alpha+\frac{1}{\beta} {/tex} & {tex}\beta+\frac{1}{\alpha}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\alpha \beta+1}{\beta}{/tex} & {tex} \frac{\alpha \beta+1}{\alpha} {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{-2}{\beta} {/tex} & {tex} \frac{-2}{\alpha}{/tex}
Let {tex}\frac{-2}{\alpha}=p {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \alpha=\frac{-2}{p}{/tex}
As {tex}\alpha{/tex}2 - 5{tex}\alpha{/tex} - 3 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow\left(\frac{-2}{p}\right)^2-5\left(\frac{-2}{p}\right){/tex} - 3 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{4}{p^2}+\frac{10}{p}{/tex} - 3 = 0
4 + 10p - 3p2 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3p2 - 10p - {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 0
Now replace ’p’ by 'x’ to get required equation
3x2 - 10x - 4 = 0

questions.question: Let O be the origin and A be the point z_1 = 1 + 2i. If B is the point z_2, Re (z_2) < 0, such that OAB is a right angled isosceles triangle with OB as hypotenuse, then which of the following is NOT true?
questions.question_raw: Let O be the origin and A be the point z1 = 1 + 2i. If B is the point z2, Re (z2) < 0, such that OAB is a right angled isosceles triangle with OB as hypotenuse, then which of the following is NOT true?
questions.explanation:
Given point A with complex number z1 = 1 + zi
Here, {tex}\angle OAB{/tex} = 90o and OB is hypotenuse, AB {tex}\perp{/tex} OA.

Therefore, {tex}(\overrightarrow{ AB })=(\overrightarrow{ OA }) \cdot e ^{-\frac{ i \pi}{2}}{/tex}
(z2 - (1 + 2i)) = (0 - (1 + 2i)){tex}\left(\cos \left(-\frac{\pi}{2}\right)+i \sin \left(-\frac{\pi}{2}\right)\right){/tex}
z2 - (1 + 2i) = -(1 + 2i)(-i)
z2 = (1 + 2i) + (i - 2) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} z2 = -1 + 3i
Now, |2z1 - z2| = |2 + 4i + 1 - 3i| = |3 + i|
|2z1 - z2| = {tex}\sqrt{9+1}=\sqrt{10} {/tex}

Then, {tex}\alpha{/tex}2 + {tex}\beta{/tex}2 = ({tex}\alpha{/tex} + {tex}\beta{/tex})2 - 2{tex}\alpha{/tex}{tex}\beta{/tex}
let f(a) = (3 - a)2 - 2(1 - 2a)
f(a) = a2 -2a + 7
f(a) = (a - 1)2 +6
f(a)min. = 6

questions.question: Let f(x) be a quadratic polynomial such that f(-2) + f(3) = 0. If one of the roots of f(x) = 0 is -1, then the sum of the roots of f(x) = 0 is equal to
questions.question_raw: Let f(x) be a quadratic polynomial such that f(-2) + f(3) = 0. If one of the roots of f(x) = 0 is -1, then the sum of the roots of f(x) = 0 is equal to
questions.explanation:
We are given that
f(-2) + f(3) = 0 & f(-1) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(x) = (x + 1)(ax + b)
Now f(-2) + f(3) = -1(-2a + b) + 4(3a + b) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2a - b + 12a + 4b = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 14a + 3b = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{-b}{a}=\frac{14}{3}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Sum of roots = {tex}\left(-1+\frac{-b}{a}\right)=-1+\frac{14}{3}=\frac{11}{3}{/tex}

questions.question: The number of distinct real roots of x^4 - 4x + 1 = 0 is:
questions.question_raw: The number of distinct real roots of x4 - 4x + 1 = 0 is:
questions.explanation:
Let f(x) = x4 - 4x + 1
Differentiate w.r.t. x.
f'(x) = 4x3 - 4 ...(i)
Take f'(x) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 1
Again differentiate equation (i) w.r.t. x
f"(x) = 12x
Put x = 1 in f"(x)
f"(1) = 12 > 0
Then x = 1 is point of minima.
Now, put x = 1 in function f(x) then,
f(1) = -2 and f(0) = 1

Hence 2 solutions.

questions.question: If z = x + iy satisfies |z| - 2 = 0 and |z - i| - |z + 5i| = 0, then
questions.question_raw: If z = x + iy satisfies |z| - 2 = 0 and |z - i| - |z + 5i| = 0, then
questions.explanation:
Given expression is |z - i| - |z + 5i| = 0
Put z = x + iy
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |x +( y -1)i| = |x +(y + 5)i|
x2 + (y - 1)2 = x2 + (y + 5)2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (y - 1)2 - (y + 5)2 = 0
(2y + 4)(-6) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = -2; {tex}\therefore{/tex} x2 + (-2)2 = 4 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 0
Here, point is (0, -2)
So, option (x + 2y + 4 = 0) satisfying the point.

questions.question: If the sum of the squares of the reciprocals of the roots $\alpha$ and $\beta$ of the equation 3x^2 + $\lambda$x - 1 = 0 is 15, then 6$\left(\alpha^3+\beta^3\right)^2$ is equal to:
questions.question_raw: If the sum of the squares of the reciprocals of the roots {tex}\alpha{/tex} and {tex}\beta{/tex} of the equation 3x2 + {tex}\lambda{/tex}x - 1 = 0 is 15, then 6{tex}\left(\alpha^3+\beta^3\right)^2{/tex} is equal to:
questions.explanation:
Given {tex}\alpha, \beta{/tex} roots of equation 3x2 + {tex}\lambda{/tex}x - 1 = 0
So, {tex}\alpha+\beta=\frac{-\lambda}{3}, \alpha \beta=\frac{-1}{3}{/tex}
According to given relation of roots,
{tex}\frac{1}{\alpha^2}+\frac{1}{\beta^2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{(\alpha+\beta)^2-2 \alpha \beta}{\alpha^2 \beta^2}{/tex} = 15
{tex}\lambda^2=9 \Rightarrow \lambda= \pm 3{/tex}
Now, {tex}\alpha^2+\beta^2=(\alpha+\beta)^2-2 \alpha \beta{/tex}
{tex}\alpha^2+\beta^2=\left(\frac{ \pm 3}{3}\right)^2+\frac{2}{3}=\frac{5}{3}{/tex}
Take, 6{tex}\left(\alpha^3+\beta^3\right)^2{/tex} = 6 {tex}\left((\alpha+\beta)\left(\alpha^2+\beta^2-\alpha \beta\right)\right){/tex}
= 6{tex}\left(\left(-\frac{( \pm 3)}{3}\right)\left(\frac{5}{3}+\frac{1}{3}\right)\right)^2{/tex} = 6(1 {tex}\times{/tex} 2)2 = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 = 24

= ln 2 + ln{tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex} + ln{tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} = -In 3

questions.question: If $e^{\left(\cos ^2 x+\cos ^4 x+\cos ^6 x+\ldots \infty\right) \log _e 2}$ satisfies the equation t^2 - 9t + 8 = 0, then the value of $\frac{2 \sin x}{\sin x+\sqrt{3} \cos x}\left(0<x<\frac{\pi}{2}\right)$ is
questions.question_raw: If {tex}e^{\left(\cos ^2 x+\cos ^4 x+\cos ^6 x+\ldots \infty\right) \log _e 2}{/tex} satisfies the equation t2 - 9t + 8 = 0, then the value of {tex}\frac{2 \sin x}{\sin x+\sqrt{3} \cos x}\left(0<x<\frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} is
questions.explanation:
{tex}e^{\left(\cos ^2 x+\cos ^4 x+\ldots \infty\right) \log _e 2}{/tex} = {tex}2^{\cos ^2 x+\cos ^4 x+\ldots \infty}=2^{\cot ^2 x}{/tex}
Now t2 - 9t + 9 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} t = 1, 8
{tex}\Rightarrow 2^{\cot ^2 x}{/tex} = 1, 8 {tex}\Rightarrow \cot ^2 x{/tex} = 0, 3
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{2 \sin x}{\sin x+\sqrt{3} \cos x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2}{1+\sqrt{3} \cot x}=\frac{2}{4}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{25(1+2 i)^6}{26 i} {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow(-i) \frac{25}{26}(7+24 i)(3-4 i){/tex}

questions.question: Let p, q and r be real numbers (p $\neq$ q, r $\neq$ 0), such that the roots of the equation $\frac{1}{x+p}+\frac{1}{x+q}=\frac{1}{r}$ are equal in magnitude but opposite in sign, then the sum of squares of these roots is equal to
questions.question_raw: Let p, q and r be real numbers (p {tex}\neq{/tex} q, r {tex}\neq{/tex} 0), such that the roots of the equation {tex}\frac{1}{x+p}+\frac{1}{x+q}=\frac{1}{r}{/tex} are equal in magnitude but opposite in sign, then the sum of squares of these roots is equal to
questions.explanation:
{tex}\frac{1}{x+p}+\frac{1}{x+q}=\frac{1}{r} {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{x+p+x+q}{(x+p)(x+q)}=\frac{1}{r}{/tex}
(2x + p + q) r = x2 + px + qx + pq
x2 + (p + q - 2r)x + pq - pr - qr = 0
Let {tex}\alpha{/tex} and {tex}\beta{/tex} be the roots.
{tex}\therefore \alpha+\beta{/tex} = -(p + q - 2r) ...(i)
& {tex} \alpha \beta{/tex} = pq - pr - qr ...(ii)
{tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\alpha{/tex} = -{tex}\beta{/tex} (given);  {tex}\therefore{/tex} in eq. (i), we get
{tex}\Rightarrow \alpha+\beta{/tex} = -(p + q - 2r) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} p + q = 2r ...(iii)
Now, {tex}\alpha^2+\beta^2=(\alpha+\beta)^2-2 \alpha \beta{/tex}
From (ii) and (iii)
= -2(pq - pr - qr) = -2pq + 2r(p + q) = p2 + q2

{tex}-\frac{\pi}{2}+\theta+\theta=\pi \Rightarrow 2 \theta=\frac{3 \pi}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\arg \left(z_2\right)=\theta=\frac{3 \pi}{4}, {/tex} {tex}\arg z_1=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}

questions.question: If z is a complex number such that $\frac{z-i}{z-1}$ is purely imaginary, then the minimum value of |z - (3 + 3i)| is:
questions.question_raw: If z is a complex number such that {tex}\frac{z-i}{z-1}{/tex} is purely imaginary, then the minimum value of |z - (3 + 3i)| is:
questions.explanation:
Let z = x + iy
Since, {tex}\frac{z-i}{z-1}{/tex} is purely imaginary number
{tex}\therefore \frac{x+i(y-1)}{(x-1)+i(y)} \times \frac{(x-1)-i y}{(x-1)-i y} {/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{x(x-1)+y(y-1)+i(-y-x+1)}{(x-1)^2+y^2} {/tex}
is purely imaginary number
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x(x - 1) + y(y - 1) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow\left(x-\frac{1}{2}\right)^2+\left(y-\frac{1}{2}\right)^2=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}

Pc = {tex}\sqrt{\left(3-\frac{1}{2}\right)^2+\left(3-\frac{1}{2}\right)^2}=\frac{5}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\left.\therefore|z-(3+3 i)|\right|_{\min }=|P C|-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{5}{\sqrt{2}}-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}=2 \sqrt{2}{/tex}

questions.question: Let a, b, c be the sides of a triangle where a $\neq$ b $\neq$ c and $\lambda$ $\in$ R. If the roots of the equation x^2 + 2(a + b + c)x + 3$\lambda$(ab + bc + ca) = 0 are real, then
questions.question_raw: Let a, b, c be the sides of a triangle where a {tex}\neq{/tex} b {tex}\neq{/tex} c and {tex}\lambda{/tex} {tex}\in{/tex} R. If the roots of the equation x2 + 2(a + b + c)x + 3{tex}\lambda{/tex}(ab + bc + ca) = 0 are real, then
questions.explanation:
{tex}\because{/tex} a, b, c are sides of a triangle and a {tex}\ne{/tex} b {tex}\ne{/tex} c
{tex}\therefore{/tex} |a - b| < |c| {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a2 + b2 - 2ab < c2 ...(i)
Similarly,
b2 + c2 - 2bc < a2 ...(ii); c2 + a2 - 2ca < b2 ...(iii)
On adding, (i), (ii) and (iii) we get
a2 + b2 + c2 < 2(ab + bc + ca)
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{a^2+b^2+c^2}{a b+b c+c a}<2{/tex} ...(iv)
{tex}\because{/tex} Roots of the given equation are equation are real
{tex}\therefore{/tex} (a + b + c)2 - 3{tex}\lambda{/tex}(ab + be + ca) {tex}\ge{/tex} 0
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{a^2+b^2+c^2}{a b+b c+c a} \geq 3 \lambda{/tex} - 2 ...(v)
From (iv) and (v), {tex}3 \lambda-2<2 \Rightarrow \lambda<\frac{4}{3}{/tex}

questions.question: Let z $\neq$ -i be any complex number such that $\frac{ z -1}{ z + i }$ is a purely imaginary number. Then z + $\frac{1}{z}$ is:
questions.question_raw: Let z {tex}\neq{/tex} -i be any complex number such that {tex}\frac{ z -1}{ z + i }{/tex} is a purely imaginary number. Then z + {tex}\frac{1}{z}{/tex} is:

options.text: any non-zero real number other than 1.

questions.explanation:
Let z = x + iy
{tex}\frac{z-i}{z+i}{/tex} is purely imaginary means its real part is zero.
{tex}\frac{x+i y-i}{x+i y+i}=\frac{x+i(y-1)}{x+i(y+1)} \times \frac{x-i(y+1)}{x-i(y+1)}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{x^2-i x(y+1)+x i(y-1)+y^2-1}{x^2+(y+1)^2}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{x^2+y^2-1}{x^2+(y+1)^2}-\frac{2 x i}{x^2+(y+1)^2}{/tex}
For pure imaginary, we have {tex}\frac{x^2+y^2-1}{x^2+(y+1)^2}{/tex} = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 + y2 = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x + iy)(x - iy) = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x + iy = {tex}\frac{1}{x-i y}{/tex} = z and {tex}\frac{1}{z}{/tex} = x - iy
z + {tex}\frac{1}{z}{/tex} = (x + iy) + (x - iy) = 2x
{tex}\left(z+\frac{1}{z}\right){/tex} is any non-zero real number.

questions.question: Let S = {x: x $\in$ $\mathrm{ℝ}$$(\sqrt{3}+\sqrt{2})^{x^2-4}$ = $(\sqrt{3}-\sqrt{2})^{x^2-4}$ = 10}. Then n(S) is equal to
questions.question_raw: Let S = {x: x {tex}\in{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{ℝ}{/tex}{tex}(\sqrt{3}+\sqrt{2})^{x^2-4}{/tex} = {tex}(\sqrt{3}-\sqrt{2})^{x^2-4}{/tex} = 10}. Then n(S) is equal to
questions.explanation:
Let {tex}(\sqrt{3}+\sqrt{2})^{ x ^2-4}{/tex} = t, {tex}(\sqrt{3}-\sqrt{2})^{ x ^2-4}=\frac{1}{ t }{/tex}
{tex}t+\frac{1}{t}=10 \Rightarrow{/tex} t2 - 10t + 1 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} t = 5 {tex}\pm{/tex} 2{tex}\sqrt 6{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow(\sqrt{3}+\sqrt{2})^{x^2-4}{/tex} = 5 + 2{tex}\sqrt{6}, 5-2 \sqrt{6}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 - 4 = 2, -2 or x2 = 6, 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex} \pm \sqrt{2}, \pm \sqrt{6}{/tex}
There are total 4 solutions.

questions.question: The equation e^4x + 8e^3x + 13e^2x - 8e^x + 1 = 0, x $\in$ $\mathrm{ℝ}$ has:
questions.question_raw: The equation e4x + 8e3x + 13e2x - 8ex + 1 = 0, x {tex}\in{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{ℝ}{/tex} has:

options.text: Two solutions and both are negative
Two solutions and only one of them is negative
Four solutions two of which are negative

questions.explanation:
Let ex = t
Now, t4 + 8t3 + 13t2 - 8t + 1 = 0
Dividing equation by t2,
t2 + 8t + 13 {tex}-\frac{8}{t}+\frac{1}{t^2}=0 \Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}t^2+\frac{1}{t^2}+8\left(t-\frac{1}{t}\right){/tex} + 13 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow\left( t -\frac{1}{ t }\right)^2{/tex} + 2 + 8 {tex}\left( t -\frac{1}{ t }\right){/tex} + 13 = 0 
Let, t - {tex}\frac{1}{t}{/tex} = y
y2 + 8y + 15 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (y + 3)(y + 5) = 0
y = -3 or y = -5
So, t - {tex}\frac{1}{t}{/tex}= -3 or t - {tex}\frac{1}{t}{/tex} = -5
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} t2 + 3t - 1 = 0 or t2 + 5t - 1 = 0
t = {tex}\frac{-3 \pm \sqrt{13}}{2}{/tex} or t = {tex}\frac{-5 \pm \sqrt{29}}{2}{/tex}
Since, t = ex must be positive,
t = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{13}-3}{2} {/tex} or {tex}\frac{\sqrt{29}-5}{2}{/tex}
So, x = ln{tex}\left(\frac{\sqrt{13}-3}{2}\right){/tex} or x = ln{tex}\left(\frac{\sqrt{29}-5}{2}\right){/tex}
Hence two solution and both are negative.

questions.question: Let $\bar z$ denote the complex conjugate of a complex number z. If z is a non-zero complex number for which both real and imaginary parts of ($\bar z$)^2 + $\frac 1{z^2}$ are integers, then which of the following is/are possible value(s) of |z|?
questions.question_raw: Let {tex}\bar z{/tex} denote the complex conjugate of a complex number z. If z is a non-zero complex number for which both real and imaginary parts of ({tex}\bar z{/tex})2 + {tex}\frac 1{z^2}{/tex} are integers, then which of the following is/are possible value(s) of |z|?
questions.explanation:
Let z = r{tex}\cdot e^{i \theta}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \bar{z}=r e^{-i \theta} {/tex}
{tex}\therefore(\bar{z})^2+\frac{1}{z^2}=r^2 e^{-2 i \theta}+{/tex}{tex}\frac{1}{r^2 e^{2 i \theta}}=\left(r^2+\frac{1}{r^2}\right) e^ \frac{-2} {\theta}{/tex} = a + ib (say),
where a, b {tex}\in{/tex} Z
So, {tex}\left(r^2+\frac{1}{r^2}\right)^2{/tex} = a2 + b2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r8 - (a2 + b2 - 2)r4 + 1 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow r^4=\frac{\left( a ^2+ b ^2-2\right) \pm \sqrt{\left( a ^2+b^2-2\right)^2-4}}{2}{/tex}
For option: |z|4 = {tex}\frac{43+3 \sqrt{205}}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a2 + b2 = 45 i.e. (a, b) = ({tex}\pm{/tex} 6, {tex}\pm{/tex} 3) or ({tex}\pm{/tex} 3, {tex}\pm{/tex} 6)
For option: |z|4 = {tex}\frac{7+\sqrt{33}}{4} \Rightarrow a^2+b^2=\frac{11}{2}{/tex}
For option: a2 + b2 = {tex}\frac{13}{2}{/tex}
For option: a2 + b2 = {tex}\frac{13}{3}{/tex}

questions.question: The integer k, for which the inequality x^2 - 2(3k - 1)x + 8k^2 - 7 > 0 is valid for every x in R, is:
questions.question_raw: The integer k, for which the inequality x2 - 2(3k - 1)x + 8k2 - 7 > 0 is valid for every x in R, is:
questions.explanation:
x2 - 2(3k - 1)x + 8k2 - 7 > 0 , {tex}\forall x \in R{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} D < 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4(3k - 1)2 - 4{tex}\cdot{/tex}1{tex}\cdot{/tex}(8k2 - 7) < 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 9k2 - 6k + 1 - 8k2 +7 < 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} k2 - 6k + 8 < 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (k - 2)(k - 4) <  0

{tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} k {tex} \in{/tex} (2, 4); So, k = 3

questions.question: If $\lambda \in$ R is such that the sum of the cubes of the roots of the equation, x^2 + (2 - $\lambda$)x + (10 - $\lambda$) = 0 is minimum, then the magnitude of the difference of the roots of this equation is
questions.question_raw: If {tex}\lambda \in{/tex} R is such that the sum of the cubes of the roots of the equation, x2 + (2 - {tex}\lambda{/tex})x + (10 - {tex}\lambda{/tex}) = 0 is minimum, then the magnitude of the difference of the roots of this equation is
questions.explanation:
Let, the roots of the equation,
x2 + (2 - {tex}\lambda{/tex})x + (10 - {tex}\lambda{/tex}) = 0 are {tex}\alpha{/tex} and {tex}\beta{/tex}
Also roots of the given equation are
{tex}\frac{\lambda-2 \pm \sqrt{4-4 \lambda+\lambda^2-40+4 \lambda}}{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\lambda-2 \pm \sqrt{\lambda^2-36}}{2}{/tex}
The magnitude of the difference of the roots is {tex}\left|\sqrt{\lambda^2-36}\right|{/tex}
So,  {tex}\alpha^3+\beta^3{/tex} = {tex}(\alpha+\beta)^3{/tex} - {tex}3 \alpha \beta(\alpha+\beta){/tex}
= ({tex}\lambda{/tex} - 2)2 - 3(10 - {tex}\lambda{/tex}) ({tex}\lambda{/tex} - 2) = ({tex}\lambda{/tex} - 2) ({tex}\lambda{/tex}2 - {tex}\lambda{/tex} - 26) = f({tex}\lambda{/tex})
As f({tex}\lambda{/tex}) attains its minimum value at {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 4.
Therefore, the magnitude of the difference of the roots is {tex}|i \sqrt{20}|=2 \sqrt{5}{/tex}.

questions.question: If equations ax^2 + bx + c = 0 (a, b, c $\in$ R, a $\ne$ 0) and 2x^2 + 3x + 4 = 0 have a common root, then a : b : c equals:
questions.question_raw: If equations ax2 + bx + c = 0 (a, b, c {tex}\in{/tex} R, a {tex}\ne{/tex} 0) and 2x2 + 3x + 4 = 0 have a common root, then a : b : c equals:
questions.explanation:
Let {tex}\alpha{/tex}, {tex}\beta{/tex} be the common roots of both the equations.
For first equation ax2 + bx + c = 0, we have
{tex}\alpha{/tex} + {tex}\beta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-b}{a}{/tex} ...(i)
{tex}\alpha \cdot \beta=\frac{c}{a}{/tex} ...(ii)
For second equation 2x2 + 3x + 4 = 0,
we have
{tex}\alpha+\beta=\frac{-3}{2}{/tex} ...(iii)
{tex}\alpha \cdot \beta=\frac{2}{1}{/tex} ...(iv)
Now, from (i) & (iii) & from (ii) & (iv)
{tex}\frac{-b}{a}=\frac{-3}{2} {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{b}{a}=\frac {\frac{3}{2}}{1}{/tex} ...(v)
{tex}\frac{c}{a}=\frac{2}{1}{/tex} ...(vi)
From (v) and (vi) a : b : c = 1 : {tex}\frac 32{/tex} : 2
= 2 : 3 : 4

questions.question: Let $r$ and $\theta$ respectively be the modulus and amplitude of the complex number $z=2-i\left(2 \tan \frac{5 \pi}{8}\right)$, then $(r, \theta)$ is equal to
questions.question_raw: Let {tex}r{/tex} and {tex}\theta{/tex} respectively be the modulus and amplitude of the complex number {tex}z=2-i\left(2 \tan \frac{5 \pi}{8}\right){/tex}, then {tex}(r, \theta){/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation:
{tex}{z}=2-i\left(2 \tan \frac{5 \pi}{8}\right){/tex}
{tex}=2-2 i \frac{\sin \frac{5 \pi}{8}}{\cos \frac{5 \pi}{8}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{2}{\cos \frac{5 \pi}{8}}\left(\cos \frac{5 \pi}{8}-i \sin \frac{5 \pi}{8}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\frac{2}{\cos \left(\pi-\frac{3 \pi}{8}\right)}\left(\cos \left(\pi-\frac{3 \pi}{8}\right)-i \sin \left(\pi-\frac{3 \pi}{8}\right)\right){/tex}
{tex}=\frac{-2}{\cos \frac{3 \pi}{8}}\left(-\cos \frac{3 \pi}{8}-i \sin \frac{3 \pi}{8}\right){/tex}
{tex}=2 \sec \frac{3 \pi}{8}\left(\cos \frac{3 \pi}{8}+i \sin \frac{3 \pi}{8}\right){/tex}
In polar form any complex no. {tex}z{/tex} can be written
{tex}z =|z|(\cos \theta+i \sin \theta){/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}|z| =r{/tex}
{tex}=2 \sec \frac{3 \neq}{8}{/tex}
{tex}\operatorname{Arg}(z) =\theta{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{3 \neq}{8}{/tex}

questions.question: Let a complex number $z,|z| \neq 1$, satisfy $\log _{\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}}\left(\frac{|z|+11}{(|z|-1)^{2}}\right) \leq 2$. Then, the largest value of $|z|$ is equal to ________.
questions.question_raw: Let a complex number {tex}z,|z| \neq 1{/tex}, satisfy {tex}\log _{\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}}\left(\frac{|z|+11}{(|z|-1)^{2}}\right) \leq 2{/tex}. Then, the largest value of {tex}|z|{/tex} is equal to ________.
questions.explanation:
Given that {tex}|z| \neq 1{/tex}
{tex}\log _{\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}}\left(\frac{|z|+11}{(|z|-1)^{2}}\right) \leq 2{/tex}
Here base of logarithm lies between 0 and 1
So,
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{|z|+11}{(|z|-1)^{2}} \geq\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{|z|+11}{(|z|-1)^{2}} \geq \frac{1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 2|z|+22 \geq(|z|-1)^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 2|z|+22 \geq|z|^{2}-2|z|+1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow |z|^{2}-4|z|-21 \leq 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow (|z|-7)(|z|+3) \leq 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow |z|-7 \leq 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow |z| \leq 7{/tex}
So, largest value of {tex}|z|{/tex} is 7.

questions.question:
Let $\arg (z)$ represent the principal argument of the complex number $z$.
Then, $|z|=3$ and $\arg (z-1)-\arg (z+1)=\frac{\pi}{4}$ intersect.
questions.question_raw:
Let {tex}\arg (z){/tex} represent the principal argument of the complex number {tex}z{/tex}.
Then, {tex}|z|=3{/tex} and {tex}\arg (z-1)-\arg (z+1)=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} intersect.
questions.explanation:
Given: {tex}|z|=3{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} It represents a circle of radius 3 and centre at {tex}(0,0){/tex}
And {tex}\arg (z-1)-\arg (z+1)=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \operatorname{agr}\left(\frac{z-1}{z+1}\right)=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow z{/tex} is on major are of circle having {tex}P Q{/tex} as chord and {tex}R(0, a){/tex} as centre of the circle.

{tex}\text { So, } \angle P A Q =\angle O R P=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow O P =O R=a=1{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Coordinates of {tex}R{/tex} is {tex}(0,1){/tex}
So, radius {tex}=R P=\sqrt{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore O B=O R+\sqrt{2}=1+\sqrt{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow B=(0,1+\sqrt{2}){/tex}

So, it is clear from figure, both curves do not intersect.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} No {tex}z{/tex} satisfy both the equation.

questions.question:
If $z$ and $\omega$ are two complex numbers such that $|z \omega|=1$ and $\arg (z)-\arg (\omega)=\frac{3 \pi}{2}$, then $\arg \left(\frac{1-2 \bar{z} \omega}{1+3 \bar{z} \omega}\right)$ is:
(Here arg(z) denotes the principal argument of complex number z)
questions.question_raw:
If {tex}z{/tex} and {tex}\omega{/tex} are two complex numbers such that {tex}|z \omega|=1{/tex} and {tex}\arg (z)-\arg (\omega)=\frac{3 \pi}{2}{/tex}, then {tex}\arg \left(\frac{1-2 \bar{z} \omega}{1+3 \bar{z} \omega}\right){/tex} is:
(Here arg(z) denotes the principal argument of complex number z)
questions.explanation:
Given, {tex}|z \omega|=1{/tex} and {tex}\arg (z)-\arg (\omega)=\frac{3 \pi}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow|z||\omega|=1{/tex} and {tex}\arg (z)=\arg (\omega)+\frac{3 \pi}{2}{/tex}
Let {tex}\omega=r e^{i \theta} \Rightarrow z=\frac{1}{r} e^{i\left(\frac{3 \pi}{2}+\theta\right)}{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{1-2 \bar{z} \omega}{1+3 \bar{z} \omega} =\frac{1-2 r e^{i \theta} \frac{1}{r} e^{i\left(-\frac{3 \pi}{2}-\theta\right)}}{1+3 r e^{i \theta} \frac{1}{r} e^{i\left(-\frac{3 \pi}{2}-\theta\right)}} {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{1-2 e^{-i 3 \pi / 2}}{1+3 e^{-i 3 \pi / 2}}=\frac{1-2 i}{1+3 i} {/tex}
{tex} \text { So, } {/tex}arg {tex} \left(\frac{1-2 \bar{z} \omega}{1+3 \bar{z} \omega}\right) =\arg \left(\frac{1-2 i}{1+3 i}\right) {/tex}
{tex} =\tan ^{-1}(-2)-\tan ^{-1}(3) {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{\pi}{4} {/tex}

questions.question: If $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are the distinct roots of the equation $x^{2}+(3)^{1 / 4} x+3^{1 / 2}=0$, then the value of $\alpha^{96}\left(\alpha^{12}-1\right)+\beta^{96}\left(\beta^{12}-1\right)$ is equal to:
questions.question_raw: If {tex}\alpha{/tex} and {tex}\beta{/tex} are the distinct roots of the equation {tex}x^{2}+(3)^{1 / 4} x+3^{1 / 2}=0{/tex}, then the value of {tex}\alpha^{96}\left(\alpha^{12}-1\right)+\beta^{96}\left(\beta^{12}-1\right){/tex} is equal to:
questions.explanation:
Given {tex}x^{2}+(3)^{1 / 4} x+3^{1 / 2}=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x^{2}+\sqrt{3}=-3^{1 / 4} x{/tex}
Squaring both sides,
{tex}\Rightarrow x^{4}+2 \sqrt{3} x^{2}+3 =\sqrt{3} x^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x^{4}+\sqrt{3} x^{2}+3 =0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x^{4}+3 =-\sqrt{3} x^{2}{/tex}
Now squaring both the sides again,
{tex}\Rightarrow x^{8}+6 x^{4}+9=3 x^{4}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x^{8}+3 x^{4}+9=0{/tex}
Put {tex}x=\alpha, \alpha^{8}=-9-3 \alpha^{4}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \alpha^{12}=-9 \alpha^{4}-3 \alpha^{8}{/tex} {tex}=-9 \alpha^{4}-3\left(-9-3 \alpha^{4}\right)=27{/tex}
Similarly {tex}\beta^{12}=27{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \alpha^{96}\left(\alpha^{12}-1\right)+\beta^{96}\left(\beta^{12}-1\right){/tex}
{tex}=(27)^{8} \times 26+(27)^{8} \times 26=52 \times(27)^{8}{/tex}
{tex}=52 \times 3^{24}{/tex}

questions.question: The probability of selecting integers $a \in[-5,30]$ such that $x^{2}+2(a+4) x-5 a+$ $64 \gt 0$, for all $x \in {R}$, is:
questions.question_raw: The probability of selecting integers {tex}a \in[-5,30]{/tex} such that {tex}x^{2}+2(a+4) x-5 a+{/tex} {tex}64 \gt 0{/tex}, for all {tex}x \in {R}{/tex}, is:
questions.explanation:
Given that {tex}x^{2}+2(a+4) x-(5 a-64) \gt 0{/tex}
Comparing given quadratic equation with general form, {tex}{A} x^{2}+{B} x+{C}{/tex}
Here, {tex}{A}=1, {~B}=2(a+4), {C}=-(5 a-64){/tex}
{tex}\text { So, } {D} \lt 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow {~B}^{2}-4 {AC} \lt 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 4(a+4)^{2}+4(5 a-64) \lt 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow (a+4)^{2}+(5 a-64) \lt 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow a^{2}+13 a-48 \lt 0{/tex}
{tex}\therefore a=\frac{-13 \pm \sqrt{169+192}}{2}=-16,3{/tex}
So, {tex}a \in(-16,3){/tex}
Since {tex}a{/tex} is integer {tex}\Rightarrow a=-5,-4,-3,-2,-1,0,1,2{/tex} as a {tex}\in[-5,30]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Required probability {tex}=\frac{8}{36}=\frac{2}{9}{/tex}

questions.question: The equation arg $\left(\frac{z-1}{z+1}\right)=\frac{\pi}{4}$ represents a circle with:
questions.question_raw: The equation arg {tex}\left(\frac{z-1}{z+1}\right)=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} represents a circle with:
questions.explanation:
Let {tex}z=x+i y{/tex}
So, {tex}\frac{z-1}{z+1}=\frac{x+i y-1}{x+i y+1}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{z-1}{z+1}=\left(\frac{x+i y-1}{x+i y+1}\right) \times\left(\frac{(x+1)-i y}{(x+1)-i y}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\frac{(x+1)(x-1)-i y(x-1)+i y(x+1)-i^{2} y^{2}}{(x+1)^{2}+y^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{z-1}{z+1}=\frac{\left(x^{2}+y^{2}-1\right)+i(x y+y-x y+y)}{(x+1)^{2}+y^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{z-1}{z+1}=\frac{\left(x^{2}+y^{2}-1\right)+2 i y}{(x+1)^{2}+y^{2}}{/tex}
Given, {tex}\arg \left(\frac{z-1}{z+1}\right)=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{2 y}{x^{2}+y^{2}-1} =\tan \frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{2 y}{x^{2}+y^{2}-1} =1{/tex}
{tex}x^{2}+y^{2}-1 =2 y{/tex}
{tex}x^{2}+y^{2}-2 y-1 =0{/tex}
{tex}(x-0)^{2}+(y-1)^{2} =(\sqrt{2})^{2}{/tex}
So, centre {tex}(0,1) \&{/tex} Radius {tex}=\sqrt{2}{/tex} Unit

questions.question: The number of real solutions of the equation $3\left(x^2+\frac{1}{x^2}\right)-2\left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)+5=0$, is
questions.question_raw: The number of real solutions of the equation {tex}3\left(x^2+\frac{1}{x^2}\right)-2\left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)+5=0{/tex}, is
questions.explanation:
{tex} 3\left(x^2+\frac{1}{x^2}\right)-2\left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)+5=0 {/tex}
{tex} 3\left[\left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)^2-2\right]-2\left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)+5=0 {/tex}
Let {tex}{x}+\frac{1}{{x}}={t}{/tex}
{tex} 3 t^2-2 t-1=0 {/tex}
{tex} 3 t^2-3 t+t-1=0 {/tex}
{tex} 3 t(t-1)+1(t-1)=0 {/tex}
{tex} (t-1)(3 t+1)=0 {/tex}
{tex} t=1,-\frac{1}{3} {/tex}
{tex} x+\frac{1}{x}=1,-\frac{1}{3} \Rightarrow \text { No solution. } {/tex}

questions.question: Let $p$ and $q$ be two positive numbers such that $\mathrm{p}+\mathrm{q}=2$ and $\mathrm{p}^4+\mathrm{q}^4=272$. Then p and q are roots of the equation:
questions.question_raw: Let {tex}p{/tex} and {tex}q{/tex} be two positive numbers such that {tex}\mathrm{p}+\mathrm{q}=2{/tex} and {tex}\mathrm{p}^4+\mathrm{q}^4=272{/tex}. Then p and q are roots of the equation:
questions.explanation:
{tex} \text { Consider }\left(p_2+q^2\right)^2-2 p^2 q^2=272 {/tex}
{tex} \left((p+q)^2-2 p q\right)^2-2 p^2 q^2=272 {/tex}
{tex} 16-16 p q+2 p^2 q^2=272 {/tex}
{tex} (p q)^2-8 p q-128=0 {/tex}
{tex} ({pq})^2-8 {pq}-128=0 {/tex}
{tex} {pq}=\frac{8 \pm 24}{2}=16,-8 {/tex}
{tex} \therefore {pq}=16 {/tex}
{tex}\therefore {/tex} Required equation: {tex}{x}^2-(2) {x}+16=0{/tex}

questions.question: Let $z_{1}, z_{2}$ and $z_{3}$ be three complex numbers on the circle $|z|=1$ with $\underset{\pi}{\arg \left(z_{1}\right)}=\frac{-\pi}{4}$, $\arg \left(z_{2}\right)=0$ and $\arg \left(z_{3}\right)=\frac{\pi}{4}$. If $\left|z_{1} \bar{z}_{2}+z_{2} \bar{z}_{3}+z_{3} \bar{z}_{1}\right|^{2}$ $=\alpha+\beta \sqrt{2}, \alpha, \beta \in Z$, then the value of $\alpha^{2}+\beta^{2}$ is:
questions.question_raw: Let {tex}z_{1}, z_{2}{/tex} and {tex}z_{3}{/tex} be three complex numbers on the circle {tex}|z|=1{/tex} with {tex}\underset{\pi}{\arg \left(z_{1}\right)}=\frac{-\pi}{4}{/tex}, {tex}\arg \left(z_{2}\right)=0{/tex} and {tex}\arg \left(z_{3}\right)=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}. If {tex}\left|z_{1} \bar{z}_{2}+z_{2} \bar{z}_{3}+z_{3} \bar{z}_{1}\right|^{2}{/tex} {tex}=\alpha+\beta \sqrt{2}, \alpha, \beta \in Z{/tex}, then the value of {tex}\alpha^{2}+\beta^{2}{/tex} is:
questions.explanation:
Given, {tex}z_{1}, z_{2}, z_{3}{/tex} are three complex numbers and lies on circle {tex}|z|=1{/tex} and
{tex}\operatorname{Arg}\left(z_{1}\right)=\frac{-\pi}{4}{/tex}
{tex}\operatorname{Arg}\left(z_{2}\right)=0{/tex}
{tex}\operatorname{Arg}\left(z_{3}\right)=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}
using euler's formula
{tex}z=e^{-i \theta}=\cos (\theta)+i \sin (\theta){/tex}
{tex}\therefore z_{1}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(1-i) \bar{z}_{1}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(1+i){/tex}
{tex}{z}_{2}=1+0 \bar{z}_{2}=1{/tex}
{tex}z_{3}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(1+i) \bar{z}_{3}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(1-i){/tex}

{tex}\therefore\left|z_{1} \bar{z}_{2}+z_{2} \bar{z}_{3}+z_{3} \bar{z}_{1}\right|^{2}{/tex}
{tex}=\left|\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(1-i)+\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(1-i)+\frac{1}{2}(1+i)^{2}\right|^{2}{/tex}
{tex}=\left|\sqrt{2}-\sqrt{2} i+\frac{1}{2}(2 i)\right|^{2}{/tex}
{tex}=|\sqrt{2}+(1-\sqrt{2}) i|^{2}{/tex}
{tex}=2+(1-\sqrt{2})^{2}{/tex}
{tex}=2+1+2-2 \sqrt{2}{/tex}
{tex}=5-2 \sqrt{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \alpha=5, \beta=-2{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \alpha^{2}+\beta^{2}=25+4=29{/tex}

questions.question: Let ${P}_{n}=\alpha^{n}+\beta^{n}, n \in {~N}$, if ${P}_{10}=123, {P}_{9}=$ 76, ${P}_{8}=47$ and ${P}_{1}=1$, then the quadratic equation having roots $\frac{1}{\alpha}$ and $\frac{1}{\beta}$ is:
questions.question_raw: Let {tex}{P}_{n}=\alpha^{n}+\beta^{n}, n \in {~N}{/tex}, if {tex}{P}_{10}=123, {P}_{9}={/tex} 76, {tex}{P}_{8}=47{/tex} and {tex}{P}_{1}=1{/tex}, then the quadratic equation having roots {tex}\frac{1}{\alpha}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{1}{\beta}{/tex} is:
questions.explanation:
{tex} {P}_n =\alpha^n+\beta^n {/tex}
{tex} {P}_{10} =\alpha^{10}+\beta^{10}=123 {/tex}
{tex} {P}_9 =\alpha^9+\beta^9=76 {/tex}
{tex} {P}_8 =\alpha^8+\beta^9=47 {/tex}
{tex} {P}_1 =\alpha+\beta=1 {/tex}
{tex} {P}_9+{P}_8 =\alpha^9+\beta^9+\alpha^8+\beta^8 {/tex}
{tex} =76+47 {/tex}
{tex} \alpha^8(\alpha+1)+\beta^8(\beta+1) =123 {/tex}
{tex} \therefore \alpha^8(\alpha+1)+\beta^8(\beta+1) {/tex} {tex}=\alpha^{10}+\beta^{10} {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \alpha^2 =\alpha+1 {/tex}
{tex} \text { and } \beta^2 =\beta+1 {/tex}
Given quadratic equation is,
{tex} x^2 =x+1 {/tex}
{tex} x^2-x-1 =0 {/tex}
{tex} \alpha+\beta =1, \alpha \beta=-1 {/tex}
{tex} \frac{1}{\alpha}+\frac{1}{\beta} =\frac{\alpha+\beta}{\alpha \beta}=\frac{1}{-1} {/tex}
{tex} \frac{1}{\alpha} \cdot \frac{1}{\beta} =\frac{1}{\alpha \beta}=-1 {/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Quadratic equation is
{tex} x^2-x(-1)-1=0 {/tex}
{tex} x^2+x-1=0 {/tex}

questions.question: Let $z$ be a complex number such that $|z|=1$. If $\frac{2+k^{2} z}{k+\bar{z}}=k z, k \in {R}$, then the maximum distance of $k+i k^{2}$ from the circle $|z-(1+2 i)|=1$ is:
questions.question_raw: Let {tex}z{/tex} be a complex number such that {tex}|z|=1{/tex}. If {tex}\frac{2+k^{2} z}{k+\bar{z}}=k z, k \in {R}{/tex}, then the maximum distance of {tex}k+i k^{2}{/tex} from the circle {tex}|z-(1+2 i)|=1{/tex} is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\frac{2+k^{2} z}{k+\bar{z}}=k z{/tex}
{tex}2+k^{2} z=k^{2} z+k z \bar{z}{/tex}
{tex}2=k z \bar{z}{/tex}
{tex}\left\{|z|^{2}=z \bar{z}(\because|z|=1)\right\}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 2=k \times 1{/tex}
{tex}k+i k^{2}=2+4 i{/tex}
{tex}\text { Let } {P}=(2,4){/tex}
Given, {tex}|z-(1+2 i)|=1{/tex}
{tex}\text { Centre of circle } =(1,2){/tex}
{tex}\text { Radius }(r) =1{/tex}

Maximum distance of P from circle {tex}={OP}+r{/tex}
{tex}({OP}+r)=\sqrt{1+4}+1=\sqrt{5}+1{/tex}

questions.question: Let $a \neq b$ be two-zero real numbers. Then the number of elements in the set $X=\left\{z \in {C}: \operatorname{Re}\left(a z^{2}\right.\right.$ $+b z)=a$ and $\left.\operatorname{Re}\left(b z^{2}+a z\right)=b\right\}$ is equal to:
questions.question_raw: Let {tex}a \neq b{/tex} be two-zero real numbers. Then the number of elements in the set {tex}X=\left\{z \in {C}: \operatorname{Re}\left(a z^{2}\right.\right.{/tex} {tex}+b z)=a{/tex} and {tex}\left.\operatorname{Re}\left(b z^{2}+a z\right)=b\right\}{/tex} is equal to:
questions.explanation:
We know that {tex}z+\bar{z}=2 \operatorname{Re}(z){/tex}
{tex}\therefore\left(a z^{2}+b z\right)+\left(a \bar{z}^{2}+b \bar{z}\right)=2 a{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow a\left(z^{2}+\bar{z}^{2}\right)+b(z+\bar{z}) 2 a{/tex} ...(1)
Add {tex}\left(b z^{2}+a z\right)+\left(b \bar{z}^{2}+a \bar{z}\right)=2 b{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow b\left(z^{2}+\bar{z}^{2}\right)+a(z+\bar{z})=2 b{/tex} ...(2)
From (i) {tex}\times b-{/tex} (ii) {tex}\times a{/tex}
{tex}\left(b^{2}-a^{2}\right)(z+\bar{z})=0{/tex}
{tex}z+\bar{z}=0 (\because a \neq b){/tex}
From (i) {tex}\times b-{/tex} (ii) {tex}\times a{/tex}
{tex}\left(a^{2}-b^{2}\right)\left(z^{2}+\bar{z}^{2}\right)=2\left(a^{2}-b^{2}\right) \left[a^{2} \neq b^{2}\right]{/tex}
{tex}z^{2}+\bar{z}^{2}=2 \Rightarrow(z+\bar{z})^{2}-2 z \bar{z}=2{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow z \bar{z}=-1 \Rightarrow 1+1^{2}=1{/tex} (No solution)
But when {tex}a=-b{/tex}
{tex}\operatorname{Re}\left(a z^{2}-a z\right)=a{/tex}
Put {tex}z=x+i y{/tex}
{tex}\therefore x^{2}-x-1=y^{2}{/tex}
For any real value of {tex}y{/tex} there two values of {tex}x{/tex}, hence infinite complex number are possible.

questions.question:
For $a \in {C}$, let ${A}=\{z \in {C}: \operatorname{Re}(a+\bar{z}) \gt \operatorname{Im}$ $(\bar{a}+z) \mid\}$ and ${B}=\{z \in {C}: \operatorname{Re}(a+\bar{z}) \lt \operatorname{Im}(\bar{a}+z)\}$.
The two statements:
(S1): If Re (a), Im (a) >0, then the set A contains all the real numbers
(S2): If Re (a), Im (a) < 0, then the set B contains all the real numbers,
questions.question_raw:
For {tex}a \in {C}{/tex}, let {tex}{A}=\{z \in {C}: \operatorname{Re}(a+\bar{z}) \gt \operatorname{Im}{/tex} {tex}(\bar{a}+z) \mid\}{/tex} and {tex}{B}=\{z \in {C}: \operatorname{Re}(a+\bar{z}) \lt \operatorname{Im}(\bar{a}+z)\}{/tex}.
The two statements:
(S1): If Re (a), Im (a) >0, then the set A contains all the real numbers
(S2): If Re (a), Im (a) < 0, then the set B contains all the real numbers,
questions.explanation:
Given that {tex}{A}=\left\{z \in {C}: \operatorname{Re}(a+\bar{z}) \gt {I}_{m}(\bar{a}+z)\right\}{/tex} and
{tex}{B}=\left\{x \in {C}: \operatorname{Re}(a+\bar{z}) \lt {I}_{m}(\bar{a}+z)\right\}{/tex}
Let {tex}z=x+{i} y{/tex} and {tex}a=p+i q{/tex}
Then, {tex}\operatorname{Re}(a+\bar{z}) \gt {I}_{m}(\bar{a}+z){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \operatorname{Re}(p+i q+x-{i} y) \gt {I}_{m}(p-{iq}+x+{i} y){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow p+x \gt y-q{/tex}
which is not true for all real values of {tex}p, q, x, y{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow {S}_{1}{/tex} is false
Again, {tex}\operatorname{Re}(a+\bar{z}) \lt {I}_{m}(\bar{a}+z){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow p+x \lt y-q{/tex}
which is also not true for all real values of {tex}p, q, x, y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {S}_{2}{/tex} is false.

questions.question: Let C be the circle in the complex plane with centre $z_{0}=\frac{1}{2}(1+3 i)$ and radius $r=1$. Let $z_{1}=$ $1+i$ and the complex number $z_{2}$ be outside the circle C such that $\left|z_{1}-z_{0}\right|\left|z_{2}-z_{0}\right|=1$. If $z_{0}, z_{1}$ and $z_{2}$ are collinear, then the smaller value of $\left|z_{2}\right|^{2}$ is equal to
questions.question_raw: Let C be the circle in the complex plane with centre {tex}z_{0}=\frac{1}{2}(1+3 i){/tex} and radius {tex}r=1{/tex}. Let {tex}z_{1}={/tex} {tex}1+i{/tex} and the complex number {tex}z_{2}{/tex} be outside the circle C such that {tex}\left|z_{1}-z_{0}\right|\left|z_{2}-z_{0}\right|=1{/tex}. If {tex}z_{0}, z_{1}{/tex} and {tex}z_{2}{/tex} are collinear, then the smaller value of {tex}\left|z_{2}\right|^{2}{/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation:
Given that {tex}z_{0}=\frac{1+3 i}{2}{/tex} is centre and {tex}r=1{/tex} is radius of circle C and {tex}z_{1}=1+i{/tex}
Now {tex}\left|z_{1}-z_{0}\right|=\left|1+i-\frac{1}{2}-\frac{3}{2} i\right|{/tex} {tex}=\left|\frac{1}{2}-\frac{1}{2} i\right|=\sqrt{\frac{1}{4}+\frac{1}{4}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow\left|z_{1}-z_{0}\right|=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}
so {tex}\left|z_{1}-z_{0}\right|\left|z_{2}-z_{0}\right|=1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow\left|z_{2}-z_{0}\right|=\sqrt{2}{/tex}
Now {tex}z_{1} z_{2}=z_{0} z_{2}-z_{0} z_{1}{/tex}

or {tex}\left|z_{1}-z_{2}\right|=\left|z_{0}-z_{1}\right|-\left|z_{0}-z_{1}\right|{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{2}-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}
and in {tex}\Delta {O} z_{1} z_{2}{/tex}
{tex}\left|z_{2}\right|^{2}=\left|z_{1}-0\right|^{2}+\left|z_{1}-z_{2}\right|^{2}{/tex}
{tex}=(\sqrt{2})^{2}+\left(\sqrt{2}-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^{2}{/tex} {tex}=2+2+\frac{1}{2}-2=\frac{5}{2}{/tex}
Hence, value of {tex}\left|z_{2}\right|^{2}=\frac{5}{2}{/tex}

questions.question: Let $a, b$ be two real numbers such that $a b \lt 0$. If the complex number $\frac{1+a i}{b+i}$ is of unit modulus and $a+i b$ lies on the circle $|z-1|=|2 z|$, then a possible value of $\frac{1+[a]}{4 b}$, where $[t]$ is greatest integer function, is:
questions.question_raw: Let {tex}a, b{/tex} be two real numbers such that {tex}a b \lt 0{/tex}. If the complex number {tex}\frac{1+a i}{b+i}{/tex} is of unit modulus and {tex}a+i b{/tex} lies on the circle {tex}|z-1|=|2 z|{/tex}, then a possible value of {tex}\frac{1+[a]}{4 b}{/tex}, where {tex}[t]{/tex} is greatest integer function, is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\text { Since, }|1+a i|=|b+i|{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow a^{2}+1=b^{2}+1 \Rightarrow a^{2}=b^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\text { And }|a+i b-1|=|2 a+2 b i| \mid{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow a^{2}+1-2 a+b^{2}=4 a^{2}+4 b^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 3 a^{2}+3 b^{2}+2 a-1=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 6 a^{2}+2 a-1{/tex} {tex}\left(\because a^2=b^2\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow a=\frac{-2 \pm \sqrt{4+24}}{12}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{-2 \pm 2 \sqrt{7}}{12}=\frac{-1 \pm \sqrt{7}}{6}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore(a, b) \equiv\left(\frac{-1+\sqrt{7}}{6}, \frac{1-\sqrt{7}}{6}\right) \text { or }\left(\frac{-1-\sqrt{7}}{6}, \frac{1+\sqrt{7}}{6}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow[a]=0{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1+[a]}{4 b}=0 \text { or } \frac{-(1+\sqrt{7})}{4}{/tex}
